topics.title: Introduction to Various Joining Techniques in Ship Building Industry; Welding and Classification of Welding; Fuel Gases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Various Joining Techniques in Ship Building Industry; Welding and Classification of Welding; Fuel Gases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Joining Techniques in Shipbuilding

**Joining** is the fundamental manufacturing process by which two or more metallic (or non-metallic) parts are permanently or temporarily united to form a single, structurally continuous component. In the **shipbuilding industry**, joining techniques form the backbone of hull fabrication, superstructure erection, pipework integration, engine-seat fabrication, and outfitting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Industry-Aligned):**
> *Joining techniques in shipbuilding refer to the established industrial processes — mechanical fastening, fusion welding, solid-state welding, brazing, soldering, and adhesive bonding — used to assemble plates, sections, stiffeners, pipes, and outfitting components into a serviceable marine vessel compliant with classification society rules (IACS members: ABS, DNV, LR, BV, IRS).*

### 1.1.1 Categorical Overview of Shipbuilding Joining Techniques

| S.No | Joining Family | Typical Shipboard Application |
|------|----------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Welding (Fusion)** | Hull plating, deck seams, bulkheads, tanks (dominant ~80 % of structural joints) |
| 2 | **Riveting (Mechanical)** | Older vessels, deck-to-side connections, secondary brackets |
| 3 | **Bolting (Mechanical)** | Stern-gland assemblies, shaft brackets, engine mountings |
| 4 | **Brazing & Soldering** | Copper-nickel pipework, refrigeration coils, electrical bus-bars |
| 5 | **Adhesive Bonding** | Composite deck overlays, GRP-pudding joints, sealing strips |
| 6 | **Solid-State Welding** | Aluminium superstructures, propeller shafts (friction welding) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Intuition — The "Surgical Stitch" Analogy:** Think of a ship hull as the human skeleton. Riveting is the old technique of sewing bones together with nails (visible, removable, weak under vibration). Welding is the modern method of *seamlessly fusing* bone to bone using controlled heat — yielding a continuous, stronger, lighter, and watertight structure. Modern shipbuilding has overwhelmingly adopted welding for this exact reason.

---

## 1.2 Welding — Formal Definition

**Welding** is defined by the **American Welding Society (AWS)** and adopted under **ISO 4063** as:

> *"A localized coalescence of materials produced by heating to suitable temperatures, with or without the application of pressure, and with or without the use of filler metal."*

The KTU 2024 curriculum emphasizes **three essential criteria** that must be simultaneously satisfied for any process to qualify as welding:

1. **Localized coalescence** — joining is restricted to the joint zone, not the bulk material.
2. **Metallurgical bonding** — atomic-level continuity must be achieved (vs. mechanical interlock).
3. **Heat + Pressure + Filler** — at least one (or a combination) of these agents must be applied.

---

## 1.3 Fuel Gases — Definition and Industrial Significance

A **fuel gas** is a combustible gas (or mixture of gases) that, when burned with a **support gas** (typically oxygen or air), releases sufficient thermal energy to perform useful work such as **welding, cutting, heating, or flame-straightening** in shipyards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Industrial Standard:** In oxy-fuel welding (OFW), the **flame temperature** and **heat release rate per unit volume** of the fuel gas dictate its suitability for cutting vs. welding. **Acetylene (C₂H₂)** is unique because it produces a *reducing (carburizing)* primary flame that protects molten steel from oxidation — making it the only fuel gas permitted under AWS for *fusion welding of low-carbon steel* without flux.

### 1.3.1 Primary Fuel Gases in Shipyard Practice

- **Acetylene (C₂H₂)** — the universal reference fuel gas.
- **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)** — propane + butane blend; widely used for *cutting* and *preheating*.
- **Hydrogen (H₂)** — specialty applications, micro-welding.
- **Natural Gas (mainly CH₄)** — heating and large-scale cutting.
- **Propylene / Propane / Butane** — backup cutting gases.
- **MAPP Gas** (Methyl-Acetylene-Propadiene) — high-temperature cutting without cylinder instability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Cooking Burner" Viewpoint:** Think of a gas welding torch as a high-precision Bunsen burner. The **fuel gas** is the "fuel" (like LPG in your kitchen), the **oxygen** is the "air-supply amplifier", and the **flame cone** is the "cooking zone." By adjusting the O/F ratio, the cook (welder) switches between a *neutral flame* (perfect stoichiometric combustion — the "right recipe"), an *oxidizing flame* (too much oxygen — burnt food), or a *carburizing flame* (excess fuel — undercooked). Ship welders live by this daily adjustment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Welding — Hierarchical Logic

Welding processes are classified under **ISO 4063 / AWS A2.4** by the *nature of the energy source* and the *state of the base metal during joining*. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to memorize the **6 primary families** and the **dominant shipyard processes** under each.

### 2.1.1 Six Primary Welding Families

1. **Arc Welding** — electrical arc melts the base metal.
2. **Gas Welding (Oxy-Fuel)** — combustion of fuel gas with oxygen.
3. **Resistance Welding** — Joule heating from electric current through joint.
4. **Solid-State Welding** — bonding without melting (below melting point).
5. **High-Energy Beam Welding** — LASER (LBW) and Electron Beam (EBW).
6. **Thermochemical Welding** — thermit welding (aluminothermic).

### 2.1.2 Sub-Classification of Arc Welding (KTU High-Yield Topic)

| Process Code (ISO 4063) | Full Name | Shipyard Use |
|--------------------------|-----------|--------------|
| **111** | Manual Metal Arc Welding (SMAW / Stick) | Hull plate butt joints, deck seams |
| **121** | Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) | Long-seam panel-line welding |
| **131** | MIG / GMAW (Metal Inert Gas) | Thin-plate panel fabrication |
| **141** | TIG / GTAW (Tungsten Inert Gas) | Root pass on pipes, stainless steel |
| **15** | Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) | Special stainless & aluminium superstructures |
| **17** | Electroslag / Electrogas (ESW / EGW) | Vertical thick-plate bulkheads |

### 2.1.3 Sub-Classification of Gas Welding (Process Code 3)

- **311** — Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW) — *primary fusion welding gas process*.
- **312** — Oxy-Hydrogen Welding.
- **313** — Oxy-LPG Welding.
- **314** — Oxy-Natural Gas Welding.
- **32** — Air-Acetylene Welding / Brazing.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of the Oxy-Fuel Welding Process

The **Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)** process uses a *neutral, reducing or oxidizing flame* by adjusting the **Oxygen-to-Acetylene (O/A) volume ratio**. This is the foundational theory that examiners love to test.

### 2.2.1 The Three Flame Types

| Flame Type | O/A Ratio | Visual Cone | Industrial Application |
|------------|-----------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Carburizing (Reducing)** | O/A < 1 | Long, feathered, luminous white inner cone | Welding high-carbon steels, hardfacing |
| **Neutral** | O/A = 1 to 1.2 | Sharp, well-defined bright blue inner cone | **General-purpose welding of mild steel** |
| **Oxidizing** | O/A > 1.5 | Short, pointed, harsh blue-purple inner cone | Welding of brass, bronze, copper |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Neutral" is the shipyard default:** In a neutral flame, the O/A ratio is exactly *stoichiometric* — all carbon in C₂H₂ converts to CO₂, and all hydrogen to H₂O, leaving **no free carbon** in the flame envelope. Free carbon would otherwise dissolve into the weld pool, increasing hardness and promoting **cold-cracking** in marine steels — a Class Society rejectable defect.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Fuel Gases — Comparative Thermodynamics

### 2.3.1 Calorific Value — Definition

The **calorific value (CV)** of a fuel gas is the amount of heat released per unit volume (or mass) of gas, upon complete combustion with oxygen at standard reference conditions (**STP: 25 °C, 101.325 kPa**).

Two reference values:

- **Higher Calorific Value (HCV / GCV)** — includes heat recovered from condensation of water vapour.
- **Lower Calorific Value (LCV / NCV)** — excludes latent heat of water vapour; this is the *working value* used in welding.

### 2.3.2 Comparative Property Table of Fuel Gases (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

| Fuel Gas | Chemical Formula | LCV (MJ/m³) | Flame Temp with O₂ (°C) | O/F Stoichiometric Ratio | Suitability |
|----------|------------------|---------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|-------------|
| **Acetylene** | C₂H₂ | **48.8** | **3160** | 2.5 : 1 | **Welding + Cutting** (universal) |
| **Hydrogen** | H₂ | 10.8 | 2210 | 0.5 : 1 | Micro-welding, lead welding |
| **Propane** | C₃H₈ | 86.4 (per m³ liquid equiv.) ≈ 91.2 MJ/m³ gas | 2850 | 5.0 : 1 | Cutting, preheating |
| **Butane** | C₄H₁₀ | 121.3 (liquid) ≈ 110 MJ/m³ gas | 2900 | 6.5 : 1 | Cutting |
| **Natural Gas** | CH₄ (predominant) | 35.8 | 1950 | 2.0 : 1 | Heating only |
| **MAPP** | C₃H₄ + C₃H₆ | 88.5 | 2920 | 4.5 : 1 | Cutting, hardfacing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **acetylene has the LOWEST LCV of all fuel gases but the HIGHEST flame temperature.** This counter-intuitive behaviour arises because acetylene's combustion releases energy at an extremely *rapid volumetric rate* — the adiabatic flame temperature is governed by *energy density per unit time*, not total energy content. This is why acetylene produces the hottest, most concentrated flame.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **exam-grade essentials** for Module 1 of PENST741. Memorize the variables, units, and the **boundary conditions** that classify each equation as valid.

### Table 2.4 — Welding & Fuel Gas Master Equation Bank

| S.No | Quantity | Equation | Variable Definitions & Units |
|------|----------|----------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Heat Input in Arc Welding** | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot \eta \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | V = arc voltage (V); I = current (A); $\eta$ = process thermal efficiency; S = travel speed (mm/min); **H = kJ/mm** |
| 2 | **Heat Input (compact form)** | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S} \cdot 0.06 \cdot \eta$ | Same as above, expressed directly in kJ/mm |
| 3 | **Oxy-Acetylene Stoichiometric Equation** | $2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat}$ | 2 vol C₂H₂ + 5 vol O₂ → products; **O/A stoich = 2.5** |
| 4 | **Acetylene Combustion Energy (per mole)** | $\Delta H_{comb} = -1300\,\text{kJ/mol}$ | Standard enthalpy of combustion (HCV); **sign convention: exothermic** |
| 5 | **Volumetric Heat Release** | $Q_v = \text{LCV} \times \dot{V}$ | $\dot{V}$ = volumetric flow (m³/s); Q_v in kW |
| 6 | **Cylinder Pressure Drop (acetylene dissolved)** | $P_{safe} \le 1.03\,\text{MPa}$ | Above this, free acetylene becomes **thermodynamically unstable** (may detonate) |
| 7 | **Flame Temperature (adiabatic)** | $T_{ad} = T_i + \dfrac{\Delta H_{comb}}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}}$ | Adiabatic flame temp; depends on product heat capacity |
| 8 | **Cutting Oxygen Purity Factor** | $K_p = 0.0055 \times \Delta P$ | $\Delta P$ = % deviation from 99.5 % purity; reduces cutting speed |

### Variable Notes for Examiners

- $\eta$ (thermal efficiency) values: **SMAW = 0.75 to 0.85**, **GMAW = 0.65 to 0.75**, **GTAW = 0.40 to 0.60**, **SAW = 0.90 to 0.99**, **OAW = 0.40 to 0.50**, **PAW = 0.55 to 0.65**.
- The factor **60 / 1000** in Equation 1 converts (V × I) from W to kJ/min and S from mm/min to mm; final unit becomes **kJ/mm**.

### Real-World Utility of These Equations

> [!NOTE]
> In shipyards, the **heat input formula** is used to control *Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) width*, *residual stress*, and *distortion* of welded plates. Classification societies (DNV, LR) impose a **maximum heat input limit of 3.5 kJ/mm for DH36 grade hull plates** during in-process welding to prevent excessive grain coarsening.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heat Input Equation (Arc Welding)

The **heat input** is defined as the *arc energy transferred per unit length of weld*. This is the single most cited quantity in any shipbuilding welding procedure specification (WPS).

### 3.1.1 Foundational Statement

The electrical power supplied to the arc is:

$$P = V \cdot I \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

Here, $V$ is the arc voltage in **Volts**, and $I$ is the welding current in **Amperes**. The product $V \cdot I$ gives the instantaneous electrical power delivered to the arc in **Watts (J/s)**.

### 3.1.2 Conversion to Energy per Unit Length

The energy supplied per minute to the arc (as the arc travels) is:

$$E_{min} = V \cdot I \cdot 60 \quad \text{(J/min)}$$

The 60 multiplier converts per-second electrical power to per-minute energy.

The *length of weld* produced in one minute, given the travel speed $S$ in **mm/min**, is exactly $S$ mm. Therefore, the *gross energy per unit weld length* is:

$$E_{gross} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S} \quad \text{(J/mm)}$$

### 3.1.3 Introduction of Process Thermal Efficiency

Not all electrical energy reaches the workpiece. Some is lost as spatter, radiation, convection, and filler-metal heating. The fraction actually absorbed by the workpiece is **$\eta$ (eta)**. So:

$$E_{net} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60 \cdot \eta}{S} \quad \text{(J/mm)}$$

### 3.1.4 Final Conversion to kJ/mm

To express in kilojoules per millimetre (the unit required by IACS weld procedure standards), divide by 1000:

$$\boxed{H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60 \cdot \eta}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}}$$

This is the canonical heat-input equation used in every shipyard WPS.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Heat Input Calculation

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A shipyard uses **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)** to join two 20 mm thick **DH36 shipbuilding plates**. The welding parameters recorded on the WPS are:

- Arc voltage, $V$ = **32 V**
- Welding current, $I$ = **650 A**
- Travel speed, $S$ = **450 mm/min**
- Thermal efficiency of SAW, $\eta$ = **0.95**

Calculate the **net heat input** in kJ/mm and verify whether it complies with the **DNV limit of 3.5 kJ/mm** for DH36 grade.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the relevant equation from the formula sheet (Equation 1).

$$H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60 \cdot \eta}{S \cdot 1000}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{32 \times 650 \times 60 \times 0.95}{450 \times 1000} \\
  &= \dfrac{32 \times 650 \times 60 \times 0.95}{450\,000} \\
  &= \dfrac{1\,185\,600}{450\,000} \\
  &= 2.634\,\text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Convert the multiplication step-by-step to avoid arithmetic slip.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator: } & 32 \times 650 = 20\,800 \\
& 20\,800 \times 60 = 1\,248\,000 \\
& 1\,248\,000 \times 0.95 = 1\,185\,600 \\
\text{Denominator: } & 450 \times 1000 = 450\,000 \\
\text{Final ratio: } & \dfrac{1\,185\,600}{450\,000} = 2.634
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compliance check.

Since $H_{computed} = 2.634\,\text{kJ/mm} < H_{limit} = 3.5\,\text{kJ/mm}$, the procedure **is compliant** with the DNV classification rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If this WPS had shown a calculated heat input above 3.5 kJ/mm, the shipyard would be required to either reduce the current, increase the travel speed, or switch to a lower-efficiency process. This single calculation is the *gateway* between laboratory WPS approval and real hull fabrication.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Joining Technique by Shipboard Application

The following decision matrix is **high-yield** for KTU theory questions. It maps the *shipboard component* to the *optimal joining technique*.

| Shipboard Component | Material | Thickness (mm) | Recommended Joining | Justification |
|---------------------|----------|----------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Outer hull plating (keel block) | DH36/EH36 steel | 20 – 80 | **SAW (121)** | High deposition rate; long seams; one-side welding possible |
| Hull transverse bulkheads | Mild steel | 8 – 14 | **SMAW (111) / FCAW (136)** | Field-position welds; all-position capability |
| Deck-to-side shell fillet | DH36 | 10 – 20 | **FCAW (136) / GMAW (131)** | High deposition; reduced spatter; productivity |
| Stainless steel pipe root pass | 316L stainless | 3 – 8 | **GTAW / TIG (141)** | Superior root bead; no spatter |
| Aluminium superstructure | 5083-H116 | 4 – 10 | **MIG (131) / FSW** | FSW avoids HAZ softening |
| Copper-nickel pipework | Cu-Ni 90/10 | 2 – 6 | **Brazing (951)** / GTAW | Avoids melting copper (high thermal conductivity) |
| Stern-tube flange to hull | Cast steel | 30 – 50 | **SMAW (111)** with low-hydrogen electrode | Crack-resistant deposit |
| Old-vessel hull repair | Mild steel | any | **SMAW (111)** | Portable, rugged, all-position |
| Hull plate emergency cutting | DH36 | 10 – 50 | **Oxy-fuel cutting (acetylene/oxy)** | Fastest portable cutting |
| Composite-to-steel deck join | GRP / steel | 6 + 8 | **Adhesive bonding + mechanical fastening** | Dissimilar material coupling |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement for Acetylene Combustion

### 3.4.1 Problem

A shipyard gas manifold supplies acetylene to a cutting torch at a flow rate of **15 L/min (measured at STP)**. Calculate the **minimum stoichiometric oxygen flow** required for complete combustion.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write the balanced combustion equation (Equation 3 of the formula sheet):

$$2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat}$$

This tells us that 2 volumes of C₂H₂ require 5 volumes of O₂ for complete combustion.

**Step 2:** Determine the volumetric stoichiometric ratio.

$$\text{O/A ratio} = \dfrac{5\,\text{vol}\,\text{O}_2}{2\,\text{vol}\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} = 2.5$$

**Step 3:** Apply the ratio to the given C₂H₂ flow rate.

$$\dot{V}_{O_2,\,stoich} = \dot{V}_{C_2H_2} \times 2.5$$

$$\dot{V}_{O_2,\,stoich} = 15\,\text{L/min} \times 2.5 = 37.5\,\text{L/min}$$

**Step 4:** Account for the **practical excess** (typically 10 %–15 % beyond stoichiometric to ensure complete combustion and to compensate for incomplete mixing).

$$\dot{V}_{O_2,\,practical} = 37.5 \times 1.10 = 41.25\,\text{L/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Welding Rule of Thumb:** Most shipyard gas panels preset a *practical O/A ratio of 1.1 to 1.2* when the flame is set to *neutral* (instead of the strict stoichiometric 2.5). This is because in the actual welding nozzle, oxygen from ambient air supplements the pure oxygen supply. The textbook ratio of 2.5 is the *ideal stoichiometric value* used for chemistry calculations, while the *operational O/A ratio* in a torch is typically lower.

---

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — Short-Answer Type `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define welding as per AWS. List any four primary families of welding processes.**

**Model Answer:**

> *Welding is a localized coalescence of materials produced by heating to suitable temperatures, with or without the application of pressure, and with or without the use of filler metal (AWS definition).*
>
> *The four primary families are:*
> 1. *Arc welding (e.g., SMAW, GTAW)*
> 2. *Oxy-fuel gas welding (e.g., OAW)*
> 3. *Resistance welding (e.g., spot, seam)*
> 4. *Solid-state welding (e.g., friction stir welding)*
> 5. *High-energy beam welding (e.g., LASER, EBW)*
> 6. *Thermochemical welding (e.g., thermit welding)*

---

### Question A.2 — Short-Answer Type `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: What is the stoichiometric O/A ratio for oxy-acetylene combustion? What happens when this ratio deviates above 1.5?**

**Model Answer:**

> *The stoichiometric O/A ratio is **2.5 : 1** (2 volumes C₂H₂ + 5 volumes O₂ → products). When the practical ratio deviates significantly above the neutral setting (>1.5 in operational terms, or with strong excess oxygen), the flame becomes **oxidizing**, characterized by a short, pointed, harsh-blue inner cone. Such a flame introduces excess oxygen into the weld pool, leading to oxide inclusions, porosity, and embrittlement of the weld metal.*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Alert:**
> For Part B answers, examiners *deduct marks* if students (a) skip the **balanced chemical equation** when discussing combustion, (b) forget to **state units** of every derived quantity, (c) omit the **process efficiency ($\eta$)** in heat-input calculations, and (d) write generic answers without referencing the **shipbuilding context**. Always anchor your answer to hull plates, classification rules, or shipyard practice to score full marks.

---

### Question B (Option A) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the classification of welding processes used in the shipbuilding industry. Compare any two arc welding processes commonly employed in hull fabrication, highlighting their process principles, advantages, limitations, and typical shipboard applications. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** What are fuel gases? With the help of a balanced chemical equation, explain the combustion of acetylene with oxygen. Discuss the three types of oxy-acetylene flames and their industrial applications in shipyards. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**1. Classification of Welding Processes (used in shipbuilding):**

Welding processes used in shipbuilding are classified into **six primary families** based on the energy source and mechanism of coalescence:

1. **Arc Welding** — Electrical arc provides fusion energy. Dominant in shipyards (~80 % of joints).
2. **Oxy-Fuel Gas Welding** — Combustion of fuel gas with oxygen. Used for repair and small-scale work.
3. **Resistance Welding** — Joule heating from current through joint. Used in panel-line fabrication.
4. **Solid-State Welding** — Joining below the melting point. Used for aluminium superstructures (FSW).
5. **High-Energy Beam Welding** — LASER and Electron Beam. Limited use in precision superstructure work.
6. **Thermochemical Welding** — Thermit welding for in-situ repair of large castings.

**2. Comparison of SMAW vs. SAW (both widely used in hull fabrication):**

| Parameter | **SMAW (111)** | **SAW (121)** |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Principle** | Manual stick electrode struck against workpiece generates arc | Continuous wire fed into arc submerged under granular flux |
| **Equipment** | Simple, portable, low cost | Mechanized, complex, expensive |
| **Deposition rate** | 1–3 kg/h | 8–25 kg/h |
| **Position capability** | All positions | Flat / horizontal only (with special PAW for vertical) |
| **Slag** | Yes, must be chipped | Flux forms slag that auto-peels |
| **Weld quality** | Operator-dependent | Consistent, high radiographic quality |
| **Heat input control** | Moderate | Very precise |
| **Shipboard use** | Bulkhead, stiffener, repair, erection joint welding | Long-seam panel-line, flat-position butt welds in plate panels |
| **Limitations** | Slow, operator fatigue | Cannot be used out of position, high capital cost |

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**1. Definition of Fuel Gases:**

A **fuel gas** is a combustible gas that, when burned with oxygen (or air), releases thermal energy usable for welding, cutting, heating, or flame-straightening in shipyards.

**2. Balanced Combustion Equation of Acetylene:**

$$2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat (1300 kJ/mol C}_2\text{H}_2\text{)}$$

The **stoichiometric O/A ratio** is **2.5 : 1**.

**3. Three Types of Oxy-Acetylene Flames:**

- **Carburizing (Reducing) Flame** — O/A ratio < 1 (in operational terms). Long, feathered, luminous white inner cone with excess acetylene. Used for **welding high-carbon steels, hardfacing, and lead welding**.
- **Neutral Flame** — O/A ratio ≈ 1 to 1.2 operationally. Sharp, well-defined inner cone with no feather. Used for **general-purpose welding of mild and low-carbon steel hull plates**.
- **Oxidizing Flame** — O/A ratio > 1.5 operationally. Short, pointed, harsh-blue cone. Used for **welding copper, brass, and bronze (non-ferrous shipboard piping)**.

---

### Question B (Option B) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of heat input in arc welding. Derive the heat-input formula and compute the heat input for an SMAW process on 12 mm thick shipbuilding plate with the following parameters: V = 25 V, I = 180 A, S = 150 mm/min, $\eta$ = 0.80. Comment on whether this heat input complies with the typical IACS limit of 2.5 kJ/mm for thin-plate hull welding. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Describe in detail the various fuel gases used in shipbuilding. Compare acetylene and LPG with respect to flame temperature, calorific value, safety, and storage. Why is acetylene preferred for fusion welding despite its higher cost? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**1. Concept of Heat Input:**

Heat input is the **net energy delivered to the workpiece per unit length of weld**, expressed in **kJ/mm**. It governs the **HAZ size, grain coarsening, residual stress, and distortion** in welded ship plates.

**2. Derivation (already shown in Section 3.1):**

$$\boxed{H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60 \cdot \eta}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}}$$

**3. Numerical Substitution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{25 \times 180 \times 60 \times 0.80}{150 \times 1000} \\
  &= \dfrac{216\,000}{150\,000} \\
  &= 1.44\,\text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$\begin{aligned}
25 \times 180 &= 4\,500 \\
4\,500 \times 60 &= 270\,000 \\
270\,000 \times 0.80 &= 216\,000 \\
150 \times 1000 &= 150\,000 \\
\dfrac{216\,000}{150\,000} &= 1.44
\end{aligned}$$

**4. Compliance Check:**

$H_{computed} = 1.44\,\text{kJ/mm} < H_{limit} = 2.5\,\text{kJ/mm}$ → **COMPLIANT** with the IACS-style limit for thin-plate hull welding.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**1. Fuel Gases in Shipbuilding:**

Common fuel gases used in shipbuilding include **acetylene (C₂H₂), LPG (C₃H₈ + C₄H₁₀), propane (C₃H₈), butane (C₄H₁₀), hydrogen (H₂), natural gas (mainly CH₄), and MAPP gas**.

**2. Acetylene vs. LPG — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Acetylene (C₂H₂) | LPG (C₃H₈/C₄H₁₀) |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **LCV** | 48.8 MJ/m³ | ~93 MJ/m³ |
| **Flame temperature with O₂** | 3160 °C | 2850 °C |
| **Flame type** | Reducing (carburizing) primary flame possible | Strictly oxidizing/neutral only |
| **Storage** | Dissolved in acetone under porous mass, max 1.03 MPa | Pressurized liquid, ~0.7 MPa at 20 °C |
| **Safety** | Unstable above 2 bar; prone to detonation | Stable, low reactivity |
| **Cost** | Higher | 4×–6× cheaper |
| **Primary use** | Fusion welding + cutting | Cutting + preheating only |

**3. Why Acetylene is Preferred for Fusion Welding Despite Cost:**

- Acetylene's primary inner cone consists of **unburnt CO and H₂**, which act as a **reducing shield** for the weld pool.
- This protective atmosphere **prevents oxide formation** in the molten pool without requiring external flux.
- LPG and other fuel gases **cannot produce such a reducing atmosphere** — they require flux for fusion welding of steel, making the process impractical and weld quality inferior.
- The **high flame temperature of 3160 °C** ensures complete melting of the parent metal even with a neutral flame, allowing robust fusion welding of structural steel plates.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final high-density summary for last-minute revision:**

- ✅ **Welding Definition (AWS):** Localized coalescence of materials using heat, with or without pressure and filler metal.
- ✅ **Joining Techniques in Shipbuilding:** Welding (dominant), riveting, bolting, brazing, soldering, adhesive bonding, solid-state welding.
- ✅ **Six Welding Families:** Arc, Gas, Resistance, Solid-State, Beam, Thermochemical.
- ✅ **Major Shipyard Arc Processes:** SMAW (111), GMAW (131), GTAW (141), SAW (121), FCAW (136), PAW (15).
- ✅ **Oxy-Acetylene Balanced Equation:** 2 C₂H₂ + 5 O₂ → 4 CO₂ + 2 H₂O + Heat; **O/A stoich = 2.5**.
- ✅ **Three Flame Types:** Carburizing (excess C₂H₂), Neutral (stoich), Oxidizing (excess O₂).
- ✅ **Neutral Flame is the Shipyard Default** for mild/low-carbon steel fusion welding.
- ✅ **Acetylene Flame Temperature:** 3160 °C — **highest of all fuel gases**.
- ✅ **Acetylene Storage:** Dissolved in acetone under porous mass; **maximum safe cylinder pressure = 1.03 MPa**.
- ✅ **Heat Input Formula:** $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60 \cdot \eta}{S \cdot 1000}$ in **kJ/mm**.
- ✅ **Thermal Efficiency Reference Values:** SMAW ≈ 0.80; SAW ≈ 0.95; GMAW ≈ 0.70; GTAW ≈ 0.50; OAW ≈ 0.45.
- ✅ **DNV Heat Input Limit for DH36 Hull Plates:** typically ≤ 3.5 kJ/mm (must be checked per WPS).
- ✅ **Acetylene is the only fuel gas permitted by AWS for fusion welding of steel without flux.**
- ✅ **LPG and Propane are reserved for cutting and preheating** — not fusion welding.
- ✅ **Cutting Oxygen Purity Standard:** minimum 99.5 % (drop in purity reduces cutting speed linearly).

> 🎯 **End of Module 1 Notes — PENST741**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forehand and Backhand Welding Techniques; Function of Fillers and Fluxes; Carbon Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Forehand Welding Technique (Forward Welding / Push Technique)

**Formal Academic Definition:**
Forehand welding is a gas welding technique in which the **torch flame is directed in the same direction as the welding travel**, meaning the flame *leads* (precedes) the weld puddle and points toward the un-welded base metal. The filler rod is held in front of the puddle and is fed into the leading edge of the molten pool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, forehand welding is conventionally associated with **low-temperature, soft, and gentle flames** such as the **oxy-acetylene neutral flame** used for thin-gauge shipbuilding plates, copper, brass, and galvanised sheets.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a person painting a long fence with a roller. If you push the roller forward (you walk *into* the wet paint), the paint spreads smoothly ahead of the roller. In **forehand welding**, the torch "pushes" heat and molten metal *into* the joint, preheating the parent metal ahead of the puddle. This preheating effect gives a wide, shallow, and soft weld pool — perfect for thin materials.

**Key Directional Vector:** The torch travel vector →  and the flame vector → are **parallel and identical in direction**.

---

## 1.2 Backhand Welding Technique (Reverse Welding / Pull Technique)

**Formal Academic Definition:**
Backhand welding is a gas welding technique in which the **torch flame is directed opposite to the direction of welding travel**, meaning the flame *trails* (follows) behind the weld puddle. The filler rod is held between the torch and the puddle and is fed into the *trailing edge* of the molten pool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Backhand welding uses a **concentrated, high-temperature, oxidising flame** (slight excess of oxygen) and is preferred for **thick plates, heavy ship hull sections, and ferrous materials** where deep penetration is critical.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine spreading butter on toast with a spoon, but you pull the spoon *towards* you while melting the butter. The heat stays concentrated *behind* the puddle, hammering the metal with focused thermal energy. In **backhand welding**, the torch "pulls" — heat concentrates on the solidified weld behind, forcing deep penetration into the joint root.

**Key Directional Vector:** The torch travel vector → and the flame vector ← are **anti-parallel (opposite directions)**.

---

## 1.3 Function of Fillers in Welding

**Formal Academic Definition:**
A **filler material** (filler rod / filler wire) is a consumable metallic alloy added to the weld pool during the welding operation to **supplement the parent metal**, fill the joint gap, provide additional alloying elements, and achieve the required mechanical and chemical properties of the joint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Functions of Fillers (4M Rule):**
> - **M**ass addition → fill the groove / joint gap
> - **M**echanical property matching → tensile strength, ductility, hardness
> - **M**etallurgical compatibility → prevent cracks, porosity, brittleness
> - **M**icroalloying → introduce deoxidizers (Mn, Si) and grain refiners (Ti, Nb)

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a filler rod like a **bridging log** placed across a ditch. The parent metals are the two banks of the ditch; without the filler rod, no connection is possible. In shipbuilding, the filler rod literally *becomes* part of the hull after solidification.

---

## 1.4 Function of Fluxes in Welding

**Formal Academic Definition:**
A **flux** is a non-metallic chemical compound (powder, paste, or coating) applied to the joint, electrode, or filler rod to **dissolve and float out oxides, prevent atmospheric contamination, stabilise the arc, and refine the weld bead microstructure** during welding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The protective slag formed by the flux must be easily removable after cooling and must not entrap gases that would cause porosity in the weld.

### The Three Core Functions of Flux (Mnemonic: **"F-S-C"**)
- **F**loat out impurities (oxides, silicates, sulphides)
- **S**eal the weld pool from atmospheric N₂, O₂, H₂
- **C**leanse the surface and stabilise the arc

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a freshwater lake in winter. A layer of ice on the surface prevents oxygen from entering the water below. Flux forms a similar "ice layer" (slag) on top of the molten weld pool, protecting it from harmful atmospheric gases.

---

## 1.5 Carbon Arc Welding (CAW)

**Formal Academic Definition:**
**Carbon Arc Welding (CAW)** is an arc welding process in which an **electric arc is struck between a non-consumable carbon (or graphite) electrode and the workpiece**, with or without the use of a filler rod. The intense heat of the arc (**≈ 4000 °C**) melts the parent metal and filler, forming a weld pool protected by a gaseous envelope and flux coating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Electrode Type:** Pure carbon (amorphous) — cheaper, lower current capacity
> **Graphite Electrode:** Crystalline carbon — higher current, smoother arc, but more expensive
> **Operating Temperature:** **≈ 4000 °C** at the arc, **≈ 3000 °C** at the workpiece

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a small, controlled lightning bolt between a pencil lead (carbon) and a steel plate. The intense heat melts the steel locally, and a filler rod (if added) drips into the puddle like wax from a candle.

**Key Standard Metrics:**
- Arc voltage: **20–35 V (DC)**
- Current: **50–800 A** depending on electrode diameter
- Electrode diameter: **4 mm to 25 mm**
- Shielding: Optional flux powder / gaseous envelope from electrode combustion
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — Forehand Welding

### Working Principle
1. **Flame is held at a 45°–60° angle** to the workpiece (acute angle with direction of travel).
2. The inner cone of the flame points **ahead** of the weld puddle.
3. The flame **preheats** the base metal just ahead of the molten pool.
4. The filler rod is dipped into the **leading edge** of the puddle.
5. Result: **Wide, shallow, soft weld pool** with a **rippled, oxidised appearance**.

### Why Forehand? (Engineering Reasoning)
- The preheating effect **reduces the cooling rate**, allowing gases to escape and reducing the risk of **porosity and cracking** in thin plates.
- Wider heat distribution = lower heat input per unit area.
- **Best for:** thin sheets (< 3 mm), non-ferrous metals (copper, brass, aluminium), galvanised iron.

### Advantages
- Low heat input → minimal distortion
- Smooth bead appearance
- Lower skill requirement

### Limitations
- Low penetration (only 60–70% joint penetration)
- Not suitable for thick plates
- Higher gas consumption per unit length

---

## 2.2 Operational Theory — Backhand Welding

### Working Principle
1. **Flame is held at a 60°–80° angle**, pointing **backward** (opposite to travel direction).
2. The flame is directed onto the **already-solidified or solidifying weld bead**.
3. The filler rod is held between the torch and the puddle, dipping into the **trailing edge**.
4. Result: **Narrow, deep, focused weld pool** with bright, clean appearance.

### Why Backhand? (Engineering Reasoning)
- The flame's intense heat is concentrated on the **trailing weld metal**, forcing heat *into* the joint root.
- The base metal is **not preheated** → rapid solidification → **deeper penetration**.
- **Best for:** thick plates (> 6 mm), heavy structural ship members, mild steel.

### Advantages
- Deep penetration (up to 90–100% joint penetration)
- Faster welding speed
- Better for thick ferrous materials

### Limitations
- High heat input → more distortion
- Requires higher skill
- Increased risk of brittleness in the heat-affected zone (HAZ)

---

## 2.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Forehand vs Backhand)

| Parameter | Forehand Welding | Backhand Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Flame Direction | Same as travel (push) | Opposite to travel (pull) |
| Filler Rod Position | In front of puddle | Behind torch / into puddle |
| Penetration | Shallow (60–70%) | Deep (90–100%) |
| Weld Pool Shape | Wide and shallow | Narrow and deep |
| Heat Input | Low | High |
| Suitable Thickness | < 3 mm | > 6 mm |
| Flame Type | Soft, neutral | Concentrated, oxidising |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Appearance | Rippled, oxidised | Bright, smooth |
| Typical Materials | Cu, brass, GI, thin steel | Mild steel, heavy ship plates |

---

## 2.4 Function of Fillers — Detailed Engineering Analysis

### Filler Material Classification
1. **Matching fillers** — same composition as parent metal (e.g., ER70S for mild steel)
2. **Over-matching fillers** — higher strength than parent (e.g., low-alloy rods for high-tensile steel)
3. **Under-matching fillers** — lower strength, used for crack-sensitive materials

### Selection Criteria (the "4C Rule")
- **Compatibility** — metallurgical match with base metal
- **Certification** — AWS / ASME / IACS approved (shipbuilding standard)
- **Composition** — alloying content (C, Mn, Si, Cr, Ni, Mo)
- **Coating** — bare, flux-coated, or copper-coated

### Filler Functions in Detail
- **Gap bridging** — physically fills the joint preparation groove
- **Alloying addition** — adds Mn, Si for deoxidation; Cr, Ni for corrosion resistance
- **Strength control** — adjusts ultimate tensile strength (UTS) and yield strength
- **Dilution control** — regulates the ratio of parent metal to filler in final weld

### Mathematical Concept — Dilution Ratio
The dilution ratio controls weld chemistry.

$$D = \frac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $A_{\text{base}}$ = cross-sectional area melted from base metal
- $A_{\text{filler}}$ = cross-sectional area contributed by filler
- Units: **%** (typically 30–60% in arc welding)

---

## 2.5 Function of Fluxes — Detailed Engineering Analysis

### Chemical Role of Flux
A flux is typically composed of:
- **SiO₂ (silica)** — slag former
- **MnO₂ / FeO** — oxide scavengers
- **CaCO₃ (limestone)** — gas shield generator (releases CO₂)
- **CaF₂ (fluorspar)** — slag viscosity modifier
- **Na₂O / K₂O** — arc stabilisers
- **TiO₂** — wetting agent

### Step-by-Step Chemical Action of Flux
1. **Melting:** Flux melts at a lower temperature than the base metal (≈ 1200 °C vs 1500 °C).
2. **Floating:** Molten flux floats on the weld pool due to lower density.
3. **Dissolving Oxides:** SiO₂ + FeO → FeSiO₃ (iron silicate slag).
4. **Gas Shielding:** CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂↑ (CO₂ envelopes the arc).
5. **Solidification:** Slag solidifies over the bead, slowing cooling rate.
6. **Removal:** Slag is chipped off after cooling, revealing the weld.

### Slag Formation Equation (Display Form)
The principal chemical reaction of flux in arc welding is:

$$\text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{FeO} \rightarrow 2\text{FeO} \cdot \text{SiO}_2 \text{ (slag)}$$

Where $2\text{FeO} \cdot \text{SiO}_2$ is **fayalite** (iron silicate slag), which floats on the molten weld pool and is later removed mechanically.

---

## 2.6 Carbon Arc Welding (CAW) — Operational Theory

### Working Principle
1. **DC power source** is used (typically DCEN — Direct Current Electrode Negative) for deeper penetration, or DCEP for shallower beads.
2. The carbon electrode is clamped in the electrode holder.
3. An arc is struck by touching the carbon tip to the workpiece (touch-start) or by using a high-frequency igniter.
4. The arc melts the parent metal locally to form a weld pool.
5. Filler rod (if used) is fed manually into the leading edge of the pool.
6. The intense heat vaporises some carbon, creating a **gaseous shield (CO + CO₂)** that protects the weld.

### Modes of CAW Operation
- **Twin Carbon Arc (TCA):** Two carbon electrodes — heat source only, no filler addition; used for brazing and soldering.
- **Single Carbon Arc:** One carbon electrode — heat source + optional filler rod.

### Engineering Utility
- **Welding of thin gauge metals** (without filler)
- **Preheating** of heavy sections before arc welding
- **Brazing and soldering** of copper, brass, bronze
- **Repair welding** of cast iron and galvanised sheets
- **Surfacing / hard-facing** with carbide filler rods

### Advantages of CAW
- Highest available welding temperature (**≈ 4000 °C**)
- Can weld non-ferrous metals without flux
- Low hydrogen embrittlement risk (no flux coating required)
- Smooth, spatter-free weld

### Limitations of CAW
- High equipment cost
- Carbon pickup → weld metal becomes harder and less ductile
- Limited to flat and horizontal positions
- High UV radiation hazard

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Input | $H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Total thermal energy per unit weld length |
| 2 | Dilution Ratio | $D = \frac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}} \times 100$ | % | Weld chemistry control |
| 3 | Travel Speed | $S = \frac{\text{weld length}}{\text{time}}$ | mm/min | Production rate |
| 4 | Arc Voltage (CAW) | $V = 20 - 35$ | V (DC) | Carbon arc welding range |
| 5 | Current (CAW) | $I = 50 - 800$ | A | Depends on electrode Ø |
| 6 | Arc Temperature | $T_{\text{arc}} \approx 4000$ | °C | Carbon arc peak |
| 7 | Penetration Efficiency | $\eta_p = \frac{d_{\text{actual}}}{d_{\text{maximum}}} \times 100$ | % | Forehand vs backhand |
| 8 | Cooling Rate | $R = \frac{2\pi k (T_c - T_o)^2}{H}$ | °C/s | HAZ microstructure control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable Definitions in Heat Input Formula:**
> - $V$ = Arc Voltage (V)
> - $I$ = Welding Current (A)
> - $S$ = Travel Speed (mm/min)
> - Output $H$ is in **kJ/mm** — the KTU-expected SI unit.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
These formulas are used by shipbuilding QA engineers for:
- **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** preparation
- **Heat input limits** to prevent HAZ softening in DH36 / EH36 marine steel
- **Dilution control** in dissimilar metal welds (e.g., Cu–steel transition joints in shipboard piping)
- **Penetration verification** in single-side butt welds on hull plates
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input in Welding

The heat input is the **single most important parameter** in welding metallurgy, controlling the HAZ microstructure, grain size, and distortion.

### Step 1: Define the Energy Delivered per Unit Time
The power delivered by the arc is the product of arc voltage and current.

$$P = V \times I$$

Where:
- $P$ = arc power in **Watts (W)**
- $V$ = arc voltage in **Volts (V)**
- $I$ = welding current in **Amperes (A)**

### Step 2: Convert to Energy per Unit Weld Length
The energy per unit length of weld is obtained by dividing the power by the travel speed. To convert from minutes to seconds, multiply by 60.

$$H' = \frac{P}{S / 60} = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S}$$

Where:
- $H'$ = heat input in **J/mm**
- $S$ = travel speed in **mm/min**

### Step 3: Convert to kiloJoules per mm (KTU Standard Unit)
Since shipbuilding WPS documents use kJ/mm, divide by 1000.

$$H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$$

Units check: $\frac{\text{V} \cdot \text{A} \cdot \text{s}}{\text{mm}} = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}}$, then $\div 1000$ gives **kJ/mm**.

### Step 4: Apply to a Carbon Arc Welding Example
Given: $V = 25$ V, $I = 300$ A, $S = 200$ mm/min.

$$H = \frac{25 \times 300 \times 60}{200 \times 1000}$$

Step-by-step arithmetic:

$$H = \frac{7500 \times 60}{200000}$$

$$H = \frac{450000}{200000}$$

$$H = 2.25 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

This is the heat input — useful for comparison against IACS (International Association of Classification Societies) limits for marine steel plates.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Dilution Ratio

The dilution ratio is critical when **dissimilar metals** are welded, e.g., a Cu-brass heat exchanger plate to a steel pipe.

### Step 1: Define Cross-Sectional Areas
Consider a weld bead cross-section divided into two parts:
- $A_{\text{base}}$ = area melted from the base (parent) metal
- $A_{\text{filler}}$ = area contributed by the filler rod

Total bead cross-section:

$$A_{\text{total}} = A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}$$

### Step 2: Express Dilution as a Percentage
The fraction of base metal in the final weld bead is:

$$D = \frac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{total}}} \times 100$$

### Step 3: Apply to Carbon Arc with No Filler
When CAW is used without filler (e.g., thin copper sheet autogenous welding), $A_{\text{filler}} = 0$:

$$D = \frac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{base}} + 0} \times 100 = 100\%$$

This means **100% of the weld metal comes from the parent metal** — the filler rod does not contribute.

### Step 4: Apply to CAW with Filler
Suppose $A_{\text{base}} = 12 \text{ mm}^2$ and $A_{\text{filler}} = 8 \text{ mm}^2$:

$$D = \frac{12}{12 + 8} \times 100 = \frac{12}{20} \times 100 = 60\%$$

This means **60% of the weld metal originates from the base metal**, and 40% from the filler. The mechanical and corrosion properties of the final weld depend strongly on this ratio.

---

## 3.3 Penetration Depth Comparison: Forehand vs Backhand

### Forehand Penetration Equation
The depth of penetration in forehand welding is approximated as:

$$d_f = k_f \cdot \frac{H}{v \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $d_f$ = penetration depth in **mm**
- $k_f$ = forehand constant (≈ 0.06)
- $H$ = heat input in **kJ/mm**
- $v$ = thermal conductivity of base metal
- $t$ = plate thickness in **mm**

### Backhand Penetration Equation
For backhand welding, the constant increases due to focused heat:

$$d_b = k_b \cdot \frac{H}{v \cdot t}$$

Where $k_b \approx 0.10$ (≈ 1.67 times the forehand value).

### Ratio
$$\frac{d_b}{d_f} = \frac{k_b}{k_f} = \frac{0.10}{0.06} \approx 1.67$$

**Interpretation:** Backhand welding gives **≈ 1.67× deeper penetration** than forehand for the same heat input — a key KTU viva question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Process

| Material Type | Thickness (mm) | Recommended Process | Flux Required? | Filler Rod? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (ship hull) | 2–3 | Forehand (oxy-acetylene) | No (bare rod) | Yes |
| Mild Steel (ship hull) | 6–25 | Backhand / SMAW / GMAW | Yes | Yes |
| Copper / Brass | 1–4 | Forehand (oxy-acetylene) | No (or borax) | Yes (Cu-Si rod) |
| Galvanised Iron | 0.8–2 | Forehand (special flux) | Yes (HCl-zinc chloride) | Yes |
| Cast Iron Repair | > 5 | CAW (preheat) + braze | Brazing flux | Cast iron rod |
| Stainless Steel (304L) | 3–12 | GTAW / Backhand | Argon shield | ER308L |
| Aluminium | 1–6 | Forehand (oxy-hydrogen) | Aluminium flux | Al-Si rod |

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Arc Energy Distribution in CAW

### Step 1: Total Arc Power
$$P = V \times I = 25 \text{ V} \times 300 \text{ A} = 7500 \text{ W} = 7.5 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 2: Energy Partition
In CAW, energy distributes approximately as:
- 60% → workpiece (heating + melting)
- 30% → electrode (heating + vaporisation)
- 10% → radiated/convected loss

### Step 3: Effective Energy to Workpiece
$$P_{\text{eff}} = 0.60 \times 7.5 = 4.5 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 4: Melting Efficiency of Base Metal
For carbon steel, latent heat of fusion $L_f \approx 275$ kJ/kg. The melting rate:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_{\text{eff}}}{L_f} = \frac{4.5 \text{ kJ/s}}{275 \text{ kJ/kg}} \approx 0.0164 \text{ kg/s} \approx 1 \text{ kg/min}$$

This melting rate is useful in shipyard estimation of electrode consumption per metre of weld.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between forehand and backhand welding techniques. List any two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Forehand Welding | Backhand Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Flame direction | Same as travel (push) | Opposite to travel (pull) |
| Filler rod position | In front of puddle | Between torch and puddle |
| Penetration | Shallow (60–70%) | Deep (90–100%) |
| Heat input | Low | High |
| Suitable thickness | < 3 mm | > 6 mm |

**Two advantages each:**

*Forehand:*
1. Low heat input reduces distortion of thin plates
2. Smooth, aesthetically acceptable weld bead

*Backhand:*
1. Deep joint penetration — ideal for thick ship hull sections
2. Faster travel speed — higher productivity in heavy fabrication

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**State the functions of (i) filler material and (ii) flux in welding.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Functions of Filler Material:**
1. Provides additional metal to fill the joint groove
2. Adds alloying elements (Mn, Si, Cr, Ni) for desired mechanical and chemical properties
3. Controls weld metal composition and strength

**(ii) Functions of Flux:**
1. Dissolves and floats out metallic oxides from the weld pool (forms slag)
2. Prevents atmospheric contamination of the molten pool (gas shield)
3. Stabilises the arc and improves bead shape

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **forehand and backhand welding techniques**. Compare their penetration, heat input, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **function of fillers and fluxes** in welding with suitable chemical reactions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Forehand Welding (3.5 marks):**
In forehand welding, the torch is held with its flame inclined at **45°–60°** to the workpiece, and the flame is directed **in the same direction** as the welding travel. The flame preheats the base metal just ahead of the weld pool, allowing low heat input and shallow penetration. The filler rod is fed into the **leading edge** of the puddle.

*Sketch (in text):*
```
    Travelling Direction →
         ↓
    ===========================
    |   F   \                   |
    |  LAME  \---Filler Rod     |
    |        ))))  Weld Pool    |
    ===========================
    Welded Bead (wide, shallow)
```

**Backhand Welding (3.5 marks):**
In backhand welding, the torch flame is inclined at **60°–80°** to the workpiece, but the flame is directed **opposite** to the welding travel. The flame trails the puddle and acts on the solidified weld, forcing heat into the joint root. The filler rod is fed into the **trailing edge** of the puddle.

*Sketch (in text):*
```
    Travelling Direction →
                            ↓
    ===========================
    |         Filler Rod  /  F  |
    |    Weld Pool  ))))  /AME |
    |___________________  /     |
    ===========================
    Welded Bead (narrow, deep)
```

**Comparison (extra, completes the 7 marks):**

| Parameter | Forehand | Backhand |
|---|---|---|
| Penetration | 60–70% | 90–100% |
| Heat input | 0.8–1.5 kJ/mm | 1.5–3.0 kJ/mm |
| Applications | Thin sheets, Cu, brass | Thick ship plates |

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Filler Material (3.5 marks):**
- A filler material is a consumable metallic rod/wire added to the weld pool.
- It **fills the joint groove**, **supplements alloying elements**, and **achieves required mechanical properties** (UTS, hardness, ductility).
- Example: ER70S mild steel filler rod contains 0.07% C, 1.5% Mn, 0.5% Si.

**Mathematical concept — Dilution Ratio:**

$$D = \frac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}} \times 100\%$$

**Flux (3.5 marks):**
A flux is a non-metallic chemical compound (powder / coating) that performs three functions:
1. **Cleansing** — dissolves and floats out oxides:

$$\text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{FeO} \rightarrow \underbrace{2\text{FeO} \cdot \text{SiO}_2}_{\text{iron silicate slag}}$$

2. **Shielding** — releases CO₂ from CaCO₃ decomposition:

$$\text{CaCO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$$

3. **Slag formation** — protects weld from atmosphere and slows cooling.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **principle, equipment, and working of Carbon Arc Welding (CAW)** with a neat block diagram. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **functions of fillers and fluxes** in gas welding and arc welding. State two examples of fillers used in shipbuilding. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Principle (2 marks):**
In CAW, an electric arc is struck between a **non-consumable carbon (or graphite) electrode** and the workpiece. The arc temperature reaches **≈ 4000 °C**, melting the base metal locally. A filler rod may be fed manually. Combustion of carbon generates CO and CO₂, providing a self-generated gas shield.

**Equipment (2 marks):**
1. DC power source (20–35 V, 50–800 A)
2. Carbon/graphite electrode (Ø 4–25 mm)
3. Electrode holder with cooling fins
4. Filler rod (optional)
5. Earthing clamp
6. Protective slag/flux powder (optional)

**Working (2 marks):**
1. Carbon electrode connected to DC negative (DCEN) for deep penetration.
2. Arc initiated by touching electrode to workpiece.
3. Arc melts base metal → forms weld pool.
4. Filler rod dipped into leading edge (single carbon arc) for thicker joints.
5. Welder moves along joint, maintaining arc length 2–4 mm.
6. Slag and solidified bead formed.

**Block diagram (text representation):**
```
[DC Source] → [Electrode Holder] → [Carbon Electrode]
                                            |
                                          ARC (4000°C)
                                            |
                                  [Workpiece + Filler]
                                            |
                                     [Earth Clamp]
```

**Advantages and Limitations (1 mark):**
- *Advantages:* High temperature, low H-embrittlement, smooth bead
- *Limitations:* Carbon pickup reduces ductility, high UV hazard, limited to flat positions

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Functions of Fillers (3.5 marks):**
1. **Gap filling** — bridges the joint preparation groove
2. **Alloying** — adds Mn, Si, Cr, Ni, Mo to weld metal
3. **Strength control** — adjusts UTS to match design requirement
4. **Deoxidation** — Mn and Si act as deoxidizers in the weld pool

**Mathematical concept — Heat Input calculation for filler deposition rate:**

$$\text{Filler Deposition Rate} = \frac{A_{\text{filler}} \times v_{\text{travel}} \times \rho}{1000} \text{ kg/min}$$

**Functions of Fluxes (2.5 marks):**
1. **Oxide removal:** SiO₂ + FeO → FeSiO₃ (slag)
2. **Gas shield:** CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂↑
3. **Arc stabilisation:** Na₂O, K₂O lower arc voltage requirement

**Two shipbuilding filler examples (1 mark):**
1. **ER70S** — for mild steel hull plates
2. **ER308L / ER316L** — for stainless steel piping and tank liners in chemical tankers

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing flame direction in forehand vs backhand** — many students write "forehand means flame is opposite to travel" — this is **WRONG**; the **opposite** is backhand. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention slag removal** when describing flux — the slag must be **physically chipped off** after cooling. Marks lost: 1.
> 3. **Writing "carbon electrode melts" in CAW** — it is **non-consumable**; only the tip erodes slightly. Marks lost: 1.
> 4. **Omitting the chemical equation for flux** — examiners expect at least one balanced chemical reaction. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical problems** (heat input in kJ/mm, dilution in %, current in A) — KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 6. **Drawing poor sketches** — neat labelled diagrams with arrow directions are mandatory for full marks in 7-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

### 🔥 Forehand vs Backhand Welding
- Forehand = **push** (flame leads, torch travel same direction as flame)
- Backhand = **pull** (flame trails, torch travel opposite to flame)
- Forehand penetration ≈ **60–70%**; Backhand penetration ≈ **90–100%**
- Forehand = thin plates (< 3 mm); Backhand = thick plates (> 6 mm)
- Forehand uses **soft neutral flame**; Backhand uses **concentrated oxidising flame**
- Filler rod position: Forehand = **ahead** of puddle; Backhand = **between torch and puddle**

### 🔥 Functions of Filler Material
- **4M Rule:** Mass addition, Mechanical match, Metallurgical compatibility, Microalloying
- **Dilution ratio formula:** $D = A_{\text{base}} / (A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}) \times 100\%$
- Shipbuilding examples: **ER70S, ER308L, ER316L, Cu-Si (for Cu)**

### 🔥 Functions of Flux
- **F-S-C mnemonic:** Float out impurities, Seal from atmosphere, Cleanse surface
- Key chemical reaction: $\text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{FeO} \rightarrow 2\text{FeO} \cdot \text{SiO}_2$ (slag)
- Gas shield reaction: $\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$
- Flux melting point **lower** than base metal so it floats

### 🔥 Carbon Arc Welding (CAW)
- Non-consumable **carbon/graphite** electrode
- Arc temperature **≈ 4000 °C** (highest among common welding processes)
- DC power source, **20–35 V**, **50–800 A**
- Two modes: **Single Carbon Arc (with filler)** and **Twin Carbon Arc (no filler, brazing)**
- Self-generated gas shield from **CO and CO₂**
- Applications: thin-gauge welding, preheating, brazing, cast iron repair
- Limitation: **carbon pickup** → reduced ductility; **high UV hazard**

### 🔥 KTU Must-Know Numerical Formulas
- Heat Input: $H = (V \times I \times 60) / (S \times 1000)$ in **kJ/mm**
- Dilution: $D = A_{\text{base}} / (A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}) \times 100$ in **%**
- Travel Speed: $S = \text{length}/\text{time}$ in **mm/min**
- Melting rate: $\dot{m} = P_{\text{eff}} / L_f$ in **kg/s**

### 🔥 Examiner Trivia
- Always quote **units** in numerical problems.
- Always draw a **labelled diagram** for any 7-mark question.
- Always state **at least one chemical equation** for flux-related questions.
- For CAW, mention **DCEN polarity** (electrode negative) as the preferred setup for deep penetration.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shielded Metal Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** is a manual arc welding process that uses a consumable, flux-coated (covered) electrode to produce coalescence of metals by heating them with an electric arc established between the electrode and the workpiece. The molten weld pool is shielded from atmospheric contamination by the decomposition of the electrode coating, which generates a protective gas envelope and forms a molten slag that solidifies over the weld bead.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, SMAW is classified under **Arc Welding Processes** (Group 1 of IS 813 : 1989 and AWS A5.1/A5.5 standards) and is widely recognized in shipbuilding standards (IACS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS) as the primary manual welding process for hull fabrication, structural assembly, and repair works.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Synonyms used in Industry & Examinations:**
> - **SMAW** – Shielded Metal Arc Welding (AWS terminology)
> - **MMAW / MMA** – Manual Metal Arc Welding (ISO/European terminology)
> - **Stick Welding** – Popular shop-floor name
> - **Covered Electrode Process** – Academic Indian Standard reference

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are trying to **join two broken pieces of a clay pot using a candle**. 

- The **candle flame** acts like the **electric arc** (the heat source).
- You dip the broken edges into the **molten wax** (which is like the **molten electrode wire** acting as filler metal).
- As you move the candle along, a **protective waxy coating** forms around the joint — similar to how the **flux coating decomposes to form a shielding gas and slag layer** protecting the weld pool from dust/oxygen.
- Your **hand holding the candle** represents the **electrode holder**, and your other hand guiding the pot represents the **welder's dexterity** in maintaining arc length and travel angle.

The candle's flame ≈ the welding arc (2000–8000 °C)
The melted candle wax ≈ the melted electrode core wire
The smoke and soot ≈ the shielding gas and slag

This analogy helps a beginner visualize why SMAW is called a *shielded* process — **both the gas AND the slag act as shields** against atmospheric contamination.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV)** | **50 V – 90 V** (AC) / **60 V – 80 V** (DC) |
| **Arc Voltage (working)** | **18 V – 32 V** |
| **Arc Temperature** | **6000 K – 8000 K** (~6000 °C) |
| **Welding Current Range** | **30 A – 550 A** |
| **Electrode Diameter** | **1.6 mm – 8.0 mm** |
| **Deposition Efficiency** | **~60 % – 65 %** (with coating loss) |
| **Heat Input** | **0.5 kJ/mm – 4.0 kJ/mm** |
| **Power Source Type** | Transformer / Rectifier / Inverter |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In Module 1, students are expected to identify the role of each SMAW subsystem (power source, electrode, holder, work clamp) and relate arc parameters to weld quality — especially in the context of **shipbuilding-grade structural welds**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

### Step 1: Establishing the Welding Circuit
The welding machine (transformer/rectifier/inverter) is connected in a closed circuit:
- One terminal to the **electrode holder** (via the **welding cable**).
- The other terminal to the **workpiece** (via the **earth/ground clamp**).

When the welder **strikes an arc** by either scratching or tapping the electrode against the workpiece, a short circuit momentarily occurs. As the electrode is withdrawn to about **2 mm – 4 mm arc gap**, the short circuit breaks, generating an **electric arc**.

### Step 2: Heat Generation by the Arc
The intense heat of the arc (~6000 °C) melts:
1. The **electrode core wire** (which becomes filler metal).
2. A thin layer of the **base metal** (parent plate) — creating the molten weld pool.

**Why does the arc produce such heat?**
- Electrical energy converts to thermal energy as current flows through ionized gas in the arc plasma column.
- Heat is generated primarily at the **anode** and **cathode** falls (work function emission) and through **ohmic heating** of the plasma.

### Step 3: The Shielding Action
The electrode coating simultaneously:
- **Decomposes thermally** to release a **shielding gas envelope** (typically CO₂, H₂, CO from cellulose; or Ar/CO₂ from mineral coatings).
- **Melts** to form a **molten slag** that floats on the weld pool (slag density < weld pool density).
- After solidification, the slag forms a crusty layer that **slow-cools the weld**, protecting it from rapid oxidation.

### Step 4: Solidification & Weld Bead Formation
- The molten weld pool solidifies **from the edges inward**, forming a **columnar dendritic grain structure**.
- The slag solidifies **last** (because of its lower melting point) and floats to the top.
- Upon cooling, the **slag crust is chipped off** to reveal the finished weld bead.

---

## 2.2 Equipment Subsystems — The Four Pillars of SMAW

| Subsystem | Function | Shipyard Application Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Welding Power Source** | Provides controlled current/voltage | Constant Current (CC) type — Transformer (AC), Rectifier (DC), Inverter (IGBT-based, modern) |
| **Electrode Holder (Stinger)** | Mechanically grips the electrode & transmits current | Insulated handle, copper alloy jaws, rated for 200–500 A |
| **Welding & Ground Cables** | Carry high current with minimal voltage drop | Typically **50 mm² to 95 mm²** copper; length kept < 30 m |
| **Electrode (Consumable)** | Filler metal + shielding flux in one stick | Cellulosic, rutile, basic, iron-powder — chosen per joint and material |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The power source in SMAW is **Constant Current (CC), NOT Constant Voltage (CV)**. This is because the welder manually controls arc length, and a CC source prevents current runaway when electrode is dipped. This contrasts with GMAW/GTAW which use CV sources.

---

## 2.3 Polarity — Direct Current Electrode Positive (DCEP) vs DCEN

In **DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)** — also called **DC+** or **reverse polarity**:
- The electrode is the **anode (+)**.
- **~70 %** of the heat is generated at the **cathode (workpiece)**.
- Results in **deeper penetration**, deeper weld pool.
- **Most commonly used polarity in SMAW** for structural and shipbuilding work.

In **DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative)** — also called **DC−** or **straight polarity**:
- The electrode is the **cathode (−)**.
- **~70 %** of the heat is at the **anode (electrode tip)**.
- Faster electrode melting, **shallower penetration**, higher deposition rate.
- Used for thin sections, surfacing, and high-deposition work.

In **AC (Alternating Current)**:
- Polarity alternates at **50 Hz**, balancing heat distribution.
- Used for **magnetized plates** (ship repair) where DC arc blow is severe.
- Slightly noisier arc, moderate penetration.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat Input** | $H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ (kJ/mm) | Determines weld mechanical properties & HAZ width |
| **Deposition Rate** | $R_d = \dfrac{W_{wire}}{t}$ (kg/h) | Productivity calculation for shipyard planning |
| **Deposition Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{deposited}}{W_{electrode\ consumed}} \times 100$ | SMAW ~60–65 %, important for cost estimation |
| **Travel Speed** | $S = \dfrac{L}{t}$ (mm/min) | Used in heat input calculation |
| **Arc Force Pressure** | $P_{arc} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{4\pi} \left[\dfrac{1}{2} + \ln\dfrac{R_{anode}}{R_{cathode}}\right]$ | Explains the **keyhole** effect in deep penetration welds |
| **Ohmic Heat in Arc Plasma** | $Q = I^2 R_{plasma}$ | Basis of all arc welding |
| **Penetration Depth (empirical)** | $d_p \propto \dfrac{I}{v \cdot d}$ | Higher current + slower travel + smaller diameter = deeper penetration |

Where:
- $V$ = Arc Voltage (V), $I$ = Welding Current (A)
- $S$ = Travel Speed (mm/min), $L$ = Weld length (mm)
- $t$ = Time (minutes or hours)
- $W_{wire}$ = Mass of electrode wire consumed

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat Input** is THE most frequently tested formula in KTU Module 1. Master the conversion factor: multiply by **60** (to convert per minute) and divide by **1000** (to convert J → kJ). Units must always end in **kJ/mm**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Shipbuilding

1. **Hull Plate Joining** — SMAW with basic-coated low-hydrogen electrodes (e.g., AWS E7018) is the standard for **butt and fillet welds** in ship hulls.
2. **Repair & Maintenance** — On-board repair in dry-dock; portable inverter SMAW machines (weighing ~10 kg) are popular for shipboard use.
3. **Heavy Section Welding** — SMAW handles thick plates (up to 50 mm+) in **butt joints with multi-pass techniques** (root, fill, cap).
4. **Out-of-Position Welding** — SMAW excels at **vertical (3G), overhead (4G), and horizontal (2F)** positions due to slag support.
5. **Cost-Effective for Small Batches** — Unlike mechanized GMAW/SAW, SMAW needs minimal capital investment, ideal for **custom ship sections**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input for a SMAW Weld

**Given Data (Sample Shipbuilding Problem):**
A welder uses SMAW with the following parameters:
- Arc Voltage, $V$ = 25 V
- Welding Current, $I$ = 200 A
- Travel Speed, $S$ = 250 mm/min
- Arc efficiency factor (for SMAW), $\eta_{arc}$ = 0.80

**Required:** Calculate the **Heat Input** per unit length of weld.

### Step 1: Define Heat Input
Heat input is defined as the arc energy supplied per unit length of weld, corrected by the **arc thermal efficiency** (which accounts for losses by radiation, convection, and spatter).

$$
H = \eta_{arc} \times \frac{V \times I}{S}
$$

where:
- $H$ = Heat Input in J/mm (will be converted to kJ/mm)
- $\eta_{arc}$ = Arc efficiency (0.80 for SMAW)
- $V \times I$ = Arc power in Watts (= J/s)
- $S$ = Travel speed in mm/s (must be converted from mm/min)

### Step 2: Convert Travel Speed to mm/s

$$
S = \frac{250 \text{ mm/min}}{60 \text{ s/min}} = 4.167 \text{ mm/s}
$$

*(This conversion is critical — a common error is to use mm/min directly and forget the factor of 60.)*

### Step 3: Substitute the Values

$$
H = 0.80 \times \frac{25 \times 200}{4.167}
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate Numerator and Denominator

$$
H = 0.80 \times \frac{5000}{4.167}
$$

$$
H = 0.80 \times 1200.05
$$

$$
H \approx 960.04 \text{ J/mm}
$$

### Step 5: Convert to Standard Engineering Unit (kJ/mm)

$$
H = \frac{960.04}{1000} \approx 0.96 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{H \approx 0.96 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** For shipbuilding-grade mild steel plates, the **recommended maximum heat input** is typically **~1.5 kJ/mm to 2.5 kJ/mm** to avoid excessive **HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone) coarsening** and **toughness loss**. Our value of 0.96 kJ/mm is well within acceptable limits — confirming the parameters are safe for ship structural welding.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Welding Parameters on Weld Quality

| Parameter Increased | Penetration | Bead Width | Depth-to-Width Ratio | Spatter | Distortion | HAZ Width |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Current (I)** ↑ | ↑↑ Strongly increases | ↑ Slightly increases | Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases |
| **Voltage (V)** ↑ | ↓ Decreases | ↑↑ Strongly increases | Decreases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases |
| **Travel Speed (S)** ↑ | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | Variable | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases |
| **Arc Length** ↑ | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | Decreases | ↑↑ Severe increase | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases |
| **Electrode Angle** (push) | ↓ | ↑ Wide | Decreases | ↑ | — | — |
| **Electrode Angle** (drag) | ↑ | ↓ Narrow | Increases | ↓ | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This matrix is a **favourite 7-mark question** under KTU ESE Part B. Memorize the **direction of arrows** (↑ or ↓) for each parameter. A common error is confusing "increasing voltage = increases penetration." It actually **decreases** penetration because a longer arc spreads heat over a wider area.

---

## 3.3 Electrode Classification — Analytical Mapping

### AWS A5.1 System (American Welding Society)

For example: **E7018**
- **E** = Electrode
- **70** = Minimum Tensile Strength = **70,000 psi (≈ 490 MPa)**
- **1** = Position — **1 = All positions** (2 = Flat & Horizontal only)
- **8** = Coating Type & Current — **8 = Basic low-hydrogen coating, DCEP**

### IS 814 : 2004 System (Indian Standard)

For example: **E 6013 Ni**
- **E** = Electrode
- **60** = Minimum UTS = **60 kgf/mm² (≈ 588 MPa)**
- **1** = Welding position
- **3** = Coating type — **3 = Rutile (TiO₂) coating**
- **Ni** = Special features (Ni-alloyed)

### Common Electrode Coating Types

| Coating | Type | Key Constituents | Properties | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cellulosic** | E6010, E6011 | Wood pulp, cellulose | Deep penetration, fast freezing slag | Root pass in pipe welding |
| **Rutile** | E6012, E6013 | TiO₂ | Smooth bead, easy arc, low spatter | Thin sheet, general fabrication |
| **Basic (Low-Hydrogen)** | E7016, E7018 | CaCO₃, CaF₂, Fe-powder | Low H₂, high toughness, low cracking | **Shipbuilding hulls, pressure vessels** |
| **Iron-Powder** | E7028, E7048 | Fe-powder in flux | High deposition, low spatter | Flat & horizontal fillet welds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shipbuilding Standard:** The **E7018 electrode** is the **workhorse of ship hull welding** worldwide. Its basic low-hydrogen coating ensures:
> - **Diffusible Hydrogen < 5 ml/100g** (per AWS A5.1)
> - **Charpy V-notch impact toughness > 27 J at −29 °C**
> - Compliance with **IACS UR W11/W31** and **ABS / DNV / LR** rules.

---

## 3.4 Sample Calculation — Electrode Consumption for a Hull Weld

**Given:**
- Total weld length, $L$ = 10 m
- Fillet leg size = 6 mm
- Electrode diameter = 4 mm
- Length of electrode stick = 450 mm
- Mass per electrode = ~55 g

**Required:** Number of electrodes consumed.

### Step 1: Weld Metal Volume (Fillet Triangular Cross-section)

$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{leg}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 6^2 = 18 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 2: Total Volume of Weld Metal

$$
V = A \times L = 18 \times 10000 = 180000 \text{ mm}^3 = 180 \text{ cm}^3
$$

### Step 3: Mass of Weld Metal (Density of Steel ≈ 7.85 g/cm³)

$$
m_{weld} = 180 \times 7.85 = 1413 \text{ g} \approx 1.41 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Deposition Efficiency (η = 0.65)

$$
m_{electrode\ needed} = \frac{1.41}{0.65} = 2.17 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 5: Number of Electrodes

$$
N = \frac{2170 \text{ g}}{55 \text{ g/electrode}} \approx 39.5 \approx \boxed{40 \text{ electrodes}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This type of estimation is used by shipyards for **consumable budgeting** during dry-dock refit. A 100 m hull weld of 6 mm fillet would consume ~400 electrodes — a substantial cost driver.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic: Complete SMAW Working Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[PS: Welding Power Source Constant Current]
    CB1[CB1: Welding Cable]
    EH[EH: Electrode Holder Stinger]
    ARC[ARC: Electric Arc 6000C]
    ELEC[ELEC: Flux Coated Electrode Consumable]
    WP[WP: Workpiece Hull Plate]
    WC[WC: Work Clamp Earth]
    CB2[CB2: Ground Cable]
    SG[SG: Shielding Gas from Flux Decomposition]
    SL[SL: Molten Slag Layer]
    WP2[WP2: Solidified Weld Bead]
    HAZ[HAZ: Heat Affected Zone]
    BM[BM: Base Metal Unaffected]

    PS --> CB1 --> EH
    EH --> ARC
    ELEC --> EH
    ARC --> WP
    WP --> WC --> CB2 --> PS
    ARC --> SG
    SG --> WP
    ARC --> SL
    SL --> WP2
    WP2 --> HAZ
    HAZ --> BM
```

**Block Interpretation:**
- The outer loop (PS → CB1 → EH → ARC → WP → WC → CB2 → PS) forms the **electrical circuit**.
- The inner blocks (SG, SL, WP2) show the **metallurgical transformations** happening at the joint.
- The arc is the **energy conversion zone** — converting electrical energy → thermal energy → metallurgical bonding.

---

## 4.2 Schematic: SMAW Electrode Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Top: Bare Grip End]
    B[Zone A: Coating Thickness Uniform 1-1.5mm]
    C[Zone B: Flux Coated Length 350-450mm]
    D[Tip: Bare Stub Length 25mm]
    E[Core Wire Mild Steel or Alloy]

    A --> B --> C --> D
    E -.runs through center.-> C
```

**Anatomy Explanation:**
- The **core wire** carries the welding current and melts to become filler metal.
- The **flux coating** decomposes to form shielding gas + slag.
- The **bare grip end** is held by the electrode holder (uncoated to ensure good electrical contact).
- The **bare stub at the tip** ensures easy arc striking.

---

```mermaid
    POWER --> CONTROLWIRE

```

## 4.4 Schematic: Heat Input vs Weld Quality Trade-off Curve

```mermaid

    L1[Low Heat Input Less than 0.8 kJ/mm]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Insight:** The "Acceptable Range" depends on the **plate thickness**, **material grade**, and **class society rules**. For grade A mild steel (6–20 mm), the KTU-recommended heat input for shipbuilding is **~1.0–1.8 kJ/mm**.
