# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) and mention any four of its advantages.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
SMAW is a manual arc welding process in which coalescence of metals is achieved by heating with an electric arc between a flux-coated consumable electrode and the workpiece. The arc is shielded by gas and slag from the decomposition of the electrode coating.

**Advantages:**
1. Equipment is simple, portable, and low-cost — ideal for shipyard and shipboard use.
2. Can be used in **all welding positions** (1G, 2F, 3G, 4G).
3. Suitable for a wide range of metals — mild steel, low-alloy steel, cast iron, stainless steel.
4. Works well in **outdoor / windy conditions** because the flux provides its own shielding — no external gas cylinder required.

---

### Q2. What is meant by "low-hydrogen" electrode? Why is it preferred for shipbuilding?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
A **low-hydrogen electrode** (e.g., AWS E7018, IS E 6018) has a **basic flux coating** made primarily of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) and calcium fluoride (CaF₂), which produces very low amounts of diffusible hydrogen (< 5 ml/100g) in the weld deposit.

**Why preferred for shipbuilding:**
1. Prevents **hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC)** in thick hull plates.
2. Provides **high Charpy impact toughness** at sub-zero temperatures (e.g., −29 °C).
3. Meets **IACS UR W11/W31** and class society (DNV, ABS, LR) requirements for hull structural welds.
4. Reduces risk of **cold cracking** in highly restrained joints.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [Chosen Path: Fundamentals + Heat Input]**

**[KTU University Exam – Module 1 Past Pattern]**

#### (a) Explain the working principle of Shielded Metal Arc Welding with a neat block diagram. List the equipment required. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
1. The welding power source supplies current through the welding cable to the electrode holder.
2. When the electrode touches the workpiece, a **short circuit** occurs.
3. As the electrode is lifted by ~2–4 mm, the arc strikes. The arc temperature (~6000 °C) melts the **electrode core wire** (filler metal) and a thin layer of the **base metal** (workpiece).
4. The molten metal forms the **weld pool**.
5. The flux coating decomposes thermally to produce:
   - A **shielding gas envelope** (CO₂, CO, H₂) that displaces atmospheric O₂ and N₂.
   - A **molten slag** that floats on the weld pool, further protecting it and slowing the cooling rate.
6. As the weld cools, the slag solidifies on top. It is later chipped off to expose the solid weld bead.

**Equipment Required:**
- Welding Power Source (Transformer/Rectifier/Inverter — Constant Current type)
- Electrode Holder (Stinger)
- Welding Cable & Ground Cable
- Work Clamp (Earth Clamp)
- Consumable Flux-Coated Electrodes
- Personal Protective Equipment (PPE): Welding helmet, gloves, apron, leather boots

**Block Diagram:**
*(Refer to SECTION 4.1 schematic in these notes.)*

**Key Functions:**
- **Power Source:** Provides controlled current at constant-C characteristic.
- **Electrode Holder:** Holds the electrode, transmits current.
- **Electrode:** Filler metal + shielding medium in one unit.

---

#### (b) A SMAW operation uses current 180 A, voltage 24 V, and travel speed 300 mm/min. If arc efficiency is 0.80, calculate the heat input. Comment on whether the heat input is suitable for a 12 mm thick mild steel hull plate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V$ = 24 V
- $I$ = 180 A
- $S$ = 300 mm/min
- $\eta_{arc}$ = 0.80

**Step 1: Convert travel speed to mm/s**

$$
S = \frac{300}{60} = 5 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Heat Input Formula**

$$
H = \eta_{arc} \times \frac{V \times I}{S}
$$

$$
H = 0.80 \times \frac{24 \times 180}{5}
$$

$$
H = 0.80 \times \frac{4320}{5}
$$

$$
H = 0.80 \times 864
$$

$$
H = 691.2 \text{ J/mm}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to kJ/mm**

$$
H = \frac{691.2}{1000} = 0.691 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 4: Comment on Suitability**

For a 12 mm thick mild steel hull plate (Grade A), the recommended heat input range is typically **0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm** (per IACS guidelines).

Our calculated value of **0.691 kJ/mm is slightly LOW**. While it provides good toughness, it may lead to:
- **Lack of fusion** between passes in multi-pass welds.
- **Undercut** at the weld toe.
- **Faster cooling** may increase hardness and risk of **hydrogen cracking** in thick sections.

**Recommendation:** Increase the current to ~220 A or reduce travel speed to ~200 mm/min to raise heat input to ~1.0 kJ/mm.

$$
\boxed{H = 0.691 \text{ kJ/mm — Slightly below recommended range}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Chosen Path: Electrode Classification + Shipbuilding Standards]**

**[KTU University Exam – Module 1 Past Pattern]**

#### (a) Classify welding electrodes as per AWS and IS systems with suitable examples. Describe the characteristics of a basic-coated low-hydrogen electrode. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**AWS A5.1 Classification Example: E7018**
- **E** → Electrode
- **70** → Minimum tensile strength = 70,000 psi (~490 MPa)
- **1** → Welding position = All positions
- **8** → Coating type = Basic (low-hydrogen), used with **DCEP**

**IS 814 Classification Example: E 6013**
- **E** → Electrode
- **60** → Minimum UTS = 60 kgf/mm² (~588 MPa)
- **1** → Welding position
- **3** → Coating = **Rutile (TiO₂)** type

**Characteristics of Basic-Coated Low-Hydrogen Electrode (E7018 / E 6018):**

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Flux Type** | Calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) + Calcium fluoride (CaF₂) |
| **Operating Current** | **DCEP only** (mandatory) |
| **Hydrogen Content** | Very low: < 5 ml/100g of weld metal |
| **Weld Bead Appearance** | Slightly convex, smooth, fine ripples |
| **Slag Removal** | Easy, self-lifting slag |
| **Toughness** | Excellent Charpy impact — > 27 J at −29 °C |
| **Mechanical Properties** | High strength, high ductility, low crack sensitivity |
| **Storage Requirement** | Must be stored in a **heated oven at 120–150 °C** to prevent moisture absorption |
| **Applications** | Hull plates, pressure vessels, bridges, offshore structures |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinguishing Feature:** Basic-coated electrodes require **DCEP** because the coating chemistry needs electrons flowing in a specific direction to ensure proper slag formation and deoxidation.

---

#### (b) Discuss the role of SMAW in shipbuilding. Mention any six advantages and three limitations of SMAW for hull fabrication. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of SMAW in Shipbuilding:**
1. **Hull Plate Joining:** SMAW is the **primary manual process** for joining mild steel hull plates in butt and fillet configurations.
2. **Structural Assembly:** Used for assembling stiffeners, brackets, bulkheads, and superstructure components.
3. **Repair & Maintenance:** Essential for shipboard and dry-dock repairs due to portability of inverter SMAW machines.
4. **Out-of-Position Welding:** SMAW is unmatched for welding in **vertical-up (3G), overhead (4G), and horizontal (2F)** positions.
5. **Root Pass Welding:** Used as the root run in pipe and stiffener joints before subsequent SAW/GMAW fill passes.
6. **Compliance:** Meets IACS UR W11, ABS, DNV, and Lloyd's Register standards for ship classification.

**Six Advantages of SMAW:**
1. **Low capital cost** — much cheaper than GMAW/SAW equipment.
2. **Portable** — inverter machines can be carried to any location on the ship.
3. **All-position capability** — excellent for vertical and overhead welding.
4. **No external shielding gas** required — works in **windy outdoor conditions**.
5. **Wide material compatibility** — from mild steel to stainless steel, cast iron, and dissimilar metals.
6. **Simple operation** — does not require highly skilled operators for routine welds.

**Three Limitations of SMAW:**
1. **Low deposition rate** (1–3 kg/h) — slow compared to SAW (up to 15 kg/h).
2. **Frequent electrode changing** — interrupts productivity; each stick lasts only a few minutes.
3. **High spatter and slag inclusion risk** — requires post-weld slag chipping and cleaning, increasing labor cost.

**Conclusion:** Despite mechanization trends (FCAW, SAW), SMAW remains **indispensable in shipbuilding** for its versatility, portability, and reliability in repair and out-of-position scenarios.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in SMAW Questions:**
>
> 1. **Heat Input Unit Error:** Always convert travel speed from mm/min to mm/s by dividing by **60**, and convert J to kJ by dividing by **1000**. Missing either factor yields a 2-mark penalty.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Arc Efficiency:** In many KTU questions, arc efficiency is given (typically 0.80 for SMAW) but students often forget to multiply it. Always state: *"For SMAW, η_arc = 0.80"*.
>
> 3. **Confusing Polarity:** DCEP (Electrode Positive) gives **deep penetration**, not DCEN. Memorize: *"Reverse polarity = deeper penetration = more heat at workpiece"*.
>
> 4. **Electrode Designation Misread:** E7018 — students often write "all-position rutile electrode." It is a **basic low-hydrogen electrode**, not rutile. The 4th digit is critical.
>
> 5. **DCEP vs AC for E7018:** Basic electrodes **MUST be used with DCEP only**. Using DCEN or AC will result in poor arc stability and porosity — and in exam answers, this is a 1-mark loss.
>
> 6. **Slag vs Gas Shielding Confusion:** SMAW uses **BOTH** shielding mechanisms (gas + slag). If asked to "explain shielding," mention both — students often mention only one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SMAW (Module 1):**
>
> ☐ **Full Form:** Shielded Metal Arc Welding (AWS) / Manual Metal Arc Welding (ISO)
>
> ☐ **Power Source Type:** Constant Current (CC), NOT Constant Voltage
>
> ☐ **Standard Voltage:** Arc voltage = 18–32 V; OCV = 50–90 V
>
> ☐ **Arc Temperature:** ~6000 °C (6000–8000 K)
>
> ☐ **Polarity Used:** DCEP (Electrode Positive) for most structural work — deep penetration
>
> ☐ **Heat Input Formula:** $H = \dfrac{\eta_{arc} \times V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ in kJ/mm
>
> ☐ **Arc Efficiency for SMAW:** 0.80 (always use this if not specified)
>
> ☐ **Deposition Efficiency for SMAW:** 60–65 % (lower than SAW/GMAW)
>
> ☐ **Preferred Electrode for Ship Hulls:** AWS E7018 (Basic low-hydrogen, DCEP, all positions)
>
> ☐ **Storage of E7018:** Keep in **oven at 120–150 °C** to avoid moisture pickup
>
> ☐ **Shielding Mechanism:** Dual — **Gas** (from flux decomposition) + **Slag** (molten flux)
>
> ☐ **AWS Designation Format:** E XX YY Z (E=Electrode, XX=UTS in ksi×10, YY=Position, Z=Coating+Current)
>
> ☐ **Shipbuilding Standards:** IACS UR W11/W31, ABS, DNV, Lloyd's Register — require low-hydrogen electrodes
>
> ☐ **Key Limitation:** Low deposition rate + frequent electrode changes → not suitable for high-volume production (where SAW/FCAW excel)
>
> ☐ **Key Strength:** All-position capability + portability + outdoor usability + low cost

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electroslag Welding (ESW)** is a highly productive, single-pass, **vertical-up** fusion welding process used for joining thick sections of ferrous and non-ferrous metals. Heat for welding is generated by passing an electric current through a **molten conductive slag bath** held between the workpieces and water-cooled copper shoes, which melts the filler wire and the parent metal edges continuously. The process is characterized by the **absence of an electric arc** in steady-state operation.

The process was developed at the **E.O. Paton Electric Welding Institute, Kyiv, Ukraine (USSR)** around **1950–1958** and is formally codified under:
- **AWS A5.25 / A5.25M** – Specification for Solid and Composite Electrodes and Fluxes for Electroslag Welding
- **ISO 14171** – Electrodes and fluxes for electroslag welding
- **IACS UR W28** – Rules for use in ship construction (highly relevant for KTU Shipbuilding syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of shipbuilding (PENST741), Electroslag Welding is used for thick-plate fabrication of hull structures, rudder horns, stern frames, propeller-shaft brackets, and deck-to-bottom plating in one single vertical pass without plate beveling in most cases.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Underground Lava Sandwich"**
> Imagine two large slices of bread standing upright, with a thin gap between them. You pour a thick layer of **hot, electrically conductive molten lava (the slag)** into the gap and keep a thin metal wire dipped into it. Now, run an electric current through the wire. The lava resists the flow, heats up to over 2000 °C, melts the wire, and slowly melts the bread edges. As the lava-solidified metal rises, you pour more lava in. Two **cool copper plates on the sides act as ice walls** to keep the lava from spilling out. The result: a deep, molten "filler sandwich" that fuses the two bread slices from bottom to top in one go. That is exactly how Electroslag Welding works on a 50–600 mm thick steel plate in a shipyard.

## 1.3 Key Process Identifiers

| Identifier | Typical Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Process Type | Arc-less, resistance-fusion | Stable melt pool, no spatter |
| Position | **Vertical-up (1G)** | Gravity-driven slag/metal separation |
| Plate Thickness | **25 mm to 600 mm** | Single-pass capability |
| Welding Current | **400 A to 1000 A** | DC (Electrode +ve, Work −ve) |
| Arc Voltage | **30 V to 50 V** | Lower than arc welding |
| Slag Temperature | **1800 °C to 2200 °C** | Above metal fusion point |
| Slag Depth | **40 mm to 60 mm** | Resistance heating zone |
| Deposition Rate | **20 kg/h to 60 kg/h** | Very high productivity |
| Heat Input | **1500 kJ/mm to 2500 kJ/mm** | Highest of all arc processes |
| Wire Diameter | **1.6 mm, 2.0 mm, 2.4 mm, 3.0 mm** | Single or multi-wire |
| Flux Type | Fluoride–oxide (CaF₂, Al₂O₃, SiO₂) | Conductive, low melting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants in Play**
> - Resistivity of molten ESW slag: **≈ 0.01 Ω·cm to 0.02 Ω·cm**
> - Melting point of typical ESW flux: **≈ 1250 °C**
> - Specific electrical energy: **V × I × t** in kJ

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

Electroslag Welding is executed in two distinct stages: **Strike Phase** and **Steady-State Phase**.

### Stage A — Strike (Transient Arc) Phase
1. The gap between two vertical workpieces (held 20–35 mm apart) is dammed at the bottom with a **start tab / run-off plate**.
2. **Water-cooled copper shoes** are mounted on either side of the joint, sliding upward mechanically as the weld progresses.
3. A powdered flux is poured into the gap and a steel wool / graphite slug is used to initiate conduction.
4. The wire electrode touches the bottom tab — a brief **arc is struck** in the flux. This arc melts the surrounding flux, forming a **molten slag pool (≈ 40–60 mm deep)**.
5. Once the slag pool is established and deep enough to submerge the wire tip, the arc is **quenched by short-circuit-like current flow** through the molten slag.

### Stage B — Steady-State (Arc-less) Phase
6. Current flows from the **+ve electrode wire** → through the **resistive molten slag** → into the **−ve workpieces** (DCEN–like behavior; but current path is through slag, not through an arc).
7. **Ohmic (Joule) heating** is generated: $Q = I^2 R t$, raising slag temperature to **≈ 2000 °C**.
8. This superheated slag melts the **consumable electrode wire** continuously as it is fed downwards.
9. Droplets of molten metal fall through the slag, are **desulphurised and dephosphorised**, and collect as a **molten metal pool** floating on top of the slag.
10. As metal is deposited, the **weld zone solidifies** at the bottom (against the start tab and water-cooled shoes), while the top surface remains molten.
11. The **copper shoes and the wire-feed nozzle** are mechanically raised at a controlled travel speed (typically **10–20 mm/min**) so the slag/metal pool maintains constant depth.
12. At the top, an **end tab / crater tab** receives the last molten metal, which is later cut off and discarded.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it Matters |
|---|---|
| Water-cooled copper shoes | Prevent slag spillage, provide directional solidification, and extract ≈ 60 % of input heat |
| DC current (Electrode +ve) | Provides **cathodic cleaning** at the work surface, suppressing porosity in the weld |
| Slag pool depth (40–60 mm) | Ensures wire tip is fully submerged → arc quenched → stable resistance heating |
| Steady travel speed | Maintains the slag pool size, preventing **"burn-through"** or **"incomplete fusion"** |
| Start and end tabs | Eliminate start/end crater cracks; provide a sacrificial extension of the joint |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All units must be consistent.** Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` in place of the vertical pipe `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$ | **Heat Input** per unit weld length | kJ/mm |
| 2 | $Q_{total} = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Total electrical energy supplied | kJ |
| 3 | $Q_{Joule} = I^2 \cdot R_{slag} \cdot t$ | Heat generated in slag (Joule's law) | J |
| 4 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Welding power | W (or kW) |
| 5 | $R_{slag} = \dfrac{\rho_{slag} \cdot L_{slag}}{A_{slag}}$ | Effective resistance of slag column | Ω |
| 6 | $m_{dep} = k \cdot I$ | Deposition rate (k is wire-feed constant) | kg/h |
| 7 | $T_{slag} = T_{m,flux} + \Delta T$ | Slag temperature above flux melting point | °C |
| 8 | $v_{travel} = \dfrac{L_{weld}}{t_{weld}}$ | Average vertical travel speed | mm/min |
| 9 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{Q_{melt}}{Q_{total}} \approx 0.30 \text{ to } 0.40$ | Thermal efficiency of ESW (high!) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $d_{slag} \geq 2 \cdot d_{wire}$ | Rule of thumb: slag depth vs wire diameter | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility of ESW Equations**
> - The heat-input equation (1) lets a **welding engineer in a shipyard** pre-calculate HAZ softening, distortion, and residual stress in thick hull plating (KTU Module-1 design connection).
> - Equation (3) is the foundation of the **arc-less** nature of ESW — understanding it lets you justify why slag chemistry and depth must be tightly controlled.
> - Equation (9) explains why ESW is preferred over SAW for very thick plates: higher thermal efficiency means more of the input energy actually melts the joint.

## 2.4 Comparison Snapshot (ESW vs. Other Heavy-Section Processes)

| Feature | ESW | SAW | MMA (SMAW) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plate thickness (single pass) | **25–600 mm** | ≤ 25 mm | ≤ 10 mm |
| Heat input (kJ/mm) | **1500–2500** | 200–600 | 80–200 |
| Arc present? | **No (steady state)** | Yes | Yes |
| Deposition rate | **Very high** | High | Moderate |
| Slag function | **Heat source** | Protection | Protection |
| Typical shipbuilding use | **Hull bottom plates, rudder horns** | Butt joints in plating | Tack, repair |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input per Unit Length

The **heat input (HI)** is the single most-asked KTU numerical for any welding process. It is the energy delivered per millimetre of weld length and directly controls HAZ size, distortion, and mechanical properties.

### Step 1: Power Delivered by the Welding Source

The electrical power supplied to the electrode–workpiece system is the product of the arc/stack voltage and the welding current.

$$
P = V \cdot I
$$

where
- $P$ = power in **watts (W)**
- $V$ = process voltage in **volts (V)**
- $I$ = welding current in **amperes (A)**

### Step 2: Total Energy Over a Welding Interval

The total energy delivered over a time interval $t$ (in seconds) is:

$$
Q_{total} = P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

where
- $Q_{total}$ = total heat in **joules (J)**

### Step 3: Express Welding Time in Terms of Travel Speed

The welding time for a joint of length $L$ (in mm) at a travel speed $v$ (in mm/min) is:

$$
t = \dfrac{L}{v} \quad \text{[seconds]}
$$

**Conversion logic:** If $v$ is in **mm/min**, dividing $L$ (in mm) by $v$ (in mm/min) gives time in **minutes**. To convert minutes → seconds, multiply by 60:

$$
t \, [\text{s}] = \dfrac{L \, [\text{mm}]}{v \, [\text{mm/min}]} \times 60
$$

### Step 4: Substitute Back into the Energy Equation

$$
Q_{total} = V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{L \cdot 60}{v}
$$

### Step 5: Heat Per Unit Length

To obtain the heat input **per millimetre of weld**, divide by $L$:

$$
HI = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{L} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v}
$$

with units: $V[\text{V}] \cdot I[\text{A}] \cdot 60[\text{s/min}] \;/\; v[\text{mm/min}] = \text{J/mm}$.

### Step 6: Convert to the Standard kJ/mm Form

Divide by 1000 to convert joules to kilojoules:

$$
\boxed{\,HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}\,}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for heat input. Memorize it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Joule Heating in the Molten Slag

ESW is fundamentally a **resistance-heating process**. The slag acts as a controlled electrical resistor.

### Step 1: Define Slag Resistance

Treat the molten slag column as a cylindrical resistor of length equal to the slag depth $L_{slag}$ and cross-section equal to the wire-contact area $A_{slag}$:

$$
R_{slag} = \dfrac{\rho_{slag} \cdot L_{slag}}{A_{slag}}
$$

where $\rho_{slag}$ is the **electrical resistivity** of molten flux (≈ 0.01–0.02 Ω·cm at 2000 °C).

### Step 2: Apply Joule's First Law

Energy dissipated in the slag in time $t$ is:

$$
Q_{Joule} = I^2 \cdot R_{slag} \cdot t
$$

### Step 3: Expand Using Resistivity

$$
Q_{Joule} = \dfrac{I^2 \cdot \rho_{slag} \cdot L_{slag} \cdot t}{A_{slag}}
$$

### Step 4: Express as Power Density

Power per unit slag volume $V_{slag} = L_{slag} \cdot A_{slag}$:

$$
\dfrac{P}{V_{slag}} = \dfrac{I^2 \cdot \rho_{slag}}{A_{slag}^2}
$$

**Insight:** This shows that **slag resistivity is the dial a welder turns** (by flux chemistry) to control the process. If $\rho_{slag}$ is too low, the current passes without enough heating — the slag won't reach melting temperature. If too high, the slag overheats and the wire feed rate must be reduced to prevent burn-through.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Efficiency and Melting Rate

### Step 1: Heat Required to Melt Filler Wire

The heat needed to melt mass $m$ of filler wire from $T_0$ to $T_m$ (and provide latent heat) is:

$$
Q_{melt} = m \cdot \left[ c_p (T_m - T_0) + L_f \right]
$$

where
- $c_p$ = specific heat of steel ≈ **0.46 kJ/(kg·K)**
- $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion ≈ **272 kJ/kg**
- $T_m - T_0$ ≈ **1500 − 25 = 1475 K**

### Step 2: Thermal Efficiency

$$
\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{Q_{melt}}{Q_{total}}
$$

For ESW, $\eta_{thermal} \approx 0.30 \text{ to } 0.40$ — meaning 30–40 % of the input energy goes into actual melting. The rest is lost to copper-shoe cooling, radiation, and convection.

### Step 3: Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A vertical ESW joint is welded at $V = 38$ V, $I = 600$ A, $v = 12$ mm/min. Find the heat input.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HI &= \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000} \\[4pt]
   &= \dfrac{38 \times 600 \times 60}{12 \times 1000} \\[4pt]
   &= \dfrac{1{,}368{,}000}{12{,}000} \\[4pt]
   &= \boxed{114 \text{ kJ/mm? No — recheck units}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Trap:** The numerical value comes out in **kJ/mm only if $v$ is in mm/min**. Re-verify: $38 \times 600 = 22{,}800$ W. Per minute, that is $22{,}800 \times 60 = 1{,}368{,}000$ J = 1368 kJ. Per mm of weld at 12 mm/min, this is $1368 / 12 = 114$ kJ/mm. The answer is **114 kJ/mm** — which is actually too low for ESW. The numbers given are SAW-like, illustrating that the formula works the same for any arc/stack-based process.

If we use the **typical ESW values** ($V = 40$ V, $I = 750$ A, $v = 12$ mm/min):

$$
\begin{aligned}
HI &= \dfrac{40 \times 750 \times 60}{12 \times 1000} \\[4pt]
   &= \dfrac{1{,}800{,}000}{12{,}000} \\[4pt]
   &= \boxed{150 \text{ kJ/mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

(For thicker plates at $v = 6$ mm/min, this rises to **300 kJ/mm**, which is the typical ESW band.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ESW Process Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[ThreePhaseAC Mains Supply] --> A2[Rectifier and WeldingTransformer]
    A2 -- DC Positive to Electrode --> A3[Consumable Wire Electrode 1.6 to 3.0 mm]
    A2 -- DC Negative to Workpiece --> A4[Workpiece Plate Top Edge]
    A3 -- Fed Downward by Wire Drive Motor --> A5[Wire Tip Submerged in Slag]
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6[Resistance Heating in Molten Slag 1800 to 2200 C]
    A6 -- Joule Heat --> A7[Melting of Electrode Wire Droplets]
    A6 -- Joule Heat --> A8[Melting of Workpiece Edges]
    A7 --> A9[Metal Droplets Fall Through Slag Desulphurisation]
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Liquid Metal Pool 10 to 15 mm Deep]
    A10 --> A11[Solidifying Weld Metal Column Water Cooled Shoes Extract Heat]
    A11 --> A12[Completed Weld Fused into Hull Plate]
    A6 --> A13[Slag Pool 40 to 60 mm Deep]
    A13 -- Floats Above --> A10
    A13 --> A14[Recirculating Slag Convection Currents]
    A14 --> A6
    A11 --> A15[Copper Water Cooled Shoes Move Upward at 10 to 20 mm per min]
    A15 --> A6
    A12 --> A16[Top Crater Tab Cut Off and Discarded]
```

## 4.2 Functional Subsystem Map of an ESW Station

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerDelivery
        A1[Mains 415 V 3 Phase] --> A2[Step Down Transformer] --> A3[SCR Controlled Rectifier] --> A4[DC Bus Bars]
    end
    subgraph WireFeed
        B1[Wire Spool] --> B2[Straightener Unit] --> B3[Drive Rolls] --> B4[Conduit and Nozzle]
    end
    subgraph SlagandMetal
        C1[Flux Hopper] --> C2[Granular Flux ANF 6 Type] --> C3[Slag Pool Bath]
        C3 --> C4[Metal Pool Floating on Slag]
        C4 --> C5[Solidified Weld Column]
    end
    subgraph CoolingandMotion
        D1[Water Tank Chiller] --> D2[Copper Shoes Cooling Circuit] --> D3[Vertical Travel Carriage Drive]
        D3 --> C5
    end
    A4 --> C3
    B4 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Sequence of Operations (Time-Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Tab Fitted at Joint Bottom] --> S2[Copper Shoes Mounted on Both Sides]
    S2 --> S3[Flux Poured into Gap 20 to 35 mm]
    S3 --> S4[Wire Fed Down and Arc Struck in Powder Transient Phase]
    S4 --> S5[Slag Pool Forms Wire Tip Submerges Arc Quenched]
    S5 --> S6[Steady State Resistance Heating Begins]
    S6 --> S7[Wire and Travel Carriage Move Upward Continuously]
    S7 --> S8[Top Crater Tab Welds Out and is Cut Off]
    S8 --> S9[Visual and NDT Inspection Radiography and Ultrasonic]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exam:** When asked "Draw a neat sketch of ESW process" in an exam, draw a vertical joint, label the workpieces, slag pool, metal pool, solidified weld, copper shoes, water inlet/outlet, wire feed nozzle, start tab, end tab, power source +ve and −ve terminals. A clear labelled diagram fetches **2–3 easy marks** in Part A or as a sub-part in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** *Define Electroslag Welding. Why is it called an "arc-less" process?*
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Electroslag Welding (ESW) is a single-pass vertical-up fusion welding process used to join thick metallic sections, in which the heat required for fusion is generated by the **electrical resistance (Joule heating)** of a molten conductive slag bath held between the workpieces and water-cooled copper shoes. It is termed "arc-less" because, in steady-state operation, no electric arc exists — the welding current flows continuously through the molten slag from the consumable electrode to the workpieces, and the wire tip remains submerged inside the slag pool.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** *List any three advantages and one limitation of Electroslag Welding in shipbuilding.*
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
**Advantages:**
1. Thick plates (up to 600 mm) can be joined in a **single vertical pass** without beveling.
2. Very **high deposition rate (20–60 kg/h)** improves shipyard productivity.
3. Slag treatment refines the weld metal, giving **low sulphur and low phosphorus** welds.

**Limitation:**
1. Process is **limited to the vertical (1G) position** and **ferrous / some non-ferrous metals only**; high heat input may cause coarse grains in the HAZ.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 1 | CO-1, CO-2]**

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **principle, setup, and sequence of operations** of the Electroslag Welding process. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for **heat input per unit length** of weld and apply it to compute the heat input for an ESW joint welded at $V = 42$ V, $I = 800$ A, and travel speed $v = 8$ mm/min. Comment on the HAZ implications. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Principle (2 marks):** ESW uses **Joule heating** in a molten conductive slag bath (CaF₂–Al₂O₃–SiO₂ type flux) to melt the consumable wire and the parent plate edges. Heat is generated by $Q = I^2 R t$ as current passes through the slag.
2. **Setup (3 marks):** Two vertical plates are clamped 20–35 mm apart. **Water-cooled copper shoes** are bolted on both sides to retain the molten pool. A start tab closes the bottom. A flux hopper feeds granular flux. A wire-feed nozzle delivers 1.6–3.0 mm wire from above. A DC source provides 400–1000 A at 30–50 V.
3. **Sequence (2 marks):** (i) Start tab fitted, (ii) flux poured, (iii) arc struck transiently, (iv) slag pool forms, (v) arc quenches, (vi) steady-state resistance heating begins, (vii) wire feeds continuously, (viii) copper shoes rise with the pool, (ix) end tab receives final metal, (x) tabs cut off and weld is NDT-inspected.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation (4 marks):** Starting from $P = V I$, total heat $Q_{total} = V I t$, and $t = (L / v) \times 60$ (with $v$ in mm/min), the heat per unit length is:

$$
HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

**Numerical Substitution (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HI &= \dfrac{42 \times 800 \times 60}{8 \times 1000} \\[4pt]
   &= \dfrac{2{,}016{,}000}{8{,}000} \\[4pt]
   &= \boxed{252 \text{ kJ/mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**HAZ Implication (1 mark):** At 252 kJ/mm, the heat-affected zone in the parent plate will be **very wide (≈ 8–12 mm)** with **coarse prior-austenite grains**, leading to reduced toughness. Pre-heating is unnecessary (the process itself acts as a pre-heat), but **PWHT (Post-Weld Heat Treatment)** is mandatory for marine-class plates to restore toughness.

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **role of molten slag** in Electroslag Welding. How is the slag chemistry chosen for shipbuilding-grade low-alloy steels? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **Electroslag Welding (ESW)** with **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** for ship hull fabrication, with specific reference to plate thickness range, deposition rate, heat input, slag function, and metallurgical quality. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Role of Slag (4 marks):**
1. **Heat source** — slag is a resistor, $Q = I^2 R t$, providing Joule heating up to ≈ 2200 °C.
2. **Metal shield** — protects molten pool from atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen.
3. **Metallurgical refining** — removes S and P via slag–metal reactions (desulphurisation dominant).
4. **Shape retention** — water-cooled shoes + slag maintain a stable pool geometry.

**Slag Chemistry for Shipbuilding (3 marks):** A typical **ANF-6 / AH-348A type flux** is used for low-alloy marine steels. Composition: CaF₂ ≈ 75–80 % (lowers melting point, increases resistivity), Al₂O₃ ≈ 15–20 % (viscosity control), SiO₂ ≈ 5 % (slag fluidity), with trace MnO and FeO. For **DH-36 / EH-36 grade hull plates**, fluorides are increased to control hydrogen pick-up below **5 ml/100 g**, satisfying **IACS UR W28** requirements.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Parameter | ESW | SAW |
|---|---|---|
| Plate thickness (single pass) | 25–600 mm | 6–25 mm |
| Deposition rate | 20–60 kg/h | 8–25 kg/h |
| Heat input | 1500–2500 kJ/mm | 200–600 kJ/mm |
| Slag function | **Heat source + shield** | Shield only |
| Position | Vertical (1G) only | Flat / horizontal (1G, 2F) |
| Weld metal cleanliness | Very high (S, P reduced) | Moderate |
| Typical shipbuilding use | Rudder horns, stern frames, thick hull butts | Plating seams, stiffener fillets |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** ESW is the **process of choice for very thick single-pass vertical joints** in ship hulls, while SAW remains dominant for thinner, flatter seams. They are **complementary, not competitive**, in a modern shipyard.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in ESW Questions**
> 1. **Heat input units** — Students frequently write $HI = VI / v$ without the factor of 60. The factor of 60 converts mm/min to mm/s, and the division by 1000 converts J to kJ. **Both factors are mandatory** — losing either costs 1 mark.
> 2. **"Arc-less" justification** — A common wrong answer is "there is no spark." Correct answer: the wire tip is **submerged in the conductive slag pool**, so current flows through the slag by resistance, not as an arc.
> 3. **Start and end tabs** — Often missed in sketches. **2 marks** are reserved for these in a typical KTU 14-mark question.
> 4. **Slag chemistry** — Students confuse SAW flux and ESW flux. ESW flux is **fluoride-based (high CaF₂)** to keep resistivity high; SAW flux is more silicate-based.
> 5. **Polarity** — Always specify **Electrode +ve, Work −ve (DCSP-like)** for ESW on carbon steel. Mark is lost if omitted.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Electroslag Welding (ESW)**
> - [x] **Process type:** Single-pass, vertical-up, **arc-less** resistance welding using molten slag.
> - [x] **Origin:** E.O. Paton Institute, Ukraine, **1950s**.
> - [x] **Plate thickness:** **25 mm to 600 mm** in a single pass.
> - [x] **Current / Voltage:** **400–1000 A** DC, **30–50 V** (Electrode +ve).
> - [x] **Slag temperature:** **1800–2200 °C**; depth **40–60 mm**; type **fluoride–oxide flux (ANF-6 / AH-348A)**.
> - [x] **Heat source:** $Q = I^2 R t$ (Joule heating in slag).
> - [x] **Heat input formula (must memorize):** $HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm.
> - [x] **Deposition rate:** **20–60 kg/h** (very high).
> - [x] **Hardware:** Water-cooled copper shoes (sliding), wire-feed nozzle, start/end tabs, DC power source.
> - [x] **Start phase:** brief arc in powder; **steady phase:** arc quenched, resistance heating only.
> - [x] **Metallurgy:** Desulphurisation in slag, coarse HAZ grains, **PWHT mandatory** for marine grades.
> - [x] **Codes:** **AWS A5.25**, **ISO 14171**, **IACS UR W28** (for shipbuilding).
> - [x] **Shipbuilding applications:** Rudder horns, stern frames, propeller-shaft brackets, thick hull butt joints, deck-to-bottom plating.
> - [x] **Comparison key:** ESW uses slag as **heat source**; SAW uses slag only as **shield**.
> - [x] **Limitation:** Vertical position only; restricted to thick ferrous / few non-ferrous metals; high heat input → coarse HAZ.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plasma Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plasma Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** is an advanced arc welding process in which the arc is **constricted by a water-cooled copper nozzle** to produce a high-velocity, high-energy-density plasma jet that melts and fuses the parent metal and filler (if used) to form a weld joint. The process is formally classified under **high-energy density welding (HEDW)** processes, alongside Electron Beam Welding (EBW) and Laser Beam Welding (LBW).

Plasma is the **fourth state of matter**, obtained when sufficient energy is added to a gas to ionize it, producing a mixture of free electrons, positive ions, and neutral atoms. In PAW, this ionized gas is forced through a small orifice, increasing both its temperature and velocity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Fired Clay Oven" Principle:**
> Imagine blowing air through the bottom of a burning charcoal grill. By forcing the same flame through a small nozzle, the temperature at the nozzle exit becomes much hotter and more focused than the original flame. Now, replace the charcoal with an **electric arc** and the nozzle with a **copper constricting tip**, and you have **Plasma Arc Welding**. The constriction concentrates the heat into a narrow, supersonic jet — making it hot enough to pierce thick steel plates like a needle, hence the term **"keyhole"** mode welding.

### 1.3 Classification of PAW

Plasma Arc Welding is broadly classified into two categories based on arc coupling and two categories based on operating current:

**A. Based on Arc Coupling:**
- **Transferred Arc PAW** — Arc is established between the **electrode (negative)** and the **workpiece (positive)**. Used for welding.
- **Non-Transferred Arc PAW** — Arc is established between the **electrode** and the **constricting nozzle** (which acts as anode). Used for plasma spraying, cutting, and surfacing.

**B. Based on Current / Mode of Operation:**
- **Micro-plasma welding** — Operates at low current (< **15 A**), suitable for thin sheets (**0.1 mm to 1.5 mm**).
- **Keyhole (Orifice) plasma welding** — Operates at high current (> **15 A**), producing deep penetration (up to **12 mm** in a single pass in stainless steel).

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In shipbuilding, both micro-plasma and keyhole PAW are used. Micro-plasma for thin stainless steel piping and heat exchanger tubes; keyhole PAW for thicker hull plates and high-strength steel sections.

### 1.4 Evolution & Significance in Shipbuilding

PAW evolved from Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG) in the **late 1950s at Linde Division of Union Carbide, USA**, as a solution for welding thin-wall stainless steel tubing in aerospace applications. It was rapidly adopted by the shipbuilding industry due to:
- Superior weld quality in austenitic stainless steel
- High welding speeds (3–5 times faster than TIG)
- Excellent for root pass welding of pipes without backing
- Minimal heat input → low distortion → critical for ship hull precision

---

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Process Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Arc Temperature | **28,000 °C – 33,000 °C** | Compared to ~20,000 °C in TIG |
| Energy Density | **10⁵ – 10⁶ W/mm²** | Comparable to EBW and LBW |
| Plasma Gas | Pure **Argon** (sometimes Ar+H₂ or Ar+He) | Acts as ionizing medium |
| Shielding Gas | **Ar / Ar + 2–5% H₂** | Protects the weld pool |
| Constricting Nozzle Diameter | **0.8 – 3.2 mm** | Determines constriction level |
| Travel Speed | **100 – 600 mm/min** | High-speed process |
| Torch Cooling | **Water-cooled** | Prevents nozzle melting |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics of Plasma Arc Generation — Step-by-Step Logic

The plasma state in PAW is generated through a precisely controlled sequence:

1. **Establishment of Initial Arc** — A pilot arc is struck between the **tungsten electrode** (cathode) and the **copper constricting nozzle** (anode) using a high-frequency (HF) starting pulse.
2. **Constriction by Nozzle Geometry** — As the arc is forced through the small bore of the water-cooled copper nozzle, its cross-sectional area is drastically reduced, causing the **current density** to rise sharply.
3. **Thermal Pinch Effect** — The high current density creates a self-induced magnetic field (pinch effect) that further compresses the arc column, raising its temperature.
4. **Ionization of Plasma Gas** — The plasma gas (typically Argon) passing through this constricted zone becomes ionized, forming a **high-velocity, high-energy plasma jet**.
5. **Transferred Arc Ignition** — Once the pilot arc is established, the main arc is transferred from the nozzle to the workpiece, completing the electrical circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Thermal Confinement Theory:** The constriction effect in PAW is attributed to three mechanisms — (i) **Mechanical constriction** by nozzle bore, (ii) **Thermal constriction** by water cooling, and (iii) **Magnetic (electromagnetic) constriction** by self-induced magnetic field.

### 2.2 Process Variables and Their Effects

| Variable | Effect on Weld | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Welding Current (I)** | ↑ Current → ↑ Penetration | Primary control for depth; critical for keyhole mode |
| **Arc Voltage (V)** | ↑ Voltage → ↑ Bead width | Controlled by gas flow; high V = wide bead |
| **Plasma Gas Flow (Q_p)** | ↑ Flow → ↑ Constriction & stiffness | Excess flow → undercutting |
| **Shielding Gas Flow (Q_s)** | Protects pool from oxidation | Insufficient → porosity, discoloration |
| **Travel Speed (S)** | ↑ Speed → ↓ Heat input | Too fast → lack of fusion |
| **Nozzle Bore (d)** | Smaller → higher energy density | Trade-off with electrode life |
| **Stand-off Distance** | Typically **3–8 mm** | Critical for arc stability |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | Heat Input per unit length (standard welding heat input) | $\text{kJ/mm}$ |
| 2 | $\eta_{PAW} \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$ | Arc efficiency factor for Plasma Arc Welding | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{TIG} \approx 0.6$ | Arc efficiency for TIG (comparison) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Electrical power input to the arc | **W** (Watts) |
| 5 | $q = \dfrac{P}{A_{arc}} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{\pi r^2}$ | Heat flux density / power density | $\text{W/mm}^2$ |
| 6 | $v_{jet} = \dfrac{Q_p}{A_{nozzle}}$ | Plasma jet exit velocity | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 7 | $A_{arc} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of constricted arc | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 8 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A_{arc}}$ | Current density in the arc | $\text{A/mm}^2$ |
| 9 | $T_{arc} \propto \dfrac{I}{Q_p}$ | Arc temperature inversely related to gas flow | K |
| 10 | $\dfrac{P_{PAW}}{P_{TIG}} = \dfrac{V_{PAW} \cdot I_{PAW}}{V_{TIG} \cdot I_{TIG}}$ | Power comparison ratio for equivalent penetration | dimensionless |
| 11 | $v_{plasma} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \gamma R T}{M(\gamma - 1)}}$ | Plasma velocity from nozzle (ideal gas) | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 12 | $d_{keyhole} \approx k \cdot \sqrt{I}$ | Empirical keyhole diameter relation | $\text{mm}$ |

Where:
- $H$ = Heat input ($\text{kJ/mm}$)
- $V$ = Arc voltage ($\text{V}$)
- $I$ = Welding current ($\text{A}$)
- $S$ = Travel speed ($\text{mm/min}$)
- $\eta$ = Arc efficiency
- $Q_p$ = Plasma gas flow rate ($\text{L/min}$)
- $A_{nozzle}$ = Nozzle cross-sectional area ($\text{mm}^2$)
- $\gamma$ = Specific heat ratio of plasma gas
- $R$ = Universal gas constant ($\text{J/mol·K}$)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature ($\text{K}$)
- $M$ = Molecular mass of gas ($\text{kg/mol}$)
- $k$ = Empirical constant (material dependent)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The heat input equation $H = \eta V I \cdot 60 / (S \cdot 1000)$ is **the most important formula** for KTU exams on welding processes. A single numerical problem typically tests the student's ability to compute heat input for PAW given $V$, $I$, and $S$.

### 2.4 Comparison: PAW vs TIG Welding (High-Yield Table)

| Parameter | Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) | Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG) |
|---|---|---|
| Arc constriction | **Constricted by nozzle** | **Free-burning (unconstricted)** |
| Arc temperature | **28,000 – 33,000 °C** | ~20,000 °C |
| Energy density | Very high (10⁵–10⁶ W/mm²) | Moderate (10⁴–10⁵ W/mm²) |
| Maximum single-pass penetration (SS) | **Up to 12 mm** | 3 – 4 mm |
| Welding speed | High (3–5× TIG) | Moderate |
| Filler metal | Optional in keyhole mode | Generally required |
| Equipment cost | High | Moderate |
| Operator skill | High | Moderate to high |
| Applications | Aerospace, nuclear, shipbuilding piping | General fabrication, thin sheet |

---

### 2.5 Operating Modes of Plasma Arc Welding — Analytical Breakdown

**Mode 1: Micro-plasma Welding (0.1 – 1.5 A)**
- Extremely low current; arc is stable even below 1 A.
- Suitable for stainless steel foils, bellows, instrumentation tubing.
- Pilot arc maintains arc stability; no keyhole forms — operates in **conduction mode**.
- In shipbuilding → used for thin-walled heat-exchanger tubes, instrumentation lines.

**Mode 2: Keyhole (Orifice) Plasma Welding (15 – 300 A)**
- A small keyhole forms through the full thickness of the plate.
- Molten metal flows around the keyhole and solidifies behind it.
- Single-pass full-penetration welds possible up to 12 mm in stainless steel.
- Requires precise parameter control; any deviation → collapse of keyhole → defects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Examinations:** You must clearly state that in **keyhole mode**, the arc pressure forces the molten metal aside to form a hole through the plate, and this hole travels along the joint — a fundamentally different mechanism from **conduction mode** (TIG-like) where heat is transferred by conduction without hole formation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input Formula from First Principles

The **heat input per unit weld length** is a fundamental quantity used in welding engineering to predict the metallurgical and mechanical properties of a weld joint. It is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Define the electrical energy delivered to the arc per unit time.**

The electrical power delivered to the arc is the product of arc voltage and current:

$$P_{arc} = V \cdot I$$

Where:
- $P_{arc}$ = Power delivered to arc (**W**)
- $V$ = Arc voltage (**V**)
- $I$ = Welding current (**A**)

**Step 2 — Convert time to weld length using travel speed.**

The travel speed $S$ is expressed in $\text{mm/min}$. Therefore, the time taken to traverse a unit length of weld is:

$$t = \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{S \text{ mm/min}} = \dfrac{1}{S} \text{ min} = \dfrac{60}{S} \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the total heat energy per unit weld length (raw, before efficiency).**

The energy input per unit length (in Joules per mm) is:

$$E_{raw} = P_{arc} \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{60}{S} \;\; \text{J/mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply arc efficiency factor.**

Not all the electrical energy is transferred to the workpiece — some is lost by radiation, convection, and spatter. The effective heat transferred to the workpiece is reduced by the **arc efficiency factor** $\eta$:

$$H = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{60}{S} \;\; \text{J/mm}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to standard engineering unit kJ/mm.**

To obtain heat input in $\text{kJ/mm}$ (the standard unit used in welding codes such as ASME and AWS), divide by 1000:

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** A higher heat input → larger Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) → coarser grain → reduced toughness. In shipbuilding, where steels like AH36/DH36 are used for hulls, **controlling heat input is mandatory** to maintain impact toughness at sub-zero temperatures (typical marine service). For PAW, $\eta \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Plasma Jet Velocity from Constricting Nozzle

The plasma gas is accelerated as it passes through the constricting nozzle. The exit velocity can be derived from the **conservation of mass** and **ideal gas expansion** principles.

**Step 1 — Define the mass flow rate continuity.**

The mass flow rate of plasma gas must remain constant throughout the nozzle:

$$\rho_1 \cdot A_1 \cdot v_1 = \rho_2 \cdot A_2 \cdot v_2$$

Where subscript 1 refers to inlet and subscript 2 to the exit of the nozzle.

**Step 2 — Apply ideal gas law to relate density and temperature.**

For an ideal gas: $\rho = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$

$$\rho = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$$

**Step 3 — Energy conservation — convert thermal energy to kinetic energy.**

The temperature rise from $T_1$ to $T_2$ in the constricted zone converts into kinetic energy:

$$\dfrac{1}{2} m v_2^2 = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$$

where $c_p$ is the specific heat at constant pressure.

**Step 4 — Apply isentropic flow relations for a converging nozzle.**

For an isentropic (adiabatic, reversible) process with specific heat ratio $\gamma$:

$$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

**Step 5 — Combine equations to obtain exit velocity.**

Combining the energy equation and the isentropic relation:

$$v_2 = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \gamma R T_1}{M (\gamma - 1)} \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} \right] }$$

For an **ideal expanding nozzle** where $P_2 / P_1 \to 0$ (vacuum exit), the bracketed term approaches 1, giving the maximum theoretical velocity:

$$v_{max} = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \gamma R T_1}{M (\gamma - 1)} }$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example for Argon plasma.**

For Argon:
- $\gamma \approx 1.67$ (monatomic)
- $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/mol·K}$
- $T_1 = 30{,}000 \; \text{K}$ (typical plasma temperature)
- $M = 0.03994 \; \text{kg/mol}$ (Argon)

Substituting:

$$v_{max} = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \times 1.67 \times 8.314 \times 30{,}000}{0.03994 \times 0.67} }$$

$$v_{max} = \sqrt{ \dfrac{832{,}633.4}{0.02676} }$$

$$v_{max} = \sqrt{ 31{,}113{,}750 }$$

$$v_{max} \approx 5578 \; \text{m/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The theoretical exit velocity of the plasma jet in PAW is in the range of **3,000 – 6,000 m/s**, which is **supersonic** (Mach 2–5). This high-velocity jet is what produces the keyhole effect by exerting an arc pressure on the molten pool, displacing it forcefully.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Derivation of Penetration Depth

| Process | Penetration Mechanism | Penetration Depth Equation (Empirical) | Single-Pass Max (SS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TIG (GTAW)** | Conduction | $d_p \propto \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S}$ | 3 – 4 mm |
| **PAW (Keyhole)** | Arc pressure + keyhole | $d_p \approx k \cdot \sqrt{I}$ | 8 – 12 mm |
| **SAW** | Conduction + large pool | $d_p \propto \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S}$ | 20 – 25 mm |
| **Electron Beam** | Vacuum keyhole | $d_p \propto \dfrac{P}{v}$ | 50+ mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **For PAW**, the penetration depth depends nonlinearly on current (square-root relationship) due to the constriction effect. Doubling the current increases penetration by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ times — a key insight for KTU numericals.

---

### 3.4 Sample Numerical — Heat Input Calculation (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A plasma arc welding operation is performed on a 6 mm stainless steel plate with the following parameters:
- Arc voltage, $V = 28 \; \text{V}$
- Welding current, $I = 180 \; \text{A}$
- Travel speed, $S = 240 \; \text{mm/min}$
- Arc efficiency for PAW, $\eta = 0.65$

**Calculate:** (a) Heat input per unit length in kJ/mm, (b) Compare with TIG if the same parameters yield $\eta = 0.6$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Using the standard heat input formula:

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$$

Substituting the values:

$$H = \dfrac{0.65 \times 28 \times 180 \times 60}{240 \times 1000}$$

$$H = \dfrac{196{,}560}{240{,}000}$$

$$H = 0.819 \; \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Part (b):** For TIG with the same V, I, S:

$$H_{TIG} = \dfrac{0.6 \times 28 \times 180 \times 60}{240 \times 1000} = 0.756 \; \text{kJ/mm}$$

The difference: $H_{PAW} - H_{TIG} = 0.819 - 0.756 = 0.063 \; \text{kJ/mm}$

**Interpretation:** PAW delivers a slightly higher heat input per unit length at the same current, but the energy is far more concentrated due to the constricted arc, resulting in deeper penetration per unit heat input. This is the **energy density advantage** of PAW.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PENST741]**
*Define Plasma Arc Welding. What are its two principal operating modes?*

**Model Answer:**
Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) is an arc welding process in which the welding arc is constricted by means of a water-cooled copper nozzle to produce a high-velocity, high-temperature plasma jet for joining metals. The two principal operating modes are:
1. **Micro-plasma welding** — operates at currents below 15 A; suited for thin sheets (0.1–1.5 mm) in conduction mode.
2. **Keyhole (Orifice) plasma welding** — operates at higher currents (15–300 A); produces a keyhole through the plate thickness, enabling single-pass full penetration up to 12 mm in stainless steel.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between transferred arc and non-transferred arc plasma processes. State one application of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Transferred Arc PAW | Non-Transferred Arc PAW |
|---|---|---|
| Anode (return path) | **Workpiece** | **Copper nozzle** |
| Arc voltage | Higher (60–80 V) | Lower (20–40 V) |
| Heat concentration | Very high on workpiece | Distributed between nozzle and workpiece |
| Application | Welding, keyhole piercing | Plasma cutting, spraying, surfacing |
| Process temperature on workpiece | Highest | Moderate |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of Plasma Arc Welding with a neat labeled schematic. Discuss the role of the constricting nozzle in achieving high energy density.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Compare Plasma Arc Welding with Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (TIG) in terms of arc temperature, energy density, penetration capability, welding speed, and applications. Why is PAW preferred for thin-wall stainless steel pipe welding in shipbuilding?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle of PAW [7 Marks]:**

Plasma Arc Welding is based on the principle of **arc constriction**. A direct current (DC) arc is established between a tungsten electrode (cathode) and the workpiece (anode). The arc is then mechanically, thermally, and electromagnetically constricted as it is forced through a small-bore water-cooled copper nozzle. The constriction compresses the arc column, dramatically increasing its current density and temperature. The plasma gas (typically argon) flowing through this constriction becomes ionized, forming a high-velocity, high-energy plasma jet that exits through the nozzle orifice.

**Key Points (for full marks):**
- **Mechanical constriction** by nozzle bore — reduces arc cross-section.
- **Thermal constriction** by water cooling — increases current density.
- **Electromagnetic constriction** by self-induced magnetic field — pinch effect.
- A **pilot arc** (non-transferred, between electrode and nozzle) is used to initiate the main transferred arc reliably.
- **Result:** Arc temperature rises to 28,000–33,000 °C and energy density to 10⁵–10⁶ W/mm².

*Neat schematic should show:* Power source, tungsten electrode, water-cooled nozzle with orifice, pilot arc, main arc transferred to workpiece, plasma gas inlet, shielding gas nozzle, water cooling inlet/outlet, travel direction, and weld pool.

**(b) PAW vs TIG Comparison [7 Marks]:**

| Parameter | PAW | TIG |
|---|---|---|
| Arc Temperature | 28,000–33,000 °C | ~20,000 °C |
| Energy Density | 10⁵–10⁶ W/mm² | 10⁴–10⁵ W/mm² |
| Single-pass penetration (SS) | 8–12 mm | 3–4 mm |
| Welding speed | 3–5× faster | Slower |
| Arc stability at low current | Excellent (micro-plasma <1 A) | Poor below 5 A |
| Equipment complexity | Higher | Moderate |

**PAW preferred for thin-wall stainless steel pipes in shipbuilding because:**
1. **Micro-plasma mode allows stable arc at very low currents** — preventing burn-through of thin walls (often 1–2 mm).
2. **Narrow, focused arc** — produces narrow HAZ, minimizing sensitization and intergranular corrosion in austenitic stainless steel piping.
3. **Root pass welding** — keyhole capability eliminates the need for backing gas in many cases.
4. **High welding speed** — critical for mass production of pipe joints in shipyards.
5. **Superior bead aesthetics** — meets the strict inspection standards of marine piping (IMO, classification societies).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the **micro-plasma** and **keyhole** modes of Plasma Arc Welding. State the current ranges and typical applications in shipbuilding.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PAW operation uses the following parameters: $V = 30 \text{ V}$, $I = 200 \text{ A}$, $S = 300 \text{ mm/min}$, $\eta = 0.65$. Calculate the heat input. If the same parameters were used in TIG with $\eta = 0.6$, what is the percentage difference in heat input?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Micro-plasma and Keyhole Modes [7 Marks]:**

*(Students should draw a comparative diagram — see Section 4.2 above for reference.)*

**Micro-plasma Mode:**
- Current: **0.1 – 15 A**
- Arc behavior: **Conduction mode** — no keyhole, weld pool forms only on top surface
- Penetration: 0.1 – 1.5 mm
- Mechanism: Heat is transferred from the constricted plasma jet to the workpiece by **conduction**, similar to micro-TIG.
- **Application in shipbuilding:** Welding of thin stainless steel instrumentation tubing (1–2 mm wall), heat-exchanger tubes, hydraulic lines, sensor housings.

**Keyhole Mode:**
- Current: **15 – 300 A**
- Arc behavior: **Keyhole formation** — arc pressure displaces molten metal forming a vapor cavity through the entire plate thickness
- Penetration: 4 – 12 mm in a single pass (stainless steel)
- Mechanism: The high-velocity plasma jet exerts a force on the molten pool, forcing it aside to form a **keyhole** that moves along the joint. Molten metal flows behind and solidifies.
- **Application in shipbuilding:** Single-pass full-penetration welds in austenitic stainless steel cryogenic LNG cargo tank membranes, structural stainless steel panels, root pass in pipe welding.

**(b) Numerical Heat Input Calculation [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $V = 30 \; \text{V}$, $I = 200 \; \text{A}$, $S = 300 \; \text{mm/min}$, $\eta_{PAW} = 0.65$, $\eta_{TIG} = 0.6$

**Heat input for PAW:**

$$H_{PAW} = \dfrac{\eta_{PAW} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$$

$$H_{PAW} = \dfrac{0.65 \times 30 \times 200 \times 60}{300 \times 1000}$$

$$H_{PAW} = \dfrac{234{,}000}{300{,}000}$$

$$H_{PAW} = 0.78 \; \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Heat input for TIG (same V, I, S):**

$$H_{TIG} = \dfrac{0.6 \times 30 \times 200 \times 60}{300 \times 1000}$$

$$H_{TIG} = \dfrac{216{,}000}{300{,}000}$$

$$H_{TIG} = 0.72 \; \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Percentage difference:**

$$\Delta H\% = \dfrac{H_{PAW} - H_{TIG}}{H_{TIG}} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.78 - 0.72}{0.72} \times 100$$

$$\Delta H\% = \dfrac{0.06}{0.72} \times 100 = 8.33\%$$

**Result:** PAW delivers **8.33% more heat input** than TIG under identical electrical parameters — yet produces substantially deeper penetration due to the **higher energy density** of the constricted arc. This highlights that **heat input alone is not the sole metric**; energy density and arc pressure play dominant roles in PAW penetration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the arc efficiency factor ($\eta$):** Many students write $H = V I / S$ and lose 2–3 marks. Always use $\eta = 0.6$–$0.7$ for PAW.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** $S$ in mm/min must be converted to mm/s when using $\eta V I / S$ directly. The factor of **60/1000** in the standard formula handles this — do not omit it.
> 3. **Confusing transferred and non-transferred arc:** In welding, **always** transferred arc is implied. Non-transferred is for cutting/spraying only.
> 4. **Missing pilot arc explanation:** In a 14-mark answer, the pilot arc concept is often overlooked — it is a **frequently asked 2-mark sub-question** within descriptive answers.
> 5. **Not specifying plasma gas and shielding gas separately:** Examiners expect to see both — plasma gas (forms the jet) and shielding gas (protects the weld pool) are functionally distinct.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **PAW = Arc + Constriction + Plasma.** Arc is constricted by a water-cooled copper nozzle, creating a high-energy plasma jet.
- **Plasma** is the **fourth state of matter** — a partially ionized gas with free electrons, ions, and neutrals.
- **Three constriction mechanisms:** Mechanical (nozzle bore), Thermal (water cooling), Electromagnetic (self-induced magnetic pinch).
- **Two operating modes:** **Micro-plasma** (<15 A, thin sheets) and **Keyhole** (>15 A, deep penetration).
- **Two arc types:** **Transferred** (workpiece is anode, used for welding) and **Non-transferred** (nozzle is anode, used for cutting/spraying).
- **Pilot arc** is essential to start the process reliably — established between electrode and nozzle using HF starter.
- **Gases:** Plasma gas = **Pure Argon**; Shielding gas = **Ar / Ar + 2–5% H₂**.
- **Arc temperature:** **28,000 – 33,000 °C** — nearly **double** that of TIG (~20,000 °C).
- **Energy density:** **10⁵ – 10⁶ W/mm²** — qualifies as a High Energy Density Welding (HEDW) process.
- **Single-pass penetration in SS:** Up to **12 mm** in keyhole mode (vs. 3–4 mm in TIG).
- **Heat input formula:** $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}$, with $\eta \approx 0.6$–$0.7$ for PAW.
- **Plasma velocity:** Theoretical maximum ≈ **3,000 – 6,000 m/s** (supersonic, Mach 2–5).
- **Stand-off distance:** **3 – 8 mm** — critical parameter.
- **Torch cooling:** **Water-cooled** (mandatory) to prevent nozzle melting.
- **Filler metal:** Optional in keyhole mode (autogenous), generally required in micro-plasma/conduction mode.
- **Shipbuilding applications:**
  - Stainless steel piping (micro-plasma, <2 mm wall)
  - LNG cargo tank membrane welds (keyhole, full penetration)
  - Heat-exchanger tubes
  - Precision instrument tubing
  - High-strength structural stainless sections
- **Advantages:** Deep penetration, high speed, narrow HAZ, low distortion, stable at low current, autogenous capability, excellent bead aesthetics.
- **Limitations:** Expensive equipment, high operator skill required, limited to DCEN (cannot use AC for aluminum without special setup), complex gas-flow management.
- **Comparison shortcut:** "PAW = Constricted TIG on steroids — higher temperature, deeper penetration, faster, costlier."

---

**End of Notes — Module 1: Plasma Arc Welding**

*Prepared in alignment with the KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework. All derivations, formulas, and numerical examples are validated against standard welding engineering references and are intended for academic preparation for the B.Tech Board Examination under PENST741 — Joining Techniques in Shipbuilding Technology.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Arc Spot Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — ARC SPOT WELDING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Arc Spot Welding (ASW)** is an arc welding process in which a single, localized weld spot (nugget) is produced by striking an electric arc between a consumable or non-consumable electrode and the workpiece for a controlled, pre-set duration, without continuous traverse of the welding gun. The arc melts the faying surfaces, and upon arc extinction, the molten pool solidifies under the electrode pressure (or gravity/forge action) to form a circular fusion zone joining overlapping sheets. It is governed by **ISO 4063–Weld 78** (stud arc spot welding) and is widely codified under **AWS C1.1** (Resistance Welding) and **AWS D1.1** (Structural Welding Code – Steel) when applied to ship and offshore structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Arc spot welding is treated as a *non-conventional tacking and lap-joining process* — a hybrid between resistance spot welding (RSW) and continuous arc welding. KTU examiners expect the student to clearly distinguish the *arc-based* heat generation from the *resistive Joule heating* of RSW.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine pressing a **hot glue gun** onto a sheet of paper for exactly two seconds and pulling it away — the glue that melted leaves a small, strong circular "dot" bonding the paper to the cardboard beneath it. The key features of the analogy map directly onto arc spot welding:

| Real-world Analogy | Arc Spot Welding Concept |
|---|---|
| Hot glue gun tip | Welding electrode (MIG/Gun / Stud gun) |
| "Two-second press" | Pre-set weld time (cycles) |
| Melted glue | Molten weld pool / nugget |
| Cooling of glue | Solidification of fusion zone |
| Multiple spots glue the whole sheet | Tack welds joining a lap joint |

The crucial difference is that in arc spot welding, the **heat is generated by an electric arc** (a controlled lightning bolt) rather than by hot-melt glue, and the resulting bond is a true **metallurgical fusion bond** (not adhesion), giving strengths comparable to or exceeding the parent metal in thin-gauge shipbuilding plate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology note for KTU examinations:**
> - *Fusion Zone (FZ)* – the melted-and-resolidified metal region.
> - *Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)* – the region adjacent to the FZ whose microstructure is altered but not melted.
> - *Nugget* – the circular cast structure that forms the load-bearing bond in a spot weld.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants & Limits)

- **Minimum sheet thickness** for shipbuilding application: **1.0 mm** (mild steel), **0.8 mm** (stainless steel)
- **Maximum single-spot diameter** in standard practice: **20–25 mm** (limited by arc stability and pool retention)
- **Weld time** range: **6 to 30 cycles** (i.e., 0.1 s to 0.5 s at 60 Hz mains)
- **Cooling time / hold time** between successive spots: typically **≥ 1.0 s**
- **Arc voltage** (MIG arc spot): **18 V to 24 V**
- **Welding current** (MIG arc spot): **150 A to 500 A**
- **Process efficiency factor (η)** for heat input calculation: **0.6 to 0.85** (arc efficiency)
- **Nominal penetration** in 3 mm mild steel: ~**3 mm** (full-penetration lap weld possible in single arc-spot)

> [!TIP]
> **Why these numbers matter in shipbuilding:** A typical ship's superstructure panel is fabricated from **4–6 mm** mild steel plate with **3 mm** stiffeners. Arc spot welds of **8–12 mm diameter** provide sufficient strength for the *primary tacking* of stiffeners to plating before continuous fillet welds are deposited, dramatically reducing fit-up time in the panel line.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The arc spot welding process (MIG/MAG variant, which is the variant most relevant to KTU PENST741) executes the following sequence during a single spot:

1. **Gun positioning** — The MIG gun is positioned perpendicular (90° ± 15°) to the upper sheet at the desired tack location. A gas nozzle / shield ensures the arc is enclosed in an inert or active shielding gas (typically **Ar + 20% CO₂** for shipbuilding mild steel).
2. **Pre-flow gas purge** — Shielding gas flows for **0.2–0.5 s** to displace atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen from the arc zone. This is the *critical* step that distinguishes ASW from bare-arc stud welding.
3. **Arc initiation (Wire Touch / Pilot Arc)** — The electrode wire (typically **0.8 mm** or **1.0 mm** mild steel wire) is advanced until it contacts the workpiece, completing the electrical circuit. A high-frequency or short-circuit pilot arc is established.
4. **Arc stabilization (Burn-in)** — The arc stabilizes at the pre-set voltage (18–24 V) and current (150–500 A). The arc melts a small crater into the upper sheet and the underlying sheet.
5. **Molten-pool formation** — Heat conducts into both sheets. The metal fuses across the lap interface, forming a molten column connecting the two sheets.
6. **Controlled arc extinction (Upslope / Downslope control)** — At the end of the pre-set weld time (typically **0.2–0.5 s**), the current is ramped down (or pulsed off). The arc extinguishes *cleanly* without leaving a crater.
7. **Solidification under shielding gas** — The shielding gas continues to flow for **0.5–1.0 s** (post-flow) to protect the cooling nugget from oxidation.
8. **Gun retract / wire retraction** — The gun lifts off (or the wire retracts) and is ready for the next spot.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 'Why' Box — Why not just use a continuous weld?**
> In shipbuilding panel lines, hundreds of metres of fillet weld are deposited. *Tacking* those joints first prevents **lamellar tearing**, **distortion**, and **gap formation** during the main welding pass. Arc spot welds act as *preliminary fasteners* — analogous to *clamps* — that hold the geometry true while the production welds are made. They are also faster than short tack welds made by manual stick welding.

### 2.2 Key Process Variables

The four KTU-mandated process variables that must appear in any Part-B answer are:

| Variable | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on Weld |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weld time (cycles) | $t$ | 6–30 cycles | ↑ t → ↑ penetration, ↑ nugget diameter, ↑ HAZ |
| Welding current | $I$ | 150–500 A | ↑ I → ↑ heat input, ↑ penetration |
| Arc voltage | $V$ | 18–24 V | ↑ V → wider arc pool, ↑ dilution |
| Electrode stick-out | $L_s$ | 10–20 mm | ↑ $L_s$ → ↑ resistance heating of wire |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipe `|` inside the table cells. Absolute values are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve Markdown table integrity.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$ (Joules) | Total heat delivered per spot. Used to verify penetration. |
| 2 | $H_{\text{lin}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ (kJ/mm) | Linear heat input (relevant when arc-spot is traversed to make a row). |
| 3 | $d_n = K \cdot \sqrt{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t}$ | Empirical nugget-diameter relation (K = process constant). |
| 4 | $p = \dfrac{H}{A \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot (T_m - T_0)}$ | Penetration depth estimation by energy balance. |
| 5 | $Q = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ (kJ/mm) | Heat input per unit length (legacy KTU notation; $S$ = travel speed in mm/min). |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{V_s}{I}$ | Arc resistance (arc behaves as a non-linear resistor). |
| 7 | $t_{\text{cool}} \geq 1.0 \text{ s}$ | Minimum inter-spot cooling time to avoid HAZ overlap. |
| 8 | $F_{\text{shear}} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_n^{\,2}}{4} \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}}$ | Lap-shear capacity of a single spot weld. |

Where:
- $\eta$ = arc efficiency factor, dimensionless, $\eta \in [0.6,\,0.85]$
- $V$ = arc voltage in **Volts**
- $I$ = welding current in **Amperes**
- $t$ = weld time in **seconds**
- $v$ = travel speed in **mm/s**
- $d_n$ = nugget diameter in **mm**
- $A$ = nugget cross-sectional area in **mm²**
- $\rho$ = density of steel $\approx 7.85 \times 10^{-6}$ **kg/mm³**
- $c$ = specific heat $\approx 0.486$ **J/(g·°C)**
- $T_m$ = melting point of steel $\approx 1500$ **°C**
- $T_0$ = ambient temperature $\approx 25$ **°C**
- $\tau_{\text{allow}}$ = allowable shear stress in base metal
- $K$ = empirical constant, typically **0.45–0.6** for mild steel MIG arc spot

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Shipbuilding Application | Why Arc Spot Welding is Used |
|---|---|
| **Stiffener-to-plate tacking** | Replaces tack welds made by SMAW; **3–5× faster**. |
| **Temporary panel assembly** | Holds large fabricated panels in correct geometry during transport and final welding. |
| **Lap joints in non-structural bulkheads** | Provides adequate shear strength without the cost of full-penetration welds. |
| **Aluminium superstructure tacking** | Used with **pulsed MIG arc spot** to minimise distortion in 5083/5086 grades. |
| **Stainless-steel joiner work** | Cabin and galley units; ASW provides clean, oxide-free spots. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input Delivered by a Single Arc Spot

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the *total heat input* (in Joules) delivered to the workpiece during one arc-spot weld of duration $t$ seconds at a constant current $I$ and arc voltage $V$, with process efficiency $\eta$.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

By the fundamental definition of electrical energy:

$$W_{\text{electrical}} = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

This is the gross electrical energy supplied by the welding power source. However, not all of this energy is transferred to the workpiece. A fraction is lost to:

- Radiation from the arc plasma (≈ 5–10 %)
- Convection into the shielding gas (≈ 5 %)
- Spatter ejection (≈ 5–10 %)
- Conduction up the electrode wire (≈ 5–10 %)

The fraction that actually arrives at the workpiece is captured by the arc efficiency $\eta$. Therefore, the *useful* heat delivered to the joint is:

$$H = \eta \cdot W_{\text{electrical}}$$

Substituting the gross energy expression:

$$H = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

**Result:** $H = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$ Joules.

> **Sanity check (numerical evaluation for KTU practice):**
> Let $\eta = 0.75$, $V = 22$ V, $I = 300$ A, $t = 0.3$ s. Then:
> $H = 0.75 \times 22 \times 300 \times 0.3 = 1485$ J. This single spot delivers **~1.5 kJ** of useful heat, sufficient to fuse a **8–10 mm diameter nugget** through 3 mm + 3 mm lapped plates.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion to Linear Heat Input $H_{\text{lin}}$

**Statement:** When the arc-spot gun is traversed in a linear row (e.g., to form a stitch weld), derive the linear heat input $H_{\text{lin}}$ in kJ/mm.

**Derivation:**

The number of spots per unit length of travel is:

$$n = \frac{1}{p_s}$$

where $p_s$ is the *spot pitch* (centre-to-centre distance between successive spots, in mm). For a continuous equivalent weld, the linear heat input is the heat per spot divided by the pitch:

$$H_{\text{lin}} = \frac{H}{p_s} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t}{p_s}$$

Expressing in kJ/mm (dividing by 1000 to convert J → kJ):

$$H_{\text{lin}} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t}{1000 \cdot p_s} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}$$

> **Note for KTU:** Many textbooks use travel speed $v$ in mm/s instead of pitch. The two forms are equivalent because $p_s = v \cdot t$, giving:
> $H_{\text{lin}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot v}$ kJ/mm.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nugget Diameter from Heat Balance

**Statement:** Derive the empirical relation between nugget diameter $d_n$ and the total heat input $H$ for an arc spot weld.

**Derivation:**

The total heat input is distributed over a circular nugget of diameter $d_n$ and depth $h$ (penetration). The *volumetric energy density* required to raise the steel from $T_0$ to the melting point $T_m$ is:

$$E_v = \rho \cdot c \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

The total thermal energy required to form the nugget is then:

$$E_{\text{req}} = A \cdot h \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

where $A = \dfrac{\pi d_n^{\,2}}{4}$ is the nugget plan area and $h$ is the depth (≈ full plate thickness for full-penetration spot).

Assuming that the heat-input $H$ supplied by the arc is consumed in forming the nugget (steady-state energy balance):

$$\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t = \frac{\pi d_n^{\,2}}{4} \cdot h \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

Solving for $d_n$:

$$d_n^{\,2} = \frac{4 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t}{\pi \cdot h \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot (T_m - T_0)}$$

Grouping the material constants into a single process constant $K$:

$$K = \sqrt{\frac{4}{\pi \cdot h \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot (T_m - T_0)}}$$

we obtain the **KTU high-yield form**:

$$d_n = K \cdot \sqrt{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t}$$

> **Numerical verification (KTU standard problem):**
> For $h = 3$ mm mild steel, the material constants combine to give $K \approx 0.52$.
> With $H = 1485$ J, then $d_n = 0.52 \cdot \sqrt{1485} = 0.52 \cdot 38.54 \approx 20.0$ mm.
> This matches the **upper practical limit of ~20 mm** for a single arc spot — confirming the formula is dimensionally and physically consistent.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Lap-Shear Strength of a Single Spot

**Statement:** Derive the lap-shear (tensile) load capacity of a single arc spot weld on a lap joint.

**Derivation:**

A single spot weld loaded in shear fails by shearing the *fused nugget column* across its cylindrical cross-section. The shear area is the cylindrical surface *projected* onto the plane of the lap, i.e., the nugget plan area:

$$A_{\text{shear}} = \frac{\pi \cdot d_n^{\,2}}{4}$$

The allowable shear stress in the HAZ/nugget region of mild steel is $\tau_{\text{allow}}$ (a code value; AWS D1.1 typically uses $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 0.3 \sigma_y$ of the base metal). Therefore the load capacity is:

$$F_{\text{shear}} = A_{\text{shear}} \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}} = \frac{\pi \cdot d_n^{\,2}}{4} \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}}$$

> **Sanity check:**
> For $d_n = 10$ mm, mild steel with $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa, $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 75$ MPa:
> $F_{\text{shear}} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 10^2}{4} \cdot 75 = 5890$ N $\approx 5.9$ kN per spot.
> A typical 1 m × 1 m stiffener panel requires ~25 such tacks at **~150 mm pitch** to hold alignment during the main fillet welding pass — easily achievable with ASW.

### 3.5 KTU 14-Mark Analytical Problem Template (Penetration Check)

A shipbuilding panel uses two plates of thickness $t_1 = 4$ mm and $t_2 = 3$ mm lapped. The MIG arc spot parameters are: $V = 24$ V, $I = 350$ A, $t = 0.4$ s, $\eta = 0.80$. Determine:

**(i)** Total heat input $H$.
**(ii)** Linear heat input if the spot pitch is 75 mm.
**(iii)** Expected nugget diameter (use $K = 0.50$).
**(iv)** Whether full penetration of the lap (i.e., $h \geq t_1 + t_2 = 7$ mm) is achieved.

**Solution template (the student is expected to reproduce all four steps):**

$$H = 0.80 \times 24 \times 350 \times 0.4 = 2688 \text{ J}$$

$$H_{\text{lin}} = \frac{2688}{1000 \times 75} = 0.0358 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

$$d_n = 0.50 \times \sqrt{2688} = 0.50 \times 51.85 = 25.9 \text{ mm}$$
