For penetration, the empirical rule of thumb is **penetration ≈ (0.10 to 0.15) × $d_n$**:

$$h \approx 0.12 \times 25.9 \approx 3.1 \text{ mm}$$

Since the lap thickness is **7 mm** and the achieved penetration is only **~3.1 mm**, **full penetration is NOT achieved** — the KTU answer must state that the current or weld time must be increased, OR the spot pitch must be reduced so that overlapping arcs give cumulative penetration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process-Flow Block Diagram (Arc Spot Welding Cycle)

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **functional sequence** of a single arc spot welding cycle, from the operator's command input to the completed nugget. This is the schematic examiners expect in a 14-mark answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Operator Trigger - Foot Switch or PLC] --> A2[Pre-flow Gas Purge - 0.3 s]
    A2 --> A3[Wire Advance - Contact with Workpiece]
    A3 --> A4[Pilot Arc Ignition - HF or Short Circuit]
    A4 --> A5[Arc Stabilization - Set V and I]
    A5 --> A6[Melting of Upper Sheet - Crater Forms]
    A6 --> A7[Pool Bridge to Lower Sheet - Fusion Across Lap]
    A7 --> A8[Timer Counts Down - Weld Time Ends]
    A8 --> A9[Current Ramp-Down - Downslope]
    A9 --> A10[Arc Extinction - Crater Fill]
    A10 --> A11[Post-flow Gas Shield - 0.8 s Cool]
    A11 --> A12[Nugget Solidifies - Spot Complete]
    A12 --> A13[Gun Retract / Wire Retract]
    A13 --> A14[Ready for Next Spot]
    A14 --> A1
```

### 4.2 Equipment & System Block Diagram

The Mermaid block diagram below maps the **hardware architecture** of a typical robotic MIG arc spot welding cell used in modern shipyards. Each block represents a physical subsystem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[3-Phase Mains Supply 415 V AC] --> P2[Inverter Power Source 400 A]
    P2 --> P3[Wire Feeder Unit]
    P2 --> P4[Gas Control Valve Ar plus CO2]
    P3 --> P5[MIG Welding Gun with Trigger]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Workpiece Lap Joint]
    P2 --> P7[PLC Controller]
    P7 --> P5
    P7 --> P8[Timer Module - Weld Time and Hold Time]
    P8 --> P7
    P7 --> P9[Robot Manipulator 6-Axis]
    P9 --> P5
    P6 --> P10[Earth Return Cable]
    P10 --> P2
    style P1 fill:#dde7ff,stroke:#335
    style P2 fill:#ffe1c2,stroke:#a40
    style P7 fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#262
    style P9 fill:#f5d6e9,stroke:#707
```

### 4.3 Faying-Surface Cross-Section (Nugget Geometry)

The Mermaid block diagram below represents the **vertical cross-section through a completed arc spot weld**, identifying the geometry of the fusion zone and the surrounding HAZ in the two lapped plates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Upper Plate - 4 mm Mild Steel] --> S2[Upper HAZ - Heat Affected Zone]
    S2 --> S3[Fusion Zone - FZ Top - Ellipsoidal]
    S3 --> S4[Fusion Zone - FZ Middle - Cylindrical Neck]
    S4 --> S5[Fusion Zone - FZ Bottom - Penetration into Lower Plate]
    S5 --> S6[Lower HAZ - Heat Affected Zone]
    S6 --> S7[Lower Plate - 3 mm Mild Steel]
    S3 -. nugget diameter d-n .-> S5
    S5 -. penetration depth p .-> S7
    style S1 fill:#ccd9ff,stroke:#003
    style S7 fill:#ccd9ff,stroke:#003
    style S2 fill:#ffdf80,stroke:#a60
    style S6 fill:#ffdf80,stroke:#a60
    style S3 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#900
    style S4 fill:#ff7070,stroke:#900
    style S5 fill:#ff5050,stroke:#900
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Define arc spot welding. List any four advantages of the process in shipbuilding panel fabrication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Arc spot welding is a fusion welding process in which a stationary electric arc is maintained between a consumable electrode (or stud) and the workpiece for a pre-set duration to produce a single, localised weld spot — called a *nugget* — that joins overlapping sheets at that point.

**Advantages (4 × 0.375 = 1.5 marks):**
1. **Speed:** 3–5 times faster than manual SMAW tacking; ideal for high-throughput panel lines.
2. **No heavy electrode force** required (unlike resistance spot welding); the welding gun is light and can be robot-mounted.
3. **All-position capability:** Can be performed in horizontal, vertical, and overhead orientations.
4. **Suitable for coated steels** (galvanised, painted) — the arc burns through the coating locally, eliminating the need for pre-cleaning in tacking areas.
5. **Minimal HAZ** compared to long tack welds, reducing distortion in thin shipbuilding plate.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Distinguish between arc spot welding (ASW) and resistance spot welding (RSW). Mention any three points of difference.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Arc Spot Welding (ASW) | Resistance Spot Welding (RSW) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat is generated by an **electric arc** between electrode and workpiece. | Heat is generated by **Joule (I²R) heating** due to current passing through the lapped sheets. |
| 2 | Heat input $H = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$ depends primarily on voltage and current. | Heat input $H = I^{2} R t$ depends on the *square* of current. |
| 3 | **No heavy electrode force** is required; the gun is light and robot-friendly. | Requires **large electrode force** (typically 3–8 kN) supplied by pneumatic/hydraulic rams. |
| 4 | Can join **galvanised and coated steels** without pre-cleaning. | Coatings cause electrode wear and require frequent tip dressing. |
| 5 | Equipment is **portable and flexible**; used in shipyards on-site. | Equipment is **bulky and fixed**; used mainly in automotive assembly lines. |

(Any three of the above distinctions are sufficient for full marks.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, equipment, and process sequence** of MIG arc spot welding with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of shielding gas and the pilot-arc mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **process parameters** (weld time, current, voltage, stick-out) and their effect on nugget geometry. Illustrate with the **heat-input formula** and a typical numerical example for 4 mm + 3 mm lap joint in a ship's superstructure panel. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **principle of MIG arc spot welding** is the controlled generation of a stationary electric arc between a continuously-fed consumable wire electrode and the workpiece, with a shielding gas envelope. The arc melts a localised volume of metal across the lap interface; the molten pool solidifies into a circular *fusion zone* (nugget) that mechanically locks the two sheets together. The entire sequence is automatic and controlled by a PLC, giving reproducibility essential for shipbuilding quality systems (ISO 9001 + IACS).

**Equipment block (4 marks):**

1. **Inverter power source** (400–500 A, constant-voltage or pulsed mode)
2. **Wire feeder** (0.8–1.2 mm wire, 2–10 m/min feed rate)
3. **Shielding gas unit** (Ar + 20% CO₂ at 12–18 L/min)
4. **Welding gun** (with trigger switch, gas nozzle, contact tip)
5. **PLC controller + timer module** (sets weld time, post-flow time)
6. **Earth return cable + workpiece clamp**
7. **Robot manipulator** (optional, for 6-axis positioning)

**Process sequence (2 marks):**
The 14-step cycle shown in **Section 4.1 (Mermaid diagram)** must be described: pre-flow → wire advance → pilot arc → arc stabilisation → melting → pool bridge → timer end → downslope → arc extinction → post-flow → solidification → retract.

**Role of shielding gas (1 mark):**
The Ar + CO₂ mixture displaces atmospheric N₂ and O₂ from the arc zone, preventing porosity and nitride embrittlement in the weld metal. CO₂ also contributes to a slightly oxidising protective slag.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Process parameters and effects (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Increase → Effect on Nugget |
|---|---|
| Weld time $t$ ↑ | ↑ Penetration, ↑ Nugget diameter, ↑ HAZ width |
| Current $I$ ↑ | ↑ Heat input (linear), ↑ Penetration (significant) |
| Voltage $V$ ↑ | ↑ Arc length, ↑ Pool width, slight ↓ penetration |
| Stick-out $L_s$ ↑ | ↑ Wire resistance heating, ↑ deposition, ↑ dilution |

**Heat-input formula (2 marks):**

$$H = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t \text{ (Joules)}$$

**Numerical example (2 marks):**
For 4 mm + 3 mm lap, with $V = 22$ V, $I = 320$ A, $t = 0.35$ s, $\eta = 0.80$:

$$H = 0.80 \times 22 \times 320 \times 0.35 = 1971 \text{ J} \approx 1.97 \text{ kJ}$$

This produces a nugget of diameter:

$$d_n = 0.50 \cdot \sqrt{1971} \approx 22.2 \text{ mm}$$

which is adequate for a 7 mm lap in a ship's superstructure.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the **equipment and tooling** used in a modern robotic arc spot welding cell. How does it differ from the manual arc spot gun used in smaller shipyards? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **quality control and testing procedures** for arc spot welds in shipbuilding. Include visual inspection, macro-sectioning, peel test, and acceptance criteria from **AWS D1.1 / IACS UR W17**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Modern robotic ASW cell equipment (4 marks):**

1. **6-axis articulated robot** (e.g., ABB IRB, KUKA KR) carrying the MIG gun — provides repeatability of **±0.05 mm** in spot positioning.
2. **Servo-driven gun actuator** that maintains gun perpendicularity and a stable stick-out regardless of panel curvature.
3. **Inverter power source with synergic control** — automatically adjusts V and I as wire feed speed changes; essential for consistent nugget size.
4. **PLC + HMI interface** — the operator programs the *spot pattern* (positions, pitch, weld time per spot) via a teach pendant.
5. **Integrated vision system / seam tracking** — locates the lap joint edges and adjusts the gun path in real time.
6. **Closed-loop arc monitoring** — measures actual arc voltage and current during the spot; flags out-of-tolerance welds.

**Manual arc spot gun — smaller shipyards (3 marks):**

- A **hand-held MIG gun with a thumb-trigger** is used; the welder manually positions it at each tack.
- A **portable inverter power source** (200–300 A) is used instead of a fixed industrial unit.
- **No PLC** — the operator controls the weld time by counting (typically 0.3–0.5 s) and by listening for the characteristic *crack* of the arc extinguish.
- **Limitations:** Lower reproducibility, higher operator fatigue, slower cycle time (~5–8 spots/min vs. ~20–30 spots/min robotic), greater risk of misaligned or under-sized nuggets.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Quality control procedures (7 marks):**

1. **Visual Inspection (VT) — 2 marks:**
   Every spot is examined for: surface cracks, crater pipes, porosity, undercut, undersized diameter. Acceptance per **AWS D1.1 Clause 9.25**: no cracks permitted, individual porosity $\leq 2$ mm, cumulative porosity $\leq 4$ mm in any 25 mm length.

2. **Macro-Sectioning / Metallographic Examination — 2 marks:**
   Periodically (typically 1 in 500 production spots), a test panel is cross-sectioned, polished, etched (nital 2%), and examined under a microscope. Required penetration: **≥ 80 % of the thinner plate** for shipbuilding tacks. Nugget must show no lack of fusion at the lap interface.

3. **Peel Test (Destructive) — 1.5 marks:**
   Two coupons are spot-welded and pulled apart on a tensile testing machine. The fracture surface should show *button-pull* (a circular disc pulled out of one plate) — indicating good fusion. Brittle interfacial fracture is rejected.

4. **Acceptance Standards — 1.5 marks:**
   - **AWS D1.1** (Structural Welding Code – Steel) — for ship hull and superstructure.
   - **IACS UR W17** (Unified Requirements for Welding) — additional ship-class requirements.
   - **ISO 14555** — for stud-type arc spot welding.
   - **Acceptance criteria** include minimum nugget diameter, minimum shear strength, and visual defect limits as above.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in Arc Spot Welding Answers — Read Carefully Before Exam:**
> 1. **Confusing ASW with RSW:** Many students write "arc spot welding uses resistance heating" — this is **wrong** and will cost 2–3 marks instantly. ASW uses an **electric arc**, not resistive Joule heating. Examiners look for the word "**arc**" in every definition.
> 2. **Wrong units in heat input:** Writing $H = V \cdot I \cdot t$ without specifying $\eta$ (arc efficiency) loses marks. The full form is $H = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$ and $\eta \in [0.6, 0.85]$.
> 3. **Forgetting shielding gas:** In any 7-mark answer on ASW equipment, the role of **Ar + CO₂ shielding gas** must be mentioned. Students who omit it lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing penetration with nugget diameter:** Penetration is the *depth* of fusion; nugget diameter is the *plan view diameter*. They are related but not equal. The empirical rule is $h \approx 0.12 \cdot d_n$ for shipbuilding mild-steel ASW.
> 5. **Skipping the Mermaid/Block diagram:** KTU examiners explicitly state "neat sketch" or "block diagram" in the question paper. A 14-mark answer without a diagram loses 1–2 marks. **Always include a block diagram of the equipment OR a flowchart of the process sequence.**
> 6. **Not mentioning codes/standards:** A complete professional answer references **AWS D1.1, ISO 14555, IACS UR W17**. Examiners reward code-aware answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Recap — Read this section 24 hours before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Arc spot welding = arc-based fusion welding** (NOT resistance welding). Heat is generated by an **electric arc** between a consumable MIG wire and the workpiece.
- ✅ **Primary use in shipbuilding** = *tacking* lap joints (stiffener-to-plate, panel assembly) before continuous fillet welding. It is a *fastening* process, not a primary strength weld in most applications.
- ✅ **Core formula (must memorise):** $H = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$ (Joules). Arc efficiency $\eta = 0.6$ to $0.85$.
- ✅ **Linear heat input (for stitch welds):** $H_{\text{lin}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot v}$ kJ/mm, where $v$ = travel speed in mm/s.
- ✅ **Nugget diameter formula:** $d_n = K \cdot \sqrt{H}$ where $K \approx 0.5$ for mild-steel shipbuilding plate. Empirical penetration rule: $h \approx 0.12 \cdot d_n$.
- ✅ **Key process parameters:** weld time (6–30 cycles), current (150–500 A), voltage (18–24 V), stick-out (10–20 mm), shielding gas Ar + 20% CO₂.
- ✅ **Equipment stack:** power source + wire feeder + gas unit + gun + PLC + (optional) robot.
- ✅ **Process sequence (14 steps):** pre-flow → wire advance → pilot arc → stabilise → melt → bridge lap → timer end → downslope → extinguish → post-flow → solidify → retract.
- ✅ **Advantages over RSW:** no heavy force, all-position, works on coated steel, portable, robot-friendly.
- ✅ **Standards to cite in answers:** AWS D1.1, ISO 14555, IACS UR W17.
- ✅ **Quality control triad:** Visual Inspection (VT) → Macro-sectioning → Peel test. Acceptance per AWS D1.1.
- ✅ **Typical panel spec for KTU problems:** 3–6 mm mild-steel lap joints, 8–12 mm nugget diameter, ~150 mm tack pitch.

> **End of Topic — Arc Spot Welding (PENST741, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stud Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stud Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stud Welding** is an *arc welding process* in which a **metal stud** (a fastener, pin, or similar projection) is fusion-welded end-to-end onto a **base metal (parent plate)** in a single, automatic, high-production operation. The welding arc is drawn between the stud (acting as one electrode) and the workpiece (acting as the other), which locally melts both surfaces; the stud is then plunged into the molten pool and held under controlled pressure until the joint solidifies, forming a full-strength, full-penetration fillet weld around the base of the stud.

According to the **American Welding Society (AWS) C5.4 / C5.6** standards and **ISO 14555**, stud welding is classified as a *resistance-projection-like arc process* that produces a joint in **milliseconds to a few seconds**, depending on stud diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST741 – Module 1):**
> Stud welding is treated as a *primary fastening process* in modern shipbuilding because it eliminates pre-drilled or punched holes, preserves the parent-plate cross-section, and produces leak-tight, high-fatigue joints — a decisive advantage over manual arc tacking or through-bolting on hull plates and superstructures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **steel plate** (the hull of a ship) and a **small metal pin** (the stud) that you want to attach upright. The traditional way would be to:
- **Drill a hole** → weaken the plate, allow water ingress.
- **Weld the pin manually** → time-consuming, inconsistent, and operator-dependent.

Now imagine a process where:
1. You **place the pin on the plate**.
2. You **push a button**.
3. A tiny **electric lightning bolt** (the arc) melts the tip of the pin and a small spot of the plate for about **0.2 – 1.5 seconds**.
4. The machine then **pushes the pin down** into the molten pool and **holds it** until it freezes.

That is stud welding. It is essentially *welding a "nail" to a steel surface in the time it takes to blink*, with a joint stronger than the stud itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Stud welding = "Automatic arc-tacking of a fastener to a parent plate using drawn-arc fusion, with no drilling required."

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **Stud diameter range:** **M3 to M25 (Ø 3 mm – 25 mm)**, with shipbuilding typically using **Ø 6 – 19 mm**.
- **Weld time:** **40 ms – 1500 ms** depending on stud size.
- **Standard:** **AWS C5.4** (Recommended Practices for Stud Welding), **ISO 14555**, **DIN 8560**.
- **Ferrule material:** **Ceramic (Al₂O₃ + MgO)** — confines the arc, shields the molten metal, and contains the spatter.
- **Power source:** **DC constant current**, drooping (constant-current) characteristic, 100 % duty cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Stud Welding Processes

Stud welding is broadly classified into **four** sub-processes under **AWS C5.4 / ISO 14555**:

| # | Sub-Process | Arc Duration | Ferrule Used? | Typical Stud Ø | Shipbuilding Use |
|---|-------------|--------------|---------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Drawn-Arc Stud Welding (DA)** | 0.2 – 1.5 s | **Yes** (ceramic) | 6 – 25 mm | **Primary process** (shear studs, insulation pins) |
| 2 | **Capacitor Discharge (CD) Stud Welding** | 1 – 6 ms | No (argon shield) | 3 – 10 mm | Light-duty fixings, thin plates |
| 3 | **Short-Cycle Stud Welding (SC)** | 0.05 – 0.2 s | Optional | 3 – 12 mm | Production lines, thin sheets |
| 4 | **Gas Metal Arc (GMA / "Cyc-Arc") Stud Welding** | Continuous | No (shielding gas) | 6 – 16 mm | Ship superstructures |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU Module 1 of Shipbuilding Joining Techniques, the focus is the **Drawn-Arc (DA) process**, which is the workhorse of shipyards worldwide (Larsen & Toubro, Cochin Shipyard, Hyundai Heavy Industries, etc.).

## 2.2 The Drawn-Arc (DA) Stud Welding Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The process follows a strict **four-stage cycle**:

1. **Stage 1 — Initial Contact:**
   The stud (with a small ceramic ferrule / arc shield slipped over its base) is pressed against the cleaned base plate by the welding gun. A small **tip-initiating material (aluminium ball or flux tip)** is often pre-placed to ensure clean arc ignition.

2. **Stage 2 — Arc Drawing (Lift-off):**
   The stud is **lifted** (typically **1.5 – 3 mm**) by a solenoid/electromagnet, drawing a controlled arc. The current (typically **300 A – 2500 A** for ship-size studs) melts:
   - the **tip of the stud**, and
   - a corresponding spot on the **base plate**.

3. **Stage 3 — Arc Maintenance:**
   The arc is held for the pre-set **weld time (t)**, during which both surfaces reach fusion temperature. A **ceramic ferrule** confines the arc, concentrates the heat, and prevents atmospheric contamination.

4. **Stage 4 — Plunge and Forging:**
   The stud is **forcibly plunged** into the molten pool by the gun spring/cylinder. The molten metal is **forged** upward into the ferrule cavity, forming a **full-strength fillet weld** around the entire circumference of the stud base. The ferrule is broken off after solidification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The total **arc energy (heat input)** is governed by:
> $$H = V \cdot I \cdot t \cdot \eta$$
> where **V** = arc voltage (≈ 20–30 V), **I** = welding current, **t** = weld time, **η** = process efficiency (≈ 0.7 for DA stud welding).
> Insufficient H → poor fusion; excessive H → burn-through on thin ship plates.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Range | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|-----------|-----------------|------|------------------|
| 1 | **Weld time (t)** | $t \approx 0.02 \cdot d^{2} + 30$ (empirical) | ms | d = stud Ø in mm; sets heat input |
| 2 | **Welding current (I)** | $I \approx 80 \cdot d + 200$ (typical) | A | d = stud Ø in mm |
| 3 | **Lift height (h)** | $h \approx 1.5 + 0.1 \cdot d$ | mm | Distance stud lifts to draw arc |
| 4 | **Plunge depth (p)** | $p \approx 0.3 \cdot d$ | mm | Forging stroke into molten pool |
| 5 | **Heat input (H)** | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{1000} \cdot \eta$ | kJ | Avoid burn-through on thin plates |
| 6 | **Stud tensile load (F)** | $F = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} \cdot \sigma_{u,stud}$ | N | Verifies joint strength |
| 7 | **Minimum base-plate thickness (t_min)** | $t_{min} \approx 0.4 \cdot d$ (steel) | mm | Prevents blow-through |
| 8 | **Ferrule OD (D_f)** | $D_{f} \approx 1.6 \cdot d$ | mm | Selection for ceramic arc shield |

> **NOTE ON TABLES:** All absolute-value bars and unit separators use `\vert` / `\mid` equivalents to preserve markdown table integrity. Example: $t_{min} \approx 0.4 \cdot d$ (where $d$ is the stud diameter).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Shipbuilding

Stud welding is the **dominant fastening method** in modern merchant and naval shipbuilding for the following:

- **Shear connectors** on **composite ship decks** (steel plate + concrete topping) — prevents slip in **DSS / DSF superstructures** (Double Skin / Double Floor).
- **Insulation pins** for lining **LNG / LPG cargo tanks** and **boiler casings** (stud + washer holds refractory).
- **Cable tray hangers, pipe supports, drip trays** throughout engine rooms.
- **Cathodic protection anodes** and **zinc ribbon holders** on hull bottoms.
- **Non-destructive fastening of outfit items** in **panoramic accommodation modules**, where drilling is forbidden (fire-zone integrity).
- **Temporary strong-backs and lifting padeyes** during block erection.

> [!TIP]
> **Why shipbuilders prefer stud welding over manual arc-tacking:**
> - **One operation = one fastener** (cycle time ≈ 1–2 s per stud → 30× faster than manual).
> - **No pre-drilling** → hull plate strength preserved (no net-section loss).
> - **No back-side marking** → ideal for **one-side welded** panels.
> - **Full-strength fillet** → exceeds stud base metal strength (typical failure mode is **stud shear**, *not* weld failure, in properly welded joints).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation of the Minimum Base-Plate Thickness

To prevent **blow-through** (the molten pool burning a hole through the base plate), the plate must absorb the arc energy without exceeding its volumetric heat capacity. The heat-affected zone of diameter $D$ on the base plate contains a molten volume $V_{m}$.

$$V_{m} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{2}}{4} \cdot t_{min}$$

The **sensible heat** required to bring this volume from ambient $T_{a}$ to melting $T_{m}$ is:

$$Q_{req} = \rho \cdot V_{m} \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{m} - T_{a})$$

The **heat input delivered** by the stud arc is:

$$Q_{in} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$$

For a safe process, $Q_{in} \le Q_{req}$ (with a safety factor $k \ge 1.5$). Substituting and solving for $t_{min}$:

**Step 1:** Equate energies and apply safety factor:

$$k \cdot \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{2}}{4} \cdot t_{min} \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{m} - T_{a}) = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $t_{min}$:

$$t_{min} = \dfrac{4 \cdot k \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t}{\pi \cdot D^{2} \cdot \rho \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{m} - T_{a})}$$

**Step 3:** Introduce the empirical diameter relation (HAZ diameter $D \approx 1.3 \cdot d$) and simplify numerically for **mild steel**:

- $\rho = 7850 \ \mathrm{kg/m^{3}}$
- $c_{p} = 470 \ \mathrm{J/(kg \cdot K)}$
- $T_{m} - T_{a} \approx 1480 - 25 = 1455 \ \mathrm{K}$
- $\eta = 0.7$, $k = 1.5$, $D = 1.3 \cdot d$

$$t_{min} = \dfrac{4 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 0.7 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t}{\pi \cdot (1.3 d)^{2} \cdot 7850 \cdot 470 \cdot 1455}$$

**Step 4:** Numerically reduce (with $V \approx 25$ V):

$$t_{min} \approx 0.45 \cdot d \quad \text{[mm]}$$

This is the famous empirical **"rule of thumb"** used in every shipyard welding procedure specification (WPS) for stud welding:

> **Final Empirical Rule:** $t_{min} \approx 0.4 \cdot d$ to $0.5 \cdot d$ for carbon-manganese shipbuilding steel.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation of Stud Weld Strength

The **shear strength of a properly welded stud** is governed by the **weld collar geometry**. The fillet weld at the base of the stud has:

- Effective throat $a$ (≈ $0.4 \cdot d$ for optimal plunge)
- Circumference $C = \pi \cdot d$

Shear force at the throat (using von Mises / AWS shear yield):

$$F_{shear} = 0.6 \cdot \sigma_{y, stud} \cdot a \cdot \pi \cdot d$$

Substitute $a = 0.4 \cdot d$:

$$F_{shear} = 0.6 \cdot \sigma_{y,stud} \cdot 0.4 \cdot d \cdot \pi \cdot d = 0.24 \cdot \pi \cdot \sigma_{y, stud} \cdot d^{2}$$

**Final step** — pull-out (tensile) strength of the stud:

$$F_{pull} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} \cdot \sigma_{u, stud}$$

For a properly welded stud, the design rule is:

$$F_{shear} \ge F_{pull} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.24 \cdot \pi \cdot \sigma_{y, stud} \cdot d^{2} \ge \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} \cdot \sigma_{u, stud}$$

**Verification:** $0.24 \cdot \sigma_{y, stud} \ge 0.25 \cdot \sigma_{u, stud}$. Since $\sigma_{u} / \sigma_{y} \approx 1.5$ for mild steel, this holds — i.e., **the stud always fails in its shank (tension) before the weld fails in shear**, which is the desired design behaviour.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A shipbuilder must weld **Ø 12 mm mild-steel shear studs** onto a **6 mm thick ship plate (grade AH36)**. The stud has $d = 12$ mm, $\sigma_{u} = 410$ MPa. Determine: (i) minimum acceptable plate thickness, (ii) recommended welding current, (iii) recommended weld time, (iv) tensile load capacity of the stud, and (v) state the failure mode.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**(i) Minimum plate thickness**

$$t_{min} \approx 0.4 \cdot d = 0.4 \cdot 12 = 4.8 \ \text{mm}$$

Since the actual plate is **6 mm > 4.8 mm** ⇒ **Safe, no blow-through**.

**(ii) Welding current**

$$I \approx 80 \cdot d + 200 = 80 \cdot 12 + 200 = 1160 \ \text{A}$$

**(iii) Weld time**

$$t \approx 0.02 \cdot d^{2} + 30 = 0.02 \cdot 144 + 30 = 32.9 \ \text{ms} \;\;(\text{use } t = 33 \ \text{ms minimum})$$

**(iv) Tensile load capacity of the stud**

$$F_{pull} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.012)^{2}}{4} \cdot 410 \times 10^{6}$$

$$F_{pull} = 1.130 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 4.10 \times 10^{8} = 46{,}346 \ \text{N} \approx \mathbf{46.3 \ \text{kN}}$$

**(v) Failure mode**

The stud shank (in tension) will yield/fracture before the weld collar shears ⇒ **Stud shank failure is the expected (and safe) mode.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Process-Flow Diagram: Drawn-Arc Stud Welding Cycle

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps the four-stage DA stud welding cycle, with all interactions between the power source, gun, stud, ferrule, and base plate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STAGE 1: INITIAL CONTACT\nStud with ferrule pressed against plate by gun] --> B[STAGE 2: ARC DRAWING\nSolenoid lifts stud 1.5-3 mm; arc strikes]
    B --> C[STAGE 3: ARC MAINTENANCE\nCurrent 300-2500 A for 0.2-1.5 s\nMolten pool forms under ferrule]
    C --> D[STAGE 4: PLUNGE AND FORGING\nStud plunged into pool; fillet weld forms]
    D --> E[COOLING AND SOLIDIFICATION\nFerrule broken off; joint complete]
    E --> F{QUALITY CHECK\nBend test, visual, torque test}
    F -->|Pass| G[NEXT STUD CYCLE]
    F -->|Fail| H[REJECT / REWORK]

    P[DC POWER SOURCE\nConstant current 100% duty] -.Supplies I.-> B
    P -.Supplies I.-> C
    GUN[STUD WELDING GUN\nSolenoid + spring] -.Controls lift plunge.-> B
    GUN -.Controls plunge.-> D
    FER[CERAMIC FERRULE\nArc shield + slag trap] -.Confines.-> C

    classDef stage fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef aux fill:#8b4513,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef qc fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class A,B,C,D,E stage
    class P,GUN,FER aux
    class F,G,H qc
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Equipment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[DC STUD WELDER\nConstant-current\n100% duty cycle] -->|Heavy current cables| CTRL[WELDING CONTROL UNIT\nTimer + Current regulator]
    CTRL -->|Signal cable| GUN[STUD WELDING GUN\nPistol / automatic head]
    GUN -->|Holds + plunges| ST[STUD + FERRULE\nAl2O3 ceramic]
    ST -->|Welds onto| PL[SHIP HULL PLATE\nAH36 / DH36]
    PS -->|3-phase 415V| SUPPLY[SHIPYARD POWER SUPPLY]
    CTRL -->|Operator WPS| OP[OPERATOR / ROBOT]
    OP -.Triggers.-> CTRL

    classDef power fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef ctrl fill:#2980b9,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef gun fill:#16a085,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef plate fill:#7f8c8d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class PS power
    class CTRL,OP ctrl
    class GUN,ST gun
    class PL,SUPPLY plate
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description (Mermaid-Limited) of the Ferrule Action

The **ceramic ferrule** performs **three** simultaneous functions during a stud weld:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[FERRULE FUNCTION 1\nARC CONFINEMENT\nConcentrates arc heat] --> R1[HIGHER LOCAL TEMPERATURE\nBetter fusion]
    F2[FERRULE FUNCTION 2\nATMOSPHERIC SHIELD\nBlocks N2 O2 H2] --> R2[NO POROSITY\nDefect-free weld]
    F3[FERRULE FUNCTION 3\nSPATTER TRAP\nHolds molten metal] --> R3[UNIFORM FILLET\nFull strength collar]
    R1 --> OUT[STUD WELD QUALITY]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT

    classDef func fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef result fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef final fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class F1,F2,F3 func
    class R1,R2,R3 result
    class OUT final
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 1]`
**Define stud welding. List the four main sub-processes of stud welding as classified by AWS C5.4.**

**Model Answer:**

Stud welding is an *arc welding process* in which a metal stud is fusion-welded onto a base metal in a single automatic operation by drawing an arc between the stud and the workpiece, then plunging the stud into the molten pool.

The four sub-processes are:
1. **Drawn-Arc (DA) Stud Welding** – uses ceramic ferrule, weld time 0.2–1.5 s; primary shipbuilding process.
2. **Capacitor Discharge (CD) Stud Welding** – very short arc, no ferrule, used for small studs ≤10 mm.
3. **Short-Cycle Stud Welding** – intermediate between CD and DA, 0.05–0.2 s.
4. **Gas Metal Arc (GMA) / "Cyc-Arc" Stud Welding** – uses shielding gas, continuous feeding.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1]`
**What is the role of the ceramic ferrule in drawn-arc stud welding?**

**Model Answer:**

The ceramic ferrule (made of **Al₂O₃ + MgO**) performs **three** functions:
1. **Arc confinement** – concentrates the arc on a small spot, ensuring high local heat input and complete fusion.
2. **Atmospheric shielding** – prevents contamination of the molten metal by N₂, O₂, and H₂ from air, eliminating porosity.
3. **Spatter containment** – traps molten metal ejected during the arc, shaping a uniform fillet weld collar around the stud base, and is then broken off after solidification.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **Drawn-Arc Stud Welding (DA) process** used in shipbuilding. Describe its four operational stages and the function of the ceramic ferrule.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A shipyard must weld **Ø 16 mm mild-steel shear studs** onto a **8 mm thick** hull plate (grade AH36). The stud has ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{u} = 410$ MPa. Determine:
  (i) The **minimum base-plate thickness** required.
  (ii) The **recommended welding current** and **weld time**.
  (iii) The **tensile load capacity** of the stud, and identify the **failure mode**.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Drawn-Arc Stud Welding (DA) Process:**

It is the most widely used stud welding process in shipyards. A stud is held by a gun, a ceramic ferrule is placed around its base, and an arc is drawn between the stud and the plate. The stud is then plunged into the molten pool to forge a fillet weld.

**Four Operational Stages:**

1. **Initial Contact:** The gun presses the stud (with ferrule) against the cleaned plate.
2. **Arc Drawing:** A solenoid lifts the stud 1.5–3 mm; an arc is struck; current of 300–2500 A flows for 0.2–1.5 s.
3. **Arc Maintenance:** Molten pool forms on both stud tip and plate surface under the ferrule.
4. **Plunge and Forging:** The stud is forced down into the pool, displacing molten metal upward into the ferrule to form a uniform fillet collar.

**Ferrule Functions:** arc confinement, atmospheric shielding, spatter containment (as in Q2 above).

**Neat Sketch (described):** [Cross-section showing stud, ferrule, arc, molten pool, plate, fillet weld collar, lift height, plunge depth].

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Minimum plate thickness:**

$$t_{min} \approx 0.4 \cdot d = 0.4 \cdot 16 = 6.4 \ \text{mm}$$

Actual plate = 8 mm > 6.4 mm ⇒ **Safe**.

**(ii) Welding current and weld time:**

$$I \approx 80 \cdot 16 + 200 = 1480 \ \text{A}$$

$$t \approx 0.02 \cdot (16)^{2} + 30 = 0.02 \cdot 256 + 30 = 35.1 \ \text{ms} \;\;(\text{use } 35{-}40 \ \text{ms})$$

**(iii) Tensile load capacity:**

$$F_{pull} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.016)^{2}}{4} \cdot 410 \times 10^{6} = 2.011 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 4.10 \times 10^{8}$$

$$F_{pull} = 82{,}441 \ \text{N} \approx \mathbf{82.4 \ \text{kN}}$$

**Failure mode:** The stud shank will fracture in tension *before* the weld collar fails in shear — confirming a **stud shank failure (tension)**, which is the *safe and expected* design behaviour.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Drawn-Arc (DA)** and **Capacitor Discharge (CD)** stud welding in terms of arc duration, ferrule use, stud diameter range, base-material thickness, and typical shipbuilding applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** List **six advantages** of stud welding over manual arc-tacking in shipbuilding. State the AWS standard governing stud welding and the safety checks performed on a completed stud weld.

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Parameter | Drawn-Arc (DA) | Capacitor Discharge (CD) |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Arc duration | 0.2 – 1.5 s | 1 – 6 ms |
| Ferrule | **Yes** (ceramic) | **No** (uses inert gas or no shield) |
| Stud Ø range | 6 – 25 mm | 3 – 10 mm |
| Plate thickness | ≥ 0.4 d (typical 4–25 mm) | Thin plates (0.5 – 3 mm) |
| Power source | DC transformer / inverter | Capacitor bank (stored energy) |
| Heat input | High | Very low |
| Shipbuilding use | Shear studs, insulation pins, padeyes | Light fittings, cable clips on thin panels |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Six Advantages of Stud Welding over Manual Tacking:**

1. **Speed:** Cycle time ≈ 1–2 s per stud (≈ 30× faster than manual).
2. **No pre-drilling** → plate strength preserved (no net-section loss).
3. **One-sided operation** → ideal for shipyard block assembly.
4. **Consistent quality** → independent of operator skill.
5. **Full-strength fillet** → typically stronger than the stud shank.
6. **Clean process** → no spatter, no slag, no fumes (with proper extraction).

**Governing Standard:** **AWS C5.4** (Recommended Practices for Stud Welding) and **ISO 14555**.

**Safety / Quality Checks Performed:**
- **Visual inspection** for fillet uniformity, undercut, porosity.
- **Bend test** (typical 30° bend) on production samples.
- **Torque test** on threaded studs.
- **Dye-penetrant test** for critical joints.
- **Macro-sectioning** for WPS qualification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating "stud welding" with "resistance spot welding":** Stud welding is an **ARC** process, not a resistance process. Many students write "resistance" — automatic ½ mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the ferrule functions:** Examiner allocates 2 marks specifically for "function of ferrule"; merely naming it costs 0 marks.
> 3. **Confusing current formula:** The KTU-expected formula is $I = 80d + 200$; writing just "$I = 100d$" loses 1 mark in derivations.
> 4. **No units in final answer:** Always include **N / kN** for force and **mm** for thickness; silent units = −½ mark.
> 5. **Skipping the safety factor** in $t_{min}$ derivation → −1 mark.
> 6. **Not stating failure mode** in strength problems → −1 mark. Always end with: *"the stud shank fails in tension, which is the safe and expected mode."*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Stud welding = arc-welding a fastener end-to-end onto a base plate in one automatic operation.
- **Primary shipbuilding process:** **Drawn-Arc (DA)** stud welding with ceramic ferrule.
- **Four stages:** Contact → Arc drawing → Arc maintenance → Plunge and forging.
- **Ceramic ferrule (Al₂O₃ + MgO):** performs 3 functions — arc confinement, atmospheric shielding, spatter containment.
- **Weld time:** $t \approx 0.02 d^{2} + 30$ ms.
- **Welding current:** $I \approx 80 d + 200$ A.
- **Lift height:** $\approx 1.5 + 0.1 d$ mm.
- **Plunge depth:** $\approx 0.3 d$ mm.
- **Min. plate thickness rule:** $t_{min} \approx 0.4 d$ (mild steel).
- **Tensile load:** $F_{pull} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \sigma_{u,stud}$.
- **Shear strength:** $F_{shear} = 0.24 \pi \sigma_{y,stud} d^{2}$.
- **Design rule:** Stud shank fails in tension *before* weld fails in shear (safe mode).
- **Standards:** **AWS C5.4** and **ISO 14555**.
- **Shipbuilding uses:** shear connectors, insulation pins, cable/pipe supports, padeyes, anode holders.
- **Advantage over manual tacking:** 30× faster, no drilling, full-strength fillet, no spatter.
- **CD process:** used for thin plates and small studs; arc duration 1–6 ms; no ferrule.
- **Quality checks:** visual, bend test (30°), torque test, dye-penetrant, macro-section.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Principle of Welding

In the context of **Shipbuilding Technology**, the **Principle of Welding** refers to the fundamental scientific mechanism by which two or more pieces of metallic material (typically steel plates, sections, and forgings used in hull, superstructure, and outfitting fabrication) are permanently joined through the application of **heat, pressure, or both**, with or without the addition of a filler material, to produce a **metallurgical continuity** at the joint interface.

The principle rests upon three foundational pillars:

1. **Generation of a controlled heat source** capable of reaching the melting point of the base metal (for steel: **~1500 °C**).
2. **Fusion and coalescence** of the base metal (and filler) into a common molten pool, known as the **weld pool**.
3. **Solidification** of the weld pool under controlled cooling to form a sound, monolithic joint possessing mechanical strength comparable to — or exceeding — the parent metal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, Module 2 specifically emphasizes the **Principle of Arc Welding**, which is the **dominant joining process in modern shipyards**, accounting for over **90%** of all hull fabrication welds.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Candle Wax" Intuition

Imagine holding two cold butter knives over a candle flame. The flame's heat melts the tips, and if you press them together while molten, they fuse into a single piece as they cool. **Welding works on the exact same logic**, except the "candle" is replaced by a **focused electric arc at ~6000 °C**, and the "butter knives" are ship-grade steel plates.

| Everyday Analogy | Welding Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Candle flame | Electric arc / gas flame |
| Melting butter | Fusion of base metal |
| Pressing knives together | Application of pressure (in resistance / forge welding) |
| Wax cooling & hardening | Weld pool solidification |
| Single solid piece | Monolithic welded joint |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in shipbuilding:** A ship's hull must behave as a **single continuous pressure vessel** against sea water, hydrostatic loads, and dynamic wave forces. Welded joints provide this structural integrity, making the *principle* of welding the cornerstone of every warship, merchant vessel, and offshore platform ever built.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Welding Principles

The principle of welding can be broadly classified based on the **state of the base metal during joining**:

- **Fusion Welding Principle** — Base metal is melted; no pressure required (e.g., SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW).
- **Solid-State Welding Principle** — Base metal is not melted; joining occurs below the melting point via pressure and diffusion (e.g., Friction Stir Welding used in aluminum superstructures).
- **Brazing & Soldering Principle** — Only the filler metal melts; base metal remains solid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 evaluation purposes, remember the **3 fundamental conditions for any welding process**: (i) Generation of sufficient heat, (ii) Clean metal-to-metal contact (free of oxides/contaminants), (iii) Atomic bonding across the joint interface.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Mechanisms Behind the Welding Principle

### Mechanism 1: Heat Generation
The heat required for fusion is derived from one of the following physical phenomena:

- **Electric arc** (most common): Heat from ionization of gas between an electrode and workpiece.
- **Resistance heating**: $I^2R$ losses (used in spot, projection, and flash welding).
- **Gas combustion**: Oxy-acetylene, oxy-hydrogen flames.
- **Thermochemical reaction**: Thermit reaction (used historically in heavy plate welding).
- **Frictional heat**: Mechanical friction between surfaces (FSW, rotary friction welding).
- **Focused energy beams**: Laser beam welding (LBW) and Electron beam welding (EBW) used in high-precision shipbuilding components.

### Mechanism 2: Heat Transfer & Distribution
Once heat is generated, it flows into the workpiece primarily by **conduction**, with some **convection** losses to the surrounding shielding gas and **radiation** from the arc plasma. The **heat distribution** follows **Gauss's law** for a moving point source on a semi-infinite plate, characterized by the **heat input parameter**.

### Mechanism 3: Metallurgical Bonding
At the atomic level, **metallic bonds** form when clean, atomically close metal surfaces achieve **atomic interdiffusion**. This is why **surface cleanliness** and **shielding from atmospheric contamination** are non-negotiable principles in any welding process.

---

## 2.2 The Heat Input Formula — The Heart of Welding Principles

The **heat input (H)** is the most critical derived quantity in the principle of welding. It governs weld pool size, HAZ width, residual stress, and distortion.

### Derivation of Heat Input (Logical Steps):

- **Step 1 — Arc Power:** The electrical power consumed at the arc is the product of arc voltage and current.
  
  $$P = V \times I$$

- **Step 2 — Energy per unit length of weld:** When the arc traverses a unit length, the time taken is inversely proportional to travel speed.
  
  $$t = \frac{60}{S} \quad \text{(seconds per cm, if S is in cm/min)}$$

- **Step 3 — Heat actually delivered to the workpiece:** Only a fraction of arc power (the **arc efficiency**, $\eta$) reaches the workpiece.

  $$H = \eta \times V \times I \times \frac{60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}$$

- **Step 4 — Thermal Efficiency:** Different processes have different efficiencies.
  - **SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc Welding):** $\eta \approx 0.75$ to **0.85**
  - **GMAW (Gas Metal Arc Welding):** $\eta \approx 0.65$ to **0.75**
  - **GTAW (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding):** $\eta \approx 0.40$ to **0.60**
  - **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding):** $\eta \approx 0.85$ to **0.95** (most efficient — used in ship panel fabrication)
  - **PAW (Plasma Arc Welding):** $\eta \approx 0.50$ to **0.65**

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arc Power | $P = V \cdot I$ | W (Watt) | Electrical power at the arc |
| 2 | Heat Generated | $Q = 0.24 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$ | cal | Heat in calories (with 0.24 = Joule's constant) |
| 3 | Heat Input | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Energy per unit weld length |
| 4 | Heat Input (alt.) | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ | J/mm | Energy per unit weld length (SI direct) |
| 5 | Travel Speed | $S = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot H}$ | mm/min | Inverse of heat input |
| 6 | Arc Force | $F_a = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot I^2}{4\pi} \cdot \ln\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$ | N | Plasma constriction pressure |
| 7 | Fusion Area | $A_f = \dfrac{H}{h_s \cdot \rho}$ | mm² | Weld cross-section from heat balance |
| 8 | Cooling Rate | $CR = 2\pi k \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_c - T_0}{H}\right)^2$ | °C/s | Rosenthal's 2-D cooling rate |
| 9 | Deposition Rate | $DR = \alpha \cdot I$ | kg/h | Mass of weld metal per hour |
| 10 | Specific Energy | $E_s = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S \cdot t_{plate}}$ | J/mm³ | Volumetric energy density |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** In KTU Board examinations, $\eta$ is often pre-specified for the welding process. For shipbuilding applications, **SAW with $\eta = 0.90$** is the conventional default for hull panel welding calculations.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above are not theoretical curiosities — they directly determine:

- **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS):** Every shipyard must qualify its welds to classification societies (e.g., **IRS, DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV**) using the heat input limits derived from the principle.
- **Residual Stress & Distortion Control:** Higher heat input $\Rightarrow$ wider HAZ $\Rightarrow$ greater distortion. This is critical in **thin-plate superstructure** welding.
- **Productivity:** In shipbuilding, a **single-paneler SAW station** uses heat input calculations to optimize the trade-off between deposition rate and quality.
- **Defect Prediction:** Cold lap, lack of fusion, and porosity are all explained by **insufficient heat input** derived from the principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Heat Generated in an Electric Arc

### Given:
A DC arc welding process is operated at an arc voltage of **V = 25 V** and a welding current of **I = 200 A** for a duration of **t = 10 minutes**. Compute the total heat generated at the arc, in both Joules and kilojoules.

### Step 1 — Identify the governing principle
The principle of heat generation in an electric arc follows **Joule's Law of Heating**, which states that the heat produced in a conductor is directly proportional to the square of the current, the resistance, and the time of current flow.

$$H_{arc} = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t \quad \text{(Joule's Law in resistive form)}$$

However, in arc welding it is more convenient to express heat in terms of arc voltage and current using the power form, because arc voltage is the more directly measurable parameter.

### Step 2 — Convert to the voltage-current-time form
Since electrical power $P = V \cdot I$, and energy is the product of power and time:

$$Q = P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values
With V = 25 V, I = 200 A, and t = 10 min = 600 s:

$$Q = 25 \times 200 \times 600$$

$$Q = 3{,}000{,}000 \text{ J}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to kilojoules

$$Q = \dfrac{3{,}000{,}000}{1000} = 3000 \text{ kJ}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to calories (using Joule's constant 0.24 cal/J)

$$Q = 0.24 \times 3{,}000{,}000 = 720{,}000 \text{ cal} = 720 \text{ kcal}$$

### Result
The arc generates **3,000 kJ of thermal energy** in 10 minutes, sufficient to fuse approximately **1.5 kg** of mild steel (using latent heat of fusion $\approx 272$ kJ/kg and sensible heat $\approx 700$ kJ/kg for raising to melting point).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The factor **0.24** is *Joule's mechanical equivalent of heat*. Always include unit conversions explicitly — failing to convert minutes $\rightarrow$ seconds is the **#1 mark-deduction error** reported by KTU Board examiners.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Process Parameters on Weld Geometry

The principle of welding predicts a direct relationship between input parameters (V, I, S) and output weld characteristics. The following analytical matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam table**:

| Input Parameter (↑ Increase) | Penetration (Depth) | Bead Width | HAZ Width | Distortion | Spatter | Porosity Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage (V) | Slight ↑ | **Strong ↑** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | Slight ↑ |
| Current (I) | **Strong ↑** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Travel Speed (S) | ↓ | **Strong ↓** | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ |
| Electrode Diameter | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ |
| Arc Length | Slight ↑ | **Strong ↑** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Heat Input (H) | ↑ | **Strong ↑** | ↑ | **Strong ↑** | ↑ | ↑ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice the **"Strong ↑"** markers. These are the dominant relationships. KTU questions on the *principle* often ask: *"If travel speed is doubled, what happens to heat input and bead width?"* — Answer: Heat input **halves** (inverse proportionality), bead width **decreases sharply**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Heat Input Calculation (Board-Standard)

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A shipyard uses **SAW** for welding 12 mm thick shipbuilding steel plates. The welding parameters are: V = 30 V, I = 500 A, travel speed S = 30 cm/min. Arc efficiency for SAW is 0.90. Calculate the **heat input in kJ/mm** and determine whether it falls within the classification society limit of **3.5 kJ/mm** for this plate thickness.

### Step 1 — Apply the heat input formula

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$$

### Step 2 — Convert travel speed units
Travel speed S = 30 cm/min. The formula above requires S in **mm/min** for direct substitution into the formula as written, OR in cm/min if the conversion constant is adjusted. Let us convert:

$$S = 30 \text{ cm/min} = 300 \text{ mm/min}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute values

$$H = \dfrac{0.90 \times 30 \times 500 \times 60}{300 \times 1000}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify numerator

$$H = \dfrac{0.90 \times 30 \times 500 \times 60}{300 \times 1000} = \dfrac{810{,}000}{300{,}000}$$

### Step 5 — Final value

$$H = 2.7 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

### Step 6 — Compare with classification limit

$$2.7 \text{ kJ/mm} < 3.5 \text{ kJ/mm} \quad \checkmark \text{Acceptable}$$

### Conclusion
The welding procedure is **within specification**. Had H exceeded 3.5 kJ/mm, the shipyard would have to either reduce current, increase travel speed, or use a multi-pass technique.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Principle of Arc Welding

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional interactions** of the key physical elements constituting the principle of arc welding — from power source to final joint.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[POWER SOURCE 220V to 440V AC Mains] --> B[RECTIFIER AND CONTROL UNIT]
    B --> C[DC WELDING CABLE POSITIVE POLE]
    C --> D[ELECTRODE HOLDER WITH CONSUMABLE ELECTRODE]
    D --> E[ARC PLASMA ZONE TEMP 6000C]
    E --> F[WELD POOL MOLTEN METAL]
    E --> G[SHIELDING GAS OR FLUX COVERAGE]
    F --> H[SOLIDIFIED WELD BEAD]
    F --> I[HEAT AFFECTED ZONE HAZ]
    F --> J[PARENT METAL UNDISTURBED]
    H --> K[INSPECTION NDT RT OR UT]
    K --> L[CLASSIFICATION APPROVED JOINT]
    G --> M[ATMOSPHERIC CONTAMINATION BLOCKED]
    M --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide:** The arc plasma is the **central node** — all other functional blocks (electrode, weld pool, shielding, parent metal) converge at the arc because the arc is the *physical embodiment* of the welding principle.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Heat Flow Schematic

The following diagram shows the **energy flow** in the principle of welding, from electrical input to mechanical output in the joint.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[ELECTRICAL INPUT V TIMES I] --> P2[ARC POWER LOSS 10 TO 15 PERCENT]
    P2 --> P3[EFFECTIVE HEAT TO WORKPIECE]
    P3 --> P4[CONDUCTION INTO BASE METAL]
    P3 --> P5[CONVECTION LOSS TO SHIELDING GAS]
    P3 --> P6[RADIATION LOSS TO SURROUNDINGS]
    P4 --> P7[PEAK TEMPERATURE IN WELD POOL]
    P7 --> P8[COOLING RATE DETERMINES HAZ]
    P8 --> P9[FINAL MICROSTRUCTURE]
    P9 --> P10[MECHANICAL PROPERTIES TENSILE IMPACT]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Principle of Metallurgical Bonding at the Weld Interface

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[CLEAN METAL SURFACE FREE OF OXIDE] --> M2[ATOMIC CLOSENESS BELOW 0.5 NM]
    M2 --> M3[ATOMIC INTERDIFFUSION ACROSS INTERFACE]
    M3 --> M4[FORMATION OF METALLIC BONDS]
    M4 --> M5[COALESCENCE INTO SINGLE PHASE]
    M5 --> M6[SOLIDIFICATION UNDER CONTROLLED COOLING]
    M6 --> M7[MONOLITHIC JOINT STRENGTH]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The "atomic closeness" threshold of **< 0.5 nm** is critical — this is why welding (achieving fusion) is fundamentally different from mechanical fastening (which only achieves macroscopic contact).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]

**State and explain the fundamental principle of welding. List the three essential conditions that must be satisfied for a sound welded joint.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **principle of welding** is the process of permanently joining two or more metallic components by establishing **metallurgical continuity** at their interface through the application of **heat, pressure, or both**, with or without filler material.

The three essential conditions are:

1. **Generation of sufficient heat** to reach the melting point of the base metal (e.g., **~1500 °C** for mild steel) — *1 mark*
2. **Clean metal-to-metal contact**, free of oxides, slag, oil, and moisture — *1 mark*
3. **Atomic bonding** across the joint interface, achieved through fusion and solidification — *1 mark*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional Pattern]

**Define "heat input" in arc welding. State its formula and the typical range of arc efficiency for SMAW and SAW processes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Heat Input (H)** is the amount of thermal energy delivered per unit length of weld, expressed in **kJ/mm**. It is the governing parameter that controls weld pool size, penetration, HAZ width, and distortion. *— 1 mark*

**Formula:** $\quad H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ *— 1 mark*

**Arc Efficiency Ranges:** *— 1 mark*
- **SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc Welding):** $\eta = 0.75$ to **0.85**
- **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding):** $\eta = 0.85$ to **0.95**

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Module 2, 14-Mark Pattern]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **principle of arc welding** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **arc plasma zone**, the **weld pool**, the **HAZ**, and the **shielding mechanism** in producing a sound weld. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **GMAW** welding operation is carried out on a shipbuilding steel plate with the following parameters: Arc voltage V = 28 V, Welding current I = 250 A, Travel speed S = 40 cm/min, Arc efficiency $\eta = 0.70$. Compute the **heat input in kJ/mm** and comment on its suitability for a 10 mm thick plate (typical shipbuilding limit: 2.5 kJ/mm). **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question A

#### Part (a) — Principle of Arc Welding (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Arc welding is a fusion welding process in which the heat required for joining is obtained from an **electric arc** struck between an **electrode** and the **workpiece**. The arc temperature ranges between **6000 °C and 7000 °C**, sufficient to melt and fuse the base metal.

**Functional Zones (4 marks):**

1. **Arc Plasma Zone (1 mark):** The arc is a sustained electrical discharge through an ionized gas column. The temperature gradient follows: *cathode spot (~2400 °C) → arc column (~6000 °C) → anode spot (~2500 °C)*. Approximately **65–80%** of the arc heat is generated at the anode for DCSP (Direct Current Straight Polarity).

2. **Weld Pool (1 mark):** A localized molten metal pool formed at the joint. Its size, shape, and temperature profile govern the **weld bead geometry**. Forces acting on the pool include **arc shear force, buoyancy, Marangoni convection, and Lorentz force**.

3. **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) (1 mark):** The base metal region adjacent to the weld that does not melt but undergoes **microstructural changes** due to the thermal cycle. The HAZ is typically the **weakest zone** in the welded joint and is the focus of most non-destructive testing.

4. **Shielding Mechanism (1 mark):** Protects the molten weld pool from atmospheric contamination (O₂, N₂, H₂O). Achieved by:
   - **Flux coating decomposition** (SMAW) — produces CO₂ shielding gas
   - **Inert/active gas envelope** (GMAW/GTAW) — Argon, CO₂, or Ar + CO₂ mixtures
   - **Granular flux blanket** (SAW) — submerged arc process

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The simultaneous and coordinated action of these four zones is the **embodiment of the arc welding principle**, transforming electrical energy into a metallurgically sound joint.

**Suggested Block Diagram (1 mark):** *(Draw or refer to Section 4.1 above)*

---

#### Part (b) — Heat Input Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert travel speed to mm/min:** *(1 mark)*

$$S = 40 \text{ cm/min} = 400 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the heat input formula:** *(1 mark)*

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:** *(1 mark)*

$$H = \dfrac{0.70 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60}{400 \times 1000}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:** *(1 mark)*

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.70 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60 = 294{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator:** *(1 mark)*

$$\text{Denominator} = 400 \times 1000 = 400{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Final result and comparison:** *(2 marks)*

$$H = \dfrac{294{,}000}{400{,}000} = 0.735 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

$$0.735 \text{ kJ/mm} < 2.5 \text{ kJ/mm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The heat input is **well within** the shipbuilding limit. The weld procedure is **acceptable**, though it may produce a narrower bead profile. The shipyard may consider increasing current to improve productivity while staying under the 2.5 kJ/mm ceiling.

---

### Question B. [Alternative Choice — 14 Marks]

**(a)** With the help of a temperature distribution diagram, explain the **heat flow mechanism** in a welded joint. Differentiate between **fusion zone, heat-affected zone (HAZ), and unaffected parent metal**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between **heat input, travel speed, and arc efficiency**. A shipyard is achieving a heat input of 1.8 kJ/mm with SMAW ($\eta$ = 0.80) at V = 25 V and S = 25 cm/min. Find the welding current. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question B

#### Part (a) — Heat Flow & Zone Differentiation (7 marks)

**Heat Flow Principle (3 marks):** When the moving arc acts as a **point heat source** on a semi-infinite plate, the temperature distribution follows the **Rosenthal solution** for transient heat conduction. The peak temperature occurs at the weld centerline and decays radially outward.

- **Isotherms (lines of constant temperature)** are elliptical near the source and become circular at large distances.
- The **thermal gradient** is steepest near the fusion boundary.
- **Heat loss modes:** conduction into the plate (~80%), convection to shielding gas (~10%), radiation to surroundings (~10%).

**Three Zones (4 marks):**

| Zone | Temperature Range | Microstructural State | Properties |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fusion Zone (FZ)** | Above melting point (~1500 °C) | Complete melting & re-solidification | Cast dendritic structure; refined grains |
| **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** | 700 °C to 1500 °C | No melting, but phase transformations occur | Coarse grain HAZ, fine grain HAZ, intercritical HAZ — *weakest zone* |
| **Unaffected Parent Metal** | Below 700 °C | No microstructural change | Retains original properties |

---

#### Part (b) — Heat Input Derivation & Numerical (7 marks)

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from the basic definition — heat input is the **effective heat energy transferred per unit length of weld**:

$$H = \dfrac{\text{Effective heat energy per unit length}}{\text{Unit length}}$$

The energy input to the arc per second is $\eta \cdot V \cdot I$ (in Watts = J/s). The time taken to traverse a length $L$ at travel speed $S$ is $t = L / S$.

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t}{L} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \quad \text{[J/mm]}$$

Expressed in conventional kJ/mm with $S$ in cm/min:

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}$$

**Rearranging for current:** *(1 mark)*

$$I = \dfrac{H \cdot S \cdot 1000}{\eta \cdot V \cdot 60}$$

**Numerical substitution:** *(2 marks)*
- $H = 1.8$ kJ/mm
- $S = 25$ cm/min = 250 mm/min
- $\eta = 0.80$
- $V = 25$ V

$$I = \dfrac{1.8 \times 250 \times 1000}{0.80 \times 25 \times 60} = \dfrac{450{,}000}{1200} = 375 \text{ A}$$

**Result:** Welding current **I = 375 A** *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion blunder (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert S from cm/min to mm/min. This is the single largest source of mark loss in heat input questions.
> 2. **Confusing "heat generated" with "heat input" (–2 marks):** Heat generated uses $t$ (time); heat input uses $S$ (speed). They are dimensionally different.
> 3. **Omitting $\eta$ (arc efficiency) (–1 mark):** Always state and use the process-specific efficiency.
> 4. **Wrong formula format (–2 marks):** Writing $H = VI$ without the travel speed denominator — this is dimensionally meaningless and attracts full penalty.
> 5. **Forgetting to compare with the classification limit** — the *interpretation step* (≤ 3.5 kJ/mm?) carries **1 mark** in 7-mark problems.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Principle of Welding"**

- ☐ **Core Definition:** Joining by heat + pressure (with/without filler) to produce metallurgical continuity.
- ☐ **Three pillars of welding principle:** Heat generation → Fusion → Solidification.
- ☐ **Electric arc temperature range:** **6000 °C to 7000 °C** — the most concentrated industrial heat source.
- ☐ **Heat Input formula (memorize verbatim):**
  $$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \text{ kJ/mm} \quad \text{with } S \text{ in cm/min}$$
- ☐ **Arc efficiency values (must memorize):** SMAW → **0.80**, GMAW → **0.70**, GTAW → **0.50**, SAW → **0.90**, PAW → **0.60**.
- ☐ **Hottest part of DC arc:** For **DCEP (reverse polarity)** — **anode (workpiece)**; for **DCSP (straight polarity)** — **cathode (workpiece)**. By convention, ~**70%** of heat goes to the anode side.
- ☐ **Joule's mechanical equivalent of heat:** **0.24 cal/J** — for converting electrical energy to thermal energy in calories.
- ☐ **Three zones in a weld cross-section:** Fusion Zone (FZ) — HAZ — Parent Metal (PM).
- ☐ **HAZ is the weakest zone** — it does not melt but undergoes phase transformation; primary target of NDT.
- ☐ **Shipbuilding limit (10 mm plate, conventional):** Heat input ≤ **2.5 kJ/mm**; for 12 mm plate ≤ **3.5 kJ/mm** (approximate IRS/DNV guidelines — confirm with current rule set).
- ☐ **Inverse proportionality:** Heat input $\propto \dfrac{1}{S}$. Doubling travel speed halves the heat input.
- ☐ **Direct proportionality:** Heat input $\propto V \cdot I$. Doubling voltage or current doubles the heat input.
- ☐ **Atomic closeness threshold for metallic bonding:** **< 0.5 nm** — explains why fusion welding is fundamentally different from mechanical fastening.
- ☐ **Five modes of heat generation in welding:** Arc, Resistance, Gas combustion, Thermochemical, Friction, Beam (laser/electron).
- ☐ **Most-used process in modern shipyards:** **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)** for hull panels; **FCAW/GMAW** for fillet welds in stiffeners and brackets; **GTAW** for root passes in critical joints.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding Arrangements and Advantages/Disadvantages of Resistance Welding
Spot Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spot Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Spot Welding

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Spot Welding** is a resistance welding process in which two or more sheets of metal are joined together at localized points by the application of heat and pressure. The heat is generated by passing a **high-amperage, low-voltage electric current** through the workpieces held between two **water-cooled copper alloy electrodes**. The heat is concentrated at the faying surfaces due to the highest electrical resistance at the interface, causing the metal to reach a plastic/semi-molten state, after which pressure from the electrodes forges the parts together, forming a solid-state **weld nugget**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Spot welding is classified under **Pressure Welding** in the **Resistance Welding** family and is governed by **Joule's Law of Heating** combined with **Forging Pressure (Squeeze Force)**. It is a *non-fusion* joining process; technically the metal does not melt in a true sense — the parent metal at the interface reaches a thermoplastic state and coalesces under pressure.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Iron & Pinch" Picture

Imagine you have two thin sheets of paper and you want to stick them at one tiny spot. You cannot glue them. Instead, you press them tightly between your thumb and forefinger, then quickly heat a needle and pass it just through that single point for a fraction of a second, then squeeze harder. The paper fibers locally melt and fuse. The result is one strong "spot" joining the two sheets. 

**Spot welding is exactly this, but at an industrial scale, with the "needle" replaced by two water-cooled copper electrodes, the "heat" supplied by hundreds to thousands of amps of electrical current, and the "squeeze" delivered by a pneumatic or hydraulic ram.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Shipbuilding Context:** In a ship, a single bulkhead panel of 3 mm thick mild steel may be held to a stiffener by **dozens to hundreds** of spot welds in a single shift. Spot welding is the *heartbeat of thin-plate shipbuilding* — used in superstructures, bulkheads, deck plates, mast ladders, ventilation ducts, and aluminium outfitting work.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics (Highlighted for Quick Recall)

- **Welding Current Range:** $I = 3 \text{ kA} \text{ to } 100 \text{ kA}$ (depending on sheet thickness)
- **Open-Circuit Voltage:** $V_{oc} = 6 \text{ V} \text{ to } 24 \text{ V}$ (low voltage, high current)
- **Electrode Force (Squeeze Force):** $F = 1.5 \text{ kN} \text{ to } 25 \text{ kN}$
- **Weld Time:** $t = 0.1 \text{ s} \text{ to } 1.5 \text{ s}$ (typical)
- **Nugget Diameter:** $d_n \approx 4 \text{ mm} \text{ to } 12 \text{ mm}$ (for typical plate thickness)
- **Sheet Thickness Range:** $0.5 \text{ mm} \text{ to } 6 \text{ mm}$ (most common)
- **Fundamental Physical Law:** **Joule's Law of Heating**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Generation Equation (Joule's Law):** The cornerstone of ALL resistance welding (and therefore spot welding) is $H = I^2 R t$, where $H$ is heat in joules, $I$ is current in amperes, $R$ is resistance in ohms, and $t$ is time in seconds.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Way Coupling of Spot Welding

Spot welding is a beautifully elegant process because it is governed by **four tightly coupled physical phenomena** occurring in a fraction of a second. KTU expects you to be able to name, define, and link all four:

| # | Phenomenon | Physical Origin | Engineering Lever |
|---|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Electrical** | $H = I^2 R t$ | Welding current (kA) |
| 2 | **Thermal** | Heat conduction, latent heat | Weld time (cycles) |
| 3 | **Mechanical** | Forging pressure at electrodes | Electrode force (kN) |
| 4 | **Metallurgical** | Recrystallization, grain growth | Hold time, post-cool |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "low voltage, high current"?** A spot welding transformer typically steps **440 V (or 11 kV)** down to **6–24 V** because we need **massive current** to get $I^2$ heating at the interface, but the resistance is tiny (micro-ohms). By Ohm's law, $V = IR$ → if $R$ is very small, we must make $V$ small too, otherwise the current would be astronomically high and dangerous.

## 2.2 The Anatomy of Resistance in a Spot Weld Stack

In a typical two-sheet spot weld, the total resistance seen by the current is the **sum of several local resistances**:

$$R_{total} = 2 R_{electrode} + 2 R_{sheet} + 2 R_{contact(electrode-sheet)} + R_{faying}$$

Where:
- $R_{electrode}$ — bulk resistance of each copper electrode
- $R_{sheet}$ — bulk resistance of each sheet between the electrode and the interface
- $R_{contact(electrode-sheet)}$ — contact resistance between electrode face and outer sheet surface
- $R_{faying}$ — contact resistance at the interface between the two sheets (this is the **largest** and the one we want heat to form in)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Magic" of Spot Welding:** Out of all five resistance components, **80–90% of the heat is generated in the faying interface**, because $R_{faying}$ is much higher than the others. This is what allows the metal to fuse at a *single spot* while the rest of the sheet (and the electrodes) stays cool.

## 2.3 The Four Distinctive Time-Phases (Squeeze–Weld–Hold–Off)

A complete spot welding cycle is structured by KTU into **four sequential time phases** controlled by the **welding timer (synchronous electronic timer)**:

| Phase | Phase Name | What Happens | Why It Matters |
|-------|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Squeeze Time** | Electrode descends and applies full force, but no current flows | Ensures workpieces are pressed together, establishes contact, expels air gaps |
| 2 | **Weld Time** | Current flows; heat generates and melts the interface | This is the actual weld. Typically 6–20 cycles (at 50 Hz) = 0.12–0.4 s |
| 3 | **Hold Time** | Current is OFF, but pressure is still ON | Allows the molten nugget to solidify under pressure, preventing cracking/porosity |
| 4 | **Off Time** | Electrode lifts, prepares for next weld | Cooling, indexing of the workpiece |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often think the electrodes heat the metal. They don't. The metal heats **itself** by its own resistance. The electrodes remain cool because (a) they are made of **high-conductivity copper alloy** (low $R$), and (b) they are **water-cooled internally**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is your **high-yield cheat sheet** for every spot welding numerical. KTU loves these equations — they appear in nearly every Part B question.

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Used For |
|---|----------|---------------|--------------|----------|
| 1 | $H = I^2 R t$ | Joule's Law | J, A, $\Omega$, s | Heat generated at the weld |
| 2 | $H = V I t$ (since $V = IR$) | Alternate form | J | Sometimes easier when $V$ is given |
| 3 | $Q_{req} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat to raise temperature | J | Calculate heat required to reach plastic state |
| 4 | $Q_{nugget} = m \cdot [C_p \Delta T + L]$ | Heat including latent heat of fusion | J | If the nugget actually melts |
| 5 | $d_n = 5 \sqrt{t}$ (approx., low-carbon steel) | Nugget diameter vs. sheet thickness | mm | Empirical sizing |
| 6 | $d_n \geq 4 \sqrt{t} + 3$ | Conservative rule of thumb | mm | KTU favourite for minimum nugget size |
| 7 | $F = k \cdot d_n$ (where $k \approx 30 \text{ N/mm}$ for low-carbon steel) | Electrode force vs. nugget size | N, mm | Sizing the pneumatic/hydraulic ram |
| 8 | $I = k_1 \cdot d_n$ (where $k_1 \approx 1000 \text{ A/mm}$ for low-carbon steel) | Welding current vs. nugget size | A, mm | Sizing the transformer secondary |
| 9 | $t_{weld} \approx \left(\frac{d_n}{4}\right)^2$ | Weld time vs. nugget diameter | s, mm | Cycle-time planning |
| 10 | $P_{elec} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos \phi$ | Electrical power drawn | W | KVA sizing of supply |
| 11 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{H_{nugget}}{H_{input}}$ | Thermal efficiency (typically 50–80%) | dimensionless | Energy audit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Discipline:** In KTU numericals, always express current in **kA**, time in **seconds**, and thickness in **mm**, then convert back. Mixed units lose easy marks. Use **$\vert$ in tables** for absolute values if needed, written as `\vert`.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Spot welding dominates in:
- **Shipbuilding:** Bulkhead-to-stiffener panels, superstructure skin panels, aluminium helicopter decks and boats.
- **Automotive:** A single car body has **3,000 to 5,000 spot welds** (now being replaced by laser welding in some EV models).
- **Aerospace:** Aluminium fuselage skins.
- **White goods:** Washing machine drums, refrigerator cabinets.
- **Battery manufacturing:** Lithium-ion cell tab welding (a hot KTU 2024 area).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Required to Form the Weld Nugget

This is **the most important derivation** for KTU Part B. We will work it out step-by-step.

**Given:** Two sheets of low-carbon steel, each of thickness $t_s = 2 \text{ mm}$, nugget diameter $d_n = 6 \text{ mm}$, efficiency $\eta = 0.7$, $C_p = 480 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $L = 270 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (latent heat of fusion of low-carbon steel), initial temperature $T_0 = 25^\circ C$, melting temperature $T_m = 1500^\circ C$, density $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Step 1: Find the volume of the weld nugget.**

We approximate the nugget as a thin cylinder (disc) of diameter $d_n$ and thickness equal to the total sheet stack $2 t_s$.

$$V_{nugget} = \frac{\pi}{4} d_n^2 \cdot (2 t_s)$$

Substituting $d_n = 6 \text{ mm} = 0.006 \text{ m}$ and $2 t_s = 4 \text{ mm} = 0.004 \text{ m}$:

$$V_{nugget} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.006)^2 \times 0.004$$

**Step 2: Compute the numerical value.**

$$V_{nugget} = 0.7854 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.004$$

$$V_{nugget} = 1.131 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Find the mass of the nugget.**

$$m_{nugget} = \rho \cdot V_{nugget} = 7850 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$m_{nugget} = 8.878 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg} \approx 0.888 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4: Find the heat required (sensible + latent).**

$$Q_{req} = m \cdot [C_p (T_m - T_0) + L]$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{req} &= 8.878 \times 10^{-4} \times [480 \times (1500 - 25) + 270 \times 10^3] \\
&= 8.878 \times 10^{-4} \times [480 \times 1475 + 270000] \\
&= 8.878 \times 10^{-4} \times [708000 + 270000] \\
&= 8.878 \times 10^{-4} \times 978000 \\
Q_{req} &= 868.3 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Apply thermal efficiency to get the heat that must be input electrically.**

$$\eta = \frac{Q_{req}}{H_{input}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_{input} = \frac{Q_{req}}{\eta} = \frac{868.3}{0.7} \approx 1240.4 \text{ J}$$

**Step 6: If the welding time is 0.2 s and the faying resistance is $100 \,\mu\Omega$ (typical), find the required current.**

$$H_{input} = I^2 R t \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = \sqrt{\frac{H_{input}}{R \, t}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \sqrt{\frac{1240.4}{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1240.4}{2 \times 10^{-5}}} \\
&= \sqrt{6.202 \times 10^{7}} \\
I &\approx 7876 \text{ A} \approx 7.88 \text{ kA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Sanity Check:** For 2 mm mild steel, a current of **7–9 kA** is exactly what you would expect in a real shipyard spot welder. If your answer came out as 78 A or 78,000 A, you have a unit error.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electrode Force & Current from Empirical Rules

Given the same 2 mm mild steel plate, $t_s = 2 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1: Minimum required nugget diameter (KTU rule).**

$$d_n \geq 4 \sqrt{t_s} + 3 = 4 \sqrt{2} + 3 = 5.66 + 3 = 8.66 \text{ mm} \approx 9 \text{ mm}$$

We choose a practical value $d_n = 9 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 2: Required welding current.**

$$I = 1000 \cdot d_n = 1000 \times 9 = 9000 \text{ A} = 9 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 3: Required electrode force.**

$$F = 30 \cdot d_n = 30 \times 9 = 270 \text{ N}$$

Wait — this is too low. The KTU rule $k = 30 \text{ N/mm}$ applies to *nugget* force, not the actual pneumatic force. The **pneumatic/hydraulic electrode force** for 2 mm mild steel in shipbuilding is typically **2.5–4 kN**.
