The $F = k \cdot d_n$ gives a *minimum forging force on the nugget*, which is a different concept. KTU sometimes mixes these — always state your assumption.

**Step 4: Weld time.**

$$t_{weld} \approx \left(\frac{d_n}{4}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{9}{4}\right)^2 = 5.06 \text{ cycles (at 50 Hz)} \approx 0.10 \text{ s}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Balance and the "Lobe Curve" Concept

In a multi-pulse spot weld schedule (often used in shipbuilding for thicker plates), KTU expects you to understand the **weldability lobe** concept.

Let the heat input be parameterized by current $I$. Plot (a) the **minimum current** $I_{min}$ below which no weld forms, and (b) the **maximum current** $I_{max}$ above which expulsion occurs (metal spits out of the nugget due to over-melting). The enclosed region between $I_{min}$ and $I_{max}$ over weld time $t$ is called the **weldability lobe** (or weld-lobe curve).

For a given $I$, the safe weld time range is:

$$t_{min}(I) \leq t_{weld} \leq t_{max}(I)$$

Below $t_{min}$: no fusion (insufficient heat).
Above $t_{max}$: expulsion, porosity, cracks.

The analytical form of the lower bound is:

$$I_{min}^2 \cdot t_{min} = \text{constant} = \frac{Q_{req}}{\eta \cdot R_{faying}}$$

Because $H \propto I^2 t$ must reach $Q_{req}/\eta$, the lower bound is a hyperbola in the $I$–$t$ plane:

$$I_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q_{req}}{\eta \, R_{faying} \, t_{weld}}}$$

The upper bound is governed by the **expulsion threshold**, an empirical relation:

$$I_{max} \approx I_{min} + k_2 \, d_n$$

where $k_2$ is a material-dependent constant (≈ 2000 A/mm for low-carbon steel).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Connection:** The weld-lobe concept is heavily used in **projection welding** (a variant of spot welding) and in **automated robotic spot welding** in shipyards, where the schedule is set by trial and verified by lobe curves.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Spot Welding Process Parameters vs. Effects

| Parameter Increase | Nugget Size | Penetration | Expulsion Risk | Electrode Wear | Indentation |
|--------------------|-------------|-------------|----------------|----------------|-------------|
| ↑ Welding Current $I$ | ↑ increases | ↑ deeper | ↑↑ strongly ↑ | ↑ increases | ↑ increases |
| ↑ Weld Time $t$ | ↑ increases | ↑ deeper | ↑ increases | ↑ increases | ↑ increases |
| ↑ Electrode Force $F$ | ↓ decreases (faying $R$ ↓) | more uniform | ↓ decreases | ↓ decreases | ↓ decreases |
| ↑ Tip Diameter $D_e$ | ↓ current density ↓ | ↓ shallower | ↓ decreases | ↓ concentrated wear | ↓ reduces |
| ↑ Hold Time | no effect on size | helps cooling | reduces porosity | slight | reduces cracks |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Spot Welding Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transformer 440V or 11kV primary] --> B[Step-down to 6 to 24V secondary]
    B --> C[High current busbar]
    C --> D[Upper electrode holder]
    D --> E[Upper copper electrode water cooled]
    E --> F[Top workpiece sheet]
    F --> G[Faying interface highest resistance Rf]
    G --> H[Bottom workpiece sheet]
    H --> I[Lower copper electrode water cooled]
    I --> J[Lower electrode holder]
    J --> K[Return to transformer secondary]
    L[Electronic timer 50Hz sync] --> M[Squeeze phase 5 to 20 cycles]
    M --> N[Weld phase 6 to 20 cycles]
    N --> O[Hold phase 5 to 30 cycles]
    O --> P[Off phase cooling]
    Q[Water cooling chiller] --> E
    Q --> I
    R[Pneumatic or hydraulic ram] --> E
    S[Workpiece fixturing] --> F
    S --> H
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Four-Phase Spot Welding Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Tmr as Welding Timer
    participant Ram as Pneumatic Ram
    participant Current as Welding Current
    participant WP as Workpiece
    Tmr->>Ram: Squeeze ON 5-20 cycles
    Ram->>WP: Apply electrode force 2 to 25 kN
    Note over Ram,WP: Air gaps close, contact established
    Tmr->>Current: Weld ON 6 to 20 cycles
    Current->>WP: I rises to 3 to 100 kA
    Note over WP: H equals I squared R t, nugget forms
    Tmr->>Current: Weld OFF
    Tmr->>Ram: Hold ON 5 to 30 cycles
    Note over WP: Nugget solidifies under pressure
    Tmr->>Ram: Electrode lift, off
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Resistance Spot Welding Station

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Supply 3 phase 50Hz] --> B[Welding Transformer]
    B --> C[Thyristor or IGBT switch]
    C --> D[Electrodes via flexible shunt]
    D --> E[Workpiece held in fixture]
    F[PLC controller] --> G[Synchronous timer]
    G --> C
    F --> H[Pressure regulator]
    H --> I[Pneumatic cylinder or servo]
    I --> D
    J[Chiller unit 15 to 25 deg C] --> D
    K[Operator panel HMI] --> F
    L[Quality monitoring: Weld current, force, displacement] --> F
```

## 4.4 Schematic Text Description of the Spot Weld Cross-Section

```
   +--------------------------------------+
   |         Upper Copper Electrode       |  (water-cooled)
   +--------------------------------------+
                  |   |
            +=====+   +=====+
            |         R_sheet 1          |
            +=====+   +=====+
                  |   |
            ###### HEAT BUILDS UP ######  ← R_faying (highest)
                  |   |
            +=====+   +=====+
            |         R_sheet 2          |
            +=====+   +=====+
                  |   |
   +--------------------------------------+
   |         Lower Copper Electrode       |  (water-cooled)
   +--------------------------------------+
                    |
             [ Forging Pressure F ]
                    |
                 _____
                |     |
                |  N  |  ← Weld Nugget (solidified, disc-shaped)
                |_____|
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Short Answer) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define spot welding. State any two advantages of spot welding in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**
Spot welding is a resistance welding process in which two overlapping metal sheets are joined at localized points by the combined action of heat generated from a high-amperage low-voltage current passing through the interface, and forging pressure applied by water-cooled copper electrodes.

**Two advantages in shipbuilding:**
1. **High production rate** — A modern robotic spot welding station in a shipyard panel line can complete 30–60 welds/minute, ideal for serial production of bulkhead panels.
2. **No filler metal required** — Saves consumable cost and avoids metallurgical incompatibilities, particularly important for **aluminium superstructures** and **stainless steel** outfitting.
3. (Bonus) Easily automated with seam-track and seam-finding sensors for long panel runs.

---

### Question 2 (Short Answer) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the role of the four time phases in a spot welding cycle.**

**Model Answer:**
The four time phases of spot welding are:

1. **Squeeze Time** — The electrodes descend and apply full pressure before the current is switched on. This ensures proper contact and uniform resistance distribution.
2. **Weld Time** — The welding current is passed; heat is generated at the faying interface by Joule's law ($H = I^2 R t$), melting the metal and forming the nugget.
3. **Hold Time** — Current is OFF, but the electrode force is maintained. This allows the nugget to solidify under pressure, eliminating porosity, shrinkage cracks, and expulsion.
4. **Off Time** — Electrodes retract; the assembly is repositioned for the next spot.

The four phases are sequenced by a **synchronous electronic timer** synchronized to the 50 Hz mains frequency.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the principle of resistance spot welding with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of (i) current, (ii) electrode force, and (iii) weld time in producing a sound weld nugget. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Two sheets of 2 mm thick low-carbon steel are to be spot welded. Calculate the required welding current, electrode force, and weld time. Given: density $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_p = 480 \text{ J/kg·K}$, melting point $T_m = 1500^\circ C$, initial temp $25^\circ C$, latent heat of fusion $L = 270 \text{ kJ/kg}$, faying resistance $R_f = 100 \,\mu\Omega$, thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.70$, current constant $k_1 = 1000 \text{ A/mm}$, force constant $k_F = 30 \text{ N/mm}$, time constant based on $t = (d_n/4)^2$ s. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q A (a)

**Principle:** Spot welding is based on **Joule's Law of Heating**: when a high-amperage, low-voltage current is passed through the workpieces held under pressure between two water-cooled copper electrodes, heat is generated in proportion to $I^2 R t$. Because the highest resistance lies at the faying interface, the heat is concentrated there, melting the metal locally. The forging pressure from the electrodes consolidates the molten pool into a solid-state weld nugget.

**Diagram:** (See SECTION 4 for the standard schematic — should be drawn on the answer sheet showing upper electrode, lower electrode, workpieces, faying interface, and forging force arrow.)

**Role of parameters:**

(i) **Current $I$:** Governs the rate of heat generation by $I^2$. Too low → no fusion. Too high → expulsion of metal from the nugget. The optimum is on the **weld-lobe plateau**.

(ii) **Electrode Force $F$:** Ensures intimate contact at the faying surface, lowering the contact resistance, and provides forging pressure during solidification. Also reduces indentation, electrode sticking, and porosity.

(iii) **Weld Time $t$:** Determines the total heat input. Combined with current and resistance, it determines whether the nugget reaches the optimum size. Typical range 6–20 cycles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often describe the electrodes as "heating" the metal. They don't — the metal heats *itself* by its own electrical resistance. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this is misstated. Also, do not write "the resistance is constant" — in spot welding, resistance is *dynamic* (changes with temperature and pressure).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q A (b)

**Step 1: Determine the minimum nugget diameter.**

$$d_n = 4 \sqrt{t_s} + 3 = 4 \sqrt{2} + 3 = 5.66 + 3 = 8.66 \text{ mm}$$

Choose $d_n = 9 \text{ mm}$ (engineering round-up).

**Step 2: Welding current.**

$$I = k_1 \cdot d_n = 1000 \times 9 = 9000 \text{ A} = 9 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 3: Electrode force.**

$$F = k_F \cdot d_n = 30 \times 9 = 270 \text{ N} = 0.27 \text{ kN}$$

(Note: KTU expects this *minimum forging force on the nugget*. Actual pneumatic force is higher — typically 2–4 kN for 2 mm steel.)

**Step 4: Weld time.**

$$t = \left(\frac{d_n}{4}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{9}{4}\right)^2 = 2.25^2 = 5.06 \text{ s}$$

This is unphysically long — the formula actually gives **cycles** (periods of 50 Hz mains). Corrected:

$$t = 5.06 \text{ cycles} = \frac{5.06}{50} = 0.10 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Sanity check via Joule's law.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{input} &= \frac{H_{nugget}}{\eta} \\
H_{nugget} &= m \cdot [C_p \Delta T + L] \\
m &= \rho \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_n^2 \cdot (2 t_s) \\
&= 7850 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.009)^2 \times 0.004 \\
&= 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} \\
H_{nugget} &= 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times [480 \times 1475 + 270000] \\
&= 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 978000 = 1956 \text{ J} \\
H_{input} &= 1956 / 0.7 = 2794 \text{ J} \\
\text{Check: } I^2 R t &= (9000)^2 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.10 = 810 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

The check shows our chosen $I$ is insufficient. To meet the heat requirement, increase $I$ to ≈ 16 kA, or extend $t$ to ≈ 0.30 s. This is the kind of **iteration** KTU examiners reward.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the various types of electrodes used in spot welding. Explain why copper alloys are preferred and what is meant by the term "electrode dressing". **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A robotic spot welding station is to be designed for 1.5 mm thick galvanised steel ship panels. Estimate the (i) welding current, (ii) electrode force, (iii) weld time, and (iv) the heat input per spot. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q B (a)

**Types of spot welding electrodes** (by shape):

1. **Pointed / Domed Electrode** — for normal structural welds. Tip diameter 5–8 mm. Most common.
2. **Flat / Truncated Electrode** — for low-current-density welds on thin sheets or coated steels.
3. **Offset / Bent Electrode** — for reaching into corners, e.g., stiffener-to-shell joints.
4. **Radius Electrode** — for cylindrical work (e.g., pipe joining).
5. **Back-up Electrode** — a stationary lower support for hand-held or single-side welding.

**Why copper alloys are preferred:**
- **High electrical conductivity** → minimal $I^2 R$ heating in the electrode itself.
- **High thermal conductivity** → rapid heat removal from the electrode tip.
- **Adequate mechanical strength** at the elevated operating temperature, especially when alloyed with **chromium, zirconium, or beryllium** (RWMA Class 2/3 alloys).
- **Resistance to mushrooming and sticking** to the workpiece.

**Electrode Dressing:** This is the periodic reshaping of the electrode tip — usually by **dressing wheels with cutter blades** or by **automatic tip dressers (ATD)** in robotic cells. The purpose is to:
- Restore the original tip geometry (which deforms by mushrooming and wear).
- Remove alloying/adhered metal from the tip face.
- Maintain consistent current density, indentation profile, and nugget size.

In a shipyard robotic cell, an ATD is invoked every 50–200 welds to keep the cell running within tolerance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "copper" without specifying the *alloy*. Pure copper is too soft and would mushroom quickly. Examiners expect **Class 2 Chromium-Copper** (RWMA standard) or equivalent.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q B (b)

**Assumptions:**
- Low-carbon steel, $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_p = 480 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $L = 270 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $T_m = 1500^\circ C$, $T_0 = 25^\circ C$.
- Galvanised coating may be ignored (or note that current must be increased by ~15% to break the coating).
- $R_f = 120 \,\mu\Omega$ (slightly higher for galvanised — zinc is resistive).
- $\eta = 0.70$.

**Step 1: Nugget diameter.**

$$d_n = 4 \sqrt{t_s} + 3 = 4 \sqrt{1.5} + 3 = 4.90 + 3 = 7.90 \text{ mm} \approx 8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Welding current.**

$$I = 1000 \cdot d_n = 1000 \times 8 = 8 \text{ kA}$$

(For galvanised, increase by ~15% → ~9.2 kA.)

**Step 3: Electrode force.**

$$F = 30 \cdot d_n = 30 \times 8 = 240 \text{ N} = 0.24 \text{ kN}$$

(Actual pneumatic: 1.8–2.5 kN for 1.5 mm steel.)

**Step 4: Weld time.**

$$t = (d_n / 4)^2 = (8/4)^2 = 4 \text{ cycles} = 4/50 = 0.08 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Heat input per spot.**

$$H_{input} = I^2 R t = (8000)^2 \times 120 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.08 = 614.4 \text{ J}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** This is a typical "shipyard panel" weld — under 1 kJ, completed in 4 mains cycles, with a current of about 8 kA. A single panel of 30 welds uses about 18 kJ of heat energy.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mark-Loss Areas in Spot Welding Answers:**
> 1. **Mixing up the "constant" formulas** — $d_n = 4\sqrt{t}+3$ is for *minimum* nugget; $d_n = 5\sqrt{t}$ is the *average*; students often quote the wrong one.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mm to m in volume and mass** — this is the single biggest arithmetic error.
> 3. **Treating copper electrodes as a heat source** — they are heat *sinks* (water-cooled), not sources.
> 4. **Ignoring the difference between *forging force on the nugget* and *pneumatic electrode force*.**
> 5. **Omitting the four-phase cycle (squeeze/weld/hold/off)** in part (a) answers — this is a 2-mark item that is consistently missed.
> 6. **Not stating the assumption of "minimum nugget diameter"** before applying empirical formulas.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (final-minute KTU prep):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Spot welding = resistance welding + localized point + forging pressure. Heat source = $I^2 R t$ (Joule's Law).
- ✅ **Voltage:** Low (6–24 V); **Current:** Very high (3–100 kA).
- ✅ **Electrodes:** Water-cooled, copper alloy (Class 2 RWMA), *not* a heat source.
- ✅ **Four Phases:** **S**queeze → **W**eld → **H**old → **O**ff. (Mnemonic: **S**eagull **W**ith **H**ard **O**bject.)
- ✅ **Heat is concentrated at $R_{faying}$** — the interface between the two sheets — because it's the largest resistance in the stack.
- ✅ **Minimum Nugget:** $d_n \geq 4\sqrt{t} + 3$ (mm).
- ✅ **Current:** $I \approx 1000 \cdot d_n$ (A).
- ✅ **Force:** $F \approx 30 \cdot d_n$ (N) — forging force on nugget.
- ✅ **Weld time:** $t = (d_n / 4)^2$ **cycles** at 50 Hz, divide by 50 for seconds.
- ✅ **Defects to know:** *Expulsion* (too much heat), *Porosity* (insufficient hold), *Cold weld* (insufficient heat), *Indentation* (excessive force), *Sticking* (excessive heat + low force).
- ✅ **Shipbuilding relevance:** Bulkhead panels, superstructure skin, aluminium outfitting, deck panels.
- ✅ **Efficiency:** Typically 50–80% — heat is lost to water cooling, to the surrounding metal, and by conduction away from the nugget.
- ✅ **Weld-lobe concept:** Plot $I$ vs $t$; the enclosed region is the safe welding window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line KTU Exam Mantra for Spot Welding:**
> *"Heat by Joule's Law, Force by Pneumatics, Time by Timer, Cool by Water, Fuse by Forging."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seam Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Seam Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Seam Welding — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Seam Welding** is a *resistance projection welding* process in which **two overlapping sheet metals are joined progressively along a continuous linear joint** by passing an electric current through the interface while **rotating copper alloy wheel electrodes** apply continuous forge pressure. The localised Joule heat ($\text{H} = \text{I}^2\text{Rt}$) generated at the faying surfaces causes a series of overlapping weld nuggets, producing a **leak-tight, gas- or liquid-proof joint** without the use of filler metal or shielding gas.

In the KTU 2024 framework, seam welding is positioned as a critical **mass-production resistance welding variant**, especially relevant to shipbuilding for fabricating **longitudinal bulkhead seams, deck panel joints, oil-tank baffles, and thin-shell plating** where corrosion-tight integrity is mandated by classification societies (IRS, DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rolling Pin & Hot Iron" Analogy

Imagine a tailor pressing two pieces of cloth together with a hot iron. Now replace the iron with **two rotating copper wheels** (like a pasta-machine roller) and heat the cloth with electricity instead of a hot plate. The wheels roll along the seam, squeezing and heating simultaneously, leaving behind a sealed stitch of fused metal. The "stitch" is actually a **chain of overlapping weld spots** — much like a sewing machine, except each "stitch" is a microscopic forge weld.

## 1.3 Position in the Shipbuilding Joining Hierarchy

| Process Family | Sub-Category | Position of Seam Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Fastening | Riveting / Bolting | Complementary — replaces rivets in **thin plates ≤ 3 mm** |
| Fusion Welding | SMAW / GMAW / GTAW | Alternative — no filler, no shielding, **higher speed on long seams** |
| **Resistance Welding** | Spot, Projection, **Seam** | **Seam welding = high-speed continuous spot welding** |
| Solid-State Welding | Friction Stir | For non-ferrous, aluminum hulls |

## 1.4 Classification-Society Acceptance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For shipbuilding, **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** members accept seam-welded joints in **secondary structural members** such as:
> - Engine-room bulkhead panels
> - Lubricating-oil tank internal baffles
> - Ventilation ducting and casings
> - Non-load-bearing stainless internal fittings
>
> **Primary hull-girder seams** still demand **arc welding (typically FCAW/GMAW)** because of impact-toughness requirements at service temperatures (typically **$-10\,^\circ\text{C}$** for North Sea, **$-30\,^\circ\text{C}$** for Arctic-class vessels).

## 1.5 Historical Context (Syllabus Note)

Seam welding dates back to **Elihu Thomson's 1885 patent**. The first dedicated **seam-welding machine** was produced by the **Thomson-Houston Electric Company** in 1897. In shipyards, the process gained prominence post-**1940s** for fabricating **welded-steel barges and LST (Landing Ship, Tank)** hulls, where mass production of identical panel modules required consistent, leak-proof longitudinal seams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Three Pillars of Seam Welding

The process is governed by **three simultaneous, mutually dependent actions** that must be balanced for a defect-free joint:

1. **Electrical Action (Heating Phase):**
   - A low-voltage, high-amperage AC current (typically **2–10 V**, **5,000–30,000 A**) is forced through the stacked sheets via the rotating wheel electrodes.
   - Resistance is highest at the **faying surface** (contact resistance between sheets) → localised Joule heating.

2. **Mechanical Action (Forging Phase):**
   - The wheels apply a **continuous static force** (typically **2,000–10,000 N** depending on sheet thickness) via spring or pneumatic-hydraulic actuation.
   - This force maintains intimate contact and consolidates the molten nugget during solidification.

3. **Thermal Action (Cooling Phase):**
   - Heat-affected zone (HAZ) is rapidly quenched by **water-cooled copper alloy wheels** (Cu-Cr, Cu-Zr, or Cu-Be alloys).
   - Cooling rate typically **$10^3$–$10^4\,^\circ\text{C/s}$** in the weld nugget.

## 2.2 Process Variables (The "Five Knobs" of Seam Welding)

| Variable | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect of Increase |
|---|---|---|---|
| Welding Current | $I$ | 5,000–30,000 A | More heat → larger nugget, possible expulsion |
| Welding Time (per spot) | $t$ | 0.02–0.5 s | More heat → deeper fusion |
| Electrode Force | $F$ | 2–10 kN | Lower contact resistance, but expels molten metal |
| Welding Speed | $v$ | 0.5–10 m/min | Fewer overlaps → may become roll-spot |
| Electrode Wheel Diameter | $D$ | 100–600 mm | Determines pressure distribution and current density |

## 2.3 Heat Generation in Seam Welding — The Physical Model

The **fundamental heat-generation equation** is identical to spot welding (Joule's First Law):

$$H = I^2 R t$$

Where:
- $H$ = total Joule heat generated (Joules, J)
- $I$ = RMS welding current (Amperes, A)
- $R$ = **dynamic** interface resistance (Ohms, $\Omega$)
- $t$ = time current flows through a given point (seconds, s)

However, in seam welding, the **resistance $R$ is time-dependent** because:

$$R(t) = R_{s0} + R_{c}(t) + R_{b}(t)$$

Where:
- $R_{s0}$ = **bulk sheet resistance** (constant, depends on resistivity $\rho$ and sheet thickness $s$)
- $R_{c}(t)$ = **contact resistance at the faying surface** (decreases as asperities flatten)
- $R_{b}(t)$ = **bulk resistance of the heated zone** (increases as temperature rises, since $\rho$ of steel increases with T)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Examinations:** You are expected to state that the **faying-surface contact resistance $R_{c}$** is the **dominant contributor (≈ 80–90 %)** to total heat at the start of the cycle, and this is what differentiates resistance welding from arc welding — the heat is generated **internally at the interface**, not at an external arc.

## 2.4 Weld Pitch (Overlap Geometry) — Critical for Continuity

The **weld pitch $p$** is the centre-to-centre spacing between successive nuggets:

$$p = v \cdot t_{spot}$$

Where:
- $p$ = pitch (mm)
- $v$ = travel speed of wheel (mm/s)
- $t_{spot}$ = time current flows at one location (s)

**Overlap ratio** is defined as:

$$\text{Overlap} = \frac{(d_{n} - p)}{d_{n}} \times 100\,\%$$

Where $d_{n}$ = nugget diameter (typically $3\sqrt{s}$ for low-carbon steel, $s$ in mm).

> For a **continuous leak-tight seam**, overlap must be **≥ 15–20 %**. If overlap is negative, individual spots appear — this is then called **Roll-Spot Welding** (intermittent, not sealed).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = I^2 R t$ | Joule heat generated | W·s = J |
| 2 | $R_{tot} = R_{s} + 2R_{c,w} + R_{c,f}$ | Total resistance: sheet + wheel contact + faying contact | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A_{c}}$ | Current density at the wheel–workpiece contact | A/mm² |
| 4 | $v = \pi D N$ | Linear travel speed from wheel rotation (D = wheel diameter, N = rpm) | mm/s |
| 5 | $p = v \cdot t_{spot}$ | Weld pitch (centre-to-centre distance between nuggets) | mm |
| 6 | $\text{Overlap} = \dfrac{d_{n} - p}{d_{n}}$ | Fractional overlap for continuous seal | dimensionless |
| 7 | $d_{n} \approx 3\sqrt{s}$ | Empirical nugget diameter (low-carbon steel) | mm |
| 8 | $H_{f} = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_{f}$ | Energy required to melt the nugget (sensible + latent) | J |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{H_{useful}}{H_{input}}$ | Thermal efficiency (typically 0.50–0.80) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $F = p_{c} \cdot A_{c}$ | Electrode force = contact pressure × contact area | N |
| 11 | $\rho(T) = \rho_{20}\left[1 + \alpha (T - 20)\right]$ | Temperature-dependent resistivity of steel | $\Omega\cdot$m |
| 12 | $W = \int_{0}^{t} I^{2}(t') R(t')\, dt'$ | Total electrical work input (integral form) | J |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Shipbuilding

> [!NOTE]
> In a **modern Korean/European shipyard**, a single **longitudinal-panel seam welder** can join **8–12 m of $\text{1.5 mm}$ mild-steel overlap per minute**. For a **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)** requiring thousands of metres of internal bulkhead seams, this is a **productivity multiplier of ≈ 4–6×** compared to manual MIG/MAG. However, the equipment is **only economically viable for batch production of identical panels**, hence the move toward modular shipbuilding in the 2020s.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Generated in One Nugget of a Continuous Seam

**Statement:** Derive the expression for total heat delivered to a single nugget of length $p$ (the pitch) in a continuous seam welding operation, given current waveform $i(t) = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$ and dynamic resistance $R(t)$.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous heat generation rate:**
The instantaneous electrical power dissipated as heat at the interface is:

$$P(t) = i^{2}(t) \cdot R(t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the sinusoidal current model:**
With $i(t) = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$:

$$P(t) = I_{0}^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \cdot R(t)$$

**Step 3 — Use the trigonometric identity $\sin^{2}(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:**

$$P(t) = \frac{I_{0}^{2} R(t)}{2}\left[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the time the current flows at a single point (one half-cycle of conduction $t_{spot}$):**

$$H_{nugget} = \int_{0}^{t_{spot}} \frac{I_{0}^{2} R(t)}{2}\left[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\right] dt$$

**Step 5 — Assume quasi-steady $R(t) \approx \bar{R}$ over the short interval (common KTU assumption):**

$$H_{nugget} \approx \frac{I_{0}^{2} \bar{R}}{2} \int_{0}^{t_{spot}} \left[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\right] dt$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{t_{spot}} \left[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\right] dt = \left[t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega}\right]_{0}^{t_{spot}} = t_{spot} - \frac{\sin(2\omega t_{spot})}{2\omega}$$

**Step 7 — Final nugget heat expression:**

$$\boxed{H_{nugget} = \frac{I_{0}^{2} \bar{R}}{2}\left[t_{spot} - \frac{\sin(2\omega t_{spot})}{2\omega}\right]}$$

> For **50 Hz mains**, $\omega = 100\pi$ rad/s. If $t_{spot}$ is several cycles (e.g., 0.1 s → 5 cycles), the sinusoidal term averages to zero, and the expression reduces to the familiar $H = I_{RMS}^{2} R t$ with $I_{RMS} = I_0 / \sqrt{2}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Weld Pitch from Wheel Kinematics

**Statement:** A copper wheel electrode of diameter $D = 200\,\text{mm}$ rotates at $N = 120\,\text{rpm}$. The weld timer holds current ON for 3 full cycles of a 50 Hz supply. Calculate (a) the linear travel speed, (b) the weld pitch, and (c) the overlap ratio if the nugget diameter is $8\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to rad/s:**

$$\omega = 2\pi N / 60 = 2\pi (120) / 60 = 4\pi \;\text{rad/s} \approx 12.566\;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Linear travel speed $v$:**

$$v = \pi D N = \pi (0.200\;\text{m})(120/60\;\text{s}^{-1}) = \pi (0.200)(2.0)$$

$$v = 1.2566\;\text{m/s} = 1256.6\;\text{mm/s}$$

**Step 3 — Time per spot $t_{spot}$ (3 cycles of 50 Hz):**

$$t_{spot} = 3 \times \frac{1}{50} = 0.060\;\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Weld pitch $p$:**

$$p = v \cdot t_{spot} = 1256.6 \times 0.060 = 75.4\;\text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Wait — this gives a huge gap. Recheck.** For a continuous seam, $p$ must be much less than nugget diameter. So either $N$ is reduced, or $t_{spot}$ is reduced. Let's use the *typical industrial* $t_{spot} = 0.02\;\text{s}$ (1 cycle):

$$p = 1256.6 \times 0.02 = 25.1\;\text{mm}$$

**Step 6 — Overlap with nugget $d_n = 8$ mm:**

$$\text{Overlap} = \frac{8 - 25.1}{8} \times 100\%$$

This is **negative** — meaning the spots are *spaced apart* and the seam is **not continuous**. To get overlap, the wheel must slow down. Setting $p = 6$ mm:

$$v = p / t_{spot} = 6 / 0.02 = 300\;\text{mm/s} = 0.30\;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 7 — Required wheel rpm:**

$$N = \frac{v}{\pi D} = \frac{0.30}{\pi (0.200)} = 0.477\;\text{rev/s} = 28.6\;\text{rpm}$$

**Step 8 — Final overlap:**

$$\text{Overlap} = \frac{8 - 6}{8} = 0.25 = 25\% \;(> 15\% \Rightarrow \text{leak-tight} \;\checkmark)$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Current Density at the Wheel–Workpiece Contact

**Statement:** A wheel electrode of face width $w = 8\;\text{mm}$ and radius $r = 100\;\text{mm}$ contacts a flat sheet. If the welding current is $I = 12{,}000\;\text{A}$, find the apparent contact-area current density.

**Step 1 — Apparent contact area (rectangular approximation for small contact patch):**

$$A_{c,app} = w \cdot b$$

Where $b$ is the longitudinal chord of contact. For a wheel on a flat surface, the Hertzian contact patch is approximately:

$$b \approx 2\sqrt{r \cdot \delta}$$

with $\delta$ = elastic indentation depth. For a typical copper-on-steel setup under $F = 4{,}000\;\text{N}$:

$$\delta \approx 0.05\;\text{mm} \Rightarrow b \approx 2\sqrt{(100)(0.05)} \approx 2\sqrt{5} \approx 4.47\;\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apparent contact area:**

$$A_{c,app} = 8 \times 4.47 = 35.8\;\text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apparent current density:**

$$J = \frac{I}{A_{c,app}} = \frac{12{,}000}{35.8} = 335\;\text{A/mm}^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **engineering** current density. The **true** (a-spot) current density across the metallic micro-contacts is **10²–10³ times higher** because current funnels through a few asperities. This high local current density is what initiates melting at the faying surface.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Required to Form a Nugget

**Statement:** Find the minimum electrical energy needed to form a nugget of diameter $d_n = 6\;\text{mm}$ in two $s = 1.2\;\text{mm}$ low-carbon steel sheets. Use $\rho_{steel} = 7850\;\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $c_p = 670\;\text{J/kg·K}$, $L_f = 247{,}000\;\text{J/kg}$, melting point $T_m = 1500\,^\circ\text{C}$, ambient $T_0 = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$.

**Step 1 — Nugget volume (cylindrical approximation):**

$$V_{n} = \pi \left(\frac{d_n}{2}\right)^{2} \cdot (2s) = \pi (3)^{2}(2.4) = 67.86\;\text{mm}^{3} = 6.786 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Nugget mass:**

$$m = \rho V_n = 7850 \times 6.786 \times 10^{-8} = 5.327 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat (to raise to melting point):**

$$Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = (5.327 \times 10^{-4})(670)(1500 - 25)$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 5.327 \times 10^{-4} \times 670 \times 1475 = 526.4\;\text{J}$$

**Step 4 — Latent heat of fusion:**

$$Q_{latent} = m \cdot L_f = (5.327 \times 10^{-4})(247{,}000) = 131.6\;\text{J}$$

**Step 5 — Total heat required in the nugget:**

$$H_{req} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent} = 526.4 + 131.6 = 658.0\;\text{J}$$

**Step 6 — With thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.65$ (typical for seam welding):**

$$H_{input} = \frac{H_{req}}{\eta} = \frac{658.0}{0.65} = 1012\;\text{J}$$

This is the **electrical energy budget** for one nugget. For a continuous seam at $v = 0.3\;\text{m/s}$ and $p = 6\;\text{mm}$, the **nugget rate** is $0.3/0.006 = 50$ nuggets/s, so the **machine must deliver** ≈ **50.6 kW** continuously to the work.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Seam Welding vs Alternatives

| Criterion | Seam Welding | Spot Welding | MIG/MAG Arc | Friction Stir |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Production rate (m/min) | **2–10** | 0.5–2 (manual) | 0.3–1.0 | 0.5–2.0 |
| Leak-tightness | **Excellent** | Poor (gaps between spots) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Sheet thickness range (mm) | **0.5–3.0** | 0.5–6.0 | 1.0–25 | 1.0–50 |
| Equipment cost | High (₹40L–1Cr) | Low–Medium | Medium | Very High |
| Skill required | Medium | Low | High | Very High |
| Shipbuilding suitability | Bulkheads, panels | Small brackets | **Primary hull** | Aluminum superstructure |
| Classification society acceptance (primary hull) | No | No | **Yes** | Limited |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Seam Welding Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3 Phase Mains 415V 50Hz] --> B[Power Contactor and Thyristor Stack]
    B --> C[Welding Transformer 415V to 2 to 10V]
    C --> D[Secondary Busbars Flexible Copper]
    D --> E[Upper Wheel Electrode Cu-Cr Alloy]
    D --> F[Lower Wheel Electrode Cu-Cr Alloy]
    E --> G[Top Sheet Workpiece]
    F --> H[Bottom Sheet Workpiece]
    G --> I[Faying Surface Contact Zone]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Rotating Weld Head Assembly]
    J --> K[Servo Motor and Gearbox Drive]
    K --> L[Encoder for Speed Feedback]
    L --> M[PLC and Timer Module]
    M --> N[Pressure Cylinder Hydraulic Pneumatic]
    N --> O[Force Sensor Load Cell]
    O --> J
    M --> B
    O --> M
    M --> P[Water Cooling Manifold]
    P --> E
    P --> F
    P --> C
    Q[Operator HMI Panel] --> M
    M --> R[Welded Continuous Seam Output]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process-Flow Diagram — Seam Welding Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Cycle] --> S2[Load Overlapping Sheets onto Mandrel]
    S2 --> S3[Lower Wheel to Set Force F]
    S3 --> S4[Close Contactor and Apply Current I]
    S4 --> S5[Heat Builds at Faying Surface I2R t]
    S5 --> S6{Is Temperature above Melting Point}
    S6 --> S7[Yes Form Nugget and Apply Forge Pressure]
    S6 --> S8[No Hold for Additional Half Cycle]
    S7 --> S9[Open Contactor Wheel Rotates Forward]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Water Cools Weld Zone Below Solidus]
    S10 --> S11{Reached End of Seam}
    S11 --> S12[Yes Raise Wheel and Unload]
    S11 --> S13[No Return to S4 for Next Nugget]
    S12 --> S14[Stop Cycle]
    S13 --> S4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Defect-Cause-Effect Tree (Failure Mode Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Seam Weld Defect Detected] --> D2{Type of Defect}
    D2 --> D3[Porosity Voids in Nugget]
    D2 --> D4[Cracks Longitudinal or Transverse]
    D2 --> D5[Insufficient Penetration]
    D2 --> D6[Surface Expulsion Splash]
    D2 --> D7[Electrode Pickup and Sticking]

    D3 --> D3A[Cause Dirty or Rusted Sheet Surface]
    D3 --> D3B[Cause Inadequate Forge Pressure]
    D3 --> D3C[Cause Excessive Current with Short Time]

    D4 --> D4A[Cause Rapid Cooling from Cu Wheels]
    D4 --> D4B[Cause High Carbon or Sulphur Content]
    D4 --> D4C[Cause Excessive Forge Force on Thin Sheet]

    D5 --> D5A[Cause Too Low Current]
    D5 --> D5B[Cause Too High Travel Speed]
    D5 --> D5C[Cause Dirty Contact on Wheel Face]

    D6 --> D6A[Cause Excessive Current Density]
    D6 --> D6B[Cause Insufficient Electrode Force]
    D6 --> D6C[Cause Mismatched Sheet Thickness]

    D7 --> D7A[Cause Wheel Alloy Softening at High T]
    D7 --> D7B[Cause Insufficient Cooling Water Flow]
    D7 --> D7C[Cause Excessive Contact Time]
```

flowchart LR
    P1[Coil Stock Uncoiler] --> P2[Shear and Leveller]
