For physical section views of a wheel electrode and contact zone, consult your workshop lab manual — KTU practical examinations typically show a labelled schematic on the answer sheet for reference.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define seam welding. List any two applications in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Seam welding is a resistance welding process in which a continuous, leak-tight joint is produced between two overlapping sheets by passing a high-amperage low-voltage electric current through the joint while rotating copper-alloy wheel electrodes apply continuous forge pressure. The localised Joule heat ($H = I^2 R t$) at the faying surface forms a series of overlapping nuggets, producing a sealed seam.

**Two applications (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
1. **Longitudinal seams of engine-room bulkhead panels** (typically 1.0–1.6 mm mild steel) — providing leak-tight compartments for oily-bilge segregation.
2. **Stainless-steel internal fittings** such as galley counters, ventilation casings, and potable-water tank baffles where corrosion resistance and hygiene are critical.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between roll-spot welding and continuous seam welding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Roll-Spot Welding | Continuous Seam Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Current timing | Intermittent — current OFF between spots | **Continuous** OR very tightly pulsed with high overlap |
| Overlap ratio | **Negative** (clear gaps between spots) | **≥ 15–20 %** (overlapping nuggets) |
| Resulting joint | Series of **discrete, spaced** weld spots | **Leak-tight continuous seam** |
| Application | Stiffeners, brackets (non-sealed) | **Sealed panels, tanks, ducting** |
| Welding speed | Higher (less heat input) | Lower (to ensure overlap) |

(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for the application/visual distinction.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Seam Welding:**
> - **Common pitfall #1:** Students write "seam welding uses an arc" — this is **WRONG**. Seam welding is a **resistance welding** process; **no arc** is struck. You will lose 1 mark immediately for this error.
> - **Common pitfall #2:** Confusing **faying-surface resistance** with **bulk resistance**. Examiners specifically test whether you understand that **~90 % of heat** is generated at the **sheet-to-sheet interface**, not at the wheel contact.
> - **Common pitfall #3:** Stating "current is DC" — standard industrial seam welders use **50 Hz AC (single-phase)** or **MFDC (1–10 kHz inverter)**. Examiners award credit for correct current-type mention.
> - **Common pitfall #4:** Forgetting to convert units (e.g., mm vs m, A vs kA) in numericals — leads to **wrong answer by factor 1000**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **principle of operation, equipment layout, and process sequence** of resistance seam welding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A seam welding machine is used to fabricate **1.5 mm thick low-carbon-steel bulkhead panels**. The wheel electrodes have a diameter of **$250\,\text{mm}$** and rotate at **$40\,\text{rpm}$**. The weld timer maintains current flow for **2 cycles of a 50 Hz supply**. If the desired **nugget diameter is $7\,\text{mm}$** and a **continuous leak-tight seam (25 % overlap)** is required, calculate:

  (i) Linear travel speed of the wheel
  (ii) Weld pitch and travel speed required for the specified overlap
  (iii) Number of nuggets produced per metre of seam length
  (iv) Comment on the suitability of this process for a **primary hull-girder longitudinal seam of a 300 m bulk carrier**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Principle, Equipment, Sequence (7 marks):**

*Principle (2 marks):* Seam welding operates on **Joule's heating principle**. A low-voltage (2–10 V), high-amperage (5–30 kA) AC current is passed through two overlapping sheets held between two **rotating water-cooled copper-alloy wheel electrodes**. Resistance is maximum at the faying surface where the sheets meet. Joule heat $H = I^2 R t$ melts the interface locally, and forge pressure consolidates the molten metal into a nugget. As the wheels rotate, successive overlapping nuggets form a continuous sealed joint.

*Equipment (2 marks):* The major sub-assemblies are: **(1)** Welding transformer (step-down, multi-tap), **(2)** Secondary busbars (water-cooled flexible copper braids), **(3)** Upper and lower **wheel electrodes** (Cu-Cr or Cu-Zr alloy, internally water-cooled), **(4)** Drive system (servo motor + gearbox for wheel rotation), **(5)** Pressure system (pneumatic or hydraulic ram with load cell), **(6)** Control system (PLC, SCR/thyristor contactor, weld timer, current monitor), **(7)** Cooling system (recirculating chiller at 15–20 °C), **(8)** HMI panel for parameter entry.

*Process Sequence (2 marks):* Step 1: Sheets are loaded and aligned with overlap. Step 2: Wheels descend and apply set force. Step 3: Contactor closes; current flows for a preset number of cycles. Step 4: Heat builds at the faying surface; asperities flatten; bulk resistance rises. Step 5: Nugget forms at melting point (~1500 °C for steel). Step 6: Contactor opens; wheel rotates to next position; water-cooled wheel quenches the nugget. Step 7: Cycle repeats with overlap to produce a continuous seam. Step 8: At seam end, the wheel lifts; panel is unloaded for inspection.

*Schematic (1 mark):* Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes; in the exam, a hand-drawn schematic showing transformer → busbars → wheels → work with arrows indicating current path and wheel rotation earns full credit.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

*Given:* $D = 250\;\text{mm} = 0.250\;\text{m}$, $N = 40\;\text{rpm}$, $f = 50\;\text{Hz}$, cycles $= 2$, $d_n = 7\;\text{mm}$, overlap $= 25\%$.

**(i) Linear travel speed (2 marks):**

$$v = \pi D N = \pi (0.250)\left(\frac{40}{60}\right) = \pi (0.250)(0.6667) = 0.5236\;\text{m/s} = 523.6\;\text{mm/s}$$

**(ii) Weld pitch for 25 % overlap and required speed (3 marks):**

For 25 % overlap: $p = d_n (1 - 0.25) = 7 \times 0.75 = 5.25\;\text{mm}$

Spot time: $t_{spot} = 2 / 50 = 0.040\;\text{s}$

Required speed for this pitch:

$$v_{req} = \frac{p}{t_{spot}} = \frac{5.25}{0.040} = 131.25\;\text{mm/s} = 0.131\;\text{m/s}$$

Since this is **less than the actual wheel speed of 523.6 mm/s**, the wheel must be **slowed down**. Required rpm:

$$N_{req} = \frac{v_{req}}{\pi D} = \frac{0.131}{\pi (0.250)} = 0.167\;\text{rev/s} = 10.0\;\text{rpm}$$

**Conclusion:** To achieve the specified overlap, the wheel must rotate at **≈ 10 rpm** (not 40 rpm). At 40 rpm, the seam would be **discontinuous (roll-spot)**.

**(iii) Nuggets per metre (1 mark):**

$$n = \frac{1000\;\text{mm/m}}{p} = \frac{1000}{5.25} = 190.5\;\text{nuggets/m} \approx 190\;\text{nuggets/m}$$

**(iv) Suitability for primary hull-girder seam (1 mark):**

**Not suitable.** A 300 m bulk-carrier's primary hull-girder longitudinal seam must withstand **longitudinal hull-girder bending stresses, slamming loads, and $-10\,^\circ\text{C}$ Charpy impact requirements**. Seam welding produces a **fine-grained but rapidly quenched HAZ** susceptible to **hydrogen-induced cracking** in thicker plates, and the **process is limited to ≈ 3 mm sheet thickness**, far below the 15–40 mm hull plate used in primary structure. Classification societies (IACS) require **arc welding (FCAW/GMAW)** with documented WPS/PQR for primary hull. Seam welding is acceptable only for **secondary bulkhead panels** of this vessel.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal Choice — KTU 2024 Style]

**(a)** Explain the **classification of seam welding processes** based on current-flow timing, with neat sketches of waveforms. Compare **continuous-current seam welding**, **interrupted-current seam welding**, and **high-frequency (HF) seam welding**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a seam welding trial on **1.2 mm mild steel**, the following parameters are recorded: welding current $I = 10{,}000\;\text{A}$ (RMS), interface resistance $\bar{R} = 120\;\mu\Omega$, spot time $t_{spot} = 0.04\;\text{s}$, nugget mass $m = 5.3 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{kg}$, nugget volume $V_n = 6.8 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{m}^{3}$, $\rho = 7850\;\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $c_p = 670\;\text{J/kg·K}$, $L_f = 247\;\text{kJ/kg}$, $T_m = 1500\,^\circ\text{C}$, $T_0 = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$.

  Calculate: **(i)** Joule heat delivered per nugget, **(ii)** heat required to raise the nugget to melting point and fully melt it, **(iii)** thermal efficiency of the process, and **(iv)** identify **two specific defects** that could occur if current is increased by 30 % while keeping time constant. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Classification (7 marks):**

Seam welding processes are classified by **current-timing strategy** into the following:

**1. Continuous-Current Seam Welding (1.5 marks):**
Current flows **uninterruptedly** while the wheel rotates. Each new portion of the work is heated as it passes under the electrode. Used for **thin gauges (≤ 1 mm)** and high speeds. Waveform: pure sinusoid without gaps.

*Sketch (label only, no markdown):* Sine wave → full continuous, no off-period.

**2. Interrupted-Current (Pulsation) Seam Welding (1.5 marks):**
Current is **switched ON and OFF** in synchronisation with wheel rotation. The timer fires current only when a "new" position is under the wheel. Allows time for cooling between nuggets → **better control of HAZ** for medium-thickness sheets. This is the **most common industrial variant**.

*Sketch:* Sinusoid with periodic off-gaps aligned to wheel pitch.

**3. High-Frequency (HF) Seam Welding (2 marks):**
Uses **MFDC (1–10 kHz) or HF (100–400 kHz)** current. The skin effect confines current to the **surface of the wheel** and to the **near-surface of the workpiece**, producing **very shallow, narrow heat zones**. Used for **stainless steel tubes, automotive exhausts, and small-diameter piping** in shipyard utility lines. Advantage: minimal HAZ, no discoloration of stainless.

**4. Roll-Spot Welding (1 mark):**
Current is **interrupted with long off-periods** so nuggets are **spaced apart** (no overlap). Produces discrete spot welds along a line. Used for **bracket-to-panel attachments** where sealing is not required.

**5. Mash-Seam Welding (1 mark):**
Wheels are flat-faced and the joint is **upset (mashed)** under high pressure. Nuggets coalesce **without melting** in the classical sense, producing a solid-state bond. Used for **aluminium and copper alloys**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Joule heat per nugget (1.5 marks):**

$$H_{input} = I^{2} \bar{R} t_{spot} = (10{,}000)^{2} (120 \times 10^{-6}) (0.04)$$

$$H_{input} = (10^{8})(1.2 \times 10^{-4})(0.04) = 480\;\text{J}$$

**(ii) Heat required to melt the nugget (2 marks):**

Sensible heat to reach $T_m$:

$$Q_{sens} = m c_p \Delta T = (5.3 \times 10^{-4})(670)(1500 - 25) = 523.4\;\text{J}$$

Latent heat of fusion:

$$Q_{lat} = m L_f = (5.3 \times 10^{-4})(247{,}000) = 130.9\;\text{J}$$

Total heat required:

$$H_{req} = Q_{sens} + Q_{lat} = 523.4 + 130.9 = 654.3\;\text{J}$$

**(iii) Thermal efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$\eta = \frac{H_{req}}{H_{input}} = \frac{654.3}{480} \times 100\%$$

Wait — this gives $\eta > 1$, which is **physically impossible**. This indicates an inconsistency in the data (typical KTU scenario). The student is expected to **comment on the discrepancy**: 

> A thermal efficiency greater than unity is impossible. The likely cause is that the recorded **$\bar{R} = 120\;\mu\Omega$ is too low** — actual faying-surface resistance in mild-steel seam welding is typically **200–500 $\mu\Omega$** depending on surface condition. With $\bar{R} = 165\;\mu\Omega$, $H_{input} = 660\;\text{J}$ giving $\eta \approx 99\%$ (still high), and with $\bar{R} = 200\;\mu\Omega$, $H_{input} = 800\;\text{J}$ giving $\eta \approx 82\%$ — within the expected 50–80 % range.

**Examiner's note:** Award 1 mark for stating $\eta > 1$ is impossible and 0.5 marks for the correct diagnosis (resistance value too low for mild steel).

**(iv) Two specific defects at 30 % higher current (2 marks — 1 each):**

New current: $I_{new} = 1.3 \times 10{,}000 = 13{,}000\;\text{A}$

Heat increases by factor $1.3^2 = 1.69$, so $H_{new} \approx 480 \times 1.69 = 811\;\text{J}$ (or with corrected $R$, even higher).

1. **Surface expulsion (splash):** Excess molten metal is squeezed out of the joint, leaving a rough, porous surface and reducing nugget diameter — classified as a **Class B defect** per AWS C1.1.

2. **Electrode pickup and sticking:** The wheel contact face temperature rises, causing Cu to alloy with steel and adhere to the wheel — wheel must be dressed (re-machined), reducing productivity.

(Mention of **cracking, voids, or burn-through** would also earn partial credit.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Seam Welding (Module 2):**

- **Definition:** Continuous leak-tight resistance welding using rotating wheel electrodes, governed by $H = I^2 R t$.
- **No arc, no filler, no shielding gas** — distinguish clearly from arc welding processes.
- **Current type:** 50 Hz AC (single-phase) or MFDC (1–10 kHz) for HF variant.
- **Voltage range:** 2–10 V open-circuit (after transformer step-down from 415 V mains).
- **Current range:** 5,000–30,000 A RMS.
- **Wheel material:** Cu-Cr, Cu-Zr, or Cu-Be alloy; **internally water-cooled**; face width 5–10 mm typical.
- **Dominant resistance:** **Faying-surface contact resistance** (~80–90 % of total) — this is the key difference from arc welding.
- **Weld pitch** $p = v \cdot t_{spot}$; for leak-tight seam, overlap ≥ 15–20 %.
- **Empirical nugget diameter:** $d_n \approx 3\sqrt{s}$ for low-carbon steel, $s$ in mm.
- **Types:** Continuous-current, interrupted-current, HF, roll-spot, mash-seam.
- **Shipbuilding applications:** **Secondary** — bulkhead panels, ducting, internal tank baffles, ventilation casings. **NOT primary hull-girder seams.**
- **IACS limitation:** Primary hull requires arc welding with WPS/PQR; seam welding accepted only for non-critical, non-load-bearing applications.
- **Common defects:** Porosity, cracks, expulsion, insufficient penetration, electrode pickup, surface cracks.
- **Productivity:** 2–10 m/min on thin sheet (≤ 2 mm) — a major advantage for batch panel production.
- **Thermal efficiency:** 50–80 %; balance of 20–50 % lost to wheel conduction, water cooling, and radiation.
- **Unit vigilance:** Always convert mm to m when mixing with SI formulas; A vs kA; m vs mm in $v = \pi D N$.
- **Examiner triggers:** State "resistance welding, not arc" in the first line of any seam-welding answer; explicitly mention **faying-surface contact resistance** as the dominant heat source.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Projection Welding
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Projection Welding

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Projection Welding** is a variant of **resistance welding** in which the welding current and the resultant heat are deliberately *localized* to small, pre-formed contact points called **projections** (or "embossments"), which are raised from the surface of one (or both) of the workpieces prior to welding. When current flows through these constrictions, the contact resistance at the projection is significantly higher than the bulk resistance of the parent metal, producing concentrated **Joule heating (I²R)** that collapses the projection and forms a forged, nugget-type weld.

In the context of **shipbuilding**, projection welding is classified under the broader family of **electrical resistance pressure welding (ERPW)** processes per **ISO 4063 (Group 21 — Resistance welding processes)**, and is particularly specified under **AWS C1.1M/C1.1** and **ISO 18594** for marine structural fabrications.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like this:** Imagine you are trying to melt two sheets of butter together by pressing a hot pin through them, instead of heating the whole sheets. The pin concentrates all the heat into one tiny spot — the butter melts only there, and the two sheets fuse locally. **Projection welding is exactly this** — instead of distributing current across a wide electrode, the current is *forced* through small bumps ("projections") pre-pressed on one of the sheets. These bumps heat up far more than the surrounding metal, collapse under the electrode pressure, and forge the joint.

In **shipbuilding terms**, when a shipyard needs to fasten a **stiffener plate** to a **deck plate**, they don't want to use hundreds of plug welds. Instead, they emboss small dome-shaped bumps on the back of the stiffener, place it on the deck, and a single press stroke fuses dozens of points simultaneously — efficient, repeatable, and clean.

---

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics & Physical Constants

The following baseline parameters govern all projection welding calculations and are universally adopted in KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus:

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|-----------|----------------------|
| **Welding current (I)** | **5 kA – 25 kA** (depending on projection count) |
| **Electrode force (F)** | **1.5 kN – 60 kN** |
| **Weld time (t)** | **0.05 s – 2.0 s** |
| **Projection density** | **1.5 – 6.5 projections/cm²** |
| **Standard projection shape** | **Spherical (dome) — preferred per AWS C1.1** |
| **Sheet thickness ratio** | **≤ 3 : 1** (lower to upper sheet) |
| **Heat generation equation** | **Q = I²Rt** (Joules) |

---

## 1.4 Why Projection Welding Matters in Shipbuilding

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipbuilding reality:** A modern vessel contains **hundreds of thousands of individual welded joints**. Conventional spot welding needs one electrode stroke per weld. Projection welding allows **simultaneous welding of 2 to 50+ points** in a single press operation — translating into **massive savings in time, electricity, and labour**, while maintaining weld consistency. This is why **naval architects in Kochi Shipyard, Cochin Shipyard Ltd., and Mazagon Dock** rely heavily on projection-welded sub-assemblies for non-critical structural members, stiffener brackets, and outfitting items.

**Key Take-away — Projection welding converts the geometry of the workpiece itself into a current-localizing device.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The projection welding process, as taught under **Module 2 of PENST741**, is governed by the following sequential logic:

### **Step 1 — Pre-Embossing (Mechanical Preparation)**
A punch-and-die operation, or cold coining, raises one or more projections on the *underside* of the upper sheet. The projection geometry is the **key design variable** controlling weld nugget size.

### **Step 2 — Stack-Up and Alignment**
- The **embossed sheet** (with projections) is placed on top of the **flat sheet**.
- The two are aligned so each projection sits directly over the intended weld point.
- Electrode heads apply **uniform compressive force (F)** across the entire assembly.

### **Step 3 — Current Application (The Heating Pulse)**
A low-voltage, high-amperage AC (or DC) current is passed between the electrodes.
- The **constricted cross-sectional area** at the projection causes the **current density (J)** to spike.
- Per **microscopic Ohm's Law**, voltage drop = J × ρ, so the projection heats preferentially.

### **Step 4 — Collapse and Forging**
- When the projection metal reaches its **plastic state (≈ 0.8 × T_melt)**, the projection physically collapses under electrode force.
- Molten/soft metal at the interface **extrudes outward**, forming an expelled flash ring around the weld — a visible quality indicator.

### **Step 5 — Current Off, Forging Hold**
- Current is cut, but electrode force is maintained for a **hold time (t_h)** to allow the nugget to solidify under pressure, eliminating porosity.

---

## 2.2 The Three Resistances in Projection Welding

The total resistance opposing current flow in the weld zone is composed of three series resistances:

$$
R_{total} = R_{1} + R_{2} + R_{c}
$$

where:

- **R₁** = Bulk resistance of the upper sheet
- **R₂** = Bulk resistance of the lower sheet
- **R_c** = **Contact resistance at the projection interface** (this is the *dominant* term, **40–80 %** of total heat is generated here)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why R_c dominates:** Because the projection cross-section is *much smaller* than the surrounding sheet, the current density at the tip is 5–15× higher than elsewhere. Since heat ∝ I²R, and R_c is locally very large, almost all the I²R heat concentrates at the projection tip — this is the **fundamental physics** behind projection welding.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = I^{2} R_{c} t$ | Heat generated at projection (dominant term) | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $Q_{total} = \int_{0}^{t} I(t)^{2} \, R(t) \, dt$ | Full heat-input integral (dynamic form) | J |
| 3 | $F_{proj} = \dfrac{F_{electrode}}{n}$ | Force per projection (n = number of projections) | N |
| 4 | $d_{proj} = k \cdot \sqrt{t_{sheet}}$ | Empirical projection diameter rule (k ≈ 0.7–1.0) | mm |
| 5 | $h_{proj} = 0.2 \cdot d_{proj}$ | Standard projection height | mm |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{nugget}}{Q_{total}}$ | Thermal efficiency (typically **0.6 – 0.85** for projection welding) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $J_{proj} = \dfrac{I}{A_{proj}}$ | Current density at projection tip | A/mm² |
| 8 | $A_{proj} = \dfrac{\pi \, d_{proj}^{2}}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of projection tip | mm² |
| 9 | $P_{el} = V \cdot I = I^{2} R$ | Electrical power consumed | Watts (W) |
| 10 | $t_{weld} = \dfrac{C \cdot I^{2}}{h_{proj}}$ | Empirical weld-time relationship (C = material constant) | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** These equations are used by shipyard weld engineers to design electrode tooling, set PLC parameters on **resistance welding machines (e.g., Sciaky, Taylor-Winfield, Medar)**, and qualify welding procedure specifications (WPS) as per **AWS C1.1**.

---

## 2.4 Material Behaviour and Weld Nugget Growth

The nugget (molten zone) grows in three stages:

1. **Initiation stage** — projection tip reaches forging temperature, begins plastic collapse.
2. **Growth stage** — current continues, R_c falls as contact area enlarges, but I is increased to maintain constant power (constant-power control mode).
3. **Stabilization stage** — nugget diameter stabilizes; further current would cause expulsion (metal rejection), a defect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Constant Power vs. Constant Current:**
> Modern **inverter-based DC projection welders** use *constant-current control* (CCC) to compensate for changing resistance during projection collapse. Older AC machines use *constant-secondary-voltage* control. The KTU Module-2 syllabus emphasizes CCC as the industry-preferred method.

---

## 2.5 Process Variants (Sub-classification per KTU 2024)

Projection welding has three recognized variants in shipbuilding:

| Variant | Description | Typical Shipyard Use |
|---------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Embossed projection welding** | Projections are pre-formed by cold coining on the upper sheet | Stiffener-to-deck joints |
| **Cross-wire projection welding** | Wires intersecting form natural contact projections | Wire mesh panels, light bulkheads |
| **T-Projection welding** | A flat sheet is welded edge-to-edge using embossed projections | Liner plate joints |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Distribution between Projection and Bulk

In projection welding, we must derive how the total I²R heat is partitioned between the **projection (P)** and the **bulk parent metal (B)**. The KTU 2024 examiner loves this derivation.

### Given Data
- Sheet thickness: t_s = 2 mm
- Material resistivity: ρ = 1.7 × 10⁻⁷ Ω·m (mild steel)
- Projection diameter: d_proj = 4 mm
- Projection height: h_proj = 0.8 mm
- Welding current: I = 8 kA
- Weld time: t = 0.3 s

### Step 1 — Contact Resistance at Projection

Using the empirical relation for contact resistance:

$$
R_{c} = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \, d_{proj}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
R_{c} = \dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.125 \times 10^{-5} \, \Omega
$$

### Step 2 — Bulk Resistance of Upper Sheet (cylindrical zone above projection)

Bulk resistance around the projection footprint:

$$
R_{b} = \dfrac{\rho \, t_{s}}{\pi \, d_{proj}^{2} / 4}
$$

$$
R_{b} = \dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times (4 \times 10^{-3})^{2} / 4}
$$

$$
R_{b} = \dfrac{3.4 \times 10^{-10}}{\pi \times 4 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{3.4 \times 10^{-10}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
R_{b} = 2.706 \times 10^{-5} \, \Omega
$$

### Step 3 — Total Resistance

$$
R_{total} = R_{c} + R_{b} = (2.125 + 2.706) \times 10^{-5} = 4.831 \times 10^{-5} \, \Omega
$$

### Step 4 — Heat Generated at Projection (Q_P)

$$
Q_{P} = I^{2} \, R_{c} \, t
$$

$$
Q_{P} = (8000)^{2} \times 2.125 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.3
$$

$$
Q_{P} = 64 \times 10^{6} \times 2.125 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.3
$$

$$
Q_{P} = 64 \times 10^{6} \times 6.375 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
Q_{P} = 408 \, J
$$

### Step 5 — Heat Generated in Bulk (Q_B)

$$
Q_{B} = I^{2} \, R_{b} \, t = (8000)^{2} \times 2.706 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.3
$$

$$
Q_{B} = 64 \times 10^{6} \times 8.118 \times 10^{-6} = 519.6 \, J
$$

### Step 6 — Heat Fraction at Projection

$$
f_{P} = \dfrac{Q_{P}}{Q_{P} + Q_{B}} = \dfrac{408}{408 + 519.6}
$$

$$
f_{P} = \dfrac{408}{927.6} = 0.4398 = 43.98 \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** About 44 % of the heat is localized at the projection even though it occupies < 5 % of the cross-section. This validates the principle: **the geometry of the projection, not its mass, drives the heat concentration.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter vs. Weld Quality

| Parameter | Too Low | Optimum | Too High | Defect Caused |
|-----------|---------|---------|----------|---------------|
| **Current (I)** | Cold weld, no fusion | Full nugget | Expulsion, burn-through | Stick welds, voids |
| **Time (t)** | Undersized nugget | Standard nugget | Excessive HAZ, grain growth | Embrittlement |
| **Force (F)** | Porosity, voids | Forged, dense nugget | Cracking, electrode deformation | Sheet distortion |
| **Projection height (h)** | Inconsistent contact | Stable collapse | Expulsion | Excessive flash |
| **Sheet fit-up** | Arcing, contact loss | Uniform pressure | Uneven current | Cold welds |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Electrode Force per Projection (Shipyard Application)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Scenario (KTU 2024 Board-style question):** A naval stiffener plate has **24 projections** embossed on it. A welding machine has a maximum electrode force capacity of **96 kN**. Find the force per projection.

### Solution:

$$
F_{proj} = \dfrac{F_{electrode}}{n} = \dfrac{96 \times 10^{3}}{24}
$$

$$
F_{proj} = 4000 \, N = 4 \, kN
$$

### Verification — Per Projection Shear Stress on Sheet

For sheet area A_proj = π d² / 4 = π × (4 mm)² / 4 = 12.566 mm²:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{F_{proj}}{A_{proj}} = \dfrac{4000 \, N}{12.566 \, mm^{2}} = 318.3 \, MPa
$$

Since **mild steel yield strength ≈ 250–350 MPa**, the projection will plastically deform as required — proving the design is feasible.

---

## 3.4 Heat Balance Verification Using Energy Conservation

A cross-check on the heat generated vs. the heat required to melt the projection tip:

### Heat required to raise projection from 25 °C to 1450 °C (mild steel):

$$
Q_{req} = m \, c_{p} \, \Delta T + m \, L_{f}
$$

Assume projection tip volume = (π × 4² × 0.8)/4 mm³ = 10.05 mm³ = 1.005 × 10⁻⁸ m³
Mass: $m = \rho_{steel} \times V = 7850 \times 1.005 \times 10^{-8} = 7.89 \times 10^{-5}$ kg

Specific heat: c_p ≈ 670 J/kg·K
Latent heat of fusion: L_f = 247,000 J/kg

$$
Q_{req} = 7.89 \times 10^{-5} \times 670 \times 1425 + 7.89 \times 10^{-5} \times 247000
$$

$$
Q_{req} = 75.36 + 19.49 = 94.85 \, J
$$

Since **Q_P = 408 J >> 94.85 J**, energy availability is confirmed — sufficient thermal margin for stable weld nugget formation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Projection Welding Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sheet Stock Preparation] --> B[Pre-Embossing Die Operation]
    B --> C[Projections Raised on Upper Sheet]
    C --> D[Stack-Up on Lower Sheet]
    D --> E[Electrode Platen Engagement]
    E --> F{Constant-Current Controller}
    F --> G[Apply Electrode Force F]
    G --> H[Initiate Welding Current I]
    H --> I[Localized I2R Heating at Projection]
    I --> J[Projection Plastic Collapse]
    J --> K[Nugget Formation]
    K --> L[Current Cut-Off]
    L --> M[Hold Pressure for Forging]
    M --> N[Electrode Retraction]
    N --> O[Finished Projection Weld]
    O --> P[Quality Inspection Flash Ring Check]
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Welding Head & Current Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    M[Three-Phase Mains Supply] --> T[Step-Down Welding Transformer]
    T --> S[SCR / IGBT Power Switch]
    S --> CT[Current Transducer Feedback]
    CT --> F[Constant Current Controller]
    F --> S
    S --> EU[Upper Electrode Arm]
    EU --> US[Upper Sheet with Projections]
    US --> LS[Lower Sheet]
    LS --> EL[Lower Electrode Arm]
    EL --> T
    H[Hydraulic / Pneumatic Ram] --> EU
    PL[Programmable Logic Controller PLC] --> F
    PL --> H
    PL --> S
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is a **closed-loop current control system**. The PLC reads current feedback, modulates IGBT firing angle, and adjusts to maintain a set current regardless of projection collapse. This is the architecture used in modern **Miyachi / Amada Weld Tech** projection welding machines used at shipyards.

---

## 4.3 Process Sequence Diagram (Time-Line View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP as Operator
    participant PLC as Machine PLC
    participant R as Hydraulic Ram
    participant W as Welding Transformer
    participant E as Electrode
    OP->>PLC: 1. Trigger Cycle
    PLC->>R: 2. Advance Ram (Squeeze Time)
    R->>E: 3. Apply Full Electrode Force
    PLC->>W: 4. Fire Weld Current (Weld Time)
    W->>E: 5. Deliver I kA Pulse
    E->>W: 6. Projection Collapses, Nugget Forms
    PLC->>W: 7. Cut Current (Weld Off)
    PLC->>R: 8. Hold Force (Hold Time)
    R->>E: 9. Retract Ram
    PLC->>OP: 10. Cycle Complete Signal
```

---

## 4.4 Sectional View — Projection Weld Geometry (Mermaid Block Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph ASolder[Weld Zone Cross-Section]
        EU[Upper Electrode] --> SU1[Upper Sheet Material]
        SU1 --> PR[Projection Embossed Geometry]
        PR --> IN[Interface Contact Zone]
        IN --> SL1[Lower Sheet Material]
        SL1 --> EL[Lower Electrode]
        PR -.Collapses to form.-> NG[Nugget Forged Zone]
        NG --> FS[Flash Ring Expelled Metal]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define projection welding. Why is it preferred over spot welding for mass production in shipyards?**

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Projection welding** is a resistance welding process in which current is concentrated at pre-formed embossments (projections) on the workpieces to localize heat and forge multiple welds simultaneously.

> **Preferred over spot welding in shipyards because:**
> 1. **Multiple welds in one operation** — up to 50 welds per press stroke, reducing cycle time.
> 2. **Lower current per weld** — total current is shared across projections, reducing transformer load.
> 3. **Consistent weld quality** — projection geometry controls nugget size automatically.
> 4. **Suitable for dissimilar thicknesses** — projection collapses compensate for mismatch.
> 5. **Reduced electrode wear** — current is spread across a larger electrode face.

---

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List four process parameters that affect the quality of a projection weld.**

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four primary process parameters affecting projection weld quality are:
1. **Welding current (I)** — controls heat input; too low → cold weld, too high → expulsion.
2. **Weld time (t)** — determines nugget diameter and HAZ width.
3. **Electrode force (F)** — ensures forged nugget, prevents porosity and cracking.
4. **Projection geometry (diameter, height, shape)** — controls current density and heat localization.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B1 (a) — 7 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the principle of projection welding with a neat schematic. Discuss the role of contact resistance in heat generation.**

### **Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
Projection welding is based on **localized Joule heating (I²R)** at pre-formed embossments on one of the sheets. The constriction in cross-section at the projection forces current density to rise sharply, and the higher local resistance causes preferential heating at the projection tip. When the metal reaches plastic state, the projection collapses under electrode force and forms a forged weld nugget.

**Schematic (refer to Section 4.4):** Shows upper electrode, upper sheet with embossed projection, contact interface, lower sheet, lower electrode, and the flash ring.

**Role of Contact Resistance:**
- The total resistance is the sum of bulk resistance and contact resistance: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + R_c.
- The contact resistance R_c at the projection is the **dominant heat-generating term (40–80 %)** of total.
- Since Q = I²Rt, and R_c is large at the projection tip while I is forced through the constriction, **Q_proj >> Q_bulk**.
- This concentration enables rapid, controlled heating without raising the temperature of the surrounding bulk metal significantly.

**Conclusion:** Contact resistance at the projection is the *physical mechanism* that makes projection welding viable.

---

### **Question B1 (b) — 7 Marks**

**A projection welding operation has 16 projections on a stiffener. Total weld current is 12 kA, weld time is 0.4 s, contact resistance per projection is 30 μΩ. Find: (i) total heat generated at projections, (ii) heat per projection, (iii) comment on whether this is sufficient to form a nugget in 2 mm mild steel.**

### **Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- n = 16
- I = 12 kA = 12,000 A
- t = 0.4 s
- R_c per projection = 30 × 10⁻⁶ Ω

**(i) Total heat at projections:**

Since all projections share current, I per projection = 12,000 / 16 = 750 A

$$
Q_{total} = n \cdot i^{2} \cdot R_{c} \cdot t
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 16 \times (750)^{2} \times 30 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.4
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 16 \times 562500 \times 30 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.4
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 16 \times 562500 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 108 \, J
$$

**(ii) Heat per projection:**

$$
Q_{proj} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{n} = \dfrac{108}{16} = 6.75 \, J
$$

**(iii) Comment on sufficiency:**

For a 2 mm mild steel sheet, typical nugget formation requires **~80–120 J** per weld assuming 60 % thermal efficiency. **108 J falls at the lower edge** of acceptable heat input. **Recommendation:** Either increase weld time to **0.6 s** or increase total current to **14 kA** to ensure full nugget formation without expulsion risk.

---

## **Question B2 (a) — 7 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Compare projection welding with spot welding. Highlight the advantages and limitations of projection welding in shipbuilding.**

### **Model Answer:**

| Feature | Spot Welding | Projection Welding |
|---------|--------------|---------------------|
| Current localization | By electrode tip | By embossed projections |
| Welds per stroke | One | Multiple (2 – 50+) |
| Current per weld | High | Distributed, lower per weld |
| Electrode wear | Moderate | Lower (larger electrode face) |
| Sheet thickness limit | ≤ 3 mm typically | Up to 6 mm (with proper design) |
| Dissimilar thickness | Difficult | Easier |
| Pre-processing needed | None | Embossing operation |
| Cycle time | Higher | Significantly lower |

**Advantages in shipbuilding:**
1. Mass production of multiple welded points (e.g., stiffener-to-deck plate).
2. Reduced labour and energy cost.
3. Suitable for **mild steel, stainless steel, aluminium** (used in naval superstructures).
4. Repeatable, automatable with **robotic weld guns**.
5. Reduced HAZ and distortion compared to arc welding.

**Limitations:**
1. Requires pre-embossing → additional tooling cost.
2. Projection design must be precise → R&D cost.
3. Not suitable for very thick plates (> 6 mm).
4. Quality inspection (flash ring) requires trained personnel.
5. Sensitive to surface contaminants.

---

### **Question B2 (b) — 7 Marks**

**Describe the process of cross-wire projection welding with a diagram. List two typical shipbuilding applications.**

### **Model Answer:**

**Process:**
In cross-wire projection welding, two sets of wires are crossed at 90° to each other. The intersection point naturally forms a **four-point contact**, which acts as a localized projection. When current is applied through the wires, the contact point heats preferentially due to the constricted contact area, and the wires fuse at the intersection, forming a welded node.

**Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4 — the schematic shows two crossed wires with current flowing in horizontally, contact resistance at intersection, and fused weld node.)

**Typical Shipbuilding Applications:**
1. **Lightweight wire mesh bulkheads** — used in crew quarters and partitions below deck.
2. **Reinforcement cages for non-critical furniture** — used in cabin joinery and outfitting items.
3. *Bonus application:* Gratings for engine room platforms and cable trays.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically LOSE marks on Projection Welding questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to divide current** by number of projections when calculating heat per projection — examiners check this in one line. (-2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing contact resistance with bulk resistance** — always clarify that R_c is the *dominant* heat generator, not R_bulk. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 3. **Not mentioning the role of forging pressure** after current cut-off — a common omission. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Wrong unit of current density** — must be **A/mm²**, not A/m². (-0.5 mark)
> 5. **Not stating the empirical rule** d_proj = k √t_sheet — examiners expect this formula explicitly. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Skipping the quality control aspect** — flash ring inspection is a 2-mark add-on frequently asked.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Projection Welding:**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Resistance welding where current is localized at embossed projections to form multiple welds simultaneously.
>
> ✅ **Key formula:** **Q = I²Rt** — heat concentrated at projection via high local R_c.
>
> ✅ **Three resistances:** R₁ (bulk upper) + R₂ (bulk lower) + **R_c (contact — dominant)**.
>
> ✅ **Standard projection shape:** **Spherical (dome)**, height = 0.2 × diameter.
>
> ✅ **Empirical diameter rule:** **d_proj = k × √t_sheet** where k = 0.7–1.0.
>
> ✅ **Weld time:** 0.05–2.0 s; **Current:** 5–25 kA; **Force:** 1.5–60 kN.
>
> ✅ **Constant-current control (CCC)** is preferred over constant-voltage control in modern inverter machines.
>
> ✅ **Three process stages:** Squeeze → Weld → Hold (with current cut during hold).
>
> ✅ **Quality indicator:** Symmetrical flash ring around the projection → acceptable weld.
>
> ✅ **Defects:** Cold weld (low Q), expulsion (high Q), porosity (low F), cracks (high F + brittle material).
>
> ✅ **Shipbuilding uses:** Stiffener-to-deck plate, bulkhead brackets, light mesh panels, cabin outfitting.
>
> ✅ **Standards governing:** **AWS C1.1M, ISO 4063, ISO 18594** — must be cited in Part B answers for full marks.
>
> ✅ **Dissimilar thickness handling:** Projection welding accommodates **≤ 3 : 1 thickness ratio**, much better than spot welding.
>
> ✅ **Industrial machines:** Miyachi, Taylor-Winfield, Sciaky, Medar — commonly installed in shipyards.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance Upset Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resistance Upset Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Resistance Upset Welding (RUW)**, also classified in some references as **Resistance Butt Welding** or **Upset Butt Welding**, is a *solid-state resistance welding process* in which two prepared workpieces are butted end-to-end, clamped between high-conductivity water-cooled copper jaws (electrodes), and a heavy **low-voltage, high-amperage alternating current** is passed through the joint. The interfacial resistance (contact resistance between the abutting faces, plus bulk resistance) generates localized **Joule heat** (I²R heating) which rapidly raises the temperature of the interface to the forging/plastic range — typically **0.8 to 0.9 of the solidus temperature** of the parent metal. Immediately after, or simultaneously with the final stage of heating, a substantial **axial upsetting (forging) force** is applied along the longitudinal axis of the workpieces, extruding the softened/plasticized metal from the joint interface as **upset (flash collar / upset metal)** and producing a forged, fine-grained weld.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Resistance Upset Welding is grouped under *Resistance Welding Processes* alongside spot, projection, seam, and flash welding. The examiner expects students to clearly differentiate **RUW** from **Flash Butt Welding (FBW)** since both involve upsetting, but only RUW uses *contact resistance heating* without prior arcing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Designation (ISO 4063 / AWS C1.1):** Process number **23 — Resistance butt welding**, with RUW being the principal industrial variant. In shipbuilding classification society rules (DNV, LR, ABS, IRS), RUW is accepted for longitudinal and transverse joints in plates, flats, and sections of mild and high-tensile structural steels.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **two sticks of soft butter** that you want to glue end-to-end to make one continuous stick. You cannot use ordinary glue because butter is greasy. What do you do?

1. You place them end-to-end on a table.
2. You grab each stick with your hands (the "jaws") and **press them together firmly** while at the same time **rubbing your hands back and forth quickly** (friction/heat) until the contact faces become warm and soft.
3. Then, you apply an even **greater squeeze** (the upset force). The soft, warm ends of the two sticks merge into a single continuous stick, with a small ring of excess butter squeezing out at the joint — the "upset collar".

**This is exactly what Resistance Upset Welding does**, except:
- The "rubbing" is replaced by **electric current flowing across the joint** which heats the interface from within (resistance heating).
- The "hands" are **massive water-cooled copper jaws** that grip the workpieces, conduct the current, and apply the upset force.
- The "butter" is **steel plate** heated to a plastic (forgeable) state at around 1200 °C.

The **"upset collar"** that squeezes out at the joint is the visible evidence of a sound RUW weld. A larger, well-formed upset generally indicates proper heat, force, and timing — though it is later trimmed off in shipbuilding plate mills.

---

## 1.3 Why Resistance Upset Welding Matters in Shipbuilding

- **Longitudinal seams** of hull plates (especially for large containerships, tankers, and bulk carriers) are often produced by RUW in plate mills before the rolled plate is even cut and shaped.
- Produces a **forged, homogeneous grain structure** across the joint — no cast dendritic structure as in arc welds.
- Very **high productivity**: full-thickness plates (up to 50 mm and beyond) can be joined in seconds.
- Eliminates filler metal, flux, and shielding gas in the joint region.
- Compatible with **standard shipbuilding steels** (ASTM A131, AH36, DH36, EH36; IRS grade plates).

> [!TIP]
> **Shipyard terminology tip:** Shipbuilding plates joined by RUW in the steel mill are often called "**spliced plates**" or "**welded wide plates**". The longitudinal weld of the original rolled coil/plate is called the "**mill weld**" or "**plate spliced joint**".

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Welding current (**I**) | **5,000 A to 80,000 A** (depending on section area) |
| Secondary open-circuit voltage | **2 V to 12 V** |
| Frequency | **50 Hz mains** (some mills use 400 Hz to localize heat) |
| Clamping/upset force (**F**) | **200 kN to 10,000 kN** |
| Joint temperature | **0.8 – 0.9 × T_solidus** (≈ 1100–1300 °C for steel) |
| Upset distance / upset metal | **3 mm to 25 mm** depending on section |
| Heating time | **1 s to 30 s** |
| Electrode (jaw) material | **Cold-rolled, water-cooled copper (Cu-ETP / Cu-DHP)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Sequence — Step-by-Step

The complete Resistance Upset Welding cycle is divided into **four distinct phases** that the KTU examiner will expect you to describe in order. Memorize these as a numbered list:

### **Phase 1 — Setup & Clamping**
- The two prepared workpieces (cut square, faces machined or sawn, oxide/grease removed by wire brushing) are placed end-to-end between the **two clamping jaws** of the welding machine.
- The jaws close and apply an initial **clamping force** sufficient to establish good **metallic contact** (low, stable contact resistance) and to carry the heavy current without arcing at the jaw–workpiece interface.
- The jaws are hollow and have **internal water-cooling channels** to extract heat from the copper itself.

### **Phase 2 — Heating (Current ON, No Upset Yet)**
- The welding transformer is energized, and a low-voltage, high-amperage AC current flows: *workpiece 1 → jaw 1 → transformer secondary → jaw 2 → workpiece 2 → across the joint interface → back*.
- Heat is generated wherever resistance is encountered, but **most heat concentrates at the joint interface** because:
  (a) Contact resistance is highest there (microscopic asperities, oxide films).
  (b) Bulk resistance is small in massive copper jaws.
- The interface temperature rises rapidly to the **plastic range** (≈ 1200 °C for steel).
- **No upsetting force is applied during this phase** — only clamping force.

### **Phase 3 — Upsetting (Forging)**
- At the end of the heating phase (or in the last 20–30 % of heating, depending on control philosophy), the **upset ram is accelerated** and applies a large axial force.
- The plasticized metal at the interface is **extruded radially** as a flash/upset collar around the circumference of the joint.
- The current may either continue through the upset (forging current) or be switched off (forging-off). Most modern machines use **current continues during upset** to keep the interface hot during deformation.
- Upset distance is precisely controlled by a **platen stop** or servo-hydraulic position control.

### **Phase 4 — Hold, Cool & Retract**
- Current is cut off (or sharply reduced).
- Upset force is **maintained (held)** while the joint cools under pressure to consolidate the forged bond.
- After cooling below ~600 °C, the jaws retract, the workpiece is removed, and the **upset collar is trimmed** by a rotary cropper or planer.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|
| Square & clean faces | Maximize uniform contact resistance; prevent arcing. |
| Heavy clamping first | Carry current without localized melting at the jaw. |
| Heat BEFORE upset | Pre-soften interface so upset force can extrude plastic metal with low force. |
| Upset while hot | Closes any micro-voids, breaks up oxide films (which are extruded into the flash), aligns grains across the joint, and produces a **forged bond** rather than a cast bond. |
| Maintain force during cool | Prevents shrinkage porosity and ensures metallurgical bond solidifies under compression. |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **core KTU-expected formulas** for Resistance Upset Welding problems. Memorize units carefully.

### **Table 2.1 — Core RUW Formulas**

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\boxed{Q = I^{2} R t}$ | Q = heat generated, I = current, R = total resistance, t = time | J (Joule) | **Joule's First Law** — total heat in joint |
| 2 | $R_{T} = R_{b} + 2 R_{c,j} + R_{c,i}$ | R_T = total, R_b = bulk, R_c,j = jaw-workpiece contact, R_c,i = interface contact | Ω (Ohm) | Resistances add in series in welding circuit |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | ρ = resistivity, L = length, A = cross-section | Ω | Bulk resistance of a conductor |
| 4 | $R_c \approx \dfrac{\rho_c}{2 a}$ | ρ_c = constriction resistivity, a = radius of contact spot | Ω | **Holm's contact resistance** — explains why interface dominates heat |
| 5 | $P = I^{2} R$ | P = electrical power converted to heat | W (Watt) | Rate of heat generation |
| 6 | $V = I R$ | V = voltage drop, I = current, R = resistance | V | Ohm's law for welding circuit |
| 7 | $F_{upset} = p_{up} \cdot A$ | F_upset = upset force, p_up = upset pressure, A = cross-sectional area | N | Upset force from required pressure |
| 8 | $p_{up} \approx 70 \text{ to } 200 \text{ MPa}$ | (typical for mild steel) | MPa | Empirical upset pressure range for steel RUW |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{joint}}{Q_{total}} \approx 0.55 \text{ to } 0.85$ | η = thermal efficiency of process | dimensionless | Fraction of heat actually deposited in joint |
| 10 | $A_{joint} = \dfrac{I}{J}$ | A = area, J = current density | mm², A/mm² | Sizing the joint from required current |
| 11 | $\Delta L = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$ | ΔL = elastic shortening, E = Young's modulus | mm | Elastic upset under forging load (elastic region) |
| 12 | $T_{interface} \approx 0.85 \, T_{solidus}$ | For steel | K or °C | Target joint temperature |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Notice that **Q = I²Rt** assumes a *constant* R. In reality, R drops sharply as the interface heats up (resistivity of steel rises but contact resistance collapses). For board problems, treat R as an *effective average* value if not given.

### **Table 2.2 — Comparison with Flash Butt Welding (Frequently asked in KTU)**

| Feature | Resistance Upset Welding (RUW) | Flash Butt Welding (FBW) |
|---|---|---|
| Heat source | Internal I²R heating at interface | Electric arc/flash from gap |
| Initial gap | Workpieces in firm contact | Workpieces separated by small gap |
| Arc | None (no flash during heating) | Continuous flash during heat phase |
| Atmosphere | Air (acceptable) | Air (flash ejects oxides anyway) |
| Metal loss | Less (no flash burn-off during heat) | More (flash burn-off can be 5–10 % of length) |
| Surface prep | Very clean, square, oxide-free required | Less strict (flash burns off oxides) |
| Power demand | Higher (no preheat via flash) | Lower per pass |
| Typical use | Plate mill splices, bars, sections | Rails, rings, large diameter pipes |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plate Mills (Shipbuilding):** Two coils/ slabs are RUW-spliced head-to-tail in a continuous plate mill to produce an effectively "infinite" plate, which is then rolled to final thickness. This is why ship plates can be supplied in lengths of 20 m+ from a single RUW-spliced plate.
- **Bar & Section Joining:** Reinforcement bars, large I-sections, and H-beams for ship superstructures are RUW joined.
- **Anchor Chain Manufacture:** Stud-less and studded anchor chains for offshore vessels are produced by RUW + flash butt combinations.
- **Ring Rolling Feedstock:** Large forged rings (e.g., for stern tubes, propeller shaft brackets) start from RUW-butt-joined billets.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Generated in the Joint (Joule's Law Applied to RUW)

We want to derive an expression for the heat *actually deposited in the interface* (Q_joint) in terms of the welding current I, heating time t, and the relevant resistances.

### **Step 1 — State the governing equation**

The instantaneous electrical power converted to heat in any resistive element is given by Joule's law:

$$P = I^{2} R$$

### **Step 2 — Integrate over the heating interval**

If the current I and resistance R are *assumed constant* over the heating time t (a common KTU board simplification), the total heat is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \int_{0}^{t} I^{2} R \, dt \\
  &= I^{2} R \int_{0}^{t} dt \\
  &= I^{2} R t
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the standard Joule heating equation.

### **Step 3 — Apply to the welding circuit**

The total circuit resistance is the sum of three series contributions:

$$
R_{T} = 2 R_{c,j} \;+\; R_{b,1} \;+\; R_{b,2} \;+\; R_{c,i}
$$

where:
- $R_{c,j}$ = contact resistance at each jaw (×2 for two jaws)
- $R_{b,1}, R_{b,2}$ = bulk resistance of the two workpieces up to the joint
- $R_{c,i}$ = contact resistance at the *interface*

**Engineering insight:** Because the copper jaws are massive and water-cooled, $R_{c,j}$ is small. Bulk resistances are also small for thick workpieces (low ρ·L/A). The dominant term is $R_{c,i}$.

### **Step 4 — Subdivide Q into useful vs. lost heat**

The heat actually deposited in the joint is a fraction (thermal efficiency η) of total heat:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{joint} &= \eta \cdot I^{2} R_{T} t \\
          &= \eta \cdot I^{2} \left(2 R_{c,j} + R_{b,1} + R_{b,2} + R_{c,i}\right) t
\end{aligned}
$$

Typical $\eta$ for RUW is **0.55 to 0.85**.

### **Step 5 — Required heat to raise the interface**

If the KTU question asks "how much current is needed?", we equate the deposited heat to the sensible heat required:

$$
Q_{required} = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T \;+\; m \cdot L_f \quad \text{(if melting involved, which it isn't in RUW)}
$$

For RUW, no melting → only sensible heat:

$$
Q_{required} = m \cdot c \cdot \left(T_{plastic} - T_{ambient}\right)
$$

Combining with the heat input:

$$
\eta \, I^{2} R_{T} t = m \, c \, \Delta T
$$

Solving for current:

$$
I = \sqrt{\dfrac{m \, c \, \Delta T}{\eta \, R_{T} \, t}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam tip:** Most KTU numericals will give you m, c, ΔT, R_T, t, and η, and ask for I. Use this formula directly. Always check units: convert all to SI (kg, J/(kg·K), K, Ω, s) before computing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Upset Force from Upset Pressure

The upset force is computed from a target *upset pressure* and the cross-section of the joint.

### **Step 1 — Define upset pressure**

Empirically (from welding handbooks), for mild steel RUW:

$$
p_{up} = 70 \text{ to } 200 \text{ MPa}
$$

For high-tensile steels, use the upper end.

### **Step 2 — Compute required force**

$$
F_{upset} = p_{up} \cdot A
$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the joint (e.g., plate thickness × plate width for a butt joint in plate).

### **Step 3 — Worked Example (KTU style)**

**Problem:** A ship plate RUW joint has dimensions **20 mm thick × 1500 mm wide**. Mild steel. Compute the upset force if the recommended upset pressure is **150 MPa**.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 20 \times 1500 = 30{,}000 \; \text{mm}^{2} = 0.03 \; \text{m}^{2} \\
F_{upset} &= p_{up} \cdot A = 150 \times 10^{6} \times 0.03 \\
          &= 4.5 \times 10^{6} \; \text{N} = 4500 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer: F_upset ≈ 4500 kN (≈ 450 tonnes-force)**

This explains why RUW machines for shipbuilding plates are *massive* — they sit in plate mills and have frames weighing hundreds of tonnes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Elastic Shortening under Upset Force

Before the interface actually starts to flow plastically, the entire workpiece column shortens elastically. KTU sometimes asks for this "spring-back" or for the upset distance.

### **Step 1 — Apply Hooke's law to the combined column**

For a workpiece of total length L, cross-section A, Young's modulus E, loaded by F:

$$
\Delta L_{elastic} = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot E}
$$

### **Step 2 — Workpiece is two halves in series**

Since the two workpieces are end-to-end (each half has its own length L₁ and L₂, but typically L₁ ≈ L₂ = L/2):

$$
\Delta L_{total} = \dfrac{F \cdot (L_1 + L_2)}{A \cdot E} = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot E}
$$

(For ship plates, the gripping length L (jaw-to-jaw) is typically 200–600 mm, A is the full cross-section, and E ≈ 200 GPa for steel. So elastic shortening is small — sub-millimetre — compared to plastic upset of 5–20 mm.)

### **Step 3 — Total upset = elastic + plastic**

$$
\Delta L_{total} = \underbrace{\dfrac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot E}}_{\text{elastic}} \;+\; \underbrace{\delta_{plastic}}_{\text{plastic upset}}
$$

Typical KTU answer requires stating both components, and noting that $\delta_{plastic}$ dominates by an order of magnitude.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Parameters on Weld Quality

This matrix is **frequently asked** as a Part B (14-mark) question in KTU. Memorize the *direction of each arrow*.

| Process Parameter ↑ (increase) | Effect on Heat Input (Q) | Effect on Joint Temperature | Effect on Upset Metal / Flash | Weld Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Current (I)** | ↑↑ (quadratic) | ↑↑ | ↑ (excess flash) | Poor if too high (burn-through) |
| **Heating time (t)** | ↑ (linear) | ↑ | ↑ | Poor if too long (overheating) |
| **Contact resistance (R_c,i)** | ↑ (linear) | ↑ (localized) | ↑ | Good if controlled, poor if unstable |
| **Upset force (F)** | Negligible on Q | Negligible on T | ↑ (larger flash collar) | Good if sufficient, poor if insufficient (lack of bonding) |
| **Upset timing (late vs. early)** | Same | Same | Larger if late | **Early upset** → cold joint, no bonding. **Late upset** → overheated, coarse grain. **Just right** → fine equiaxed grain. |
| **Surface cleanliness** | Affects R_c,i | Affects R_c,i | Affects uniformity | **Clean** → uniform, sound joint. **Dirty** → arcing, pitting, porosity. |
| **Joint alignment (parallelism)** | Affects R_c,i distribution | Hot spots at one side | Asymmetric flash | **Mis-aligned** → uneven upset, weak joint. **Aligned** → symmetric. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question Type:** *"Explain with a sketch the effect of (a) welding current, (b) upset force, and (c) upset timing on the quality of a resistance upset weld."* — Use the table above as the structured answer.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Resistance Upset Welding Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[AC Mains Supply 3 Phase 415V 50Hz] --> B[Primary Contactor and Welding Transformer]
    B -->|Steps down to 2 to 12 V| C[Secondary Busbars Water Cooled]
    C --> D[Left Clamping Jaw Cu-DHP Water Cooled]
    C --> E[Right Clamping Jaw Cu-DHP Water Cooled]
    D --> F[Workpiece 1 Plate Section]
    F --> G[Joint Interface Contact Plane]
    G --> H[Workpiece 2 Plate Section]
    H --> E
    I[Hydraulic or Pneumatic Clamping Cylinder] --> D
    I --> E
    J[Upset Ram Servo Hydraulic Cylinder] --> D
    J --> E
    K[Platen Stop and Position Sensor] --> J
    L[Control Cabinet PLC HMI] --> B
    L --> I
    L --> J
    M[Water Cooling Circuit Closed Loop] --> D
    M --> E
    M --> C
```

### **Reading the diagram (top-to-bottom logic):**
1. **Power path:** A → B → C → D → F → G → H → E → back to C. Current flows across the joint interface **G** where heat is generated.
2. **Force path:** The clamping cylinder **I** and upset ram **J** push the jaws D and E together along the axial direction of the workpieces.
3. **Control path:** The PLC **L** coordinates current, clamping force, upset force, upset timing, and platen position **K**.
4. **Cooling path:** **M** supplies chilled water to the jaws and busbars to prevent copper from overheating.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — The Four Phases of the RUW Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP as Operator
    participant PLC as Control System
    participant JAW as Clamping Jaws
    participant XMR as Welding Transformer
    participant RAM as Upset Ram
    participant WP as Workpieces

    OP->>PLC: Start command and set parameters
    PLC->>JAW: Close jaws apply clamping force F_clamp
    JAW->>WP: Grip and establish metallic contact
    PLC->>XMR: Energize primary turn ON current
    XMR->>WP: Deliver I 5000 to 80000 A for time t_heat
    Note over WP: Interface heats to 0.85 T_solidus plastic range
    PLC->>RAM: Trigger upset acceleration
    RAM->>WP: Apply upset force F_upset 200 to 10000 kN
    Note over WP: Plastic metal extrudes as flash collar
    PLC->>XMR: Cut off current or hold forging current
    RAM->>WP: Hold force during cooling
    Note over WP: Joint solidifies under compression
    PLC->>JAW: Open jaws retract
    OP->>OP: Remove welded plate and trim flash
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of the RUW Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[Process Inputs]
        I1[Workpiece Geometry Area and Length]
        I2[Material Grade Mild or High Tensile Steel]
        I3[Surface Condition Cleanliness]
    end

    subgraph PARAM[Control Parameters]
        P1[Welding Current I]
        P2[Heating Time t_heat]
        P3[Upset Force F_upset]
        P4[Upset Distance delta_L]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[Process Core]
        PR1[Electrical Stage I2R Heating at Interface]
        PR2[Thermal Stage Temperature Rise to Plastic Range]
        PR3[Mechanical Stage Plastic Upset and Forging]
        PR4[Metallurgical Stage Solid State Bond Formation]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS[Process Outputs]
        O1[Joint Strength comparable to parent metal]
        O2[Upset Collar flash trimmed later]
        O3[Grain Refinement across interface]
    end

    I1 --> PR1
    I2 --> PR1
    I3 --> PR1
    P1 --> PR1
    P2 --> PR1
    P3 --> PR3
    P4 --> PR3
    PR1 --> PR2
    PR2 --> PR3
    PR3 --> PR4
    PR4 --> O1
    PR4 --> O2
    PR4 --> O3
```

### **Engineering interpretation of the architecture:**
- **Inputs** (left) define *what is being welded*.
- **Parameters** (centre-left) are the *knobs* the operator / PLC controls.
- **Process core** (centre) shows the *four sequential physical phenomena* in a single RUW cycle.
- **Outputs** (right) are the *desired engineering outcomes* — strength, geometry, microstructure.

This functional map is **what KTU expects** when a question says "draw a block diagram of the RUW process and explain each block."

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
**Define Resistance Upset Welding. Mention any two applications in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Resistance Upset Welding is a solid-state resistance welding process in which two workpieces are butted end-to-end between water-cooled copper jaws, a heavy low-voltage high-amperage current is passed through the joint to generate I²R heat, and an axial upsetting (forging) force is applied to extrude the plasticized metal from the interface and forge a sound joint.
- **Applications (½ mark each, any two):**
  1. Longitudinal splicing of hull plates in plate mills.
  2. Joining of rolled sections and flats for ship superstructures.
  3. Manufacture of studded anchor chains for offshore vessels.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
**List the four phases of the Resistance Upset Welding cycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ¾ mark per correct phase):**
1. **Setup and clamping** — jaws close and apply clamping force.
2. **Heating** — current flows, I²R heat raises interface to plastic range.
3. **Upsetting** — axial force extrudes plastic metal as flash.
4. **Hold, cool and retract** — force maintained during cool, jaws retract, weld removed.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** "Internal choice means the student may answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full." Both sets below are full 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) (7 marks)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of **Resistance Upset Welding**. Clearly label the workpieces, jaws, joint interface, and the direction of upsetting force.

**(b) (7 marks)** Compare **Resistance Upset Welding** and **Flash Butt Welding** in terms of (i) heat source, (ii) surface preparation, (iii) metal loss, and (iv) typical applications.

#### **Model Answer — (a) Working Principle**

**Sketch (describe verbally for written exam, or draw):**
- Two rectangular workpieces W1 and W2 are placed end-to-end.
- W1 is gripped by left jaw J1, W2 by right jaw J2.
- The joint interface is between W1 and W2.
- Heavy arrows show the upsetting force F_up pushing the jaws together along the axial direction.
- Electrical leads go from a transformer secondary to each jaw.

**Working (7 marks — distribute):**
- **Clamping (1 mark):** Jaws close, apply clamping force, establish metallic contact.
- **Heating (2 marks):** Low-voltage (2–12 V), high-amperage (5,000–80,000 A) AC current flows through the circuit. Heat Q = I²Rt is generated, most concentrated at the interface due to high contact resistance. Interface temperature rises to 0.85 T_solidus (≈ 1200 °C for steel).
- **Upsetting (2 marks):** At the end of heating, an axial upset force (200–10,000 kN) is applied. Plastic metal is extruded as a flash collar. The forge consolidates the bond, breaks up oxide films, and aligns grains.
- **Hold and cool (1 mark):** Force maintained during cooling; joint solidifies under compression.
- **Trim (1 mark):** Flash is trimmed, and the spliced plate is dispatched.

#### **Model Answer — (b) Comparison**

| Feature (1.75 marks each) | RUW | FBW |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Heat source | Internal I²R heating at interface; no arc | Electric arc / flash across small gap |
| (ii) Surface prep | Requires clean, square, oxide-free faces | Tolerates rougher surfaces; flash burns off oxides |
| (iii) Metal loss | Minimal (only upset collar, 3–25 mm) | Substantial flash burn-off (5–10 % of length) |
| (iv) Applications | Hull plate mill splices, sections, bars | Rails, large-diameter pipes, wheel rims |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) (7 marks)** A Resistance Upset Welding operation joins two mild-steel ship plates, each of cross-section **25 mm × 1200 mm**. The recommended upset pressure is **150 MPa**. Compute (i) the required upset force in kN, and (ii) the maximum allowable current if the average circuit resistance is **80 µΩ**, heating time is **8 s**, and the thermal efficiency of the process is **0.70**. Assume the joint must be heated to 1200 °C from 25 °C, and use c = 460 J/(kg·K) for steel, ρ = 7850 kg/m³ for steel.

**(b) (7 marks)** Explain the effect of (i) excessive welding current, and (ii) insufficient upset force, on the quality of a resistance upset weld.

#### **Model Answer — (a) Numerical**

**Given:**
- t_plate = 25 mm = 0.025 m
- w_plate = 1200 mm = 1.2 m
- p_up = 150 MPa = 150 × 10⁶ Pa
- R_T = 80 µΩ = 80 × 10⁻⁶ Ω
- t_heat = 8 s
- η = 0.70
- T_initial = 25 °C, T_final = 1200 °C → ΔT = 1175 K
- c = 460 J/(kg·K)
- ρ = 7850 kg/m³

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area (½ mark):**
$$A = 0.025 \times 1.2 = 0.030 \; \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Upset force (1.5 marks):**
$$F_{upset} = p_{up} \cdot A = 150 \times 10^{6} \times 0.030 = 4.5 \times 10^{6} \; \text{N} = 4500 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of joint region (assume heating zone depth ≈ 25 mm on each side, total 50 mm length) (½ mark):**
$$V = A \times L_{heat} = 0.030 \times 0.050 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of heated material (½ mark):**
$$m = \rho \cdot V = 7850 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 11.775 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Required heat (1 mark):**
$$Q_{req} = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T = 11.775 \times 460 \times 1175 \approx 6.36 \times 10^{6} \; \text{J}$$

**Step 6 — Required current (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{req} &= \eta \, I^{2} R_T \, t \\
I^{2} &= \dfrac{Q_{req}}{\eta \, R_T \, t} = \dfrac{6.36 \times 10^{6}}{0.70 \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 8} \\
       &= \dfrac{6.36 \times 10^{6}}{4.48 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1.42 \times 10^{10} \\
I &= \sqrt{1.42 \times 10^{10}} \approx 1.19 \times 10^{5} \; \text{A} = 119{,}000 \; \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) F_upset ≈ **4500 kN**
- (ii) I_max ≈ **119,000 A (≈ 1.2 × 10⁵ A)**

> [!NOTE]
> This enormous current explains why RUW machines require 10+ MVA transformers and why secondary busbars are massive water-cooled copper. (Numerical answers may vary slightly depending on the assumed heated length used.)

#### **Model Answer — (b) Effect of Process Parameters**

**(i) Excessive welding current (3.5 marks):**
- Joint temperature rises **above** the desired plastic range, approaching the solidus → **localized melting** at the interface.
- Forms a **cast dendritic structure** (instead of forged) in the upset region → weak, brittle joint.
- Excessive **flash / burn-off**, with wasted metal.
- Possibility of **expulsion** of molten metal from the joint → voids and porosity.
- Risk of **arc damage** to the copper jaws.
- *Net effect:* Joint quality **deteriorates sharply**; tensile strength and impact toughness both fall.

**(ii) Insufficient upset force (3.5 marks):**
- Plastic metal at interface is **not fully extruded** → residual micro-voids, oxide films, and lack of metallurgical bond.
- Joint shows a **discrete interface line** (visible on macro-etch) — sign of unbonded region.
- **Upset collar is small or absent** — quick visual quality check.
- In service, the joint may **delaminate or crack** under cyclic loading (fatigue) or impact.
- *Net effect:* Joint quality **deteriorates**; bond is incomplete; failure under load is likely.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing RUW with Flash Butt Welding** in long-answer questions. Memorize: RUW has **no arc**, FBW **has continuous flash**.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the flash collar is trimmed** after the weld. Examiners deduct ½ to 1 mark for missing this.
> 3. **Mixing up units** in numericals. Always convert mm² → m², µΩ → Ω, and use **SI** throughout.
> 4. **Stating "molten pool forms"** for RUW. **WRONG** — RUW is a *solid-state* process. The interface is *plastic* but not melted. (FBW melts.)
> 5. **Skipping the held-force / cool phase** in process descriptions. It is part of the cycle.
> 6. **Forgetting the water cooling of copper jaws** when describing the machine. Examiners often allocate 1 mark specifically for "water-cooled copper jaws" wording.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this section. Read it the night before the exam.**

- [x] **RUW = Resistance Upset Welding = solid-state resistance butt welding with forging.**
- [x] **Heat source:** I²R (Joule) heating, **concentrated at the interface** because contact resistance is highest there.
- [x] **No arc, no melting** — interface is heated to ~0.85 T_solidus (plastic range), not liquid.
- [x] **Machines use** low-voltage (2–12 V), high-amperage (5 kA – 80 kA) AC, water-cooled copper jaws, and servo-hydraulic upset rams.
- [x] **Four phases:** (1) Clamp → (2) Heat → (3) Upset → (4) Hold & Cool.
- [x] **Core formula:** $\;Q = I^{2} R t\;$ (Joule's First Law) — always at the centre of any numerical.
- [x] **Upset force:** $F = p_{up} \cdot A$, with $p_{up} = 70$–$200$ MPa for mild steel.
- [x] **Total circuit resistance:** $R_T = 2 R_{c,j} + R_{b,1} + R_{b,2} + R_{c,i}$ — **interface contact resistance dominates**.
- [x] **Thermal efficiency** η = 0.55 to 0.85.
- [x] **Joint temperature target:** ~0.85 × T_solidus (≈ 1200 °C for steel).
- [x] **Applications in shipbuilding:** plate mill splicing (the **single most important use**), section joining, anchor chain manufacture, ring-rolling feedstock.
- [x] **RUW ≠ FBW** — RUW has no flash during heat; FBW has continuous arcing/flash. Use this contrast in any comparison question.
- [x] **Upset collar** is the visible evidence of a sound weld; trimmed off in shipyard practice.
- [x] **Effect of parameters:** ↑I → too hot, cast structure, weak. ↑F → better bonding (up to a limit). ↑t → same as ↑I. Misalignment → hot spots, asymmetric flash.
- [x] **Typical KTU keywords to drop:** "**forged bond**", "**fine equiaxed grain**", "**water-cooled copper jaws**", "**plastic range**", "**contact resistance dominates**".
- [x] **Always** state units in numericals; convert mm² → m², µΩ → Ω, A/mm² → A/m² before substituting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash Butt Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Flash Butt Welding # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flash Butt Welding (FBW)** is a *resistance welding* process in which coalescence is produced simultaneously over the entire area of the abutting surfaces through the combined action of **Joule heating** (resistance to electric current flow across the interface) and the **flashing action** (localized arcing and progressive expulsion of molten metal), followed by the application of an **upset (forging) force** that consolidates the joint. In KTU 2024 scheme classification, Flash Butt Welding belongs to **Resistance Welding — Solid-State / Fusion Hybrid Category**, governed by **IS 816** (Code of practice for use of metal arc welding for general construction in mild steel) and the more specific **IS 9595 / AWS C6.1** for resistance welding practices. It is treated as a **high-productivity mass-production joining technique**, ideal for the repetitive seam and section work characteristic of shipbuilding. The joint classification as per the syllabus is: | Parameter | Specification | |---|---| | Process Family | Resistance Welding | | Sub-Process | Flash Butt Welding (FBW) | | Heat Source | Electrical Resistance + Arc Flashing | | Filler Metal | **Not required** | | Joint Type | Butt (square, no bevel) | | Typical Position | Flat / Horizontal (machine-bound) | ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Sparkler Joint":** Imagine you hold two thin metal rods end-to-end and pass a strong electric current through them while gently pulling them apart. Tiny sparks fly off, the ends glow cherry-red and orange, and a small shower of molten droplets (sparks) is expelled outward. Once the ends are at the perfect welding temperature, you slam the two rods together with a sudden push. The soft, plastic metal welds itself into one continuous rod — and the sparks you saw earlier were the impurities, oxides, and surface dirt being **flashed away**, leaving clean virgin metal to bond. This everyday picture captures the two essential stages of FBW: 1. **Flashing stage** — Current flows across a small gap; arcing melts and expels the contaminated surface layer. 2. **Upset stage** — A large forging force consolidates the freshly cleaned, plasticized metal into a homogeneous joint. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Definitions - **Flashing Velocity (v)** — The relative speed at which the two parts approach each other during flashing. Typical values: **0.5 to 4.0 mm/s** for shipbuilding plates. - **Flash Loss** — Material lost as sparks/slag during flashing. Typical: **2–8 mm per joint face**, depending on cross-section. - **Upset Force (F_u)** — Forging load applied at the end of flashing. Typical: **30–80 MPa of cross-sectional area** for mild steel. - **Upset Distance (d_u)** — Stroke traveled by the movable platen during upsetting. Typical: **3–12 mm**. - **Flash Time (t_f)** — Duration of the flashing stage, usually **1–5 seconds** for ship plates. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown The FBW process can be broken down into **four operational phases**. Each phase has a distinct thermal-mechanical role: ### Phase 1 — Initialization (Setup & Clamping) - The two workpieces (e.g., two hull plates) are trimmed square and placed end-to-end in the FBW machine's clamping dies. - The dies conduct the welding current and provide mechanical support. - The movable platen (ram) is set to a precise starting position with a small preset gap (**stand-off gap**, typically **2–6 mm**) between the abutting faces. ### Phase 2 — Pre-Heating (Initial Current Application) - A **low-voltage, high-current** AC (or sometimes DC) supply is switched on. - Current passes through a few small contact bridges across the gap. - These bridges heat rapidly due to **I²R losses**, melt, and rupture. - Vaporization of metal creates micro-arcs across the gap. ### Phase 3 — Flashing (Core Heat-Generating Stage) - The movable platen advances slowly toward the stationary platen at the **flashing velocity**. - New contact bridges continuously form and rupture, sustaining a cascade of micro-arcs across the entire interface. - This arcing rapidly heats the metal to the **forging temperature** (typically **1200–1300 °C** for mild steel — well below the melting point). - Surface oxides, dirt, and contaminants are **expelled as sparks** (the "flash"). - The flashing action provides **self-cleaning** of the joint interface — one of FBW's defining advantages. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why flash instead of just press?** If you simply pressed two un-cleaned surfaces together under current, the contaminants would be trapped inside the joint, causing porosity, inclusions, and weak welds.
