Flashing physically **ejects** the contaminated layer before upset consolidates the clean parent metal. ### Phase 4 — Upset (Forging Consolidation) - At the precise moment the interface reaches forging temperature, the platen motion is **rapidly accelerated** into the upset stroke. - A **large forging force (F_u)** is applied — typically **2–4× the steady flashing load**. - The plastic metal is squeezed together; oxide inclusions and any remaining molten film are **squeezed out** as a flash collar (upset burr) around the joint. - The current is cut off, but **upset pressure is maintained** while the joint cools under constraint. - The result is a **fine-grained, fully consolidated metallurgical bond** with no cast structure — essentially a forged weld. ## 2.2 Process Parameters — The "Five Pillars" of FBW Control The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes mastery of **five governing parameters**, each directly affecting weld quality: 1. **Primary Voltage (V)** — Determines current; typically **5–15 V** secondary on the welding transformer. 2. **Welding Current (I)** — Typically **10,000–60,000 A** for ship plates (very high amperage, low voltage). 3. **Flashing Velocity (v)** — Dictates heat input distribution; too fast ⇒ cold joint; too slow ⇒ excessive metal loss. 4. **Upset Force (F_u)** — Determines consolidation; insufficient force ⇒ porous weld. 5. **Upset Distance (d_u)** — Volume of plastic metal displaced; should equal or slightly exceed flash loss. ## 2.3 Types of Flash Butt Welding The KTU syllabus requires knowledge of the **three principal variants**: | Variant | Principle | Typical Use in Shipbuilding | |---|---|---| | **Continuous Flash Butt Welding** | Constant flashing velocity throughout | Most common — hull plate seams | | **Pulsed (Intermittent) Flash Butt Welding** | Alternating current pulses during flashing | Heavier sections, better temperature control | | **Precise Flash Butt Welding (Küttner / Mafi)** | Extremely short flashing time, accurate upset | Pipe joints, thin-walled sections, high-precision work | ## 2.4 Heat Generation — Joule's Law (KTU Formula Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The single most important equation in FBW theory is **Joule's First Law**, which governs the heat generated at the interface. $$ H = I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t \quad \text{[Joules]} $$ Where: - $H$ = Heat energy generated (J) - $I$ = Welding current (A) - $R$ = Total electrical resistance across the gap (Ω) - $t$ = Time the current flows (s) The instantaneous power dissipated is: $$ P = I^{2} \cdot R \quad \text{[Watts]} $$ The total electrical resistance $R$ has three components in series: $$ R = R_{bulk1} + R_{interface} + R_{bulk2} $$ During flashing, $R_{interface}$ dominates because of the many micro-arc contact bridges. ### 2.4.1 Heat Required to Reach Forging Temperature For a workpiece of cross-sectional area $A$, specific heat $c$, density $\rho$, and a temperature rise $\Delta T$: $$ H_{required} = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T = (A \cdot L \cdot \rho) \cdot c \cdot \Delta T $$ Where $L$ is the heated length at the interface. Equating heat input to heat required (ignoring losses): $$ I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t_{f} \;\geq\; (A \cdot L \cdot \rho) \cdot c \cdot \Delta T $$ ### 2.4.2 Upset Force Design Equation For a joint of cross-sectional area $A$, the upset pressure $p_u$ needed for full consolidation: $$ F_{u} = p_{u} \cdot A \quad \text{[N]} $$ For mild steel at forging temperature, $p_u \approx 30 \text{–} 80 \text{ MPa}$. ### 2.4.3 Flash Loss Estimation $$ \Delta L_{flash} = v_{flash} \cdot t_{flash} \quad \text{[mm]} $$ For ship plate work, typical flash loss is **2–8 mm per joint face**. ### 2.4.4 Upset Volume Relation $$ V_{upset} = A \cdot d_{u} \;\geq\; V_{flash\ lost} $$ ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this table for KTU exam night revision.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $H = I^{2} R t$ | Joule's heat generated | J | | 2 | $P = I^{2} R$ | Instantaneous power dissipated | W | | 3 | $R = R_{bulk1} + R_{int} + R_{bulk2}$ | Total resistance | Ω | | 4 | $H_{req} = m c \Delta T$ | Heat to raise workpiece temperature | J | | 5 | $F_{u} = p_{u} A$ | Upset forging force | N | | 6 | $\Delta L_{flash} = v_{flash} \cdot t_{flash}$ | Material lost during flashing | mm | | 7 | $V_{upset} = A \cdot d_{u}$ | Volume displaced during upset | mm³ | | 8 | $\eta = H_{useful} / H_{total}$ | Thermal efficiency of process | — | ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Shipbuilding Engineering Flash Butt Welding is the **workhorse process** for shipyard plate and section fabrication because of: - **Speed** — A typical 12 mm thick × 6 m long hull plate seam is welded in **30–60 seconds** vs. several minutes for arc welding. - **No consumables** — Saves filler wire, flux, and shielding gas costs. - **Consistency** — Machine-controlled, less operator-dependent than arc welding. - **Strength** — Forged weld zone often **matches or exceeds** parent metal strength. - **Compatibility** — Used for mild steel, HSLA, and stainless steel hull plates; also for **T-sections, L-sections, and bulb-plate stiffeners**. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Heat Required vs. Heat Delivered > **Objective:** Derive a relation between FBW parameters (current, flashing time) and the temperature rise achieved in the workpiece interface. ### Step 1 — Define Workpiece Mass at the Heated Zone Consider a plate of cross-sectional area $A$ and heated interface length $L$. The volume of metal being heated is $V = A \cdot L$. The corresponding mass is: $$ m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot L $$ Where: - $\rho$ = density of steel ≈ **7850 kg/m³** (mild steel) - $A$ = cross-sectional area in m² - $L$ = heated interface length in m ### Step 2 — Define Required Heat for Temperature Rise To raise this mass from ambient temperature $T_0$ to forging temperature $T_f$: $$ H_{required} = m \cdot c \cdot (T_{f} - T_{0}) = \rho \cdot A \cdot L \cdot c \cdot \Delta T $$ For mild steel: - $c$ = specific heat ≈ **460 J/(kg·K)** - $T_0 \approx 25 \text{ °C}$ (room temperature) - $T_f \approx 1250 \text{ °C}$ (forging temperature) - $\Delta T \approx 1225 \text{ K}$ ### Step 3 — Define Heat Delivered by Welding Current By Joule's law, the heat delivered during the flashing time $t_f$ is: $$ H_{delivered} = I^{2} \cdot R_{int} \cdot t_{f} $$ Here, $R_{int}$ is the dominant interface resistance during flashing, approximately: $$ R_{int} = \frac{\rho_{el}}{A} \cdot L $$ Wait — let me reconsider. For an interface with micro-arcs, the effective resistance is non-linear, but a useful engineering approximation is: $$ R_{int} \approx k_{R} \cdot \frac{1}{A} $$ Where $k_R$ is a process-specific constant that accounts for the contact bridge behaviour (units: Ω·m²). ### Step 4 — Equate Delivered and Required Heat (Energy Balance) For a perfectly efficient process (no losses): $$ I^{2} \cdot R_{int} \cdot t_{f} = \rho \cdot A \cdot L \cdot c \cdot \Delta T $$ Substituting the resistance approximation: $$ I^{2} \cdot \frac{k_{R}}{A} \cdot t_{f} = \rho \cdot A \cdot L \cdot c \cdot \Delta T $$ ### Step 5 — Solve for Flashing Time $$ t_{f} = \frac{\rho \cdot A^{2} \cdot L \cdot c \cdot \Delta T}{I^{2} \cdot k_{R}} $$ This shows the **strong dependence** of flashing time on cross-sectional area squared ($A^2$) — a key practical insight for ship plate sizing. ### Step 6 — Solve for Required Current $$ I = \sqrt{\frac{\rho \cdot A^{2} \cdot L \cdot c \cdot \Delta T}{t_{f} \cdot k_{R}}} $$ This is the **machine rating equation** — it tells the design engineer what current capacity the FBW transformer must deliver for a given ship plate. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A mild-steel ship hull plate of cross-section $A = 6000 \text{ mm}^2$ and heated interface length $L = 8 \text{ mm}$ is to be flash butt welded from 25 °C to forging temperature 1250 °C. Specific heat $c = 460 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$, density $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The interface resistance constant is $k_R = 1.8 \times 10^{-6} \;\Omega \cdot m^2$. If the available current is $I = 25{,}000 \text{ A}$, find the required flashing time. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Convert all units to SI:** - $A = 6000 \text{ mm}^2 = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$ - $L = 8 \text{ mm} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ - $\Delta T = 1250 - 25 = 1225 \text{ K}$ **Step 2 — Compute mass (conceptually):** $$ m = \rho A L = 7850 \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ m = 7850 \times 48 \times 10^{-6} = 0.3768 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 3 — Compute required heat:** $$ H_{req} = m c \Delta T = 0.3768 \times 460 \times 1225 $$ $$ H_{req} = 0.3768 \times 563{,}500 = 212{,}327 \text{ J} $$ **Step 4 — Apply energy balance:** $$ t_{f} = \frac{\rho A^{2} L c \Delta T}{I^{2} k_{R}} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute values:** $$ t_{f} = \frac{7850 \times (6 \times 10^{-3})^{2} \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 460 \times 1225}{(25{,}000)^{2} \times 1.8 \times 10^{-6}} $$ **Step 6 — Numerator calculation:** $$ (6 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 36 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ 7850 \times 36 \times 10^{-6} \times 8 \times 10^{-3} = 7850 \times 288 \times 10^{-9} = 2.2608 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ 2.2608 \times 10^{-3} \times 460 \times 1225 = 2.2608 \times 10^{-3} \times 563{,}500 = 1273.96 $$ **Step 7 — Denominator calculation:** $$ (25{,}000)^{2} = 6.25 \times 10^{8} $$ $$ 6.25 \times 10^{8} \times 1.8 \times 10^{-6} = 6.25 \times 1.8 \times 10^{2} = 1125 $$ **Step 8 — Final division:** $$ t_{f} = \frac{1273.96}{1125} \approx 1.13 \text{ seconds} $$ > **Result:** The flashing time needed is approximately **1.13 seconds**, which is consistent with shipyard experience (typical flashing times for hull plates are 1–3 seconds). ## 3.3 Upset Force Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** For the same plate ($A = 6000 \text{ mm}^2$), determine the upset forging force if the upset pressure is $p_u = 50 \text{ MPa}$. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Convert units:** $$ A = 6000 \text{ mm}^2 = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ p_{u} = 50 \text{ MPa} = 50 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Apply formula:** $$ F_{u} = p_{u} \cdot A = 50 \times 10^{6} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ F_{u} = 300{,}000 \text{ N} = 300 \text{ kN} $$ > **Result:** The upset ram must deliver a forging force of approximately **300 kN** — equivalent to the weight of about 30 metric tons. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 FBW Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Plate A: Hull Section"] --> B["Clamping Die Stationary"] C["Plate B: Hull Section"] --> D["Clamping Die Movable Platen"] B --> E["Stand-Off Gap 2-6 mm"] D --> E E --> F{"Transformer Energized?
Low V High I AC"} F -->|Yes| G["Phase 1: Pre-Heat
Contact Bridges Form"] G --> H["Phase 2: Flashing
v_flash 0.5 to 4 mm per s
Sparks Expelled
T reaches 1200 to 1300 C"] H --> I{"Interface at Forging Temp?"} I -->|Yes| J["Phase 3: Upset Stroke
F_u 30 to 80 MPa
d_u 3 to 12 mm"] J --> K["Current Cut Off
Pressure Held"] K --> L["Phase 4: Cool Under Constraint
Forged Weld Zone Forms"] L --> M["Flash Collar Removed
by Grinding"] M --> N["Completed FBW Joint"] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic — Cross-Section of an FBW Joint ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Plate A
Parent Metal"] -->|"AButting Face
Trimmed Square"| J["Forge Weld Zone
Fine Grained
No Cast Structure"] J -->|"B Abutting Face"| P2["Plate B
Parent Metal"] J --> O["Upset Burr
Flash Collar
Trimmed Off"] P1 -->|"Heat Affected Zone"| HAZ1["HAZ A
Coarse Grain"] P2 -->|"Heat Affected Zone"| HAZ2["HAZ B
Coarse Grain"] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — FBW Machine Subsystems ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PowerUnit["Power Supply Subsystem"] PU1["Mains Transformer"] PU2["Welding Transformer
Step Down to 5-15 V"] PU3["Current Control SCR"] end subgraph MechanicalUnit["Mechanical Subsystem"] MU1["Stationary Platen"] MU2["Movable Platen Ram"] MU3["Hydraulic Cylinder
Provides F_u"] MU4["Clamping Jaws"] end subgraph ControlUnit["Control Subsystem"] CU1["PLC Controller"] CU2["Stroke Position Sensor"] CU3["Temperature Sensor
Optical Pyrometer"] CU4["Current Sensor"] end PU3 --> MU2 MU4 --> MU1 MU4 --> MU2 CU1 --> PU3 CU2 --> CU1 CU3 --> CU1 CU4 --> CU1 ``` ## 4.4 Process Parameter Influence Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Current I"] --> Q["Joint Quality"] B["Flashing Velocity v_flash"] --> Q C["Flashing Time t_flash"] --> Q D["Upset Force F_u"] --> Q E["Upset Distance d_u"] --> Q A --> L["Flash Loss mm"] B --> L C --> L D --> S["Joint Strength"] E --> S A --> S ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **Define Flash Butt Welding. Mention any two of its salient features.** > **Model Answer:** > Flash Butt Welding is a resistance welding process in which the heat required for joining is generated by passing a high current across the abutting surfaces of two workpieces, combined with a controlled flashing action that expels surface impurities. The molten interface is then consolidated by an upset forging force. > > **Salient features:** > 1. **Self-cleaning** — the flashing action removes oxides, scale, and dirt before upset, producing a clean forge weld. > 2. **No filler metal required** — the joint is formed entirely from parent metal. > 3. **High productivity** — typically 30–60 seconds per joint in ship plate work, ideal for shipyard mass production. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Differentiate between the flashing stage and the upset stage in FBW.** > **Model Answer:** > > | Aspect | Flashing Stage | Upset Stage | > |---|---|---| > | Purpose | Heat the interface; expel impurities | Consolidate the joint | > | Action | Slow approach with arcing | Rapid forging press | > | Temperature | Rises to 1200–1300 °C | Held near forging temperature | > | Force | Light, steady load | Heavy upset force (30–80 MPa) | > | Output | Sparks, scale ejection | Flash collar formation, weld consolidation | > > **Conclusion:** Flashing prepares the joint thermally and chemically; upsetting performs the actual metallurgical bonding. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. Many students confuse **flashing** with **upsetting** and lose 3–4 marks for vague descriptions. > 2. Heat input equation $H = I^2 R t$ is often written without defining the terms — examiners deduct for "undefined variables." > 3. Students forget to convert mm² to m² in numerical problems, causing the final answer to be off by a factor of **10⁶**. > 4. Always draw a **labelled sketch** of the FBW process — this alone can earn 2–3 easy marks. --- ### QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **working principle of Flash Butt Welding**. Discuss the role of flashing and upsetting in producing a sound joint. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A mild-steel plate of cross-sectional area $A = 4500 \text{ mm}^2$ and interface heated length $L = 6 \text{ mm}$ is flash butt welded. Specific heat $c = 460 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$, density $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $k_R = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \;\Omega \cdot m^2$. The available welding current is $I = 22{,}000 \text{ A}$. Calculate the **required flashing time** to raise the interface from 25 °C to 1250 °C. Also determine the **upset force** if the upset pressure is 45 MPa. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Define the process:** Flash Butt Welding is a resistance welding process where two prepared workpieces are clamped in dies, brought close together with a small gap, and a high current is passed. The flashing action melts and expels the surface impurities, and an upset force consolidates the joint. **Step 2 — Describe each stage:** - **Pre-heat:** Initial current creates contact bridges that melt and rupture. - **Flashing:** Continuous arcing expels molten metal as sparks, heating the interface to forging temperature (~1250 °C). Surface oxides are ejected. - **Upset:** A rapid forging press consolidates the plastic metal into a forged joint. Flash collar forms externally. - **Cool:** Pressure maintained until joint solidifies. **Step 3 — Role of flashing:** Flashing serves three functions: (1) heats the interface to forging temperature, (2) cleans the surface by expelling oxides and dirt, (3) creates a controlled layer of plasticized metal for upset. **Step 4 — Role of upsetting:** Upsetting performs the actual solid-state forging weld — it consolidates the clean plastic metal, expels any remaining inclusions outward as a flash collar, and refines the grain structure. **Step 5 — Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.1 flowchart and Section 4.2 cross-section diagrams.) #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - $A = 4500 \text{ mm}^2 = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$ - $L = 6 \text{ mm} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ - $c = 460 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ - $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $k_R = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \;\Omega \cdot m^2$ - $I = 22{,}000 \text{ A}$ - $T_0 = 25 \text{ °C}$, $T_f = 1250 \text{ °C}$, $\Delta T = 1225 \text{ K}$ **Step 1 — Apply the flashing time equation:** $$ t_{f} = \frac{\rho \cdot A^{2} \cdot L \cdot c \cdot \Delta T}{I^{2} \cdot k_{R}} $$ **Step 2 — Compute numerator:** $$ A^{2} = (4.5 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 20.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4 $$ $$ \rho A^{2} L = 7850 \times 20.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} = 7850 \times 121.5 \times 10^{-9} $$ $$ = 9.53775 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ \text{Numerator} = 9.53775 \times 10^{-4} \times 460 \times 1225 $$ $$ = 9.53775 \times 10^{-4} \times 563{,}500 = 537.55 $$ **Step 3 — Compute denominator:** $$ I^{2} = (22{,}000)^{2} = 4.84 \times 10^{8} $$ $$ \text{Denominator} = 4.84 \times 10^{8} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6} = 726 $$ **Step 4 — Divide:** $$ t_{f} = \frac{537.55}{726} \approx 0.74 \text{ seconds} $$ **Step 5 — Upset force:** $$ F_{u} = p_{u} \cdot A = 45 \times 10^{6} \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3} = 202{,}500 \text{ N} $$ $$ \boxed{t_{f} \approx 0.74 \text{ s}, \quad F_{u} \approx 202.5 \text{ kN}} $$ --- ### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** List and briefly explain the **five main process parameters** that govern the quality of a Flash Butt Weld in shipbuilding. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the **functional subsystems of an industrial Flash Butt Welding machine** as used in modern shipyards. Mention the typical current, voltage, and upset force ranges. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) The five governing parameters are: 1. **Welding Current (I):** Typically **10,000–60,000 A**. Too low ⇒ insufficient heating; too high ⇒ excessive metal loss. Direct effect on heat input per Joule's law. 2. **Flashing Velocity (v_flash):** Typically **0.5–4.0 mm/s**. Controls the rate of heat generation along the interface. Too fast ⇒ cold joint; too slow ⇒ excessive flashing loss. 3. **Flashing Time (t_f):** Typically **1–5 seconds** for ship plates. Dictates total energy delivered; must be sufficient to reach forging temperature across the section. 4. **Upset Force (F_u):** Typically **30–80 MPa** of cross-sectional area. Required to forge out inclusions and consolidate the weld. Insufficient force leads to porous, weak joints. 5. **Upset Distance (d_u):** Typically **3–12 mm**. Defines the volume of metal displaced; should be slightly greater than flash loss to ensure full consolidation. > **Quality rule of thumb:** $d_u \geq \Delta L_{flash}$, $F_u \geq 30$ MPa for mild steel. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Functional subsystems** (refer to Section 4.3 architecture): 1. **Power Supply Subsystem:** - Mains transformer steps down grid voltage. - Welding transformer further steps to **5–15 V secondary** for very high current. - SCR-based current control regulates the welding amperage. 2. **Mechanical Subsystem:** - Stationary platen and movable platen ram. - Hydraulic cylinder delivers upset force (**typically 200–1000 kN** for ship plate). - Clamping jaws hold plates rigidly. 3. **Control Subsystem:** - PLC coordinates the timing of current, motion, and upset. - Stroke position sensors monitor platen travel. - Optical pyrometer monitors interface temperature (for closed-loop control in modern Küttner machines). - Current sensors provide feedback to the SCR control. **Typical ranges for shipbuilding FBW machines:** - Current: **10,000–60,000 A** - Voltage: **5–15 V** (secondary) - Upset force: **200–1500 kN** - Plate thickness range: **3–50 mm** - Plate width: **up to 5 m** (for hull panels) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flash Butt Welding — High-Density Summary for KTU 2024** - **Definition:** Resistance welding process using Joule heating + flashing + upset force. - **Heat Source:** Electrical resistance (Joule's law: $H = I^2 R t$). - **Filler Metal:** **None required** — forged weld from parent metal. - **Key Stages:** Pre-heat → Flashing → Upset → Cool-under-constraint. - **Flashing Velocity:** **0.5–4.0 mm/s** for ship plates. - **Forging Temperature:** **1200–1300 °C** for mild steel. - **Upset Pressure:** **30–80 MPa**. - **Upset Distance:** **3–12 mm** (≥ flash loss). - **Welding Current:** **10,000–60,000 A**. - **Welding Voltage:** **5–15 V** secondary. - **Three Variants:** Continuous, Pulsed, Precise (Küttner/Mafi). - **Shipbuilding Applications:** Hull plate seams, T-section stiffeners, keel plates, pipe joints. - **Major Advantage:** **Self-cleaning** flashing + **forged joint quality** + **no consumables**. - **Major Limitation:** High capital equipment cost; limited to butt joint configurations. - **Quality Rule:** $d_u \geq \Delta L_{flash}$ and $F_u \geq 30$ MPa for sound consolidation. - **Standards Applicable:** IS 816, IS 9595, AWS C6.1. - **Examiner Tip:** Always convert **mm² → m²** before substituting into any formula.

topics.title: Percussion Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Percussion Welding** ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Percussion Welding** is a resistance-based fusion welding process in which coalescence between two faying surfaces is produced by heat obtained from an **electric arc** struck between the parts, immediately followed by the application of a **sudden percussive force** (impact pressure) that forges the molten interface together. The process is governed by the rapid sequence of: *arc ignition → arc heating → sudden impact → forging upset*. It is classified as a variant of **Arc Pressure Welding** (alongside stud welding, flash butt welding, and projection welding) under the broader family of **Resistance Welding Processes** as per AWS C1.1 and IS 2818/ISO 4063 — Process Code **42 / 42.1 (Percussion)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Sparkler Strike":** > Imagine striking a match against the side of a matchbox. The friction *arcs* and ignites the chemicals (heat generation), and the very next instant, the mechanical impact of striking **presses** the burning head onto the striking surface, fusing the chemical into the strip. In percussion welding, the "match head" is one workpiece, the "matchbox strip" is the other, the spark/arc supplies heat, and the mechanical "tap" applies the upset force — all in a few milliseconds. A more engineering-oriented analogy is **drop forging of two heated rods**: the surfaces are softened instantly by an arc (not by a furnace), and then slammed together by an impact hammer — the trapped molten metal squirts out as **flash**, leaving a solid forged bond. ### 1.3 Core Process Characteristics | Parameter | Typical Value / Range | |---|---| | **Arc duration** | **2 – 30 milliseconds** (1 to 1.5 electrical cycles on 50 Hz) | | **Welding current** | **5,000 – 50,000 A** (very high, short duration) | | **Open circuit voltage** | **80 – 200 V** | | **Upset / percussion force** | **4 – 14 kgf/mm²** of weld area | | **Joint completion time** | **< 0.1 second** total cycle | | **Filler metal required** | **None** (autogenous fusion) | | **Shielding gas / flux** | **Not required** (arc is too brief for contamination) | | **Metals that can be joined** | Dissimilar metals possible (Cu–Al, Cu–Steel, etc.) | ### 1.4 Distinguishing Features vs. Other Resistance Welding Processes > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Vs Flash Butt Welding:** Percussion welding uses an *arc*, not a flashing action; the arc is struck by mechanical separation, not by gradual parting. > - **Vs Stud Welding:** Stud welding is a *slow draw-up* arc; percussion welding is *abrupt* and far shorter. > - **Vs Spot Welding:** Spot welding uses resistance heat through the interface; percussion welding uses an *open arc* above the interface. > - **Key Identifier:** The defining feature is the **percussive impact** that follows the arc — without impact, it is merely arc heating. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic) The percussion welding cycle proceeds through five distinct stages, each governed by precise electromechanical control: 1. **Stage 1 — Pre-positioning:** The two workpieces (typically a **stud/rod** and a **plate/tube end**) are aligned on a common axis with a controlled initial gap of **1.5 mm to 6 mm**. The workpiece to be moved is held in the **moving platen** driven by a pneumatic or hydraulic cylinder backed by an **accelerator spring**. 2. **Stage 2 — Arc Ignition (Percussion Trigger):** As full welding current is switched on (via an **Ignitron / Thyristor / IGBT stack**), the moving platen is suddenly accelerated **away** from the fixed part. This rapid separation stretches the contact point and draws an **arc** — analogous to a switch being snapped open under load. The arc voltage rises to **80–200 V** within microseconds. 3. **Stage 3 — Arc Heating Phase:** The open arc transfers energy to both faying surfaces through ionic bombardment and electron impact. Surface temperature rises to the **melting point of the lower-melting constituent (≈ 660 °C for Al, ≈ 1085 °C for Cu, ≈ 1538 °C for Fe)**. Molten metal begins to form a thin liquid film on each surface. The arc duration is **electrically timed** (typically 1 to 1.5 cycles of mains supply). 4. **Stage 4 — Current Cut-off & Percussive Impact:** At the preset moment, the **SCR/IGBT stack fires a reverse bias** (or a crowbar circuit) to extinguish the arc. Simultaneously, the **accelerator spring releases**, propelling the moving workpiece back at high velocity (3–15 m/s) toward the fixed part. Kinetic energy converts to forging pressure. 5. **Stage 5 — Forging / Upset:** The impact forges the molten layer, expelling it radially as **flash**, exposing clean parent metal underneath, and producing a **fine-grained forged weld zone**. A holding pressure of **4–14 kgf/mm²** is maintained for a fraction of a second while the joint solidifies. ### 2.2 The "Why & How" Behind Each Step > [!NOTE] > **Why an arc and not resistance heating?** > Because the surfaces to be joined are often **dissimilar metals** (e.g., copper to aluminium) with very different resistivities and melting points. A conventional spot weld would overheat the lower-resistance metal. The open arc delivers heat *equally to both surfaces*, regardless of their bulk resistivity — making percussion ideal for **bimetallic joints**. > [!NOTE] > **Why the abrupt separation (percussion)?** > The mechanical snap-action stretches the contact faster than the molten bridge can reform, guaranteeing **stable arc initiation**. A slow draw-out would either re-bridge with molten metal (no arc) or cause arcing instability. > [!NOTE] > **Why such high current for such a short time?** > Heat needed: $H = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$. For ~1 g of interface metal heated ~1000 K, ~1 kJ is needed. Delivering 1 kJ in 10 ms requires ~100 kW, hence **tens of thousands of amps at low voltage**. The short duration keeps the Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) minimal. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > All quantities use **SI base units** unless noted. Voltage in **V**, current in **A**, time in **s**, resistance in **Ω**, force in **N**, area in **m²**, energy in **J**. | # | Formula | Description / Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---| | 1 | $H = I^{2} R t$ | **Joule's First Law** — heat generated by the arc/contact resistance during welding. | | 2 | $P = \dfrac{H}{t} = I^{2} R$ | Average **arc power** during the heating interval. | | 3 | $E_{arc} = V_{arc} \cdot I \cdot t_{arc}$ | **Arc energy** delivered (preferred over $I^2Rt$ when arc voltage is measured). | | 4 | $F_{percussion} = m \cdot a$ | **Impact force** — product of moving mass and deceleration during the impact. | | 5 | $E_{kinetic} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | **Kinetic energy** of moving platen released as forging work. | | 6 | $P_{upset} = \dfrac{F_{percussion}}{A_{weld}}$ | **Upset pressure** on the weld interface (typical 40–140 MPa). | | 7 | $v_{impact} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,K}{m}}$ | Velocity at impact from **spring constant K** (N/m) and platen mass m (kg). | | 8 | $t_{arc} = \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f}$ | Arc duration from number of mains cycles and supply frequency (**50 Hz in India**). | | 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{H_{joint}}{H_{input}}$ | **Thermal efficiency** of percussion welding — typically **0.60 – 0.85**. | | 10 | $H_{joint} = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_{f}$ | Heat required to raise interface to melting point **and** supply latent heat of fusion $L_f$. | > [!WARNING] > **Common mistake in KTU valuation:** Students write $H = I^2 R t$ without specifying that the resistance $R$ here is **dynamic arc resistance**, which varies from ~0 (cold contact) to a few ohms during the arc — it is *not* the static bulk resistance of the workpiece. Examiners mark down if this distinction is missing. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility (Shipbuilding Context) In shipbuilding technology, percussion welding is employed for: - **Stud-to-plate welding** for cable trays, pipe supports, and non-structural fittings on decks. - **Tube-to-tubesheet joints** in marine heat exchangers and condensers (often **Cu-Ni to Cu-Ni** or **Ti tubes to Cu-Ni tubesheets**). - **Battery terminal welding** in submarine lead-acid battery banks. - **Bimetallic transition joints** — copper-to-aluminium bus-bar joints in shipboard electrical distribution where galvanic corrosion is managed by the joint itself. - **Anchor chain component repairs** where dissimilar high-strength steel links require localised forge welding without HAZ degradation. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arc Energy Required for a Given Joint **Problem Statement (Worked Example):** A percussion weld is required between a copper stud (m = 0.8 g of interfacial metal) and an aluminium plate. The interface metal must be heated from **27 °C (300 K)** to the **melting point of aluminium 660 °C (933 K)** and then supplied with its **latent heat of fusion**. Given: - Specific heat of aluminium $c_p$ ≈ **900 J/(kg·K)** - Latent heat of fusion of aluminium $L_f$ ≈ **397,000 J/kg** - Arc voltage $V_{arc}$ = **120 V**, supply frequency **50 Hz**, arc duration = **1.5 cycles** - Thermal efficiency $\eta$ = **0.75** **Find:** (a) Heat required at the interface (b) Required welding current. #### Step-by-step solution **Step 1 — Compute the temperature rise** $$\Delta T \;=\; T_{melt} \;-\; T_{initial} \;=\; 933 \;-\; 300 \;=\; 633 \; \text{K}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the heat needed to raise the temperature (sensible heat)** $$H_{sensible} \;=\; m \, c_{p} \, \Delta T \;=\; (0.8 \times 10^{-3}) \times 900 \times 633$$ Carrying out the arithmetic term by term: $$H_{sensible} \;=\; 0.0008 \times 900 \times 633$$ Multiplying $0.0008 \times 900$ first: $$0.0008 \times 900 \;=\; 0.72$$ Then $0.72 \times 633$: $$0.72 \times 633 \;=\; 455.76 \; \text{J}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the latent heat of fusion** $$H_{latent} \;=\; m \, L_{f} \;=\; (0.8 \times 10^{-3}) \times 397{,}000$$ $$H_{latent} \;=\; 0.0008 \times 397{,}000 \;=\; 317.6 \; \text{J}$$ **Step 4 — Total heat that must reach the interface** $$H_{joint} \;=\; H_{sensible} \;+\; H_{latent} \;=\; 455.76 \;+\; 317.6 \;=\; 773.36 \; \text{J}$$ **Step 5 — Account for thermal losses (input heat needed)** $$H_{input} \;=\; \dfrac{H_{joint}}{\eta} \;=\; \dfrac{773.36}{0.75} \;=\; 1031.15 \; \text{J}$$ **Step 6 — Compute arc duration in seconds** $$t_{arc} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f} \;=\; \dfrac{1.5}{50} \;=\; 0.030 \; \text{s}$$ **Step 7 — Solve Joule's equation for current** The energy equation is: $$H_{input} \;=\; V_{arc} \cdot I \cdot t_{arc}$$ Rearranging for $I$: $$I \;=\; \dfrac{H_{input}}{V_{arc} \cdot t_{arc}}$$ Substituting values: $$I \;=\; \dfrac{1031.15}{120 \times 0.030}$$ $$I \;=\; \dfrac{1031.15}{3.6} \;\approx\; 286.4 \; \text{A}$$ > [!NOTE] > Note that although we used $V \cdot I \cdot t$, when the *arc* resistance is used the equivalent $I^2 R t$ form gives the same result, since $V = IR$ during arcing. For KTU board exam clarity, present whichever form matches the data given in the question. **Final Answer (boxed):** $$\boxed{H_{joint} \;\approx\; 773 \; \text{J}, \qquad I \;\approx\; 286 \; \text{A}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Percussive Impact Force from a Spring-Loaded Platen **Problem Statement:** A percussion welding machine has a moving platen of mass **m = 4 kg** accelerated by a compression spring with stiffness **K = 25,000 N/m** preloaded to a deflection **x = 40 mm**. **Find:** (a) Stored spring energy (b) Impact velocity (c) Theoretical peak impact force if the platen decelerates over **s = 2 mm** of plastic upset. #### Step-by-step solution **Step 1 — Spring potential energy stored** $$E_{spring} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} K x^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 25{,}000 \times (0.040)^{2}$$ $$E_{spring} \;=\; 12{,}500 \times 0.0016 \;=\; 20 \; \text{J}$$ **Step 2 — Convert spring energy into kinetic energy** By conservation of energy: $$\tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \;=\; E_{spring}$$ $$v^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2 E_{spring}}{m} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \times 20}{4} \;=\; 10 \; \text{(m/s)}^{2}$$ $$v \;=\; \sqrt{10} \;\approx\; 3.16 \; \text{m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Peak impact force from deceleration distance** Using the work-energy principle over the upset distance $s$: $$F_{peak} \cdot s \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$ $$F_{peak} \;=\; \dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}}{s} \;=\; \dfrac{20}{0.002} \;=\; 10{,}000 \; \text{N}$$ **Step 4 — Express as upset pressure on a 50 mm² weld** $$A_{weld} \;=\; 50 \; \text{mm}^{2} \;=\; 50 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^{2}$$ $$P_{upset} \;=\; \dfrac{F_{peak}}{A_{weld}} \;=\; \dfrac{10{,}000}{50 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 2 \times 10^{8} \; \text{Pa} \;=\; 200 \; \text{MPa}$$ **Final Answer (boxed):** $$\boxed{E_{spring} = 20 \; \text{J}, \quad v = 3.16 \; \text{m/s}, \quad F_{peak} = 10 \; \text{kN}, \quad P_{upset} = 200 \; \text{MPa}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A typical percussion weld requires an upset pressure of **40–140 MPa**. The above result (200 MPa) is at the upper end, indicating this machine is suitable for **heavier bimetallic joints** such as **Cu-steel bus bars**. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Defect Causes & Remedies | Defect Observed | Probable Cause | Engineering Remedy | |---|---|---| | **Weak / porous joint** | Arc duration too short → insufficient melting | Increase cycle count (e.g., 1.5 → 2.0 cycles) | | **Excessive flash / burn-through** | Excess arc energy OR low impact velocity | Reduce current OR increase spring preload | | **Cracks at interface** | Insufficient upset pressure | Increase platen mass or spring stiffness | | **Misalignment / offset** | Poor fixturing, worn guides | Re-machine locating jig, replace bushings | | **Arc blow / instability** | Low open-circuit voltage or contamination | Clean surfaces, raise OCV to 150 V minimum | | **Humping / incomplete fusion at edges** | Uneven current density due to small contact area | Increase initial gap, use a centering pilot | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Percussion Welding Machine The figure below is a Mermaid *block-level functional architecture flow* (used in lieu of a physical cross-section drawing, per protocol). It maps the interactions between the electrical, mechanical, and control subsystems during one complete welding cycle. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Power Supply
3-phase mains 415 V 50 Hz] --> B[Step-down Transformer
80 to 200 V secondary] B --> C[Ignitron or IGBT Stack
High current switch] C --> D[Weld Head Copper Electrodes
Current path to workpieces] D --> E1[Fixed Platen and Base
holds lower workpiece] D --> E2[Moving Platen
holds upper workpiece] E2 --> F[Accelerator Spring
stores percussion energy] E2 --> G[Pneumatic or Hydraulic Cylinder
initial gap and reset] F --> H[Percussive Impact on Interface
forging action] E1 --> H H --> I[Flash Expulsion
molten metal radially ejected] I --> J[Solidified Forged Weld Zone] K[Timer and Control Unit
1 to 1.5 cycles at 50 Hz] --> C K --> G L[Operator Console
start, current, gap, force] --> K M[Safety Interlocks
two-hand start, guarding] --> K ``` ### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Time Line of One Welding Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR P0[T0
parts aligned
initial gap 3 mm] --> P1[T1
current on
platen retracts] P1 --> P2[T2
arc ignition
open arc voltage 120 V] P2 --> P3[T3
arc heating
duration 1.5 cycles] P3 --> P4[T4
current cut off
spring released] P4 --> P5[T5
percussive impact
upset pressure peak] P5 --> P6[T6
hold and cool
forged weld formed] P6 --> P7[T7
platen reset
ready for next cycle] ``` ### 4.3 Functional State Machine — Welding Head Behaviour ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Charged : Spring compresses
gap opens to 3 mm Charged --> Arcing : Current on
platen accelerates away Arcing --> Impact : Timer fires
arc cut off Impact --> Holding : Spring impacts platen
upset pressure 100 MPa Holding --> Done : Hold time 0.2 s Done --> Idle : Pneumatic reset Arcing --> Fault : Arc unstable or no ignition Impact --> Fault : Excessive flash detected Fault --> Idle : Operator acknowledge and reset ``` ### 4.4 Reading Aid — How to Interpret These Diagrams for KTU Viva > [!NOTE] > In the **architecture diagram**, follow the **left-side column** for the *electrical power path* (A → B → C → D) and the **right-side column** for the *mechanical actuation path* (G → F → E2 → H). The **bottom block** I → J represents the *metallurgical outcome*. In the **state diagram**, identify that `Arcing → Impact` is the only irreversible transition — this is the moment the arc extinguishes and the spring releases, and it is the **single critical instant** that determines weld quality. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** Define percussion welding. List any **four distinguishing features** that separate it from resistance spot welding. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Percussion welding** is a resistance-based fusion welding process in which an electric arc is struck between the two workpieces by sudden mechanical separation, and the parts are then brought together rapidly under percussive force to forge the joint. Distinguishing features (any four): 1. Heat source is an **open electric arc**, not bulk resistance through the joint. 2. Welding current is **extremely high (5,000 – 50,000 A)** for a **very short duration (1–2 mains cycles)**. 3. The process is completed in **< 0.1 s**, producing a very narrow **HAZ**. 4. **No filler metal, no flux, no shielding gas** are required. 5. Particularly suitable for joining **dissimilar metals** (e.g., Cu–Al, Cu–steel). *(Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any four features.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State **Joule's law of heating** as applicable to percussion welding and write the formula for **arc energy** delivered during the process. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Joule's First Law:** The heat generated by an electric current passing through a resistance is directly proportional to the square of the current, the resistance, and the time of flow. $$H \;=\; I^{2} R t \quad \text{[Joules]}$$ The **arc energy** delivered during the open-arc phase is expressed as: $$E_{arc} \;=\; V_{arc} \cdot I \cdot t_{arc} \quad \text{[Joules]}$$ where $V_{arc}$ is the open-arc voltage (typically **80–200 V**), $I$ is the welding current in amperes, and $t_{arc}$ is the arc duration in seconds. *(Mark split: 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for $I^2Rt$, 1 mark for $V \cdot I \cdot t$ with units.)* --- ### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Based on Process Fundamentals + Calculation* **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, Q-Set A]** **(a)** With the help of a **labelled schematic**, describe the working principle of the percussion welding process. Mention the role of the **accelerator spring** and the **arc timing circuit**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a percussion welding operation on a **copper-to-aluminium joint**, the following data is recorded: - Mass of interfacial metal melted: **m = 1.2 g** - Initial temperature: **27 °C** - Melting point of aluminium: **660 °C** - Specific heat of aluminium: **c_p = 900 J/(kg·K)** - Latent heat of fusion of aluminium: **L_f = 397 kJ/kg** - Arc voltage: **V = 110 V** - Arc duration: **2.0 cycles** on a **50 Hz** supply - Thermal efficiency of the process: **η = 0.70** Calculate the **welding current** required. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **Part (a) — Process Description (7 marks):** The working parts of a percussion welding machine consist of a **fixed platen** (clamping the base workpiece) and a **moving platen** (clamping the second workpiece), connected through an **accelerator spring** and a **pneumatic/hydraulic actuator**. The cycle proceeds as follows: 1. The two workpieces are aligned and held with a controlled gap (1.5–6 mm). The moving platen is pulled back, **compressing the accelerator spring**. *(1 mark)* 2. On pressing the start button, the **SCR/IGBT stack** conducts, allowing **high secondary current** to flow through the electrodes and workpieces. *(1 mark)* 3. Simultaneously, the moving platen is suddenly released (or actively driven) so that the contact snaps open — this **stretches the contact** and strikes an **arc**. *(1 mark)* 4. The **arc timing circuit** (set by a digital timer or synchronous contactor) counts a preset number of mains cycles (e.g., 1.5 cycles at 50 Hz = 30 ms). During this time the arc melts a thin layer on both surfaces. *(1 mark)* 5. At the end of the timed interval, the timing circuit **extinguishes the arc** (current cut-off) and simultaneously releases the **accelerator spring**, which propels the moving platen at high velocity (3–15 m/s) into the fixed workpiece. *(1 mark)* 6. The kinetic energy is converted into **forging pressure** (40–140 MPa), expelling the molten metal as flash and forming a **fine-grained forged weld**. *(1 mark)* 7. A hold time of ~0.2 s is applied under maintained pressure before the **platen resets** and the cycle is complete. *(1 mark)* **Role of the accelerator spring:** Stores mechanical energy during platen retraction and releases it instantaneously to provide the **percussive forging force** — without the spring, the pneumatic cylinder alone is too slow to deliver impact in milliseconds. **Role of the arc timing circuit:** Determines **arc duration** to within fractions of a mains cycle. If too short, the metal is not melted; if too long, the metal vaporises or excessive flash forms. Typical settings: **1 to 1.5 cycles on 50 Hz (20–30 ms)**. --- **Part (b) — Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Temperature rise** $$\Delta T \;=\; 660 \;-\; 27 \;=\; 633 \; \text{K} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ **Step 2 — Sensible heat required** $$H_{sensible} \;=\; m \, c_{p} \, \Delta T \;=\; (1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 900 \times 633$$ $$H_{sensible} \;=\; 0.0012 \times 900 \times 633 \;=\; 0.0012 \times 569{,}700 \;=\; 683.64 \; \text{J} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ **Step 3 — Latent heat required** $$H_{latent} \;=\; m \, L_{f} \;=\; (1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 397{,}000$$ $$H_{latent} \;=\; 476.4 \; \text{J} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ **Step 4 — Total useful heat at interface** $$H_{joint} \;=\; 683.64 \;+\; 476.4 \;=\; 1160.04 \; \text{J} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ **Step 5 — Input heat (accounting for efficiency)** $$H_{input} \;=\; \dfrac{H_{joint}}{\eta} \;=\; \dfrac{1160.04}{0.70} \;\approx\; 1657.2 \; \text{J} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ **Step 6 — Arc duration in seconds** $$t_{arc} \;=\; \dfrac{2.0}{50} \;=\; 0.040 \; \text{s} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ **Step 7 — Solve for current** $$I \;=\; \dfrac{H_{input}}{V \cdot t_{arc}} \;=\; \dfrac{1657.2}{110 \times 0.040} \;=\; \dfrac{1657.2}{4.4}$$ $$\boxed{I \;\approx\; 376.6 \; \text{A}} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice — Equipment & Applications* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Q-Set B]** **(a)** Describe the **construction and working** of a percussion welding machine. Draw a **functional block diagram** showing the power source, transformer, switching stack, timing circuit, platen assembly, and accelerator spring. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare percussion welding with **flash butt welding** and **stud arc welding**. List **four marine/shipbuilding applications** of percussion welding. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **Part (a) — Construction & Block Diagram (7 marks):** A percussion welding machine consists of: 1. **Three-phase power input** at 415 V, 50 Hz, fed through a main isolator and MCB. *(0.5 mark)* 2. **Step-down welding transformer** with a high turns ratio giving secondary voltage of **80–200 V** and current capacity up to **50,000 A**. *(0.5 mark)* 3. **Switching stack** — historically **Ignitron tubes**, now universally **SCR/thyristor** or **IGBT modules** — to switch the high current on and off in synchronism with the mains. *(0.5 mark)* 4. **Timer / Synchronising Circuit** — phase-locked to the mains frequency; counts the preset number of cycles to determine arc duration. *(0.5 mark)* 5. **Secondary bus bars and water-cooled copper electrodes** — carry the high current to the weld head with minimum impedance. *(0.5 mark)* 6. **Weld Head Assembly** comprising: - **Fixed platen** with locating fixture (holds lower workpiece). - **Moving platen** mounted on linear guides. - **Accelerator spring** (helical compression spring) for percussion. - **Pneumatic / hydraulic actuator** to pre-position and reset. - **Dashpot / shock absorber** to absorb rebound. *(2 marks for the head assembly description)* 7. **Operator console** with current selector, cycle counter, force adjuster, and **two-hand safety interlock**. *(0.5 mark)* 8. **Cooling system** — recirculating water for transformer, switch stack, and electrodes. *(0.5 mark)* 9. **Schematic / Block diagram** *(1 mark for a labelled diagram showing: Power → Transformer → Switch Stack → Electrodes → Platen ↔ Spring/Actuator; Timer controls Switch Stack and Actuator)*. **Functional Block Diagram:** ``` [3-Phase 415 V Mains] | [Main Isolator / MCB] | [Step-down Transformer (80–200 V sec)] | [SCR / IGBT Switching Stack] | [Water-cooled Bus Bars and Electrodes] | [Weld Head: Fixed Platen + Moving Platen] | ^ [Workpieces] [Accelerator Spring + Pneumatic Cylinder] | [Synchronous Timer / Control Unit] | [Operator Console + Safety Interlocks] ``` --- **Part (b) — Comparison & Applications (7 marks):** **Comparison Table:** *(5 marks — 1 mark each row, plus 1 mark overall presentation)* | Feature | Percussion Welding | Flash Butt Welding | Stud Arc Welding | |---|---|---|---| | **Heat source** | Open arc by abrupt separation | Progressive flashing + resistance | Drawn arc with ceramic ferrule | | **Arc duration** | 20–30 ms (very short) | Several seconds | 0.1–1.0 s | | **Current** | 5,000–50,000 A | 5,000–100,000 A | 300–3,000 A | | **Upset / forge action** | Percussive impact (impulsive) | Gradual upsetting (continuous) | Slight upsetting at end | | **Dissimilar metal capability** | Excellent | Limited (similar metals preferred) | Good | | **Joint appearance** | Small flash ring, clean | Heavy flash, requires machining | Small fillet at stud base | | **Typical applications** | Stud-to-plate, tube-to-tube | Rail, chain, rod joining | Stud welding to plates | **Marine / Shipbuilding Applications (any four):** *(2 marks — 0.5 each)* 1. **Bimetallic transition joints** for copper-to-aluminium bus bars in shipboard switchboards. 2. **Tube-to-tubesheet joints** in marine condensers and heat exchangers (Cu-Ni / Titanium). 3. **Stud-to-deck welding** for cable trays, handrail stanchions, and pipe supports. 4. **Battery terminal welding** in submarine battery banks (lead-to-copper). 5. **Repair welding** of dissimilar anchor chain links without large HAZ. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks in percussion welding questions:** > 1. **Confusing arc duration in cycles vs. seconds** — Always convert using $t = N/f$. On 50 Hz Indian mains, **1 cycle = 0.02 s**, not 0.0167 s (60 Hz). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this error. > 2. **Forgetting the efficiency factor η** — Many students compute $I$ directly from $H_{joint}$ instead of $H_{input} = H_{joint}/\eta$, leading to ~25–30% error in the answer. > 3. **Confusing percussion welding with stud welding** — They look superficially similar (arc + pressure) but stud welding uses a slow draw-up arc with a ceramic ferrule; percussion uses *abrupt separation* and no ferrule. Examiners expect the distinction. > 4. **Omitting the latent heat** in heat-balance problems — Aluminium and copper have high latent heat; neglecting it underestimates the current by 30–40%. > 5. **Drawing the diagram without labelling the arc timing circuit or accelerator spring** — both are *essential* components worth 1–2 marks each. > 6. **Using the wrong current type** — Percussion welding uses **single-phase AC** at the secondary, not three-phase. The primary is three-phase but the welding transformer is single-phase step-down. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Points — Percussion Welding** - **Definition:** Resistance-based fusion welding using an **arc struck by abrupt mechanical separation** followed by a **percussive forging impact**. *(Module 2 anchor concept.)* - **Process family:** Classified under **Arc Pressure Welding** (AWS/IS 2818); process code **42 / 42.1**. - **Five stages:** Pre-position → Arc ignition → Arc heating → Current cut-off + Impact → Forging upset + Hold. - **Arc duration:** **1 to 1.5 cycles** on 50 Hz ⇒ **20–30 ms**. - **Welding current range:** **5,000 – 50,000 A** (very high, very brief). - **Open-circuit voltage:** **80 – 200 V**. - **Upset pressure:** **40 – 140 MPa** (4 – 14 kgf/mm²). - **Total cycle time:** **< 0.1 second**. - **No filler, no flux, no shielding gas.** - **Key equation — Heat:** $H = I^{2} R t$ (Joule's law) and arc form $E = V_{arc} I t$. - **Key equation — Impact:** $E_{spring} = \tfrac{1}{2} K x^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ and $F_{peak} = E_{spring}/s_{decel}$. - **Two essential sub-systems:** **Accelerator spring** (percussion energy) and **Arc timing circuit** (cycle-counted control). Both must appear in any descriptive answer. - **Thermal efficiency:** **60–85%** — always include η in numerical problems. - **Dissimilar metal capability:** Excellent — Cu/Al, Cu/Steel, Al/Steel all feasible. - **Marine applications:** Bus-bar joints, tube-to-tubesheet, stud-to-deck, battery terminals, chain link repair. - **Distinguishing it from neighbours:** *vs Flash Butt* — no progressive flashing; *vs Stud Welding* — no ferrule, abrupt snap; *vs Spot Welding* — open arc, not bulk resistance. - **Common exam traps:** Cycle-to-second conversion at 50 Hz, omission of latent heat, omitting η, missing the timing circuit or spring in diagrams.

topics.title: High Frequency Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** High Frequency Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Frequency Welding (HFW / HFIW)** is a solid-state, electrical resistance welding process in which the heat required to fuse the parent metal is generated by passing a **high-frequency alternating current (typically 20 kHz – 500 kHz)** through the workpiece. The current is concentrated into a narrow, localized weld zone by exploiting two electromagnetic phenomena — the **skin effect** and the **proximity effect** — allowing extremely rapid, narrow, and high-integrity welds to be produced, most commonly during the continuous longitudinal or spiral seaming of metallic tubes, pipes, and profiled sections used in shipbuilding.

The frequency range falls between conventional mains-frequency AC resistance welding (**50/60 Hz**) and true induction heating, and is the cornerstone process for modern tube and pipe mills servicing the marine, offshore, and petrochemical industries.

### 1.2 Industry-Standard Frequency Bands

| Frequency Band | Process Sub-Name | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **100 – 400 kHz** | High Frequency Welding (HF) | Tube/pipe seaming, thin strip |
| **10 – 70 kHz** | Intermediate Frequency | Thicker sections |
| **50/60 Hz** | Low Frequency Resistance Welding | Standard ERW pipe |
| **> 1 MHz** | Radio Frequency (RF) | Plastics, specialized metals |

> [!NOTE]
> In shipbuilding technology, when students refer to "HF welding", they almost always mean the **400 kHz class** used in tube and longitudinal-seam welding mills.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Rivers" Model

Imagine two parallel rivers of water flowing side-by-side but in **opposite directions**. Where the rivers meet at the centre, the water pushes hard against each other and creates turbulence (heat). Now replace "river" with "high-frequency electric current", and the place where the rivers collide is the **Vee-point of the weld**.

A second analogy — **the butter-knife rule**: A knife heated only at its edge melts butter far more efficiently than a hot knife held flat. The HF current behaves like that hot edge — it lives only on the *skin* of the strip, so virtually all the heat is concentrated exactly where the two edges of the metal are about to touch, and almost nowhere else.

This is why HF welding is so efficient: **power goes only where the weld is**.

## 1.4 Why HF Welding Matters in Shipbuilding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern shipyards consume enormous quantities of **electric-resistance-welded (ERW) and HFI-welded pipes** for ballast lines, fuel systems, cooling circuits, fire-fighting mains, and structural tubes. HF welding produces these at speeds of **30 – 120 m/min** with weld quality that meets **IACS, ASTM A53, API 5L, and EN 10219** standards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Electromagnetic Pillars of HF Welding

### 2.1.1 Skin Effect
When alternating current flows through a conductor, the self-induced eddy currents force the current to crowd into a thin outer "skin" of the conductor. The depth of this skin is called the **skin depth (δ)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Skin effect is what makes HF heating possible.** At 400 kHz, the current effectively lives in only the outermost ~0.1 mm of the steel strip — exactly where we want the heat.

### 2.1.2 Proximity Effect
When two conductors carrying AC in opposite directions are placed close together, the currents are repelled into the *facing* surfaces of the two conductors. In tube welding, the two open edges of the strip act as these two conductors. The current crowds into the small wedge-shaped region where the strip edges are about to be pressed together — the **impingement point** or **Vee-point**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Logic

1. **Strip preparation**: Cold-rolled steel strip (skelp) is uncoiled and edge-trimmed.
2. **Forming**: The strip is progressively bent by a series of rolls into an open tube shape (O-shape).
3. **Vee formation**: Just before the squeeze rolls, the strip edges converge into a sharp **V-angle (typically 2° – 6°)**.
4. **Current injection**: HF current is introduced either:
    - **By contact electrodes** sliding on the strip just upstream of the Vee-point (**HF Contact / Conveyor welding**), OR
    - **By an induction coil** encircling the tube (**HF Induction welding**).
5. **Concentration**: Current flows along one strip edge, jumps the small Vee-gap to the other edge, and returns. Heat is generated **only in the Vee-gap region**.
6. **Forging**: Squeeze rolls forge the heated edges together, expelling oxides and creating a forged solid-state weld.
7. **Heat treatment**: The weld bead may be **normalized** by an in-line induction coil to refine the grain structure.
8. **Sizing & cutting**: The tube is sized, straightened, and cut to length.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\omega \mu}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$ | **Skin depth** — depth of current penetration | m |
| 2 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency | rad/s |
| 3 | $P = I^{2} R$ | Heat generated in weld zone | W |
| 4 | $R \approx \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | Resistance of conducting path (L = strip edge length carrying current) | Ω |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{f \cdot s}{n}$ | Welding line speed — *f* feed, *s* length, *n* number of pulses | m/min |
| 6 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{P_{weld}}{P_{input}}$ | Thermal efficiency of HF process (typically **80 – 90 %**) | — |
| 7 | $Q_{required} = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat required to bring weld zone to forging temperature (~1300 °C for steel) | J |
| 8 | $\eta_{electrical} = \dfrac{R_{load}}{R_{load} + R_{circuit}}$ | Electrical efficiency | — |
| 9 | $\dfrac{I_{r}}{I_{0}} = e^{-x/\delta}$ | Current density decay with depth *x* below surface | — |

**Where:**
- $\rho$ = electrical resistivity of steel ≈ **1.4 × 10⁻⁷ Ω·m** (at 20 °C)
- $\mu$ = magnetic permeability of steel ≈ **$\mu_{0}\mu_{r}$**, with $\mu_{r}$ ≈ 100 for cold-rolled mild steel
- $f$ = supply frequency in Hz (400 kHz for tube welding)
- $L$ = path length of induced eddy current loop
- $A$ = effective cross-sectional area of current path

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that **skin depth is inversely proportional to $\sqrt{f}$**. Quadrupling the frequency halves the skin depth. This is precisely why higher frequency = tighter, faster, narrower weld.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

HF welding is the backbone of mass-produced **structural hollow sections (SHS)** and **ERW pipes** used in ship hulls, offshore jackets, deck structures, and bulkheads. Compared to submerged-arc welding (SAW):
- **10× faster** line speed
- **Narrower heat-affected zone (HAZ)** → less distortion
- **Continuous seam** without stop-start defects
- **Lower post-weld machining** because the flash is small and can be trimmed in-line

In naval architecture, every kilogram of welded tube represents a trade-off between **strength-to-weight** and **corrosion fatigue**. HF welding's narrow HAZ produces a fine-grained weld that, after normalizing, often meets the fatigue performance of the parent metal — a critical requirement for **Class A and Class B pressure piping** in marine engines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Skin Depth from Maxwell's Equations

We start from the differential form of Maxwell's equations for a good conductor (no displacement current dominates) and obtain the **1-D wave equation for current density**.

**Starting assumptions:**
- Plane conductor extending infinitely in $y$ and $z$ directions.
- Sinusoidal current $J = J(x)e^{j\omega t}$ flowing in the $z$-direction.
- Magnetic field $H$ exists only in the $y$-direction.
- Material has conductivity $\sigma = 1/\rho$ and permeability $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Apply Faraday's Law** (curl of E):

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\dfrac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$$

For the assumed geometry, only the $y$-component survives:

$$\dfrac{\partial E_{z}}{\partial x} = -j\omega \mu H_{y}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ampère's Law** (neglecting displacement current at HF in a good conductor):

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$$

Again only the $y$-component survives:

$$-\dfrac{\partial H_{y}}{\partial x} = \sigma E_{z}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two equations**. Differentiate Step 2 with respect to $x$ and substitute Step 1:

$$\dfrac{\partial^{2} E_{z}}{\partial x^{2}} = j\omega \mu \sigma E_{z}$$

This is the **Helmholtz equation** with complex propagation constant:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega \mu \sigma} = (1+j)\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

**Step 4 — Solve** with boundary condition $E_{z}(0) = E_{0}$ at the surface:

$$E_{z}(x) = E_{0}\, e^{-\gamma x} = E_{0}\, e^{-x\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}\, e^{-j x\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the skin depth** as the distance $x = \delta$ at which amplitude falls to $1/e$ of surface value:

$$\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\omega \mu}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most tested formula** in HFW questions on the KTU ESE. Memorize it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Skin Depth for Steel at 400 kHz

Given:
- $\rho_{steel}$ ≈ **$1.4 \times 10^{-7}\ \Omega\cdot m$** at 20 °C
- $\mu_{r}$ ≈ **100** for cold-rolled mild steel
- $\mu_{0}$ = **$4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ H/m$**
- $f$ = **400 kHz** = $4 \times 10^{5}$ Hz

Step 1 — Compute permeability:

$$\mu = \mu_{0}\mu_{r} = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(100) = 4\pi \times 10^{-5}\ H/m$$

Step 2 — Substitute into skin depth formula:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.4 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \cdot 4 \times 10^{5} \cdot 4\pi \times 10^{-5}}}$$

Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator:

$$\pi \cdot 4 \times 10^{5} \cdot 4\pi \times 10^{-5} = 16\pi^{2} \times 10^{0} = 16\pi^{2}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.4 \times 10^{-7}}{16\pi^{2}}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{1.4 \times 10^{-7}}}{4\pi}$$

Step 4 — Compute the numerator:

$$\sqrt{1.4 \times 10^{-7}} = \sqrt{1.4}\times 10^{-3.5} \approx 1.1832 \times 3.1623 \times 10^{-4} \approx 3.742 \times 10^{-4}$$

Step 5 — Final result:

$$\delta = \dfrac{3.742 \times 10^{-4}}{4\pi} \approx \dfrac{3.742 \times 10^{-4}}{12.566}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 2.98 \times 10^{-5}\ m \approx 0.03\ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At 400 kHz, the current lives in only the outermost ~30 microns of the steel.** That is why the heat is so localized and why the line speed can be so high — the strip does not need to "soak through".

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Required for the Weld Zone

For a strip of width $w$, thickness $t$, moving at line speed $v$:

**Mass of metal entering weld zone per second** (length $L_{wz}$):

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{steel} \cdot w \cdot t \cdot v$$

**Heat needed to raise this from ambient ($T_{0}$ = 25 °C) to forging temperature ($T_{f}$ = 1300 °C):**

$$\dot{Q}_{required} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{f} - T_{0}) = \rho_{steel}\, w\, t\, v\, c_{p}\, \Delta T$$

**Numerical check** for $w$ = 100 mm, $t$ = 3 mm, $v$ = 1 m/s, $c_{p}$ = 500 J/kg·K, $\Delta T$ = 1275 K, $\rho_{steel}$ = 7850 kg/m³:

$$\dot{m} = 7850 \times 0.1 \times 0.003 \times 1 = 2.355\ kg/s$$

$$\dot{Q}_{required} = 2.355 \times 500 \times 1275 \approx 1.5\ MW$$

This is the **order of magnitude** of HF power an industrial tube mill consumes — explaining why HF generators for tube mills are typically rated **100 kW – 1.2 MW**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HFW vs Conventional ERW vs SAW

| Parameter | HF Welding (HFW) | Conventional ERW (60 Hz) | Submerged-Arc (SAW) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | **100 – 500 kHz** | 50/60 Hz | DC/AC arc |
| Skin depth (steel) | **~0.03 mm** | ~10 mm | n/a |
| Line speed | **30 – 120 m/min** | 10 – 30 m/min | 0.5 – 2 m/min |
| Weld width | **Very narrow** | Wide | Wide |
| HAZ size | **Very small** | Moderate | Large |
| Heat input | **Low** | Moderate | High |
| Distortion | **Minimal** | Moderate | Significant |
| Application thickness | 0.4 – 16 mm | 1 – 20 mm | > 6 mm (heavy) |
| Power efficiency | **80 – 90 %** | 50 – 60 % | 60 – 70 % |
| Suitable for shipbuilding pipes | **YES (preferred)** | Yes (older plants) | Limited |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of an HF Tube Welding Mill

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[coilA: Uncoiler\nPay-off Reel] --> B[nodeB: Edge Trimmer\n& Cleaning]
    B --> C[nodeC: Forming\nCage Rolls]
    C --> D[nodeD: Vee-Point\nImpingement Zone]
    D --> E[nodeE: HF Generator\n400 kHz, 600 kW]
    E -->|Contact or\nInduction Coupling| D
    D --> F[nodeF: Squeeze Rolls\nForging Pressure]
    F --> G[nodeG: In-line\nNormalizer Coil]
    G --> H[nodeH: Cooling\n& Bead Scarfing]
    H --> I[nodeI: Sizing &\nStraightening]
    I --> J[nodeJ: Cut-off\nFlying Saw]
    J --> K[nodeK: Finished\nERW/HFI Pipe]
    L[nodeL: Impedance\nMatching Network] -.-> E
    M[nodeM: Control\nConsole PLC] -.-> E
    M -.-> L
    M -.-> F
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows (`-->`) indicate **physical material flow** (the strip/tube moving through the line).
- Dotted arrows (`-.->`) indicate **control and electrical signal flow**.
- The HF generator only delivers power; the impedance network maximizes power transfer into the very low-impedance Vee-point load.
- The PLC synchronises welding current with strip speed (energy-per-unit-length control).

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Comparison of HF Contact vs HF Induction Welding

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0[stripP0: Cold-Rolled\nSteel Strip Skelp]
    P0 --> P1[nodeP1: Forming\ninto open tube]
    P1 --> P2[nodeP2: Vee-Point\napproach angle]

    subgraph HF_CONTACT[HF CONTACT Conveyor Method]
        C1[nodeC1: Sliding\nContact Electrode 1] --> P2
        C2[nodeC2: Sliding\nContact Electrode 2] --> P2
        C3[nodeC3: HF Generator\nvia transformer] --> C1
        C3 --> C2
    end

    subgraph HF_INDUCTION[HF INDUCTION Method]
        I1[nodeI1: Induction\nCoil encircles tube] -.-> P2
        I2[nodeI2: HF Generator\nfeeds coil] --> I1
    end

    P2 --> P3[nodeP3: Squeeze\nRolls forge weld]
    P3 --> P4[nodeP4: Normalize\n& Cool]
    P4 --> P5[nodeP5: Sized\nERW Pipe Output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HF Contact welding** is preferred for **carbon steel tubes up to ~500 mm diameter** (the marine industry's standard pipe size). **HF Induction welding** is preferred for **stainless steel, aluminium, and large diameters** because it avoids the wear and arcing problems of sliding contacts.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Skin & Proximity Effect Visual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BULK[Strip Cross-Section]
        B1[nodeB1: Bulk\nMetal] --> B2[nodeB2: Outer\nSkin ~0.03 mm]
    end

    subgraph EDGES[Two Open Edges Approach Each Other]
        E1[nodeE1: Left\nStrip Edge] -->|Current flows\nrightward| EVP[nodeEVP: Vee-Point\nImpingement]
        EVP -->|Current returns\nleftward| E2[nodeE2: Right\nStrip Edge]
    end

    B2 -.->|Current confined\nto skin| E1
    B2 -.->|Current confined\nto skin| E2

    H1[nodeH1: Heat\nGenerated Here] --> EVP
```

**Key takeaway:** Current enters only the skin, is steered by the proximity effect into the Vee-gap, and generates heat exactly where the squeeze rolls will forge the joint.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]
**Define High Frequency Welding. State any two advantages of HF welding over conventional resistance welding.**

**Model Answer:**
High Frequency Welding (HFW) is a resistance welding process in which the heat for joining is generated by passing a high-frequency alternating current (100 kHz – 500 kHz) through the workpiece, exploiting the **skin effect** and **proximity effect** to localize the heat at the joint interface.

**Advantages:**
1. Very localized heating → narrow HAZ → minimal distortion and grain growth.
2. Higher welding speeds (30 – 120 m/min) and improved thermal efficiency (80 – 90 %).
3. Better surface finish and consistent weld quality suitable for mass production.
4. Lower power consumption per metre of weld compared to 50 Hz ERW.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]
**Explain the term "skin effect" as applied to HF welding. Write the formula for skin depth.**

**Model Answer:**
**Skin effect:** The tendency of alternating current to flow near the surface of a conductor, with current density decaying exponentially with depth from the surface. This effect intensifies with frequency.

**Formula:**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$$

where $\rho$ = resistivity, $f$ = frequency, $\mu$ = permeability of the material.

For mild steel at 400 kHz, $\delta \approx 0.03\ mm$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**A. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **High Frequency Induction Welding** process used for tube manufacturing. Discuss the role of the **impedance matching network** in the system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Process description:**
1. Cold-rolled steel strip is uncoiled, trimmed, and formed progressively through a series of forming rolls into an open circular tube.
2. The two open edges of the tube converge at an angle of 2° – 6° to form a sharp **Vee-point** immediately before the squeeze rolls.
3. An **HF induction coil** (single or multi-turn water-cooled copper coil) encircles the tube just upstream of the Vee-point. The coil is fed by a high-frequency (200 – 500 kHz) current from a solid-state oscillator.
4. The high-frequency magnetic field of the coil induces **eddy currents** in the tube wall. By the proximity effect, these eddy currents are concentrated in a narrow band on the inner surface of the tube facing the Vee-point.
5. The induced current flows along one strip edge, crosses the Vee-gap (where its $I^{2}R$ heating raises the metal to forging temperature ~1300 °C), and returns along the other edge.
6. **Squeeze rolls** forge the heated edges together, expelling oxides and forming a solid-state forged weld.
7. An **in-line induction normalizer** may follow to refine the grain in the HAZ.

**Role of Impedance Matching Network:**
- The Vee-gap load has very low impedance (milliohms), while the HF source has 50 Ω output.
- Without matching, more than 90 % of the power would be reflected.
- The matching network (typically an **LC tank or transformer-based**) transforms the load impedance to the source impedance, achieving near-unity VSWR and **maximum power transfer**.
- It also suppresses harmonics that could disturb communication systems.

---

**A. (b)** Derive the expression for **skin depth** and calculate its value for mild steel at a frequency of 400 kHz, given $\rho = 1.4 \times 10^{-7}\ \Omega m$ and $\mu_{r} = 100$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:** (as in Section 3.1 above, summarised here)

From Faraday's and Ampère's laws, for a plane conductor:

$$\dfrac{\partial^{2} E_{z}}{\partial x^{2}} = j\omega \mu \sigma E_{z}$$

Solving with $E_z(0) = E_0$:

$$E_z(x) = E_0\, e^{-(1+j)x\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

Defining the depth at which amplitude falls to $1/e$:

$$\boxed{\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $\rho = 1.4 \times 10^{-7}\ \Omega m$
- $\mu = \mu_0\mu_r = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 100 = 4\pi \times 10^{-5}\ H/m$
- $f = 400 \times 10^{3}\ Hz$

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.4 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times 4 \times 10^{5} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-5}}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.4 \times 10^{-7}}{16\pi^{2}}} \approx 2.98 \times 10^{-5}\ m \approx 0.03\ mm$$

**Conclusion:** At 400 kHz, current penetrates only the outermost ~30 microns, localising the heat extremely well.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**B. (a)** Compare **HF contact (conveyor) welding** with **HF induction welding**. Discuss their relative suitability for shipbuilding pipe manufacture. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | HF Contact (Conveyor) | HF Induction |
|---|---|---|
| Current transfer | Sliding copper electrodes touch strip | No contact; coil induces eddy current |
| Wear / arcing | Yes — electrodes wear, need dressing | No contact wear |
| Suitability for stainless / Al | Poor (contact marks, surface damage) | Excellent |
| Suitability for carbon steel | Excellent (most shipbuilding pipes) | Acceptable |
| Electrical efficiency | **85 – 90 %** | **75 – 82 %** (coil losses) |
| Equipment cost | Lower | Higher |
| Strip speed | Up to 120 m/min | Up to 80 m/min typical |
| Diameter range | Up to ~ 500 mm | Up to ~ 1000 mm |

**Suitability for shipbuilding:**
- **HF contact** dominates the marine pipe market because most shipboard piping is **carbon steel** in diameters 25 – 500 mm (ballast, fuel, fire-main, bilge). Higher efficiency and lower capex make it the economic choice.
- **HF induction** is used when the yard specifies **stainless steel exhaust pipe**, **aluminium structural tubes**, or **large-diameter vent trunks**.

---

**B. (b)** A continuous HF welding line produces **114 mm OD × 4 mm wall thickness** mild steel pipes at a line speed of **60 m/min**. Calculate the **theoretical HF power** required at the weld zone. Assume steel density = 7850 kg/m³, specific heat = 500 J/kg·K, ambient = 25 °C, forging temperature = 1300 °C, and overall process efficiency = 85 %. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of strip entering weld:**

$$A = \pi \cdot D \cdot t = \pi \times 0.114 \times 0.004 = 1.432 \times 10^{-3}\ m^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate at line speed v = 60 m/min = 1 m/s:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v = 7850 \times 1.432 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 = 11.24\ kg/s$$

**Step 3 — Heat required per second (sensible heat only, ignoring latent):**

$$\dot{Q}_{req} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 11.24 \times 500 \times (1300 - 25)$$

$$\dot{Q}_{req} = 11.24 \times 500 \times 1275 = 7.165 \times 10^{6}\ W \approx 7.17\ MW$$

**Step 4 — Required input power accounting for efficiency:**

$$P_{input} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{req}}{\eta} = \dfrac{7.17 \times 10^{6}}{0.85}$$

$$\boxed{P_{input} \approx 8.43\ MW}$$

**Engineering sanity check:** Commercial tube mills of this size typically operate with HF generators in the **600 kW – 1.2 MW** range. The discrepancy (calculated 8.4 MW vs commercial 1 MW) arises because (i) only the small Vee-zone mass is heated — not the full wall section, (ii) much heat is recovered by conduction back into the bulk, (iii) much of the heat is supplied by conduction from the squeeze-rolled zone where deformation also generates frictional heat. The simplified problem over-estimates by assuming all mass in the cross-section must reach forging temperature simultaneously — which is a common textbook simplification.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes** — Forgetting to convert **m/min to m/s** (divide by 60) is the #1 mark-loser.
> 2. **Efficiency placement** — If the question gives **input power** and asks for weld heat, *divide* by efficiency. If it asks for input given heat, *divide* by efficiency. Students frequently invert this.
> 3. **Skin depth units** — Final answer must be in **mm or µm** (not metres), because examiners visually expect a small number.
> 4. **Permeability** — Always state $\mu = \mu_0\mu_r$ explicitly; never quote $\mu$ without the relative component for ferromagnetic steel.
> 5. **Process vs equipment distinction** — HFW *process* answers should mention Vee-angle, squeeze rolls, and flash removal. Do not merely describe the oscillator circuit unless the question explicitly asks for equipment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: HF welding = resistance welding using AC at 100 kHz – 500 kHz, exploiting **skin** and **proximity** effects.
- **Two physical pillars**: **Skin effect** (current confined to surface) and **proximity effect** (current crowds into facing conductors).
- **Master formula**: $\delta = \sqrt{\rho / (\pi f \mu)}$. Skin depth for steel at 400 kHz ≈ **0.03 mm**.
- **Two process variants**:
    * **HF Contact / Conveyor** — sliding electrodes — best for carbon-steel marine pipes.
    * **HF Induction** — encircling coil — best for stainless, aluminium, large diameters.
- **Mill line-up**: Uncoiler → Trimmer → Forming rolls → **Vee-point (HF injection)** → Squeeze rolls → Normalizer → Scarfing → Sizing → Cut-off.
- **Typical Vee-angle**: 2° – 6° (too large → poor concentration; too small → arcing).
- **Forging temperature** for mild steel: ~1300 °C; line speed: 30 – 120 m/min.
- **Power range**: 100 kW – 1.2 MW for industrial tube mills.
- **Frequency choice rationale**: higher $f$ → smaller $\delta$ → tighter, faster, narrower weld, but harder impedance matching.
- **Efficiency**: HFW thermal efficiency ≈ **80 – 90 %**, vs 50 – 60 % for 50 Hz ERW.
- **Marine applications**: ballast lines, fuel & lube oil lines, fire-main, bilge & cooling systems, structural hollow sections (SHS) for decks, offshore jacket legs.
- **Standards relevant**: ASTM A53, API 5L, EN 10219, IACS UR W (for shipboard welded pipes).
- **Post-weld treatment**: In-line induction **normalizing** refines the coarse columnar grains in the HAZ and restores toughness.
- **Common defects to know**: cold weld (insufficient heat), black hat / oxide inclusion (poor squeeze), weld "hook" crack (excessive Vee-angle), and "cold lap" (current too low for the chosen speed).
- **Examiner hot-buttons**: numerical skin-depth calculation, comparison with conventional ERW, sketch of tube mill, and the role of impedance matching.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cold Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cold Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cold Welding (also known as Contact Welding, Cold Pressure Welding, or Solid-State Diffusion Bonding)** is a solid-state joining process in which two metallic workpieces are united at ambient temperature (or near-ambient temperature, typically below the recrystallization temperature of the parent metal) by the application of substantial external pressure, without the use of any external heat source, filler metal, or shielding gas.
