The metallurgical bond is achieved through plastic deformation at the microscopic contact asperities, which simultaneously expels surface contaminants and oxide films, exposing nascent (virgin) metallic surfaces that coalesce via atomic-level metallic bonding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — Cold Welding):**
> Cold welding is classified as a *solid-state welding process* per **ISO 4063 (Welding and Allied Processes — Nomenclature and Numbering)**, process designation **04 — Solid-State Welding, Sub-group 04.1 — Cold Welding**. The process is uniquely distinguished from fusion welding by the **complete absence of a liquid phase** during joining.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Tag Reference:** Under the KTU 2024 PENST741 scheme, Cold Welding falls under Module 2 (Solid-State Welding Processes) and is a high-frequency topic in Part A (3-mark) questions of End Semester Evaluations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pressing two pieces of **freshly cleaned, soft modelling clay (plasticine)** together with your thumbs. Even though the clay is at room temperature, the two pieces fuse into one inseparable mass because the pressure flattens the surface roughness and brings the inner molecules into direct contact. There is no glue, no heat — just intimate molecular contact.

Cold welding works on **exactly the same principle**, but with metals:
- The metal surfaces look smooth to the naked eye, but under a microscope they are rough (full of peaks called *asperities* and valleys).
- A thin invisible layer of **oxide**, **oil**, **moisture**, or **dirt** coats every metal surface — this is the enemy of cold welding.
- When you squeeze two metals together, the pressure first **plastically deforms the asperities**, then **laterally squeezes out the surface contaminants** (similar to how a sponge expels water when compressed).
- The clean, virgin metal underneath touches clean virgin metal — and **the atoms, being metallic, instantly form metallic bonds**, just like a single continuous piece of metal.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way:** Cold welding is essentially a *mechanical theft* of the oxide layer. The pressure acts like a bulldozer, scraping out the contamination to expose pure metal, which then "welds" itself at the atomic level — all without any heat.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Process Temperature | **20 – 50 °C** (room temperature) | Degrees Celsius |
| Required Deformation Ratio (R) | **60 – 90 %** reduction in thickness | Percentage (%) |
| Typical Bond Strength Achieved | **> 90 %** of parent metal strength | Percentage of base metal UTS |
| Contact Pressure Range | **500 – 2000 MPa** | Megapascals |
| Asperity Deformation Threshold | **> 0.1 µm** peak flattening | Micrometres |
| Recrystallization Limit | **< 0.5 × T_melt (K)** | Absolute temperature |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Threshold:** For the cold weld to be metallurgically sound, the **deformation ratio (R)** — defined as the percentage reduction in original thickness — **must exceed ~ 60 %** for most non-ferrous metals. Below this threshold, residual oxide films at the interface prevent proper atomic bonding, and the joint will fail.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage Mechanism of Cold Welding

The cold welding process occurs in a well-defined sequence of physical stages. Understanding these stages is critical for KTU Module 2 examinations.

**Stage 1 — Surface Preparation (Pre-Weld Cleaning)**
- Workpiece surfaces are mechanically degreased (acetone/alcohol wipe) and wire-brushed.
- For aluminium, a **steel-wire scratch brush** is used to remove the tenacious **Al₂O₃ oxide film** (which reforms within minutes in air).
- Surface roughness is intentionally induced to provide mechanical interlocking sites.

**Stage 2 — Initial Asperity Contact**
- The two workpieces are brought together under low preload.
- Contact occurs only at the tips of microscopic asperities (true contact area ≈ **0.01 – 1 %** of apparent area).
- Localized elastic deformation at asperity tips.

**Stage 3 — Plastic Deformation of Asperities**
- As pressure increases, asperity tips yield plastically and flatten.
- The true contact area expands rapidly (Amontons' law of friction, modified for plastic flow).
- Surface contaminants (oxides, adsorbed gases, moisture) are **extruded laterally** from the interface.

**Stage 4 — Oxide Film Disruption**
- Brittle oxide layers (Al₂O₃, Cu₂O, Fe₂O₃) fracture and fragment under the biaxial compressive stress.
- Fresh (nascent) base metal is exposed through the cracks in the oxide film.
- Virgin metal-to-virgin metal contact zones appear (these are called **"bonded islands"**).

**Stage 5 — Metallic Bond Formation**
- Within the bonded islands, interatomic distances fall below the **threshold bonding distance** (typically **< 0.5 nm** for metallic bonds).
- Free electrons of the two metal lattices delocalize across the interface → **metallic bond** forms.
- Grain boundaries migrate across the original interface over time (aided by room-temperature diffusion and subsequent annealing).

**Stage 6 — Bond Consolidation**
- Continued pressure consolidates the bond by closing residual microvoids.
- Final joint strength approaches parent metal strength once the deformation ratio **R ≥ 80 %**.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind Each Stage

| Stage | Physical Driver | Engineering "Why" |
|---|---|---|
| Surface Prep | **Surface Energy Minimization** | Clean surfaces have higher surface energy → drive to bond when contacted |
| Asperity Contact | **Hertzian Contact Mechanics** | Real contact area is microscopic; stress concentration is enormous |
| Plastic Flow | **Von Mises Yield Criterion** | Once local stress exceeds σ_y, plastic flow extrudes contaminants |
| Oxide Rupture | **Brittle Fracture Mechanics** | Oxides are brittle; the ductile substrate deforms plastically around them |
| Metallic Bonding | **Metallic Bond Theory (Drude-Lorentz)** | Delocalized electrons create non-directional bonds across clean interfaces |
| Consolidation | **Diffusion Creep (Coble/Nabarro-Herring)** | Vacancy migration closes residual pores at the interface |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Card — Cold Welding Module 2**

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$R = \frac{t_0 - t_f}{t_0} \times 100$$ | R = deformation ratio, t₀ = initial thickness, t_f = final thickness | %, mm | Determines if sufficient bonding has occurred |
| 2 | $$P_{req} = k \cdot \sigma_y \cdot A_0$$ | P_req = required upset force, k = constraint factor (2–3), σ_y = yield strength, A₀ = initial area | N, MPa, mm² | Calculate press tonnage |
| 3 | $$\sigma_{bond} = \sigma_{UTS,parent} \cdot f(R)$$ | σ_bond = bond strength, f(R) = efficiency function (≈ 0.9 at R = 80%) | MPa | Predict joint strength |
| 4 | $$\frac{A_f}{A_0} = \frac{1}{1 - R}$$ | A_f = final area, A_0 = initial area, R = deformation ratio | mm² | Track material flow |
| 5 | $$R_{crit} = 1 - \frac{\sigma_y}{E}$$ | R_crit = critical deformation, E = Young's modulus | dimensionless | Minimum R for sound weld |
| 6 | $$W_{plastic} = V \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$ | W_plastic = plastic work, V = volume | J, mm³ | Energy input calculation |
| 7 | $$\tau_{shear} = 0.577 \cdot \sigma_{UTS}$$ | τ_shear = shear strength (von Mises) | MPa | Joint shear capacity |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL — Markdown Table Rule:** In all rendered markdown tables, the **vertical bar `\|`** is a reserved table delimiter. Therefore, all absolute-value bars in the formulas above have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` style spacing. Do not type raw `|x|` in KTU exam answers — use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Shipbuilding Engineering

Cold welding finds specialized but important applications in maritime and shipbuilding contexts:

1. **Aluminium Hull and Superstructure Fabrication** — Modern high-speed vessels (Fast Patrol Crafts, naval combatants, catamarans) use **marine-grade aluminium alloys (5083, 5086, 5383)**. Cold welding is preferred for joining thin aluminium sheets where **fusion welding risks HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone) softening** and distortion.
2. **Pipe Coupling Systems** — **Hydraulic piping, fuel lines, and seawater cooling systems** aboard ships use cold-welded aluminium-copper transition joints.
3. **Electrical & Grounding Connections** — Shipboard **cathodic protection systems, lightning protection busbars, and EMC shielding** rely on cold-welded joints to maintain low contact resistance over decades of saltwater exposure.
4. **Cryogenic LNG Carrier Construction** — Inner tanks of LNG carriers use **aluminium alloy 5083-O**; cold welding is one of the few processes that can join these without sensitizing them to **stress corrosion cracking (SCC)**.
5. **Repair of Propeller Shafts and Rudder Stocks** — Cold build-up welding (a related variant) is used for marine shaft reclamation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Deformation Ratio (R_crit) for Cold Welding

**Statement to Prove:** The minimum deformation ratio required to achieve a metallurgically sound cold weld is given by:

$$R_{crit} = 1 - \frac{\sigma_y}{E}$$

**Starting Premise — Volume Constancy:**
For an incompressible metal undergoing plastic deformation during cold welding, volume is conserved (no porosity forms in the bulk).

$$V_0 = V_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_0 \cdot t_0 = A_f \cdot t_f$$

**Step 1 — Express the area ratio in terms of R:**
By definition of deformation ratio:

$$R = \frac{t_0 - t_f}{t_0}$$

Rearranging to solve for the final thickness:

$$t_f = t_0 (1 - R)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the volume constancy equation:**

$$A_0 \cdot t_0 = A_f \cdot t_0 (1 - R)$$

Cancelling $t_0$ from both sides:

$$A_0 = A_f (1 - R)$$

Therefore, the area ratio is:

$$\frac{A_f}{A_0} = \frac{1}{1 - R}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the True Stress – True Strain Relationship:**
For plastic deformation beyond yield, true stress follows:

$$\sigma_{true} = \sigma_y \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \sigma_y \cdot (1 - R)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Von Mises Yield Criterion for Interface Failure:**
The interface fails by shear when true stress exceeds the shear strength of the unbonded interface. By Von Mises:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577 \, \sigma_y$$

For bonding to occur, the interfacial contact pressure must at least equal the material's elastic recovery stress. Setting the true stress equal to the elastic recovery:

$$\sigma_y \cdot (1 - R) = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{E}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for R:**

Divide both sides by $\sigma_y$:

$$1 - R = \frac{\sigma_y}{E}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{R_{crit} = 1 - \frac{\sigma_y}{E}}$$

**Interpretation:** Since for most metals $\sigma_y / E \approx 0.001 - 0.01$, the theoretical $R_{crit}$ is between **0.1 % and 1 %**. However, **practical $R_{crit}$ is 60 – 90 %** because the theoretical value assumes perfectly clean, perfectly flat surfaces — conditions that are physically impossible in real shipbuilding environments due to oxide layers and asperities.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Plastic Work (Energy) Required for Cold Welding

**Statement:** The plastic deformation energy input during cold welding is:

$$W_{plastic} = V \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Define Instantaneous Force:**
At any instant during upsetting, the applied force is:

$$F = \sigma_y \cdot A_{instantaneous}$$

**Step 2 — Define True Strain:**
True strain in the thickness direction is:

$$\varepsilon = \ln\left(\frac{t_0}{t_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{A_f}{A_0}\right)^{-1} = -\ln\left(\frac{A_f}{A_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Work Done = Integral of Force × Displacement:**
The incremental work done in reducing the thickness by $dt$ is:

$$dW = F \cdot dt = \sigma_y \cdot A \cdot dt$$

But by volume constancy: $A \cdot dt = -dV = -A_0 \cdot dt_0$ (the displaced volume).

Using $t = t_0 (1 - R)$ and $dR = -dt / t_0$, we can write:

$$dW = \sigma_y \cdot V \cdot d\varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from initial to final state:**

$$W = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma_y \cdot V \, d\varepsilon = \sigma_y \cdot V \cdot \varepsilon_f$$

Substituting the expression for $\varepsilon_f$:

$$\boxed{W_{plastic} = V \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)}$$

**Numerical Example — Aluminium 5083 Plate Cold Welding:**

- Plate dimensions: 100 mm × 50 mm × 5 mm → V = 25,000 mm³
- $\sigma_y$ of Al 5083-O = **130 MPa** = 130 N/mm²
- Deformation ratio R = 80 % → $A_0 / A_f = 1 / (1 - 0.8) = 5$

$$W = 25000 \times 130 \times \ln(5)$$

$$W = 3{,}250{,}000 \times 1.6094$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 5.23 \, \text{Joules}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** Cold welding requires only **Joules to kilojoules** of energy input, compared to **mega-joules** for fusion welding. This is why cold welding is sometimes called the "**zero-energy welding process**" — ideal for energy-constrained shipyard operations.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cold Welding vs. Other Solid-State Processes

| Parameter | **Cold Welding** | Friction Welding | Diffusion Bonding | Explosion Welding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | **Room Temp (~ 25 °C)** | Below T_melt (~ 0.8 T_melt) | 0.5 – 0.8 T_melt | Room Temp (adiabatic) |
| Heat Source | **None** | Frictional | External furnace | Chemical explosive |
| Atmosphere | Air (cleaned) | Air | Vacuum / Inert gas | Air / Water medium |
| Deformation Required | **Very high (60–90 %)** | Moderate (10–30 %) | Very low (< 5 %) | High (cyclic) |
| Cycle Time | **Seconds** | Seconds | Hours | Milliseconds |
| Equipment Cost | **Low–Medium** | Medium | High (vacuum furnace) | Specialized |
| Joint Strength vs Parent | **90 – 100 %** | 95 – 100 % | 80 – 95 % | 95 – 100 % |
| Shipbuilding Suitability | **High for Al, Cu thin sheets** | High for shafting | Limited (size constraints) | Limited (cladding only) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cold Welding Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below represents the operational sequence, material flow, and process control variables of a cold welding workstation used in shipbuilding aluminium plate fabrication.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Al 5083 Plate Input] --> B1[Mechanical Degreasing Stage]
    B1 --> B2[Acetone / Alcohol Wipe Unit]
    B2 --> C1[Oxide Removal Stage]
    C1 --> C2[Rotary Steel Wire Brush]
    C2 --> D1[Surface Activation Stage]
    D1 --> D2[Fresh Surface Exposure within 30 sec]
    D2 --> E1[Upsetting Press Stage]
    E1 --> E2[Hydraulic Cylinder 500 to 2000 MPa]
    E2 --> F1[Plastic Deformation Zone]
    F1 --> F2[Asperity Flattening and Oxide Extrusion]
    F2 --> G1[Metallic Bond Formation Zone]
    G1 --> G2[Virgin Metal Contact Below 0.5 nm]
    G2 --> H1[Consolidation Hold Stage]
    H1 --> H2[Hold Pressure for 2 to 10 sec]
    H2 --> I1[Quality Inspection Stage]
    I1 --> I2[Ultrasonic C-scan NDT]
    I2 --> J1{Acceptance Check R greater than 60 percent}
    J1 -- Yes --> K1[Approved Cold Welded Plate Output]
    J1 -- No --> L1[Rejection and Recycle Loop]
    L1 --> B1

    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    style E2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    style G2 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px
    style J1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    style K1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    style L1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Material State Transition Diagram

This diagram illustrates how the two parent metal pieces transition from "separate workpieces" to a "monolithic bonded assembly" through the four key material states of cold welding.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateA: Workpiece 1 and 2 Loaded
    StateA: State A — Two Separate Bodies with Native Oxide Layer
    StateA --> StateB: Cleaning and Brushing Applied
    StateB: State B — Oxide Disrupted, Asperities Exposed
    StateB --> StateC: Pressure Exceeds Yield Stress
    StateC: State C — Plastic Flow and Lateral Oxide Extrusion
    StateC --> StateD: True Contact Area Exceeds Threshold
    StateD: State D — Metallic Bond Established, Monolithic Joint
    StateD --> [*]: Joint Ready for Service

    note right of StateA
      Two distinct parent pieces
      Bonded islands: 0 percent
      Interface: continuous oxide
    end note

    note right of StateC
      Bonded islands: 30 to 70 percent
      Oxide fragments being squeezed out
    end note

    note right of StateD
      Bonded islands: greater than 95 percent
      Interface indistinguishable from bulk
    end note
```

## 4.3 Cold Welding Equipment Schematic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PS[Power Supply Unit]
        PS1[3 phase AC Mains] --> PS2[Hydraulic Pump]
        PS2 --> PS3[Pressure Intensifier]
    end

    subgraph UP[Upsetting Press Assembly]
        UP1[Lower Anvil Die] --> UP2[Workpiece Clamping Jaws]
        UP2 --> UP3[Upper Anvil Die]
        UP3 --> UP4[Load Cell 0 to 5000 kN]
    end

    subgraph CC[Control Console]
        CC1[PLC Controller] --> CC2[Pressure Display]
        CC1 --> CC3[Deformation Encoder]
        CC1 --> CC4[Cycle Timer]
    end

    PS3 -- High Pressure Hydraulic Fluid --> UP3
    UP4 -- Force Feedback Signal --> CC1
    CC3 -- LVDT Position Signal --> CC1
    CC1 -- Control Signal --> PS2

    style PS fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    style UP fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    style CC fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Adaptation Note:** Per the operational safeguard, all node IDs above are pure alphanumeric identifiers (e.g., `PS1`, `UP3`, `CC2`) — no reserved Mermaid keywords are used. All node labels are raw uppercase text without bold/italic tags, ensuring clean rendering across the KTU e-learning platform.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define cold welding. Why is it classified as a "solid-state" joining process?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Cold welding is a solid-state joining process in which two metallic workpieces are united at ambient temperature by the application of high pressure alone, **without the use of external heat, filler metal, or shielding gas**. *(1 mark)*

It is classified as "solid-state" because **no melting or fusion of the parent metal occurs** during the process — the metal remains in the solid phase throughout joining. *(1 mark)*

The metallic bond is formed by plastic deformation of surface asperities, which extrudes oxide films and brings clean (nascent) metal surfaces into intimate atomic contact (interatomic distance **< 0.5 nm**), enabling metallic bonding at room temperature. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**List the four essential prerequisites for achieving a metallurgically sound cold weld.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each, then 1 mark for coherence):**

1. **Clean, oxide-free surfaces** — Degreasing and wire-brushing within 30 seconds before welding.
2. **Sufficient deformation ratio (R ≥ 60 %)** — Ensures complete extrusion of surface contaminants.
3. **Adequate contact pressure (P > σ_y of the softer metal)** — Typically 500–2000 MPa.
4. **Ductile parent metals** — The process is best suited for non-ferrous, ductile materials (Al, Cu, Pb, Zn, Au) because brittle metals fracture before adequate plastic flow occurs.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Pattern]**

**"Discuss the cold welding process in detail with reference to its mechanism, process parameters, advantages, limitations, and applications in shipbuilding technology."**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Mechanism and Process Parameters**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Mechanism of Cold Welding — Stagewise Explanation (4 marks):**

The cold welding mechanism proceeds through six sequential stages:

1. **Surface Preparation** — Workpieces are degreased and wire-brushed to remove the oxide film, oil, and adsorbed contaminants. This is the most critical step because **Al₂O₃ on aluminium reforms within 30 – 60 seconds** in air.
2. **Asperity Contact** — Under low preload, the workpieces touch only at microscopic asperity tips. True contact area is initially only **0.01 – 1 %** of the apparent area.
3. **Plastic Deformation of Asperities** — As pressure rises, asperity tips yield and flatten, dramatically increasing the true contact area.
4. **Oxide Film Disruption and Extrusion** — The brittle oxide layer fractures; fragments are squeezed laterally out of the interface, exposing nascent metal.
5. **Metallic Bond Formation** — Clean metal surfaces are brought within the bonding threshold distance (less than 0.5 nm), and free-electron delocalization across the interface forms metallic bonds.
6. **Consolidation** — Continued pressure closes residual microvoids, and the bond strength approaches parent metal strength when **R ≥ 80 %**.

**(ii) Critical Process Parameters (3 marks):**

- **Deformation Ratio (R):** Must be **> 60 %** for acceptable bond strength; ideally **80 – 90 %**.
- **Contact Pressure:** Between **500 – 2000 MPa**, governed by the yield strength of the softer workpiece.
- **Surface Cleanliness:** Oxide thickness must be **< 0.01 µm** immediately before welding.
- **Strain Rate:** Moderate (0.1 – 10 s⁻¹) to allow time for oxide extrusion without generating excessive heat.
- **Welding Time:** Typically **2 – 10 seconds** under full pressure.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Advantages, Limitations, and Shipbuilding Applications**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Advantages (2 marks):**

1. **No heat input** → No HAZ, no distortion, no residual thermal stresses.
2. **No filler metal / shielding gas** → Low consumable cost.
3. **High joint efficiency** → 90 – 100 % of parent metal strength achievable.
4. **Environmentally clean** → No fumes, UV radiation, or sparks.
5. **Suitable for dissimilar metals** → Al–Cu, Al–Pb transitions.
6. **Low energy consumption** — Energy input is orders of magnitude lower than fusion welding.

**(ii) Limitations (2 marks):**

1. **Limited to ductile, non-ferrous metals** — Cannot be used for steel, cast iron, or titanium without heating.
2. **High deformation required** → Not suitable for thick sections or pre-machined parts.
3. **Strict surface preparation** — Any contamination causes bond failure.
4. **Equipment size** — High tonnage presses (up to 5000 kN) are required.
5. **No gap-bridging ability** — Workpieces must fit precisely before welding.

**(iii) Shipbuilding Applications (3 marks):**

1. **Aluminium hull and superstructure fabrication** for high-speed vessels (Fast Patrol Crafts, naval ships, hydrofoils) using alloys 5083, 5086, 5383 — cold welding avoids HAZ softening.
2. **Pipe joining** in hydraulic, fuel, and seawater cooling systems.
3. **Electrical busbar and grounding connections** in cathodic protection systems.
4. **LNG carrier inner tank construction** — Aluminium 5083-O for cryogenic applications.
5. **Heat exchanger tube-to-tube-sheet joints** in marine condensers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**"Explain the metallurgical principles of cold welding. Derive the expression for the critical deformation ratio. Discuss the effect of surface oxide layers on bond formation."**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Metallurgical Principles & Derivation**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Metallurgical Principles (3 marks):**

Cold welding relies on the fundamental principle of **metallic bonding**, in which delocalized free electrons of the metallic lattice are shared between adjacent atoms. For two separate metal pieces to behave as one continuous body, this metallic bond must extend across the original interface.

Three metallurgical conditions must be satisfied:

1. **Surface Cleanliness** — The native oxide film, which is a ceramic insulator, must be physically removed or ruptured to allow metal-metal contact.
2. **Atomic Proximity** — The interatomic distance at the interface must fall below the **bonding threshold (~ 0.5 nm)**, achieved through plastic deformation.
3. **Energy Barrier Overcome** — The pressure must provide sufficient work to plastically deform asperities and extrude the oxide, equivalent to the activation energy for metallic bond formation.

**(ii) Derivation of R_crit (4 marks):**

Starting from volume constancy: $A_0 t_0 = A_f t_f$

By definition: $R = (t_0 - t_f) / t_0$ → $t_f = t_0 (1 - R)$

Substituting: $A_f / A_0 = 1 / (1 - R)$

True stress at the interface: $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_y \cdot (A_0 / A_f) = \sigma_y \cdot (1 - R)$

For bonding: $\sigma_{true} \geq \sigma_y^2 / E$ (elastic recovery criterion)

Solving:

$$1 - R = \frac{\sigma_y}{E}$$

$$\boxed{R_{crit} = 1 - \frac{\sigma_y}{E}}$$

**Numerical Example:** For aluminium 5083, $\sigma_y = 130$ MPa, $E = 70$ GPa:

$$R_{crit} = 1 - \frac{130 \times 10^6}{70 \times 10^9} = 1 - 0.00186 = 0.998$$

The theoretical $R_{crit} \approx 0.2$ % is impractical; **practical $R_{crit} = 60 - 90$ %** due to oxide film and asperity effects.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Effect of Surface Oxide Layers on Bond Formation**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Nature of Oxide Films (2 marks):**

- Oxides (Al₂O₃, Cu₂O, Fe₂O₃) are **brittle ceramics** with high hardness but low ductility.
- They form spontaneously upon exposure to air — aluminium oxide grows at ~ **2 nm/min** at room temperature.
- They act as **diffusion barriers** and **electrical insulators** that prevent metallic bond formation.
- Oxide melting points are very high (Al₂O₃: 2072 °C) compared to the parent metal.

**(ii) Mechanisms of Oxide Disruption (3 marks):**

1. **Mechanical Fracture** — The brittle oxide fractures under the biaxial compressive stress of the upset operation, exposing the underlying ductile metal through the cracks.
2. **Lateral Extrusion** — Once fractured, the oxide fragments are squeezed sideways out of the bonding zone, much like water being wrung from a sponge.
3. **Dissolution into Parent Metal** — Some oxide dissolves into the parent metal lattice under the high local stress and strain.

**(iii) Effect on Bond Quality (2 marks):**

- **Insufficient oxide removal** → Residual oxide islands act as crack initiation sites → Joint strength drops to **30 – 50 %** of parent metal.
- **Optimum R > 80 %** → Less than **5 %** residual oxide at interface → Joint strength reaches **90 – 100 %** of parent.
- **In marine service** — Residual oxide + saltwater exposure can promote **galvanic / crevice corrosion** at the interface, reducing service life.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "cold welding" with "cold metal transfer (CMT)"** — CMT is a MIG/MAG fusion welding variant. Cold welding is *purely* solid-state.
> 2. **Do NOT state that cold welding requires no pressure** — it requires *enormous* pressure (500 – 2000 MPa), not zero force.
> 3. **Do NOT give R_crit in percentage without units** — always write it as a fraction (e.g., 0.6 or 60 %) and clarify which form is being used.
> 4. **Do NOT claim cold welding is suitable for steel at room temperature** — it is restricted to non-ferrous, ductile metals at ambient conditions. Steel requires warm cold welding (heated to 0.3 – 0.5 T_melt).
> 5. **Do NOT forget the surface preparation step** — examiners award 1 mark specifically for the wire-brush / degrease step. Skipping it costs easy marks.
> 6. **In numerical problems, always state the assumption of volume constancy** (incompressibility) before deriving any area / thickness ratio.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 2 — Cold Welding: Last-Minute Revision Card**

- ✅ Cold welding is a **solid-state, room-temperature, pressure-only** joining process.
- ✅ No heat, no filler, no shielding gas — only **massive pressure (500 – 2000 MPa)**.
- ✅ Mechanism = **6 stages**: Clean → Contact → Deform → Rupture Oxide → Bond → Consolidate.
- ✅ Critical deformation ratio **R ≥ 60 %** (practical) and **R_crit = 1 − σ_y/E** (theoretical).
- ✅ Best suited for **Al, Cu, Pb, Zn, Au, Ag** — NOT for steel or cast iron at room temperature.
- ✅ Volume constancy: **A₀ t₀ = A_f t_f** — use this for all area / thickness calculations.
- ✅ Bond strength approaches **90 – 100 %** of parent metal strength when R = 80 %.
- ✅ Shipbuilding uses: **Aluminium hulls (5083, 5086), LNG inner tanks, marine pipes, electrical busbars**.
- ✅ Key equipment: **Hydraulic upsetting press** (up to 5000 kN capacity) with steel wire brush prep station.
- ✅ Key NDT method: **Ultrasonic C-scan** to detect unbonded zones at the interface.
- ✅ Marine disadvantage: **Residual oxide + saltwater → galvanic / crevice corrosion** at interface.
- ✅ ISO 4063 process code: **04.1 (Cold Welding under Solid-State Welding Group 04)**.
- ✅ Memory aid: **"C-C-C-P-D-B"** = Clean → Contact → Crush → Push oxide out → Bond.
- ✅ Energy input is in **Joules**, not kilojoules — that's why it's called a *zero-energy* process.
- ✅ Always mention **surface preparation within 30 seconds** of brushing — oxide regrows fast on Al.
- ✅ Remember: **Cold welding does NOT produce a HAZ** — this is its biggest advantage over fusion welding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusion Welding
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diffusion Welding

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Diffusion Welding (DW)**, also known as **Diffusion Bonding (DB)**, is a **solid-state joining process** in which two metallic surfaces are united through the application of **controlled pressure at elevated temperature** (typically **0.5 to 0.85 Tm**, where Tm is the absolute melting point of the base material) for a predetermined dwell time, without macroscopic deformation, melting, or relative motion of the components.

The metallurgical bond is established by **atomic diffusion** across the faying interface, eliminating voids, breaking up oxide films, and producing a joint whose mechanical and chemical properties approach those of the parent metal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Diffusion Welding is fundamentally distinct from fusion welding — there is **NO liquid phase** formed. The interface bonds via atomic movement in the solid state, making it ideal for **dissimilar metals** and **advanced marine alloys**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two slices of soft butter being pressed together at room temperature:
1. You apply gentle, steady pressure (no sliding, no melting).
2. Over time, the butter molecules slowly migrate across the original contact line.
3. Eventually, the original boundary becomes invisible — the two slices have merged into one continuous piece.

**Diffusion Welding works on the exact same principle, but with metals at high temperature.** Atoms vibrate more vigorously at elevated temperatures, allowing them to slowly "creep" across the interface when held under pressure. The original joint line vanishes, replaced by a metallurgically continuous region.

---

### 1.3 Standard Process Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|
| **Bonding Temperature** | **0.5 – 0.85 Tm** | Below melting; enables atomic mobility |
| **Applied Pressure** | **1 – 20 MPa** | Lower than forging; promotes asperity contact |
| **Holding Time** | **15 min – 4 h** | Allows diffusion across full interface |
| **Vacuum Level** | **10⁻² to 10⁻⁵ Torr** | Prevents oxidation during joining |
| **Surface Roughness (Ra)** | **< 0.8 µm** | Critical for intimate metal-to-metal contact |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant Used:** The **Boltzmann constant k = 8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K** and the **Universal Gas Constant R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** are standard for diffusion calculations.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Diffusion Welding

Diffusion welding is broadly classified into three industrial variants:

1. **Solid-State Diffusion Welding (SSDW)** — Pure solid-state joining with no interlayer.
2. **Diffusion Brazing (DFB)** — Uses a transient liquid phase (TLP) interlayer.
3. **Superplastic Forming + Diffusion Bonding (SPF/DB)** — Combined forming and bonding, used for titanium ship hull panels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Five Sequential Bonding Stages

The diffusion bonding mechanism is universally described by a **five-stage model** (originally proposed by King & Owczarski, refined by Derby). Each stage must be understood sequentially for KTU board-level answers.

**Stage 1 — Initial Asperity Contact and Plastic Deformation**
- Real surfaces have microscopic peaks (asperities) and valleys.
- Under applied pressure, asperities yield plastically, increasing the true contact area.
- Linear deformation contact: $A_c = P / \sigma_y$, where $\sigma_y$ is yield strength at temperature.

**Stage 2 — Oxide Film Disruption and Breakdown**
- Surface oxides (Al₂O₃, TiO₂, Cr₂O₃) are brittle at bonding temperature.
- Plastic flow of the underlying metal fractures the oxide layer.
- Disrupted oxide fragments disperse into the surrounding matrix.

**Stage 3 — Diffusion-Controlled Interface Migration**
- Atoms diffuse from the bulk toward the interface.
- Material transport mechanisms: **volume diffusion, grain boundary diffusion, surface diffusion, and creep**.
- Voids at the interface progressively shrink.

**Stage 4 — Power-Law Creep Void Closure**
- At bonding temperature, the remaining voids close primarily by **power-law creep** of the surrounding matrix.
- This is the rate-controlling stage for most industrial applications.

**Stage 5 — Final Homogenization and Grain Boundary Migration**
- The original interface disappears as new grains grow across the joint.
- Composition gradients (especially in dissimilar metal joints) gradually equalize.
- The joint achieves parent-metal-equivalent mechanical properties.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This formula table is **exam-critical**. Master these equations for KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) derivations.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | $J = -D \, \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | **Fick's First Law** — Diffusion flux | atoms/(m²·s) |
| 2 | $D = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | **Arrhenius Diffusion Equation** | m²/s |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$ | **Fick's Second Law** — Transient diffusion | — |
| 4 | $t_{b} = \dfrac{kT \gamma \Omega}{P \delta D_0} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | **Derby's Bonding Time Model** (Stage 4) | seconds |
| 5 | $x = \sqrt{Dt}$ | **Diffusion penetration depth** | meters |
| 6 | $P_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{3\sigma_y A_0}{A}$ | **Contact pressure for full plastic flow** | Pa |
| 7 | $T_{\text{bond}} = \alpha \, T_m$ | **Bonding temperature ratio** (α = 0.5–0.85) | K |
| 8 | $J_{\text{total}} = J_{vol} + J_{gb} + J_{surf}$ | **Sum of diffusion mechanisms** | atoms/(m²·s) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Encoding Rule:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside LaTeX expressions within tables. This prevents markdown parser breakage.

**Where:**
- $D_0$ = Frequency factor (m²/s)
- $Q$ = Activation energy for diffusion (J/mol)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $\gamma$ = Surface energy (J/m²)
- $\Omega$ = Atomic volume (m³/mol)
- $\delta$ = Grain boundary width ≈ **0.5 nm**
- $P$ = Applied bonding pressure (Pa)
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Marine & Shipbuilding Engineering

Diffusion welding is critical in shipbuilding for the following high-value applications:

1. **Titanium Hull Structures for Naval Submarines** — Russian Alfa-class and newer deep-diving submarines use titanium alloy hulls joined by diffusion bonding.
2. **Aluminum Superstructure Fabrication** — Lightweight 5083/6082 marine aluminum alloys.
3. **Honeycomb Sandwich Panels** — Used in warship decks and bulkheads for high stiffness-to-weight ratio.
4. **Dissimilar Metal Transitions** — Joining titanium propeller shafts to steel hull fittings.
5. **Heat Exchanger Plates** — Compact marine heat exchangers.
6. **Repair of In-Service Components** — Blade-tip restoration of marine turbine rotors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Derby's Bonding Time Equation (Stage 4 – Power-Law Creep)

This is a **frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU examinations. Below is the complete, step-by-step derivation with no skipped steps.

**Assumptions:**
- The bonding interface is modelled as a row of cylindrical voids of radius $r$ and spacing $2r$.
- Voids close by **power-law creep** of the surrounding matrix.
- Volume diffusion supplies matter to the void surface.

**Step 1 — Stress on the Void Surface**

The applied pressure $P$ creates a hydrostatic stress on the cylindrical void surface of radius $r$:

$$\sigma_{\text{void}} = \dfrac{P \cdot 2r}{2r} = P$$

**Step 2 — Power-Law Creep Strain Rate**

For the surrounding matrix undergoing power-law creep at high temperature:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = A \, \sigma^{n} \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

where $n$ is the creep exponent (typically **3–5** for metals) and $A$ is the creep constant.

**Step 3 — Rate of Void Closure**

The volumetric strain rate of the void relates to the radial closure rate:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{dr}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Creep Law**

$$\dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{dr}{dt} = A \, P^{n} \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

Rearranging for the rate of closure:

$$\dfrac{dr}{dt} = -r \cdot A \cdot P^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Separate Variables and Integrate**

$$\int_{r_0}^{r} \dfrac{dr}{r} = -A \cdot P^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right) \int_{0}^{t_b} dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r}{r_0}\right) = -A \cdot P^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right) \cdot t_b$$

**Step 6 — Express the Void Volume Fraction**

If the initial void fraction is $\theta_0$ and the current void fraction is $\theta$:

$$\theta = \theta_0 \, \dfrac{r}{r_0} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{r}{r_0} = \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_0}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for Bonding Time $t_b$**

$$t_b = -\dfrac{\ln(\theta/\theta_0)}{A \cdot P^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{\;t_b = \dfrac{\ln(\theta_0/\theta)}{A \, P^{n}} \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)\;}$$

**Step 8 — Physical Interpretation**

This equation demonstrates that **bonding time decreases exponentially with temperature** (through the Arrhenius term), explaining why diffusion welding requires high-temperature operation. It also shows that **higher pressure dramatically reduces bonding time** (since $n > 1$).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Process Parameters on Joint Quality

| Parameter | Increase Effect on Bonding Rate | Effect on Final Joint Strength | KTU Board Note |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------|
| Temperature ↑ | **Exponential increase** (Arrhenius) | ↑ (up to ~0.85 Tm) | Most influential parameter |
| Pressure ↑ | Linear-to-power increase | ↑ (saturates at full contact) | Limited by workpiece deformation |
| Time ↑ | Logarithmic increase | ↑ (saturates at full diffusion) | Subject to economic limits |
| Surface roughness ↓ | Faster initial contact | ↑ (fewer voids to close) | Ra < 0.8 µm is industry norm |
| Vacuum quality ↑ | Prevents re-oxidation | ↑ Critical for reactive metals | Mandatory for Ti, Zr, Be |
| Grain size ↓ | Faster grain boundary diffusion | ↑ (more GB diffusion paths) | Fine-grained alloys bond faster |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A shipbuilding-grade **Ti-6Al-4V** alloy plate is to be diffusion bonded at **800 °C** with an applied pressure of **5 MPa**. Given:
- $D_0 = 1.9 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- $Q = 142$ kJ/mol
- Initial void fraction $\theta_0 = 0.20$, final void fraction $\theta = 0.01$
- Creep exponent $n = 3$, creep constant $A = 2.5 \times 10^{-8}$ MPa⁻³·s⁻¹

**Find:** Required bonding time $t_b$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert Temperature**
$$T = 800 + 273 = 1073 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Arrhenius Exponent**

$$\dfrac{Q}{RT} = \dfrac{142\,000}{8.314 \times 1073} = \dfrac{142\,000}{8920.9} = 15.92$$

$$\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right) = e^{-15.92} = 1.21 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Logarithmic Void Ratio**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\theta_0}{\theta}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.20}{0.01}\right) = \ln(20) = 2.996$$

**Step 4 — Compute $A \cdot P^{n}$**

$$P^n = (5)^{3} = 125 \text{ MPa}^3$$

$$A \cdot P^{n} = 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \times 125 = 3.125 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into Derby's Equation**

$$t_b = \dfrac{2.996}{3.125 \times 10^{-6}} \times e^{15.92}$$

$$\boxed{\;t_b = 9.587 \times 10^{5} \times 8.27 \times 10^{6} = 7.93 \times 10^{12} \text{ s}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Realistic Engineer's Note**

Such an astronomical value indicates that **pure power-law creep alone is insufficient** for void closure in Ti-6Al-4V at 800 °C. Real industrial practice uses:
- Higher temperature (900–950 °C, closer to $\beta$-transus)
- Surface-activated diffusion bonding with Ni or Cu interlayers
- Lower initial void fraction via precision lapping

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Diffusion Welding Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Power Supply and Control Unit] --> B[Heating Element Array]
    B --> C[Vacuum Chamber Environment]
    C --> D[Upper Platten with Integrated Heater]
    C --> E[Lower Platten with Integrated Heater]
    D --> F[Upper Workpiece Fixture]
    E --> G[Lower Workpiece Fixture]
    F --> H[Faying Interface - Bonding Zone]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Thermal Mass Distribution Plate]
    I --> J[Hydraulic or Pneumatic Pressure Ram]
    J --> K[Load Cell and Pressure Transducer]
    K --> L[PID Temperature Controller]
    L --> A
    H --> M[Vacuum Pump System]
    M --> N[Residual Gas Analyzer]
    N --> O[Process Data Logger]
    O --> P[Operator HMI Interface]
```

**Architectural Function Mapping:**

| Block | Function | Engineering Principle |
|-------|----------|----------------------|
| Power Supply Unit | Provides regulated electrical input | Thyristor-controlled AC |
| Heating Element Array | Reaches bonding temperature | Resistance or induction heating |
| Vacuum Chamber | Prevents oxidation | Mechanical + diffusion pumps |
| Platten Fixtures | Apply uniform pressure | Tool steel platens with surface flatness < 5 µm |
| Pressure Ram | Delivers controlled load | Hydraulic precision actuator |
| PID Controller | Maintains thermal stability | ± 5 °C accuracy typical |
| RGA | Monitors chamber purity | Detects O₂, H₂O, N₂ partial pressures |
| HMI | Records process parameters | PLC + SCADA logging |

---

### 4.2 Process Flow Sequence for a Marine Component Diffusion Bonding Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Surface Preparation] --> S2[Stage 2 Chemical Cleaning]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Drying and Storage in Clean Room]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Fixture Assembly and Alignment]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Chamber Loading]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 Vacuum Evacuation to 10expneg4 Torr]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7 Heating Ramp at 10 C per min]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8 Application of Bonding Pressure]
    S8 --> S9[Stage 9 Isothermal Hold at T bond]
    S9 --> S10[Stage 10 Controlled Cooling under Pressure]
    S10 --> S11[Stage 11 Vacuum Venting and Unloading]
    S11 --> S12[Stage 12 Non Destructive Testing of Bond]
```

---

### 4.3 Five-Stage Bonding Mechanism Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M1[Stage 1 Asperity Deformation] --> M2[Stage 2 Oxide Film Disruption]
    M2 --> M3[Stage 3 Diffusion Across Interface]
    M3 --> M4[Stage 4 Power Law Creep Void Closure]
    M4 --> M5[Stage 5 Grain Boundary Migration and Homogenization]
    M5 --> M6[Parent Metal Equivalent Joint]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**"Define diffusion welding. State any four process parameters that influence joint quality."**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Diffusion welding is a solid-state joining process in which two metallic surfaces are bonded by atomic diffusion under the combined action of **heat (typically 0.5–0.85 Tm)**, **pressure**, and **time**, without melting or macroscopic plastic deformation of the base materials.

**Four Process Parameters (1 mark — ¼ mark each):**
1. **Bonding temperature** — controls atomic mobility and creep rate.
2. **Applied pressure** — promotes asperity contact and void closure.
3. **Holding time** — allows sufficient diffusion across the interface.
4. **Surface condition / roughness** — determines initial contact area and oxide behaviour.

*(Additional valid parameters: vacuum level, interlayer material, atmosphere type.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**"Explain why diffusion welding is preferred for joining dissimilar metals in shipbuilding. Give two examples."**

**Model Answer:**

**Reasoning (2 marks):**
Diffusion welding avoids the formation of **brittle intermetallic phases** that typically form during fusion welding of dissimilar metals. Because there is no liquid phase, the **thermal stress** and **distortion** are greatly reduced, and **compositional gradients** can be controlled through diffusion.

**Two Examples (1 mark — ½ mark each):**
1. **Titanium-to-steel transition joints** for marine propeller shafts.
2. **Aluminum-to-copper joints** in shipboard electrical bus-bar connections.

*(Other valid examples: titanium honeycomb panel cores, bimetallic marine valve seats.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions feature **internal choice**. Both alternatives are given below with full model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Module 2 — Internal Choice Set A]**

**(a) Describe in detail the five sequential stages of the diffusion bonding mechanism with suitable diagrams. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The diffusion bonding mechanism proceeds through **five overlapping stages**, each contributing to the progressive elimination of the original interface.

**Stage 1 — Asperity Contact and Plastic Deformation (1.5 marks)**
The faying surfaces, however well-prepared, possess microscopic peaks (asperities) of height typically 0.1–5 µm. Under the applied bonding pressure, these asperities yield plastically as the local contact stress exceeds the yield strength at bonding temperature. The real contact area increases to ~10–20 % of the nominal area.

**Stage 2 — Oxide Film Disruption (1 mark)**
Brittle surface oxides (Al₂O₃, TiO₂, Cr₂O₃) fracture under the plastic strain of the underlying matrix. The fragmented oxide islands disperse into the surrounding metal, exposing fresh metal-to-metal contact points.

**Stage 3 — Diffusion Across the Interface (1.5 marks)**
At elevated temperature, atoms migrate across the contact zone via **volume diffusion** (through the lattice), **grain boundary diffusion** (along grain boundaries — dominant at lower temperatures), and **surface diffusion** (along free surfaces — does not close voids, but redistributes matter).

**Stage 4 — Power-Law Creep Void Closure (2 marks)**
The remaining lenticular voids at the interface shrink predominantly by **power-law creep** of the surrounding matrix. This stage is governed by **Derby's bonding time equation**:

$$t_b = \dfrac{\ln(\theta_0/\theta)}{A \, P^{n}} \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Stage 5 — Homogenization and Grain Boundary Migration (1 mark)**
Once voids are eliminated, the original bond plane disappears as new grains nucleate and grow across the former interface. In dissimilar metal joints, compositional gradients gradually equalize by interdiffusion.

**Schematic Diagram (mandatory for full marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.

---

**(b) Derive the bonding time equation for diffusion welding using power-law creep, starting from Fick's first law assumptions. State the significance of each term. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Refer to the **complete 8-step derivation** in Section 3.1 of these notes. The final result is:

$$\boxed{\;t_b = \dfrac{\ln(\theta_0/\theta)}{A \, P^{n}} \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)\;}$$

**Significance of Each Term (3 marks):**

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|------|------------------|
| $t_b$ | Total time required to close voids from initial to final fraction |
| $\ln(\theta_0/\theta)$ | Required void reduction ratio — logarithm of initial-to-final porosity |
| $A$ | Material creep constant (microstructure dependent) |
| $P^n$ | Pressure dependence with creep exponent $n$ (3–5 for metals) |
| $Q$ | Activation energy for self-diffusion in the matrix |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K) |
| $T$ | Absolute bonding temperature |
| $\exp(Q/RT)$ | Arrhenius factor — strongly temperature-dependent |

**Physical Interpretation (1 mark):** The bonding time decreases exponentially with increasing temperature and polynomially with increasing pressure. This guides process designers to optimize $T$ and $P$ for economic cycle times.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Module 2 — Internal Choice Set B]**

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the diffusion welding process for marine-grade Ti-6Al-4V alloy. Discuss the role of surface preparation and vacuum environment. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Process Description (3 marks):**
Diffusion welding of Ti-6Al-4V for marine applications (submarine hulls, propeller shafts) is conducted at **850–950 °C** under **3–10 MPa pressure** for **30 min to 4 hours** in a vacuum of **10⁻⁴ Torr or better**. The setup consists of a vacuum chamber, induction or resistance heating platens, hydraulic ram, and load cell for closed-loop pressure control.

**Surface Preparation (2 marks):**
1. **Mechanical lapping** to Ra < 0.4 µm using progressively finer SiC paper.
2. **Chemical etching** in Kroll's reagent (HF + HNO₃) to remove the work-hardened layer.
3. **Ultrasonic cleaning** in acetone followed by ethanol.
4. **Storage in clean-room conditions** for maximum 2 hours before bonding.

**Role of Vacuum Environment (2 marks):**
Titanium has a strong affinity for oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen. At bonding temperatures, these gases form brittle surface layers (α-case) that prevent diffusion bonding. Vacuum levels better than **10⁻⁴ Torr** suppress reoxidation and ensure **clean metallic contact**.

---

**(b) Compare diffusion welding with fusion welding for joining marine aluminum alloys. State four advantages and two limitations of diffusion welding in this context. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Diffusion Welding | Fusion Welding (e.g., MIG/TIG) |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| Phase change | None (solid-state) | Melting and solidification |
| HAZ | None / Minimal | Significant |
| Distortion | Very low | High |
| Dissimilar metal joining | Excellent | Limited (intermetallics form) |
| Equipment cost | High | Moderate |
| Cycle time | Long (hours) | Short (minutes) |
| Operator skill | Specialized | Standard |

**Four Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **No solidification cracking** — eliminates hot tearing in Al-Mg-Si alloys.
2. **No porosity or gas inclusions** typical of fusion welds.
3. **Preserves parent metal microstructure** — no grain coarsening.
4. **Enables joining of dissimilar aluminum grades** (e.g., 5083 to 6082) without brittle phases.

**Two Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **High capital cost** of vacuum chambers and hydraulic presses.
2. **Long cycle times** make it uneconomical for mass production; best suited for **critical defense and high-value marine structures**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Arrhenius term** $\exp(Q/RT)$ in the bonding time derivation → loses **2 marks** in Part B derivations.
> 2. **Confusing "diffusion welding" with "diffusion brazing"** — brazing uses a **transient liquid phase interlayer**; welding does not. Mixing the two is a **fatal conceptual error** costing 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Not stating the bonding temperature range as 0.5–0.85 Tm** — vague answers like "high temperature" lose **1 mark** in Part A definitions.
> 4. **Skipping the oxide breakdown stage** in mechanism descriptions — examiners expect all **five stages** explicitly listed.
> 5. **Writing pipe symbols `|` inside LaTeX expressions in tables** — breaks markdown parsing in online answer submission portals. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Diffusion Welding = Solid-state joining** with **no melting**, **no HAZ**, **no filler metal** (in pure SSDW).
- Bonding temperature is **0.5–0.85 Tm** of the base metal.
- Applied pressure is **low (1–20 MPa)** compared to forging.
- Vacuum level of **10⁻⁴ Torr or better** is mandatory for reactive metals (Ti, Zr, Be).
- The **five-stage bonding model** (asperity deformation → oxide disruption → diffusion → creep void closure → homogenization) is the **fundamental framework** for all answers.
- **Derby's bonding time equation** is the most important derivation:

$$t_b = \dfrac{\ln(\theta_0/\theta)}{A \, P^{n}} \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

- **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D(\partial C / \partial x)$ governs atomic transport.
- **Arrhenius Diffusion:** $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ governs temperature dependence.
- Diffusion welding is the **preferred process** for **dissimilar metals**, **honeycomb panels**, and **titanium marine structures**.
- **Surface roughness Ra < 0.8 µm** is the industry-standard prerequisite.
- **Interlayer materials** (Ni, Cu, Ag) are often used to enhance diffusion in TLP-diffusion brazing variants.
- **Shipbuilding applications:** titanium hulls, aluminum superstructures, bimetallic shaft joints, heat exchanger plates, and marine turbine blade repairs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Welding # Module 2 — Ultrasonic Welding ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):** > *Ultrasonic Welding (USW) is a solid-state welding process in which coalescence of materials is produced by the application of localized high-frequency vibratory energy (typically 20–40 kHz) acting under a static compressive force. The joint is formed without macroscopic melting of the parent materials, primarily through shear-stress-induced plastic deformation and frictional heating at the faying interface.* ### 1.1 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine pressing two sheets of **aluminium foil** together on a table using the flat of your thumb. If you could make your thumb **vibrate at 20,000 times per second** while pressing down, the friction at the interface would generate localized heat, the metal would soften without melting, and the two sheets would **fuse into one piece**. That, in essence, is ultrasonic welding — only the "thumb" is a precision-engineered **sonotrode (horn)** vibrating tens of thousands of times a second against the clamped workpieces. In a shipyard, this is the same principle that joins **thin aluminium superstructure panels**, **copper busbars in the electrical switchboard room**, and **delicate dissimilar-metal sensor housings** without the heat distortion of arc welding. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** > Ultrasonic welding is a **solid-state (no fusion) process**. No molten pool, no spatter, no shielding gas. The temperature at the interface stays below the melting point — this is critical for shipbuilding because the **mechanical properties and corrosion resistance** of marine-grade aluminium are preserved. ### 1.2 Key Operating Metrics (Standard Values) | Parameter | Typical Range | KTU-Relevant Constant | |---|---|---| | **Operating Frequency** | **20 – 40 kHz** | **20 kHz** (industrial standard) | | **Vibration Amplitude** | **10 – 50 µm** | 20 – 30 µm (aluminium) | | **Static Clamping Force** | **100 – 1500 N** | Depends on material thickness | | **Weld Time** | **0.1 – 1.0 s** | Spot welding: ~0.5 s | | **Power Consumption** | **50 W – 3 kW** | Small parts: ~200 W | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Physical Mechanism — Step-by-Step The formation of an ultrasonic weld occurs in **four sequential physical stages**: 1. **Stage I — Initial Contact (Asperity Engagement):** The two faying surfaces are pressed together under a static axial force $F_s$. Microscopically, only the tips of surface asperities (roughness peaks) touch. The real area of contact is < 1% of the apparent area. 2. **Stage II — Frictional Energy Dissipation:** The sonotrode vibrates tangentially at frequency $f$ with amplitude $A$. Relative sliding between surfaces generates frictional shear stress: $$\tau_{friction} = \mu \cdot p$$ where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction and $p$ is the local contact pressure. 3. **Stage III — Thermal Softening (Below Melting Point):** Frictional work converts into heat, raising the interface temperature to roughly **50 – 80% of the absolute melting temperature** $T_m$. This dramatically reduces yield strength, allowing **plastic flow** to occur. 4. **Stage IV — Metallurgical Bonding (Solid-State Diffusion):** Under sustained pressure and elevated temperature, clean metal-to-metal contact enables short-range atomic diffusion across the interface, producing a true **metallurgical bond** without a liquid phase. > [!NOTE] > **Why it works for shipbuilding:** Aluminium has $T_m \approx 660°\text{C}$, but its recrystallization temperature is ~ 150 °C. Ultrasonic welding operates in this sweet spot — softening the metal without weakening the grain structure. ### 2.2 Governing Equations of Ultrasonic Welding The process is governed by a small but tightly-coupled set of equations. Every KTU question in this area tests **at most two** of these. Memorize the units. #### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P = F_s \cdot v_{peak}$ | Instantaneous power input | W (watts) | Power-supply sizing | | 2 | $v_{peak} = 2\pi f A$ | Peak tangential velocity | m/s | Vibration analysis | | 3 | $Q = \mu \cdot F_s \cdot v_{peak} \cdot t$ | Total frictional heat | J (joules) | Heat input calculation | | 4 | $P_{avg} = \mu \cdot F_s \cdot 2\pi f A$ | Average power | W | Energy balance | | 5 | $\dot{\epsilon} = \pi f$ | Effective shear strain rate | 1/s | Plastic-flow analysis | | 6 | $E_d = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot v_{peak}^2$ | Volumetric energy density | J/m³ | Weld-quality prediction | | 7 | $A_{peak} = \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot P_{elec}}}{\rho \cdot c \cdot \omega}$ | Acoustic horn amplitude | m | Sonotrode design | Where: - $F_s$ = static clamping force (N) - $f$ = ultrasonic frequency (Hz) - $A$ = vibration amplitude (m) - $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (dimensionless, ≈ 0.3 for Al–Al) - $t$ = weld time (s) - $\rho$ = density of horn material (kg/m³) - $c$ = speed of sound in horn (m/s) - $\omega = 2\pi f$ (rad/s) - $P_{elec}$ = electrical input power (W) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Equation (2) and (4) are the **two most-tested** formulas. Any 14-mark question on USW will almost certainly involve substituting values into $P_{avg} = \mu F_s 2\pi f A$. ### 2.3 Equipment Architecture — Functional Subsystems A complete ultrasonic welding installation has **five functional blocks**: 1. **High-Frequency Generator (Power Supply):** Converts mains 50 Hz AC into 20–40 kHz high-frequency AC, typically rated 0.5 – 3 kW. 2. **Transducer:** A **piezoelectric** (PZT — lead zirconate titanate) or magnetostrictive element that converts electrical energy into mechanical vibration. 3. **Booster (Velocity Transformer):** Mechanically amplifies (or attenuates) the vibration amplitude by a fixed gain factor (commonly 1:1 to 1:2.5). 4. **Sonotrode (Horn/Welding Tip):** The shaped acoustic waveguide that delivers vibration to the workpiece. Can be a **spot tip**, **seam wheel**, or **ring tip**. 5. **Pneumatic/Hydraulic Clamp:** Provides the static axial force $F_s$ during welding. > [!NOTE] > **In shipbuilding context:** For long aluminium superstructure panel seams, the **continuous (seam) ultrasonic welding** configuration is preferred — the sonotrode is a rotating wheel that produces a hermetic, leak-tight joint suitable for marine atmospheric exposure. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Peak Tangential Velocity from Vibration Kinematics The sonotrode executes **simple harmonic motion** along the tangential direction. Its instantaneous displacement is: $$x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$$ **Step 1 — Differentiate displacement to get velocity:** Differentiating with respect to time $t$: $$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = A \cdot 2\pi f \cdot \cos(2\pi f t)$$ **Step 2 — Identify the peak (maximum) value:** The cosine function reaches its maximum value of $1$. Therefore: $$v_{peak} = 2\pi f A$$ This is the **peak tangential sliding velocity** at the weld interface. Units check: $[\text{Hz}] \times [\text{m}] = [\text{m/s}]$ ✓ ### 3.2 Derivation: Average Power Dissipated at the Interface **Step 1 — Instantaneous frictional power.** At any instant, the frictional force opposing sliding is $F_f = \mu F_s$. The instantaneous power dissipated as heat is: $$P(t) = F_f \cdot v(t) = \mu F_s \cdot 2\pi f A \cdot \cos(2\pi f t)$$ **Step 2 — Average over one complete vibration cycle.** The average of $\cos(2\pi f t)$ over a full period is zero — but the power dissipated is the **absolute value** of frictional work (energy is always positive). Taking the rectified average over a full cycle: $$P_{avg} = \mu F_s \cdot 2\pi f A \cdot \langle \vert \cos(2\pi f t) \vert \rangle = \mu F_s \cdot 2\pi f A \cdot \frac{2}{\pi}$$ **Step 3 — Simplify the constant:** $$\frac{2}{\pi} \approx 0.6366$$ So the engineering form used in practice is: $$P_{avg} \approx 0.637 \cdot \mu F_s \cdot 2\pi f A \approx 4 \mu F_s f A$$ > [!NOTE] > The $\frac{2}{\pi}$ factor is often **dropped in textbook approximations** to give the cleaner form $P_{avg} = \mu F_s \cdot 2\pi f A$, which is what KTU examiners expect. ### 3.3 Derivation: Total Heat Input Over Weld Time $t_w$ **Step 1 — Energy is the time-integral of power.** $$Q = \int_0^{t_w} P_{avg} \, dt$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the constant power expression.** Assuming steady-state welding (constant $F_s, f, A$): $$Q = \mu F_s \cdot 2\pi f A \cdot t_w$$ This is the **total frictional energy** deposited at the interface, measured in joules. It must be sufficient to soften the metal but not so large as to cause melting or workpiece damage. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **[Question]** An ultrasonic spot welding machine operates at $f = 20\text{ kHz}$ with a sonotrode amplitude of $A = 30\;\mu\text{m}$. The static clamping force is $F_s = 600\;\text{N}$ and the coefficient of friction between two aluminium sheets is $\mu = 0.35$. Calculate: > (i) the peak sliding velocity at the interface, > (ii) the average power dissipated, > (iii) the total heat input for a weld time of $t_w = 0.4\text{ s}$. **Solution:** **(i) Peak velocity:** $$v_{peak} = 2\pi f A = 2\pi \times 20000 \times 30 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$v_{peak} = 2\pi \times 0.6 = 3.77 \;\text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Average power:** $$P_{avg} = \mu F_s \cdot 2\pi f A = 0.35 \times 600 \times 3.77$$ $$P_{avg} = 211.1 \times 3.77 \approx 795.8 \;\text{W}$$ **(iii) Total heat input:** $$Q = P_{avg} \times t_w = 795.8 \times 0.4 = 318.3 \;\text{J}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity check:** For 0.4 s of welding two thin aluminium sheets, ~320 J of localized heat is realistic — equivalent to a small soldering-iron's energy, but concentrated in a spot the size of a coin. ### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — USW vs. Other Welding Processes (in Shipbuilding Context) | Criterion | Ultrasonic | TIG/MIG Arc | Resistance Spot | Laser | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Heat input to workpiece** | **Very Low** | High | Medium | Low | | **Melting occurs?** | **No (solid-state)** | Yes | Yes | Yes (localized) | | **Suitable for Al–Al thin sheets** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | | **Dissimilar metal joining** | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★ | | **Electrical/electronic parts** | ★★★★★ | ✗ | ✗ | ★ | | **Thick plate capability** | ✗ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | | **Distortion of workpiece** | **Negligible** | High | Medium | Low | | **Initial equipment cost** | Medium | High | Low | Very High | | **Skill level required** | Low | High | Medium | Very High | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Ultrasonic Welding System ```mermaid flowchart LR AC[Mains 50 Hz AC Supply] --> GEN[High-Frequency
Generator 20 to 40 kHz] GEN -->|HF Electrical Signal| TRD[Transducer
Piezoelectric PZT] TRD -->|Mechanical Vibration| BST[Booster
Velocity Transformer] BST -->|Amplified Vibration| SON[Sonotrode
Horn Tip] SON -->|Shear Vibration at f| WP1[Upper Workpiece
Aluminium Sheet] WP1 --- INT[(Faying Interface
Solid-State Bond)] INT --- WP2[Lower Workpiece
Aluminium Sheet] WP2 --> ANV[Anvil / Base Plate] CLM[Pneumatic Clamp] -->|Static Force Fs| SON CTRL[Timer and Pressure Controller] --> GEN CTRL --> CLM ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow — Bond Formation Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Stage 1: Initial Clamp
Asperity Contact under Fs] --> B[Stage 2: Vibration Initiated
Sonotrode at f Hz] B --> C[Stage 3: Frictional Shear
Heat Generated mu Fs v] C --> D[Stage 4: Thermal Softening
T reaches 0.5 to 0.8 Tm] D --> E[Stage 5: Plastic Flow
Asperities Deform] E --> F[Stage 6: Atomic Diffusion
Solid-State Metallurgical Bond] F --> G[Stage 7: Vibration Stopped
Hold Force Maintained 0.2 s] G --> H[Stage 8: Clamp Released
Weld Complete] ``` ### 4.3 Sonotrode / Horn Variants Used in Shipyards ```mermaid flowchart TB HORNS[Ultrasonic Horn Variants] --> SPOT[Spot Horn
Discrete Welds] HORNS --> SEAM[Seam Wheel Horn
Continuous Leak Tight Joints] HORNS --> RING[Ring Horn
Circular Seals and Gaskets] HORNS --> BLADE[Blade Horn
Long Linear Seams] SPOT --> USES1[Electrical Contact Tabs
on Switchboards] SEAM --> USES2[Aluminium Superstructure
Panel Seams] RING --> USES3[Sealing Sensor Housings] BLADE --> USES4[Ship Interior Partition
Panel Joining] ``` ### 4.4 Acoustic Amplification Cascade ```mermaid flowchart LR GEN[Generator
Electrical Power] -->|Amplitude x1| TRD[Transducer
Amplitude x1] TRD -->|Booster Gain G| BST[Booster
Amplitude xG] BST -->|Horn Gain H| SON[Sonotrode Tip
Final Amplitude 10 to 50 micrometer] SON --> WP[Weld Interface] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023 Model]** *List any three advantages of ultrasonic welding over conventional fusion welding processes for joining thin aluminium sheets in ship superstructure fabrication.* **Model Answer:** 1. **No melting / solid-state process** — preserves the original metallurgical structure, grain size, and corrosion resistance of marine-grade aluminium. 2. **Negligible heat-affected zone (HAZ)** — eliminates thermal distortion and warping of thin panels, which is critical for fairing of superstructure plates. 3. **No spatter, no fumes, no shielding gas** — produces clean, cosmetically acceptable joints suitable for visible interior bulkhead panels and switchboard-mounted electrical components. (Any three of the above, or other valid points such as suitability for dissimilar metals, low power consumption, automation-friendly.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]** *Define the term "sonotrode" in ultrasonic welding. State its two primary functions.* **Model Answer:** A **sonotrode** (also called a *horn* or *welding tip*) is the shaped acoustic waveguide that forms the final stage of the ultrasonic vibration transmission system, coupling mechanical vibration directly into the workpiece. **Two primary functions:** 1. **Mechanical amplification** of the vibration amplitude delivered by the booster to a level sufficient to cause interfacial sliding and bonding (typically 10–50 µm). 2. **Mechanical coupling and shaping** of the contact region — its tip geometry (spot, seam wheel, ring, or blade) determines the **weld footprint** and hence the joint configuration. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **ESE Module Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional subsystems** of an ultrasonic welding machine and explain the role of the **transducer** and the **booster** in the vibration transmission chain. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two sheets of marine-grade aluminium, each **1.2 mm thick**, are to be spot-welded ultrasonically. Given: - Operating frequency $f = 20\text{ kHz}$ - Sonotrode amplitude $A = 25\;\mu\text{m}$ - Static clamp force $F_s = 500\;\text{N}$ - Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.30$ - Weld time $t_w = 0.5\text{ s}$ Calculate: (i) The peak sliding velocity at the weld interface. (ii) The average power dissipated as heat. (iii) The total heat energy delivered to the joint. Comment on whether the calculated heat input is appropriate for a solid-state bond in aluminium ($T_m = 660°\text{C}$). **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Question A:** **(a) Functional Subsystems (7 marks):** A complete ultrasonic welding installation consists of the following five interconnected subsystems: 1. **High-Frequency Generator** — converts standard 50 Hz mains supply into 20–40 kHz high-frequency alternating current at a power level of 0.5–3 kW. 2. **Transducer (PZT / Piezoelectric)** — converts the high-frequency electrical signal into **mechanical vibration** at the same frequency. The most common type uses **lead zirconate titanate (PZT)** ceramic discs that deform microscopically when an AC voltage is applied (the *inverse piezoelectric effect*). 3. **Booster (Velocity Transformer)** — a tuned mechanical resonator that **amplifies (or de-amplifies)** the vibration amplitude from the transducer by a fixed gain ratio (typical gains: 1:1.5, 1:2.0, 1:2.5). It also provides mechanical mounting isolation. 4. **Sonotrode (Horn / Welding Tip)** — the **shaped acoustic waveguide** that delivers the vibration to the workpiece and defines the geometry of the weld. Common shapes: spot tip, seam wheel, ring, blade. 5. **Clamping System** — pneumatic or servo press that applies the **static axial force** $F_s$ during the weld cycle, holding the workpieces in intimate contact. > **Role of Transducer:** Acts as the **electro-mechanical energy conversion element**. Without it, electrical energy from the generator cannot produce the mechanical vibration necessary for friction-based bonding. > **Role of Booster:** Acts as the **mechanical impedance matcher and amplitude amplifier**. Transducer amplitudes (~5–10 µm) are too small to drive a weld; the booster scales them up to the working range (15–50 µm) while also optimizing the energy transfer between transducer and sonotrode. (Include a labelled block diagram: *Generator → Transducer → Booster → Sonotrode → Workpiece*, with the clamp pressing from above.) --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Peak sliding velocity:** $$v_{peak} = 2\pi f A = 2\pi \times 20{,}000 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$v_{peak} = 2\pi \times 0.5 = 3.142 \;\text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Average power dissipated:** $$P_{avg} = \mu F_s \cdot 2\pi f A = 0.30 \times 500 \times 3.142$$ $$P_{avg} = 150 \times 3.142 = 471.2 \;\text{W}$$ **(iii) Total heat delivered:** $$Q = P_{avg} \times t_w = 471.2 \times 0.5 = 235.6 \;\text{J}$$ **Comment on suitability:** Aluminium melts at 660 °C and undergoes significant softening at ~250–300 °C. For two 1.2 mm sheets with a typical weld-spot area of ~25 mm² (mass ~ 0.135 g of aluminium), an energy input of ~ 235.6 J raises the interface temperature by: $$\Delta T = \frac{Q}{m \cdot c_p} = \frac{235.6}{0.135 \times 10^{-3} \times 900} \approx 1940°\text{C}$$ — which **on paper** exceeds the melting point. However, in practice only ~ 30–40% of this energy is retained at the interface (the rest is lost to the surrounding metal, the anvil, and the sonotrode). The **effective** temperature rise is in the range **500–650 °C**, which is ideal for **solid-state bonding without macroscopic melting**. Hence the calculated heat input is **appropriate** for producing a defect-free ultrasonic weld. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]` **(a)** Explain the **four stages** of bond formation in ultrasonic welding, clearly distinguishing between the roles of **frictional heating** and **atomic diffusion**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare ultrasonic welding with **resistance spot welding** for the application of joining thin-gauge aluminium panels in ship superstructure fabrication. Discuss at least **five comparison points** and state, with justification, which process is more suitable for the given application. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Question B:** **(a) Four Stages of Bond Formation (7 marks):** | Stage | Name | Mechanism | Role of Friction | Role of Diffusion | |---|---|---|---|---| | I | **Initial asperity contact** | Under static force $F_s$, micro-peaks of the two surfaces touch | None (no sliding yet) | None | | II | **Vibration + heat generation** | Sonotrode vibrates at $f$ Hz; friction dissipates $Q = \mu F_s v_{peak} t$ | **Primary** — converts mechanical work into interfacial heat | None | | III | **Plastic deformation** | Heat softens metal (T rises to 0.5–0.8 $T_m$); asperities deform, real contact area grows | Continued — drives softening | Begins (short-range) | | IV | **Metallurgical bonding** | Sustained pressure + heat → atoms cross interface | Minor — just maintains T | **Primary** — completes the bond | **Synthesis:** Stage II is **friction-dominated**; Stage IV is **diffusion-dominated**. The two are *sequential*, not parallel — a common student error is to claim both occur simultaneously from $t = 0$. **(b) Comparison — USW vs. Resistance Spot Welding (7 marks):** | Parameter | Ultrasonic Welding (USW) | Resistance Spot Welding (RSW) | |---|---|---| | **Heat source** | Frictional shear (mechanical) | Joule heating $I^2R t$ (electrical) | | **Melting?** | No (solid-state) | Yes (fusion zone with HAZ) | | **Suitability for aluminium** | Excellent (Al has high friction coefficient) | Difficult (high thermal & electrical conductivity needs very high currents) | | **Joint appearance** | Smooth, flush, cosmetically clean | Visible indentations from electrodes | | **Dissimilar metals** | Excellent (Al–Cu, Al–SS) | Very difficult | | **Thickness range** | Best for thin sheets (< 2 mm) | Wide range, but RSW dominates thick plates | | **Energy efficiency** | ~ 60–80% delivered to interface | ~ 25–40% (most lost to water-cooled electrodes) | | **Equipment cost** | Moderate | Lower for basic units | **Conclusion with justification:** For **thin-gauge aluminium superstructure panels (1–2 mm)** in shipbuilding, **ultrasonic welding is the more suitable process** because: 1. Aluminium's high thermal conductivity makes resistance spot welding energy-inefficient and prone to "stick welds" or expulsion. 2. Marine-grade aluminium (5083, 5086, 6082) is **corrosion-sensitive to HAZ microstructural changes** — USW preserves the original grain structure. 3. Superstructure panels are **cosmetic / fairing-critical** — USW produces flush joints without electrode-mark indentations. 4. Shipyards increasingly join **aluminium to copper or to stainless inserts** for electrical bonding — only USW handles this reliably. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > A frequent error in 14-mark answers is **conflating ultrasonic welding with vibration welding**. Vibration welding uses **low-frequency (50–250 Hz), large-amplitude (1–10 mm)** linear motion between two parts under high pressure — typically for large thermoplastic or large metallic components. Ultrasonic welding uses **high-frequency (20–40 kHz), low-amplitude (10–50 µm)** motion concentrated at a small spot. Writing "ultrasonic welding works at 100 Hz" is an **instant 4-mark deduction** in valuation. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Ultrasonic welding is a SOLID-STATE process** — no melting, no fusion zone, no HAZ. - [ ] **Standard frequency = 20 kHz** (range 20–40 kHz); amplitude = 10–50 µm; pressure = 100–1500 N. - [ ] **Master formula:** $P_{avg} = \mu F_s \cdot 2\pi f A$ — this is the **single most important equation** for the exam. - [ ] **Peak velocity formula:** $v_{peak} = 2\pi f A$ — derived from simple harmonic motion. - [ ] **Total heat:** $Q = P_{avg} \times t_w$ — direct proportionality with weld time. - [ ] **Five equipment blocks:** Generator → Transducer (PZT) → Booster → Sonotrode → Clamp. - [ ] **Transducer = electro-mechanical converter** (piezoelectric / magnetostrictive). - [ ] **Booster = mechanical amplifier** (gain 1:1.5 to 1:2.5 typical). - [ ] **Sonotrode = acoustic waveguide** that defines weld geometry. - [ ] **Four bonding stages:** Asperity contact → Frictional heating → Plastic flow → Atomic diffusion. - [ ] **Three main USW variants:** Spot, Seam (continuous), Ring. - [ ] **Best-suited materials in shipbuilding:** Thin aluminium sheets, copper busbars, Al–Cu dissimilar joints, aluminium superstructure panels, electrical contact tabs. - [ ] **Not suitable for:** Thick steel plates, structural load-bearing hull seams, materials with very low friction coefficient. - [ ] **Key shipbuilding advantage:** Preserves corrosion resistance of marine aluminium (no HAZ, no grain coarsening). - [ ] **Examiner red flags to avoid:** confusing USW with vibration welding; stating "melting occurs"; writing frequency as 50 Hz or 200 Hz; omitting the $\mu$ in the power equation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam-Day Mantra for USW:** > *"20 kHz, 30 µm, 500 N, no melting — and the master formula is $\mu F_s \cdot 2\pi f A$."*

topics.title: Friction and Inertia Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
