> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Friction and Inertia Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Friction Welding — Formal Definition

**Friction Welding (FW)** is a **solid-state joining process** in which the heat required for coalescence is produced *solely* by the mechanical friction between two workpieces when one is held stationary and the other is rotated (or oscillated) against it under a controlled axial compressive force. No external heat source, filler metal, or shielding gas is required. Once a plasticized layer (the "plastic metal ring") forms at the interface, the rotation is stopped and a higher **upset (forge) pressure** is applied to complete the bond.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Friction welding belongs to the family of *Solid-State Welding* processes. It is **NOT** fusion welding because the base metal never reaches its melting point — the joint is created below the solidus temperature by thermoplastic deformation and dynamic recrystallization.

### 1.2 Inertia Welding — Formal Definition

**Inertia Welding (IW)** is a *kinetic-energy variant* of rotary friction welding. In this process, a **flywheel** is accelerated to a preset rotational speed (storing a known quantity of kinetic energy), then **disengaged from the driving motor** and simultaneously brought into contact with the stationary workpiece under axial pressure. The kinetic energy stored in the flywheel is the *sole* heat source, and the weld cycle ends naturally when the flywheel decelerates to zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Board Exam:**
> In **Continuous-Drive Friction Welding** → motor stays engaged throughout the cycle.
> In **Inertia Friction Welding** → motor is **disengaged**; flywheel inertia drives the process.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are trying to rub two metal rods together really fast. After a few seconds, your palms would burn — that's **friction heat**. Now imagine pressing the two rods together *hard* while doing this. The contact surface would soften and the rods would stick. **That is exactly friction welding.**

For **Inertia Welding**, think of a **spinning fidget spinner** (the flywheel) that has stored rotational energy. Now push a stationary pencil into the spinning spinner. The spinner will slow down, the contact point will heat up, and if you press hard enough, the two parts will fuse. The motor only *charged* the system; it did not drive the welding.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:**
> - *Friction Welding* = "Rub-and-Press"
> - *Inertia Welding* = "Spinning Flywheel + Press" (motor disconnects)

### 1.4 Process Variables & Standard Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Standard Range (Indicative) |
|---|---|---|
| Rotational speed | **N** | **500 – 3000 rpm** |
| Axial friction pressure | **P_f** | **30 – 100 MPa** |
| Upset (forge) pressure | **P_u** | **2 – 3 × P_f** |
| Coefficient of friction | **μ** | **0.3 – 0.5** (steel-on-steel at plastic state) |
| Burn-off / axial shortening | **ΔL** | **1 – 5 mm** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Friction Welding — Process Stages (Operational Logic)

The friction welding cycle is divided into **four sequential stages** that the KTU examiner expects you to reproduce:

1. **Pre-stage (Clamping & Alignment):** Workpieces are mounted in chucks; one rotates, the other is held axially movable but rotationally fixed. Precise alignment is critical — misalignment causes **asymmetric flash** and weak joints.
2. **Friction Stage (Heating Phase):** The rotating workpiece is brought against the stationary one under friction pressure **P_f**. Frictional heat raises the interface temperature to **0.8 T_m – 0.9 T_m** (where T_m = melting point in Kelvin). A plasticized layer forms and is extruded outward as **flash (upset metal)**.
3. **Upset / Forging Stage (Consolidation Phase):** Rotation is abruptly stopped (or decelerated rapidly) and the axial pressure is *increased* to **P_u**. This extrudes remaining contaminants, oxide films, and brittle intermetallics as flash, and consolidates the joint through **dynamic recrystallization**.
4. **Cooling & Holding Stage:** Pressure is maintained until the joint cools below the recrystallization temperature, preventing cracking or shrinkage voids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the bond form below melting?**
> At the interface, the metal is in a super-plastic state. Atoms on either side inter-diffuse across the clean, freshly-created metallic surfaces under high pressure, forming a metallurgical bond identical to the parent metal — without any fusion zone, HAZ cracking, or porosity.

### 2.2 Inertia Welding — Process Logic (Why it's Special)

- **Energy Storage:** Before contact, the flywheel is brought to a preset angular velocity **ω₀** by the drive motor.
- **Energy Decoupling:** The motor is **disengaged via clutch** the moment contact begins.
- **Energy Discharge:** The kinetic energy of the flywheel is converted into heat at the interface. Because the energy is *finite and predetermined*, the process is **self-limiting** — when the flywheel stops, the weld is complete.
- **No Time Parameter:** Unlike continuous-drive FW, the *control variable is energy*, not time.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \mu \cdot F \cdot v \cdot t$ | Heat generated by friction | J (Joules) |
| 2 | $v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \,/\, 60$ | Relative sliding velocity at interface | m/s |
| 3 | $P = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T \,/\, 60$ | Friction power dissipated at interface | W (Watts) |
| 4 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}\, I\, \omega^{2}$ | Kinetic energy stored in flywheel | J |
| 5 | $\omega = 2\pi N \,/\, 60$ | Angular velocity of flywheel | rad/s |
| 6 | $I = \tfrac{1}{2}\, m\, r^{2}$ | Moment of inertia of solid disc flywheel | kg·m² |
| 7 | $E = \pi^{2} m r^{2} N^{2}\,/\, 1800$ | Expanded flywheel energy form | J |
| 8 | $\eta = Q_{\text{useful}} \,/\, Q_{\text{total}}$ | Process thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $t_{w} = I\,\omega \,/\, T_{f}$ | Theoretical weld (deceleration) time | s |

> Where:
> - $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (dimensionless)
> - $F$ = axial friction force (N)
> - $v$ = relative velocity (m/s)
> - $t$ = friction time (s)
> - $D$ = outer diameter of interface (m)
> - $N$ = rotational speed (rpm)
> - $T$ = torque (N·m)
> - $I$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²)
> - $m$ = mass of flywheel (kg)
> - $r$ = radius of flywheel (m)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> Friction welding is used for **ship propeller shafts**, drill pipes, automotive axle half-shafts, valve bodies, and dissimilar-metal joints (e.g., copper-aluminium, steel-titanium) where fusion welding would form brittle intermetallics.

### 2.4 Sub-Categories of Friction Welding (Exam-Relevant)

- **Rotary (Continuous Drive) Friction Welding** — round cross-sections, most common.
- **Inertia Friction Welding** — flywheel-driven variant.
- **Orbital Friction Welding** — non-circular cross-sections; one part orbits a small radius.
- **Linear Friction Welding** — for non-circular or large parts (e.g., turbine blades); oscillation is linear.
- **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** — uses a non-consumable tool; widely used in **ship panel fabrication** (aluminium hulls, decks).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Heat Generated in Friction Welding

We start from the basic principle that the **mechanical work done by friction is fully (or partially) converted into heat** at the rubbing interface.

**Step 1 — Identify the frictional force.**

The frictional force resisting relative motion between the two workpieces is:

$$F_{f} = \mu \cdot F_{a}$$

where $F_{a}$ is the applied axial compressive force (N), and $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction at the interface (dimensionless).

**Step 2 — Identify the relative sliding velocity at the interface radius.**

For a workpiece of outer diameter $D$ (m), rotating at $N$ revolutions per minute, the linear (tangential) velocity at the rubbing surface is the circumference divided by the time per revolution:

$$v = \frac{\pi D}{60\,/\,N} = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the power dissipated.**

Power is force times velocity (in the direction of motion):

$$P = F_{f} \cdot v = \mu F_{a} \cdot \frac{\pi D N}{60}$$

This is the **instantaneous rate of heat generation** at the rubbing surface (Watts).

**Step 4 — Integrate power over the friction time.**

Heat is energy, so $Q = P \cdot t$. Multiplying the power expression by the friction duration $t$:

$$Q = \mu \cdot F_{a} \cdot \frac{\pi D N}{60} \cdot t$$

This is the **total heat generated** at the interface in Joules. A fraction $\eta$ of this heat is actually used to raise the interface temperature, the rest being lost to conduction into the bulk, to flash extrusion, and to radiation.

$$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{interface}} = \eta \cdot \mu \cdot F_{a} \cdot \frac{\pi D N t}{60}\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Energy Stored in a Solid-Disc Flywheel (Inertia Welding)

The kinetic energy of a rotating rigid body about its spin axis is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\, I\, \omega^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Moment of inertia of a solid disc.**

For a uniform solid disc of mass $m$ and radius $r$ rotating about its central (symmetry) axis:

$$I = \frac{1}{2}\, m\, r^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Angular velocity in rad/s.**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\pi N}{30}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the kinetic energy equation.**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\, m\, r^{2}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\pi N}{30}\right)^{2}$$

$$E = \frac{1}{4}\, m\, r^{2} \cdot \frac{\pi^{2} N^{2}}{900}$$

$$\boxed{\,E = \frac{\pi^{2}\, m\, r^{2}\, N^{2}}{3600}\,} \quad [\text{Joules}]$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Weld (Deceleration) Time in Inertia Welding

In inertia welding, once the motor disengages, the only torque acting on the flywheel is the **friction torque $T_f$** at the interface. Using Newton's rotational dynamics (torque = rate of change of angular momentum):

$$T_{f} = I\, \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

Assuming $T_f$ is approximately constant during the weld:

$$T_{f} \, \Delta t = I\, (\omega_{0} - \omega_{f})$$

Since the flywheel decelerates from $\omega_0$ to $0$ ($\omega_f = 0$):

$$\boxed{\,\Delta t_{w} = \frac{I\, \omega_{0}}{T_{f}} = \frac{I\, \omega_{0}}{\mu\, F_{a}\, r_{\text{interface}}}\,}$$

### 3.4 Energy-Balance Analytical Matrix

| Quantity | Continuous-Drive FW | Inertia FW |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Continuous motor torque | Pre-stored flywheel KE |
| Heat equation | $Q = \eta\,\mu F_a \pi D N t / 60$ | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ |
| Control parameter | Time $t$ + Speed $N$ | Energy $E$ + Speed $N_0$ |
| End-of-cycle signal | Timer / PLC stop command | Flywheel reaches $\omega = 0$ |
| Burn-off control | Upset distance / time | Natural, energy-driven |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Friction Welding — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Motor Drive Unit] --> B[Spindle and Chuck A]
    B --> C[Rotating Workpiece]
    C --> D[Weld Interface Plasticized Layer]
    E[Hydraulic or Servo Actuator] --> F[Chuck B Axially Movable]
    F --> G[Stationary Workpiece]
    G --> D
    D --> H[Flash Extrusion Ring]
    D --> I[Upset Forging Stage]
    I --> J[Completed Solid State Joint]
    K[PLC Controller] --> A
    K --> E
    K --> L[Parameter Monitor Speed Pressure Time]
    L --> K
```

### 4.2 Inertia Welding — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Motor] --> B1[Clutch Engagement Position 1]
    B1 --> C1[Flywheel Acceleration to Preset omega0]
    C1 --> D1[Clutch Disengagement Position 2]
    D1 --> E1[Axial Thrust Brings Workpieces Together]
    E1 --> F1[Friction Interface Plastic Deformation]
    F1 --> G1[Flywheel Decelerates omega to 0]
    G1 --> H1[Energy Exhausted Weld Cycle Complete]
    H1 --> I1[Upset Pressure Held for Consolidation]
    I1 --> J1[Solid State Bond Formed]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Process Flow — Friction vs Inertia

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Friction Welding] --> A1[Clamp Both Parts]
    A1 --> A2[Rotate Part A at Speed N]
    A2 --> A3[Apply Friction Pressure Pf]
    A3 --> A4[Stop Rotation]
    A4 --> A5[Apply Upset Pressure Pu]
    A5 --> A6[Hold and Cool]
    A6 --> A7[Finished Joint]
    B0[Inertia Welding] --> B1[Spin Flywheel to N0]
    B1 --> B2[Disengage Motor]
    B2 --> B3[Apply Axial Pressure Pf]
    B3 --> B4[Flywheel Drives Weld]
    B4 --> B5[Flywheel Stops Naturally]
    B5 --> B6[Apply Upset Pressure]
    B6 --> B7[Finished Joint]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Differentiate between **fusion welding** and **friction welding** in terms of heat source and metallurgical state of the joint.

**Model Answer:**
- **Fusion Welding:** External heat source (arc, flame, laser) raises the base metal **above its melting point**. A **cast fusion zone** with a distinct **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** forms. Risk of porosity, solidification cracking, and HAZ softening.
- **Friction Welding:** Heat is generated by **mechanical friction at the interface**. The metal reaches a **plastic but solid** state (≈ 0.8 T_m – 0.9 T_m). No fusion zone, no HAZ in the classical sense. Joint forms by **dynamic recrystallization** under upset pressure.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`** Why is **inertia welding** classified as a *self-limiting* process?

**Model Answer:**
Inertia welding uses the **kinetic energy pre-stored in a flywheel**. Once the motor is disengaged, there is no further energy input. The process stops **automatically when the flywheel decelerates to zero angular velocity** ($\omega = 0$). Hence the cycle is **self-terminating** and the heat input is precisely controlled by the initial flywheel energy — no external timer is required.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Choice Pattern:** KTU typically allows students to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from the same module.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of continuous-drive rotary friction welding**. List any **four process parameters** that influence joint strength. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mild-steel shaft of **outer diameter 60 mm** is to be joined to a flange by friction welding. The coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.4$, the axial friction force $F_a = 25{,}000$ N, the rotational speed $N = 1500$ rpm, and the friction time $t = 6$ s. Calculate the **total heat generated** at the interface. Assume thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle:**

1. Workpiece **A** is clamped in the rotating chuck driven by a motor; workpiece **B** is clamped in a stationary, axially-movable chuck.
2. Motor brings **A** to the preset rotational speed **N**.
3. Hydraulic actuator pushes **B** axially against **A** — friction pressure **P_f** is applied.
4. Frictional heat raises the interface to plastic state; **flash** extrudes outward.
5. Rotation is stopped; **upset pressure P_u** (≈ 2–3 × P_f) consolidates the joint.
6. Pressure is held during cooling to prevent shrinkage voids.

**Four critical process parameters:**
- Rotational speed **N** (rpm)
- Friction pressure **P_f** (MPa)
- Friction (heating) time **t** (s)
- Upset pressure **P_u** (MPa)
- *(Any four of the above; burn-off length is also acceptable.)*

**(b) Numerical:**

Step 1 — Compute the sliding velocity at the interface.

$$v = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.060 \times 1500}{60}$$

$$v = \frac{\pi \times 90}{60} = 1.5 \pi \approx 4.712 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 2 — Compute the frictional force.

$$F_f = \mu \cdot F_a = 0.4 \times 25{,}000 = 10{,}000 \text{ N}$$

Step 3 — Compute total mechanical work (heat equivalent) at the interface.

$$Q_{\text{mech}} = F_f \cdot v \cdot t = 10{,}000 \times 4.712 \times 6$$

$$Q_{\text{mech}} \approx 282{,}744 \text{ J} \approx 282.7 \text{ kJ}$$

Step 4 — Apply thermal efficiency.

$$Q_{\text{interface}} = \eta \times Q_{\text{mech}} = 0.85 \times 282{,}744$$

$$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{interface}} \approx 240{,}333 \text{ J} \approx 240.3 \text{ kJ}\,}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle of inertia friction welding** with a labelled schematic. Derive the expression for the **kinetic energy stored in a solid-disc flywheel** in terms of mass, radius, and rotational speed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A solid-disc flywheel has **mass 80 kg**, **radius 0.25 m**, and is spun to **3000 rpm** before being disengaged. Compute (i) the **moment of inertia**, (ii) the **angular velocity**, (iii) the **stored kinetic energy**, and (iv) the **deceleration time** if the friction torque at the interface is constant at **T_f = 60 N·m**. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Principle:**

A flywheel is accelerated by a motor to a preset speed $N_0$. The motor is then disengaged via a clutch, and simultaneously the rotating flywheel-spindle is thrust axially against a stationary workpiece. The stored kinetic energy dissipates as frictional heat at the interface. As the flywheel slows, heat generation decreases; the process naturally terminates when $\omega = 0$.

**Derivation of stored energy:**

$$I = \frac{1}{2}\, m r^{2}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N_0}{60}$$

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\, I\, \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\, m r^{2} \cdot \left(\frac{2\pi N_0}{60}\right)^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,E = \frac{\pi^{2}\, m\, r^{2}\, N_{0}^{2}}{3600}\, \text{ Joules}\,}$$

**(b) Numerical:**

(i) Moment of inertia of a solid disc:

$$I = \frac{1}{2}\, m r^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 80 \times (0.25)^{2}$$

$$I = 40 \times 0.0625 = 2.5 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

(ii) Angular velocity:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi N_0}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 100 \pi \approx 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

(iii) Stored kinetic energy:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\, I\, \omega_{0}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times (314.16)^{2}$$

$$E = 1.25 \times 98{,}696.5 \approx 123{,}370 \text{ J} \approx 123.4 \text{ kJ}$$

(iv) Deceleration time:

$$\Delta t_w = \frac{I\, \omega_0}{T_f} = \frac{2.5 \times 314.16}{60}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta t_w \approx 13.09 \text{ s}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing $N$ and $\omega$:** Many students forget to convert **rpm → rad/s**. KTU deducts **1 mark** for unit inconsistency.
> 2. **Using $r$ in the energy equation:** The flywheel radius $r$ enters the moment of inertia; the *interface radius* $D/2$ enters the friction torque. Mixing them up is a **fatal conceptual error** worth **2 marks**.
> 3. **Ignoring thermal efficiency $\eta$:** Numerical answers without $\eta$ considered when given in the data are penalized.
> 4. **Not stating "solid-state" explicitly:** KTU awards the metallurgy marks only when the answer explicitly mentions **dynamic recrystallization / plastic deformation below solidus**.
> 5. **Omitting the four process parameters** in 7-mark theory questions — the examiner expects a *named list*, not just a paragraph.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- Friction welding is a **solid-state** process — *no melting, no filler, no shielding gas.*
- Heat is generated by **mechanical friction** at the interface.
- Two principal variants: **Continuous-Drive FW** (motor stays engaged) and **Inertia FW** (motor disengages, flywheel stores the energy).
- Process stages: **Clamping → Friction Heating → Upset Forging → Cooling under Pressure.**
- Governing heat equation: $Q = \eta\, \mu F_a \pi D N t / 60$.
- Governing flywheel energy equation: $E = \pi^{2} m r^{2} N^{2} / 3600$.
- **Upset pressure** (P_u) is typically **2–3 × Friction pressure** (P_f).
- Inertia welding is **self-limiting** because energy is finite; cycle ends when $\omega = 0$.
- Interface temperature reaches **0.8 T_m to 0.9 T_m** (sub-solidus).
- Bond forms by **dynamic recrystallization** + atomic inter-diffusion under pressure.
- Applications in shipbuilding: **propeller shafts, valve bodies, hull panel seams (via FSW), dissimilar-metal transition joints.**
- Always remember to convert **rpm → rad/s** using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Radiant Energy Welding (Electron Beam and Laser Beam)
Underwater Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Underwater Welding

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Underwater Welding** is defined as a specialized fusion-joining process performed in a submerged aqueous environment, where the welding arc is struck, sustained, and shielded against the surrounding water to produce a metallurgically sound joint between parent metal components. It is officially classified by the American Welding Society (AWS) D3.6M – *Underwater Welding Code* as a Category A, B, or C weld depending on the structural criticality.

From the KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST741) perspective, **underwater welding** is treated as the **primary in-situ repair methodology for ship hulls, offshore jackets, submarine pipelines, and dock-gate structures** where dry-docking is either uneconomical or operationally infeasible.

The process combines the principles of:
- **Arc physics** (ionization of gas molecules in a conductive water medium)
- **Marine metallurgy** (rapid quenching effects of water on the weld pool)
- **Hyperbaric mechanics** (pressure-induced changes in arc behavior)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** AWS D3.6M:2010 (Class A – Hyperbaric) and Class B – Wet Welding are the two governing codes adopted by most classification societies including IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, and ABS for ship repair welding.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lighter Underwater" Analogy

Imagine trying to light a matchstick and keep it burning while holding it **completely submerged in a glass of water**. The flame dies instantly because water rapidly steals the heat (high thermal conductivity ≈ **0.6 W/m·K**) and chokes the flame by displacing oxygen.

Now, suppose we coat the match head with a **thick waterproof wax layer that releases a cloud of non-flammable gas** when struck. Suddenly, the flame survives because a tiny "bubble of protection" forms around it. **This is exactly how underwater welding works!**

The **flux coating** on a wet-welding electrode acts like that wax layer. When the arc strikes, the flux decomposes to produce a thick **gaseous shield** (primarily CO, CO₂, H₂, and water vapor) that physically displaces water from the immediate weld zone, allowing the arc to survive long enough to melt the parent metal and filler.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** Underwater welding is NOT welding *despite* water — it is welding *because* the consumable creates a localized, transient gaseous cavity (called the **bubble envelope**) that protects the arc.

## 1.3 Why Underwater Welding is Critical for Shipbuilding

| Application | Justification |
|---|---|
| Emergency hull repair at sea | Avoids 3–6 weeks of dry-docking downtime |
| Offshore platform leg reinforcement | Cost of dry-docking ≈ **$ 2–5 million USD/day** |
| Submarine hull crack remediation | Cannot dry-dock a submarine mid-mission |
| Pier, lock-gate, and jetty repair | In-situ accessibility without dewatering |
| Pipeline (seabed) tie-ins | Hyperbaric habitat welding at 50–300 m depths |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Benchmark:** Modern VLCCs and container vessels operating with hull damage of > 10% plate thickness are permitted by class societies to perform underwater emergency welding to reach the next port — *provided an AWS D3.6M Class B qualified procedure is followed.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Underwater Welding (KTU Module 3)

Underwater welding is broadly classified into **three macro-families**, and the KTU 2024 scheme expects students to clearly differentiate among them:

### Family 1 — Wet Welding (Open Water / Direct Contact)
- Arc struck **directly in the surrounding water**.
- No physical enclosure.
- Diver-welder operates with hand-held electrode holder.
- Maximum practical depth ≈ **100 m** (limited by diver physiology, not welding physics).

### Family 2 — Dry Welding (Hyperbaric Habitat / Chamber Welding)
- Weld zone is enclosed inside a **pressurized steel habitat** purged of water.
- Habitat is pressurized to match ambient hydrostatic pressure (so the steel walls don't collapse).
- Conventional welding equipment (GTAW, GMAW, SMAW, SAW) used inside.
- Depths achievable: **up to 300 m and beyond** (current record ≈ 470 m for pipeline tie-ins).

### Family 3 — One-Atmosphere Welding (Dry Chamber at 1 atm)
- Chamber is sealed and filled with dry gas at **surface pressure (101.325 kPa)**.
- No hyperbaric effects on arc — metallurgically equivalent to surface welding.
- Robotic manipulators used because humans cannot operate at 1 atm inside a deep habitat without full saturation diving.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Memory Hook:** *"Wet = Wetsuit; Dry = Habitat; One-atm = Robot."*

## 2.2 Detailed Process Sub-Taxonomy (Inside Each Family)

| Family | Process Variant | Polarity / Electrode Type | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wet | Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) | DCEP, cellulosic / rutile flux | Hull plate patching, doubler plates |
| Wet | Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW) | DCEP, seamless tubular wire | Long seams, pipeline repair |
| Wet | Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) | DCEP with local gas shroud | Experimental, limited adoption |
| Dry | SMAW / GMAW / FCAW / SAW | Standard surface parameters | Critical structural welds |
| Dry | Orbital GTAW | Mechanized orbital torch | Pipeline girth welds |
| One-Atm | Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW) | Remote mechanized | Submarine reactor compartments |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Sequence (Wet SMAW — KTU Focus)

Since wet SMAW is the **most commonly tested process in KTU ESE**, here is the operational logic:

1. **Surface Preparation:** Diver cleans the weld zone using a hydraulic grinder / needle scaler to remove marine growth, rust, paint, and salt deposits within a 50 mm band on each side of the joint.
2. **Joint Geometry:** A **V-groove (60° included angle)** with 1–2 mm root face and 2–3 mm root gap is preferred to compensate for the rapid solidification and poor fusion typical underwater.
3. **Electrode Selection:** Use AWS E6013 / E7014 type with **extra-thick waterproof flux coating** (cellulosic-rutile, ~25–35% by mass of electrode).
4. **Arc Initiation:** Striking is done with a sharp scratch or lift technique. Arc length is maintained at **2–4 mm** (shorter than surface welding) to minimize bubble escape.
5. **Travel Speed:** Reduced to **60–100 mm/min** (vs 150–250 mm/min in air) to ensure sufficient heat input.
6. **Bubble Formation:** Flux decomposes → forms CO, CO₂, H₂ → displaces water → creates the protective envelope.
7. **Weld Bead:** Solidifies rapidly due to high heat extraction by surrounding water.
8. **Inspection:** Visual + magnetic particle (MPI) or ultrasonic (UT) by qualified inspection diver.

## 2.4 The Physics of the Underwater Arc — "Why" It Burns

When a bare metal electrode touches wet steel and is withdrawn, an arc forms. Three concurrent phenomena allow survival:

| Phenomenon | Mechanism | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermionic emission** | Electrode tip reaches > 3000 °C | Releases electrons to sustain arc |
| **Flux dissociation** | Cellulose (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ + H₂O → CO + H₂ + CO₂ | Generates shielding gas bubble |
| **Joule heating** | I²R heating in arc plasma column | Maintains plasma temperature > 5000 K |
| **Steam jacket** | Water vapor from arc periphery | Provides partial thermal insulation |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examination-grade and have been verified against AWS D3.6M and AWS A2.4 (Standard Symbols).**

### A. Hydrostatic & Hyperbaric Pressure

$$
P_{\text{hydro}} \;=\; \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

where:
- $\rho_{\text{sw}} = 1025 \;\text{kg/m}^3$ (standard seawater density, slightly higher than freshwater **1000 kg/m³**)
- $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$
- $h$ = depth in metres
- $P_{\text{hydro}}$ in Pascals

The **total ambient pressure** experienced by the arc is:

$$
P_{\text{amb}} \;=\; P_{\text{atm}} + P_{\text{hydro}} \;=\; 101325 \;+\; 10058.25 \cdot h \;\;\text{[Pa]}
$$

(where $10058.25 \cdot h$ comes from $\rho g = 1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25$ corrected)

### B. Heat Input Equation (Modified for Underwater)

$$
H \;=\; \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \;\;\; \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

Underwater, the thermal efficiency $\eta$ **drops from 0.7–0.85 (surface) to 0.60–0.70 (wet)** due to convective losses.

- $V$ = arc voltage (V)
- $I$ = welding current (A)
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/s or mm/min — check unit consistency)

### C. Critical Depth for Hydrogen Cracking Susceptibility

$$
C_{\text{eq}} \;=\; C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15} \;\;\text{[wt.\%]}
$$

Wet welds at depths > 50 m often require **$C_{\text{eq}} < 0.40$** electrodes to avoid underbead cracking.

### D. Cooling Rate (Newton's Law of Cooling Adapted)

$$
\frac{dT}{dt} \;=\; \frac{-h_c \cdot A \cdot (T - T_w)}{m \cdot c_p}
$$

where:
- $h_c$ ≈ **1500–5000 W/m²·K** (forced convection in water, ~30× higher than in air at ~30 W/m²·K)
- $T_w$ = water temperature (~10–25 °C)
- $m, c_p$ = mass and specific heat of the weld pool

> This explains why underwater welds have **finer grain but higher hardness** than equivalent surface welds.

### E. Diver Physiological Constraint (Bonus – Often Asked)

$$
\text{Maximum practical wet-welding depth} \;\approx\; 100 \;\text{m} \;\;\text{(surface-supplied air diving)}
$$

Beyond this, **saturation diving** with mixed-gas (He-O₂) is mandatory.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure as a Function of Diving Depth

We start from the fundamental principle that **pressure at any depth in a static fluid** equals the weight of the fluid column per unit area above it.

**Step 1:** Consider an elemental horizontal slab of water at depth $h$ with cross-sectional area $A$ and thickness $dh$.

The mass of this slab is:

$$
dm \;=\; \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot A \cdot dh
$$

**Step 2:** The weight (force due to gravity) of this slab is:

$$
dF \;=\; dm \cdot g \;=\; \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot A \cdot g \cdot dh
$$

**Step 3:** Pressure is defined as force per unit area:

$$
dP \;=\; \frac{dF}{A} \;=\; \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot g \cdot dh
$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from the free surface ($h = 0$, where $P = P_{\text{atm}}$) to a depth $h$:

$$
\int_{P_{\text{atm}}}^{P_{\text{total}}} dP \;=\; \int_{0}^{h} \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot g \cdot dh
$$

$$
P_{\text{total}} - P_{\text{atm}} \;=\; \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{total}} \;=\; P_{\text{atm}} + \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot g \cdot h\,}
$$

**Numerical Evaluation** (standard seawater, $h = 30$ m — typical KTU numerical):

$$
P_{\text{total}} \;=\; 101325 \;+\; (1025)(9.81)(30)
$$

$$
P_{\text{total}} \;=\; 101325 \;+\; 301657.5
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{total}} \;\approx\; 4.03 \times 10^{5} \;\text{Pa} \;\approx\; 4.03 \;\text{bar}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** At just 30 m, the diver is breathing air at 4 atm absolute. The arc, being a gaseous plasma, is also at ~4 atm. This increases arc **stiffness**, reduces arc **cone angle**, and elevates **voltage requirement by ~1 V per 5 m depth.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Input Calculation for an Underwater Wet Weld

**Given data (KTU Sample Problem style):**
- Voltage $V = 28$ V
- Current $I = 180$ A
- Travel speed $v = 120$ mm/min
- Underwater thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.65$
- Specific heat of water $c_p = 4186$ J/kg·K

**Step 1:** Convert travel speed to mm/s for SI consistency:

$$
v \;=\; \frac{120 \;\text{mm/min}}{60} \;=\; 2.0 \;\text{mm/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the heat input formula:

$$
H \;=\; \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}
$$

> Note: dividing by 1000 converts J/mm to kJ/mm.

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values:

$$
H \;=\; \frac{(0.65)(28)(180)}{(2.0)(1000)}
$$

$$
H \;=\; \frac{3276}{2000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,H \;=\; 1.638 \;\text{kJ/mm}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparative Analysis:**

A surface weld with $\eta = 0.80$, same $V,I$, would yield:

$$
H_{\text{air}} \;=\; \frac{(0.80)(28)(180)}{(2.0)(1000)} \;=\; 2.016 \;\text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Percentage loss due to underwater convection:**

$$
\Delta H \;=\; \frac{2.016 - 1.638}{2.016} \times 100\% \;\approx\; 18.75\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even with identical electrical parameters, an underwater weld receives ~19% less effective heat into the plate. This mandates the welder to compensate by **slower travel speed** or **higher current** — which is why wet welding is inherently slower.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cooling Rate and Resulting Hardness Relationship

**Empirical formula (Duren / Bailey correlation for C-Mn steel wet welds):**

$$
t_{8/5} \;=\; \frac{E}{2\pi \lambda \rho c_p} \cdot \left[\frac{1}{(500 - T_o)^2} - \frac{1}{(800 - T_o)^2}\right]^{-1}
$$

where:
- $t_{8/5}$ = cooling time between 800 °C and 500 °C (critical for transformation)
- $E$ = heat input per unit length (J/m)
- $\lambda$ = thermal conductivity of steel ≈ **45 W/m·K**
- $\rho c_p$ = volumetric heat capacity ≈ **$7.12 \times 10^6$ J/m³·K** for steel
- $T_o$ = preheat / interpass temperature

For our derived $H = 1.638$ kJ/mm and assuming $T_o = 25$ °C (no preheat):

$$
E \;=\; 1.638 \;\text{kJ/mm} \times 1000 \;\text{mm/m} \;=\; 1638 \;\text{kJ/m} \;=\; 1.638 \times 10^6 \;\text{J/m}
$$

**Plug into $t_{8/5}$ formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{8/5} \;&=\; \frac{1.638 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 45 \times 7.12 \times 10^6} \cdot \left[\frac{1}{(500-25)^2} - \frac{1}{(800-25)^2}\right]^{-1} \\[8pt]
&=\; \frac{1.638 \times 10^6}{2.013 \times 10^9} \cdot \left[\frac{1}{225625} - \frac{1}{600625}\right]^{-1} \\[8pt]
&=\; 8.13 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \left[4.43 \times 10^{-6} - 1.665 \times 10^{-6}\right]^{-1} \\[8pt]
&=\; 8.13 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \left[2.766 \times 10^{-6}\right]^{-1} \\[8pt]
&=\; 8.13 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 3.615 \times 10^{5} \\[8pt]
&\approx\; 293.9 \;\text{seconds} \;\approx\; 4.9 \;\text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hardness Estimation (Empirical – AWS D3.6M Annex):**

$$
\text{HV}_{10} \;\approx\; 350 + 100 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-0.012 \cdot t_{8/5}}\right)
$$

$$
\text{HV}_{10} \;\approx\; 350 + 100 \cdot (1 - e^{-3.527}) \;\approx\; 350 + 100 \cdot (0.9706) \;\approx\; 447 \;\text{HV}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Hardness > 400 HV in C-Mn steel is in the cold-cracking susceptibility zone.** Wet welds typically require post-weld tempering or use of low-C_eq austenitic stainless electrodes (e.g., AWS E309LMo) for marine repair.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Process Selection

| Criterion | Wet SMAW | Wet FCAW | Dry Habitat SMAW | Dry Habitat GMAW | One-Atm GTAW |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max Depth (m)** | 100 | 80 | 300 | 350 | 1000+ |
| **Weld Quality (AWS D3.6M class)** | B | B | A | A | A |
| **Productivity (kg/h)** | 1.5–3 | 2–4 | 3–5 | 5–10 | 6–12 |
| **Equipment Cost (relative)** | Low | Medium | Very High | Very High | Extreme |
| **Setup Time** | < 1 hr | < 1 hr | 24–72 hr | 24–72 hr | Days–Weeks |
| **Hydrogen Risk** | High | High | Moderate | Low | Very Low |
| **Mechanical Properties vs Surface Weld** | 60–80% | 70–85% | 95–100% | 95–100% | 100% |
| **KTU ESE Frequency** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Underwater Welding] --> B[Wet Welding]
    A --> C[Dry Welding]
    A --> D[One-Atmosphere Welding]

    B --> B1[SMAW - Cellulosic Electrode]
    B --> B2[FCAW - Tubular Wire]
    B --> B3[GMAW - Local Gas Shroud]

    C --> C1[Habitat Hyperbaric]
    C --> C2[Cofferdam Dry]
    C1 --> C3[SMAW inside Habitat]
    C1 --> C4[GMAW inside Habitat]
    C1 --> C5[FCAW inside Habitat]
    C1 --> C6[SAW inside Habitat]

    D --> D1[Robotic Manipulators]
    D --> D2[Remote GTAW Torch]
    D --> D3[Plasma Arc]

    B1 --> E1[Max Depth 100 m]
    B2 --> E2[Max Depth 80 m]
    C3 --> E3[Max Depth 300 m]
    D2 --> E4[Max Depth 1000 m plus]
```

## 4.2 Wet SMAW Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Surface_Deck[Surface Deck - Welding Machine]
        P[DC Power Source]
        C[Cable Reel]
    end

    subgraph Underwater_Zone[Underwater Work Site]
        D[Diver-Welder]
        EH[Electrode Holder]
        E[Cellulosic Flux Electrode]
        W[Parent Metal Hull Plate]
        B[Bubble Envelope Shielding Gas]
    end

    P --> C
    C --> EH
    EH --> E
    E -->|Arc| W
    B -.->|Displaces Water| E

    W --> H[Heat Affected Zone HAZ]
    W --> WM[Welded Metal - Rapidly Quenched]
    W --> UZ[Unaffected Base Metal]
```

## 4.3 Hyperbaric Habitat (Dry Welding) Schematic — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HabitatPressureVessel[Habitat - Pressurized Steel Vessel]
        T[Torch and Electrode Holder]
        P1[Welder inside at Ambient Habitat Pressure]
        V[Viewport - Acrylic]
        S[Sleeve Entry for Arms]
        G[Gas Inlet - Argon or Air Mix]
        X[Exhaust - Filtered]
    end

    subgraph ExternalEnvironment[External Hyperbaric Environment]
        SEA[Seawater Ambient Pressure]
        SEABED[Seabed or Hull Surface]
    end

    SEABED -->|Welded Component| HabitatPressureVessel
    SEA -->|External Force| HabitatPressureVessel
    P1 --> T
    T -->|Arc and Weld Pool| SEABED
    G --> HabitatPressureVessel
    X --> HabitatPressureVessel
```

## 4.4 Underwater Electrode Cross-Sectional Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Outer Waterproof Lacquer Layer] --> F[Flux Coating - Cellulosic and Rutile]
    F --> EX[Extrusion - Powder Compaction]
    EX --> CR[Core Wire Mild Steel]
    CR --> CT[Current Pickup Tip]

    F -.-> G1[CO - Reduces Surface Tension]
    F -.-> G2[CO2 - Shields Arc]
    F -.-> G3[H2 - Reduces Oxygen Partial Pressure]
    F -.-> G4[Slag - Insulates Bead from Water]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between wet welding and dry welding underwater. Mention any two applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Wet Welding:** Performed directly in the surrounding water without any enclosure around the weld zone. The arc is shielded only by the gaseous decomposition products of the electrode flux coating.
> **Applications:** (i) Emergency hull plate patch at sea. (ii) Pier pile reinforcement below waterline.
>
> **Dry Welding:** Performed inside a sealed pressurized habitat (cofferdam or chamber) that is purged of water. The arc burns in a controlled gaseous atmosphere at ambient hydrostatic pressure.
> **Applications:** (i) Critical structural welds on offshore platforms (Class A welds). (ii) Submarine pipeline girth welds using orbital GTAW.
>
> *Word count ≈ 90; expected time: 5 min.*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is cellulosic-type electrode preferred for wet underwater SMAW?**

**Model Answer:**

> Cellulosic electrodes (e.g., AWS E6010, E7010) are preferred because:
> (i) Their flux contains ~30% cellulose, which on heating decomposes to **CO + H₂**, producing a thick gaseous envelope that displaces water and shields the arc.
> (ii) They generate a deeply penetrating, fast-freezing arc suitable for vertical and overhead wet positions.
> (iii) The hydrogen evolved helps reduce iron oxide on the rusty, wet plate, improving weld metal cleanliness.
> (iv) Their DCEN/DCEP compatibility works reliably with surface-supplied welding generators used by divers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of underwater welding processes** with neat diagrams. Discuss the **advantages and limitations of wet SMAW.** *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A wet SMAW operation is being performed at a depth of **25 m in seawater** using the following parameters: Arc Voltage = 30 V, Current = 200 A, Travel speed = 100 mm/min. Given $\eta = 0.65$ for underwater conditions, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, and $g = 9.81$ m/s². Calculate:
(i) The hydrostatic pressure at the weld location. *(3 Marks)*
(ii) The total ambient pressure acting on the arc. *(2 Marks)*
(iii) The heat input per unit length. *(2 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification & Wet SMAW Discussion:**

**Classification:**

| Family | Sub-Process | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Wet** | SMAW, FCAW, GMAW (local shield) | Arc directly in water |
| **Dry** | Habitat SMAW / GMAW / FCAW / SAW / Orbital GTAW | Pressurized chamber |
| **One-Atmosphere** | Remote GTAW, PAW | 1 atm dry chamber with robots |

**Advantages of Wet SMAW:**
1. **Low setup cost** — only a standard DC generator, waterproof electrodes, and a diver are needed.
2. **Rapid deployment** — emergency repair within hours, not days.
3. **Mobility** — diver can access complex geometries inaccessible to habitats.
4. **No need for hyperbaric chamber logistics** — suitable for ships in remote anchorages.

**Limitations of Wet SMAW:**
1. **High hydrogen content** in weld metal → risk of **underbead and HAZ cracking**.
2. **Rapid quenching** → coarse, hard microstructure (HV ~400+).
3. **Reduced ductility and impact toughness** (~60–80% of surface weld).
4. **Visible porosity** due to steam generation at the solidification front.
5. **Limited to non-critical welds** (AWS Class B) by classification societies.
6. **Diver safety hazards:** electric shock, decompression sickness (the "bends"), hypothermia, marine life.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Hydrostatic Pressure:**

$$
P_{\text{hydro}} = \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 25
$$

$$
P_{\text{hydro}} = 251{,}381.25 \;\text{Pa} \;\approx\; 2.51 \;\text{bar}
$$

**(ii) Total Ambient Pressure:**

$$
P_{\text{amb}} = P_{\text{atm}} + P_{\text{hydro}} = 101{,}325 + 251{,}381.25
$$

$$
P_{\text{amb}} = 352{,}706.25 \;\text{Pa} \;\approx\; 3.53 \;\text{bar}
$$

**(iii) Heat Input:**

Convert speed: $v = 100/60 = 1.667$ mm/s

$$
H = \frac{\eta V I}{v \times 1000} = \frac{0.65 \times 30 \times 200}{1.667 \times 1000} = \frac{3900}{1667} \approx 2.34 \;\text{kJ/mm}
$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a schematic, describe the **hyperbaric habitat welding method** for underwater applications. Explain **why preheat and interpass temperature control are critical** in this process. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **metallurgical problems encountered in underwater wet welds**, namely **(i)** hydrogen cracking, **(ii)** porosity, and **(iii)** loss of toughness. For each, state **one preventive measure.** *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hyperbaric Habitat Welding:**

A hyperbaric habitat is a **sealed, windowed steel chamber** lowered over the weld site and pressurized (typically with air or He-O₂ mix) to balance the external hydrostatic pressure. The diver-welder works inside the dry chamber at **ambient external pressure** using conventional welding equipment.

**Key components:**
- Steel habitat structure (rated for design depth pressure)
- Acrylic viewports
- Welding sleeve entries for the diver
- Gas inlet/outlet for pressure regulation
- Tool and electrode feed-through ports
- Internal lighting and ventilation

**Why preheat & interpass control are critical:**

1. At elevated pressures, **hydrogen solubility in the weld pool increases**, raising the risk of **cold (hydrogen-induced) cracking** in the HAZ. Preheat (typically 75–150 °C) drives off dissolved hydrogen and slows the cooling rate.
2. Maintaining interpass temperature **≤ 230 °C** for C-Mn steel prevents excessive grain coarsening in the HAZ.
3. AWS D3.6M specifies **mandatory preheat/interpass tables** based on combined thickness and $C_{\text{eq}}$.

**(b) Metallurgical Problems and Preventive Measures:**

| Problem | Mechanism | Preventive Measure |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Hydrogen Cracking** | High H₂ partial pressure dissolves in austenite, precipitates at martensite laths on cooling → HIC | Use low-hydrogen electrodes (e.g., E7016 type) + preheat to 100 °C + PWHT where feasible |
| **(ii) Porosity** | Steam bubbles (H₂O → H₂ + ½O₂) get trapped at the rapidly solidifying weld front | Slower travel speed + higher voltage to allow gas escape + proper joint cleaning |
| **(iii) Loss of Toughness** | Rapid quenching by water produces hard bainite/martensite; coarse columnar grains | Use austenitic stainless filler (E309L, E309LMo) which tolerates rapid cooling without phase transformation; or apply temper-bead technique |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **In Numerical Problems:** Students commonly *forget to add atmospheric pressure* when asked for "total ambient pressure" — only reporting hydrostatic. Deduct **1 mark**.
> 2. **In Theory Answers:** When listing "limitations of wet welding," many students *miss the electrical-shock hazard to the diver*. Always mention diver safety as a separate point.
> 3. **In Heat Input:** Watch unit consistency — if travel speed is in mm/min, the denominator becomes $(v/60) \times 1000$. Incorrect unit conversion = lose **1 mark**.
> 4. **Do NOT confuse** "dry welding" (hyperbaric, pressurized) with "one-atmosphere welding" (1 atm dry chamber). KTU examiners frequently set traps on this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These 12 Points Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- [x] **Underwater welding is governed by AWS D3.6M** (Class A = hyperbaric dry; Class B = wet).
- [x] **Three families:** Wet, Dry (Hyperbaric), One-Atmosphere (1 atm dry chamber with robots).
- [x] **Wet SMAW** uses **cellulosic electrodes** (E6010/E7010) producing **CO + H₂ shielding gas**.
- [x] **Hydrostatic pressure formula:** $P = \rho_{sw} g h$ — use $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, NOT 1000.
- [x] **Total pressure at depth** = atmospheric + hydrostatic.
- [x] **Arc voltage rises ~1 V per 5 m depth** due to gas density increase.
- [x] **Thermal efficiency drops from ~0.80 (air) to ~0.65 (wet)** because of convective heat loss.
- [x] **Heat input formula:** $H = \eta V I / (v \times 1000)$ kJ/mm.
- [x] **Cooling rate underwater is ~30× faster than in air** → hard, brittle martensite/bainite.
- [x] **Maximum practical wet-welding depth ≈ 100 m** (diver physiological limit, not welding).
- [x] **Dry habitat welding can reach ~300 m**; one-atmosphere can reach **1000+ m**.
- [x] **Common defects in wet welds:** porosity, hydrogen cracking, slag inclusions, lack of fusion, undercut.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Underwater Welding**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, PENST741 — Joining Techniques in Shipbuilding Technology.*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wet, Dry, Hyperbaric and Cavity Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Underwater Welding in Shipbuilding

Ship hulls operate in a corrosive, fatigue-loaded marine environment. Damage events (groundings, collisions, corrosion wastage, propeller-shaft fractures) frequently occur **below the waterline**. Conventional shop-floor arc welding cannot be used because the surrounding seawater violently cools the weld pool, introduces hydrogen, and disrupts arc stability. This created the need for a family of in-situ marine joining processes collectively called **Underwater Welding (UWW)**.

Underwater welding is broadly classified into four process families based on how water is dealt with at the arc:

| S.No | Process Family | Water Handling Strategy |
|------|----------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Wet Welding** | Water remains in direct contact with the arc |
| 2 | **Dry Welding** | Water locally displaced by a small chamber |
| 3 | **Hyperbaric Welding** | Water completely displaced by a pressurized habitat |
| 4 | **Cavity Welding** | Water temporarily excluded by forming a sealed cavity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 explicitly mandates comparative study of wet, dry, hyperbaric and cavity welding with reference to ship repair scenarios, weld quality, hydrogen cracking and applicable AWS / EN standards.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Board-Standard Terminology)

### Wet Welding
A fusion welding process in which the arc and weld pool are **deliberately exposed to the surrounding water**, and no physical barrier separates the electrode from the aqueous environment. Specially coated "waterproof" cellulosic or rutile electrodes are used. The process is governed by **AWS D3.6M — Class C** (lowest quality underwater classification).

### Dry Welding
An underwater welding process in which water is **locally excluded** from the immediate weld zone using a small open-bottomed chamber (habitat) bolted or magnetically sealed against the hull plate. The welder may work in the dry chamber at near-atmospheric pressure or under moderate pressure.

### Hyperbaric Welding
A subclass of dry welding performed inside a **pressurized habitat** where internal pressure equals the external hydrostatic pressure of the operating depth. The arc therefore behaves under elevated ambient pressure (typically 1 bar to 35 bar). Governed by **AWS D3.6M — Class A** (highest quality, equivalent to surface welds).

### Cavity Welding
A semi-dry process where a **temporary sealed cavity** (using epoxy, cement, or rubber blankets) is constructed over the damaged area, water is pumped out, and welding proceeds in the dried cavity. The cavity is broken down after weld completion. Used for emergency plate patches up to ~10 m depth.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Cooking in the Rain":**
> - **Wet Welding** = trying to light a campfire in heavy rain. You use special "rain-proof" matches (waterproof electrodes) and accept that the food (weld) will be partially ruined.
> - **Dry Welding** = setting up a small tent with a hole overhead, then cooking inside it. The rain (water) is locally pushed away.
> - **Hyperbaric Welding** = building a fully sealed pressurized submersible, draining the water inside, and cooking with normal gas burners — pressure is matched to outside.
> - **Cavity Welding** = digging a small pit, sealing it with plastic, draining the puddle, and cooking in the dry pit.

This analogy directly maps to **arc stability, weld quality and operational cost** trade-offs in ship repair.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in This Module

- **Density of seawater:** **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³**
- **Specific heat of water:** **c_p,w = 4186 J/(kg·K)**
- **Thermal conductivity of water:** **k_w = 0.60 W/(m·K)**
- **Hydrostatic pressure gradient:** **1 bar per 10 m depth** (seawater)
- **Hydrogen solubility in weld metal at 1 atm water contact:** ~**40 mL/100 g** (vs. ~**4 mL/100 g** in air — a 10× increase)
- **AWS D3.6M weld-class voltage ceiling:** Class A = **30 V**, Class B = **35 V**, Class C = **45 V**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Physics: The Four Process Families

### 2.1.1 Wet Welding — Process Logic

**Why it exists:** Cheapest, fastest, requires no habitat — diver just strikes an arc with a stick electrode under water.

**How it works (step-by-step logic):**
1. Diver positions waterproof electrode (organic coating decomposes to form a **gaseous envelope** around the arc).
2. Arc ignition occurs inside a steam + CO + H₂ bubble generated by arc energy vaporizing adjacent water.
3. Metal transfer occurs across this bubble.
4. On cooling, the bubble collapses; the weld solidifies surrounded by water → **very high cooling rate** (often >100 °C/s vs. ~10 °C/s in air).
5. Fast cooling traps hydrogen → **cold cracking susceptibility increases dramatically**.

**Key consequences for KTU exam:**
- Excessive **Hardness (HV)** in HAZ (>400 HV typical in C-Mn steels)
- Undercut, porosity, lack of fusion are common defects
- Maximum practical depth: ~**100 m** (above this, arc becomes unstable)
- Productivity: ~**100–150 mm/min** deposition

### 2.1.2 Dry Welding — Process Logic

**Why it exists:** Wet welds cannot meet fatigue-critical or high-strength-hull requirements (submarines, naval vessels, FPSOs).

**How it works:**
1. A small open-bottom habitat (~0.5 m × 0.5 m × 0.3 m) is clamped to the hull.
2. Water inside is displaced by **draining + gas purging** (usually air or argon).
3. Welder works in essentially atmospheric conditions inside the chamber.
4. Joint quality approaches surface-shop quality.
5. After welding, chamber is removed — leaves no permanent structure.

**Sub-categories:**
- **Local dry spot (LDS) welding** — small chamber, atmospheric pressure, shallow depths
- **Habitat hyperbaric welding** — large chamber, pressurized

### 2.1.3 Hyperbaric Welding — Process Logic

**Why it exists:** At greater depths (>30 m), atmospheric-pressure habitats require complex life-support. Pressurizing the habitat to ambient depth-pressure eliminates differential pressure, simplifying diver access.

**How it works:**
1. Habitat pressure **P = P_atm + ρ·g·h** is established (≈ 1 bar per 10 m depth).
2. Increased pressure raises arc voltage for the same arc length (because gas density increases).
3. Voltage rises roughly **+1 V per 100 m depth** per mm of arc gap.
4. Shielding gas composition changes (Helium-rich mixtures often replace Argon because He has higher ionization potential suited to high-pressure arcs).
5. Welds qualify to **AWS D3.6M Class A** — radiographically equivalent to surface welds.

### 2.1.4 Cavity Welding — Process Logic

**Why it exists:** Provides a **temporary dry zone** without building a full habitat — economical for one-off emergency patches.

**How it works:**
1. Damaged plate area is ground clean.
2. A frame (steel, wood, or plastic) is fixed around the area; epoxy resin or quick-set cement seals the perimeter.
3. Water inside the cavity is pumped out.
4. Standard surface-welding electrodes used inside the dry cavity.
5. After weld + NDT, cavity frame is broken; epoxy is chipped off.

**Limitations:** shallow depths (<10 m typical), single-side access, time-consuming setup.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU-board examinable**. Memorize units and constants.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Heat Input | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | V=Volts, I=Amps, S=mm/min |
| 2 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P_{hyd} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa | ρ_sw=1025 kg/m³, g=9.81, h=depth(m) |
| 3 | Depth-to-Pressure | $P \approx 1 + 0.1 \cdot h$ | bar | Approx. 1 bar/10 m seawater |
| 4 | Cooling Time t8/5 (Inagaki) | $t_{8/5} = \dfrac{k \cdot H^{n}}{(T_c - T_0)}$ | seconds | k=constant, H=heat input |
| 5 | Hydrogen-Induced Cracking Index (Pcm) | $P_{cm} = C + \dfrac{Mn+Cu+Cr}{20} + \dfrac{Si}{30} + \dfrac{Ni}{60} + \dfrac{Mo}{15} + \dfrac{V}{10} + 5B$ | wt% | Ito-Bessyo formula |
| 6 | Arc Voltage Correction (depth) | $V_{depth} = V_0 + \beta \cdot h$ | V | β ≈ 0.01 V/m for SMAW |
| 7 | Effective Heat Transfer (water vs. air) | $q_{water} \approx 25 \cdot q_{air}$ | — | Empirical multiplier for cooling |
| 8 | Cavity Volume (rectangular) | $V_c = L \cdot B \cdot H$ | m³ | L=length, B=breadth, H=height |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, **vertical bars in `|x|` form are avoided**; bracket notation `\vert x \vert` is intentionally replaced by descriptive text to keep the markdown table parser safe.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Wet welding** — emergency hull-plate patches on tankers, rig legs, sheet-pile walls; shipyard divers complete ~70% of casualty repairs this way.
- **Dry welding** — submarine pressure-hull repairs and offshore wind monopile strengthening.
- **Hyperbaric welding** — deepest-known welded joint on a fixed platform was at **~300 m** (Norwegian North Sea).
- **Cavity welding** — fast turnaround on tugs and inland-vessel casualties.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Heat Input Calculation for Wet SMAW

A shipyard diver is repairing a 12 mm thick AH36 hull plate using wet Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW). The welding parameters recorded are:
- Arc Voltage, V = **28 V**
- Welding Current, I = **180 A**
- Travel Speed, S = **150 mm/min**

**Step 1 — Recall the Heat Input formula.**

$$
H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
H = \dfrac{28 \times 180 \times 60}{150 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the numerator step-by-step.**

$$
28 \times 180 = 5040
$$

$$
5040 \times 60 = 302400
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the denominator step-by-step.**

$$
150 \times 1000 = 150000
$$

**Step 5 — Divide numerator by denominator.**

$$
H = \dfrac{302400}{150000} = 2.016
$$

**Step 6 — State result with units.**

$$
\boxed{H = 2.016 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A heat input of ~2 kJ/mm is **within the AWS D3.6M Class C limit** (≤ 2.5 kJ/mm for AH36 wet welding). Beyond this, HAZ hardness and cracking risk escalate sharply.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Hydrostatic Pressure at Operating Depth

A naval dry-dock hull repair is being done by **hyperbaric welding** at a depth of **h = 60 m** below the waterline. Determine the absolute habitat pressure required.

**Step 1 — Atmospheric pressure at surface.**

$$
P_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar} \approx 1 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 2 — Hydrostatic component (using depth-to-pressure rule: 1 bar per 10 m).**

$$
P_{hyd} = \dfrac{h}{10} = \dfrac{60}{10} = 6 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 3 — Absolute habitat pressure (gauge + atmospheric).**

$$
P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{hyd} = 1 + 6 = 7 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with full hydrostatic equation for rigor.**

$$
P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

$$
P_{abs} = 1.013 \times 10^{5} + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 60
$$

$$
P_{abs} = 1.013 \times 10^{5} + 603{,}315 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
P_{abs} = 704{,}315 \text{ Pa} = 7.04 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

$$
\boxed{P_{abs} \approx 7.04 \text{ bar}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** At 7 bar habitat pressure, the diver breathes a He-O₂ mix and the arc voltage rises by ~0.6 V vs. surface operation (using β ≈ 0.01 V/m). Process control becomes critical.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Cooling Time t8/5 (Inagaki) for Wet vs. Dry Weld

For the same AH36 plate, compute the **t8/5 cooling time** under wet and dry conditions. Use the simplified form:

$$
t_{8/5} = \dfrac{k \cdot H}{(T_c - T_0)}
$$

with k_wet = 0.30 s·mm/kJ, k_dry = 0.45 s·mm/kJ (for 12 mm plate), T_c = 800 °C, T_0 = 25 °C, H = 2.016 kJ/mm.

**Wet condition:**

$$
t_{8/5,wet} = \dfrac{0.30 \times 2.016}{(800 - 25)}
$$

$$
t_{8/5,wet} = \dfrac{0.6048}{775} = 7.80 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \cdot \text{mm}/\text{kJ} \cdot \text{kJ}/\text{mm} \cdot \text{K}/\text{K}
$$

> Note: in this simplified form, the units cancel to give a relative index. KTU board accepts numeric value:

$$
\boxed{t_{8/5,wet} \approx 0.78 \text{ (normalized index)}}
$$

**Dry condition:**

$$
t_{8/5,dry} = \dfrac{0.45 \times 2.016}{(800 - 25)} = \dfrac{0.9072}{775}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{8/5,dry} \approx 1.17 \text{ (normalized index)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**

$$
\dfrac{t_{8/5,dry}}{t_{8/5,wet}} = \dfrac{1.17}{0.78} \approx 1.50
$$

> The dry weld cools **50% slower** than the wet weld — which is precisely why dry/hyperbaric welds exhibit lower HAZ hardness and lower hydrogen-cracking susceptibility.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU Board-Ready)

| Parameter | Wet Welding | Dry Welding | Hyperbaric Welding | Cavity Welding |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Pressure | 1 bar + depth | ~1 bar (atmospheric) | 1 bar → 35+ bar | ~1 bar |
| AWS D3.6M Class | C | B | A | A (if executed well) |
| Typical Depth | 0–100 m | 0–30 m | 30–300 m | 0–10 m |
| Productivity | High | Medium | Low | Very Low |
| Weld Quality | Low–Medium | High | Highest | Medium |
| Hydrogen Risk | Very High | Moderate | Low–Moderate | Moderate |
| Equipment Cost | Low | Medium | Very High | Low |
| Diver Skill | High | High | Very High | Medium |
| Cooling Rate (qualitative) | ~25× air | ~3–5× air | ~3–5× air | ~2× air |

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Process Selection Logic in Ship Repair

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Damage Below Waterline] --> B{Is the vessel in dry dock?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Conventional Surface Welding]
    B -- No --> D{Can a habitat be mobilised?}
    D -- Yes --> E{Is depth greater than 30 m?}
    D -- No --> F[Wet Welding Selected]
    E -- No --> G[Dry Welding Selected]
    E -- Yes --> H[Hyperbaric Welding Selected]
    I{Emergency patch only?} -- Yes --> J[Cavity Welding Selected]
    I -- No --> F
    A --> I
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Functional Architecture — Wet vs. Dry vs. Hyperbaric vs. Cavity

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Wet[Wet Welding Module]
        W1[Waterproof Electrode] --> W2[Arc inside Steam Bubble]
        W2 --> W3[High Cooling Rate]
        W3 --> W4[Risk of H2 Cracking]
    end

    subgraph Dry[Dry Welding Module]
        D1[Small Open Habitat] --> D2[Air or Argon Purge]
        D2 --> D3[Atmospheric Arc]
        D3 --> D4[Shop-Like Quality]
    end

    subgraph Hyper[Hyperbaric Welding Module]
        H1[Pressurised Habitat] --> H2[Helium Rich Shield Gas]
        H2 --> H3[High Pressure Arc]
        H3 --> H4[AWS Class A Quality]
    end

    subgraph Cav[Cavity Welding Module]
        C1[Epoxy Sealed Frame] --> C2[Water Pumped Out]
        C2 --> C3[Standard Electrode]
        C3 --> C4[Temporary Patch]
    end

    Wet --> Outcome[Compare on Quality Cost Speed Depth]
    Dry --> Outcome
    Hyper --> Outcome
    Cav --> Outcome
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Hyperbaric Welding Execution Chain

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant E as Engineer
    participant H as Hyperbaric Habitat
    participant D as Diver Welder
    participant Q as QC Inspector

    E->>H: Position and clamp habitat
    E->>H: Pressurise to depth-equivalent
    H-->>D: Confirm breathable atmosphere
    D->>H: Strike arc using He-Ar mix
    H-->>Q: Weld completed, request NDT
    Q->>H: Visual and MPI/RT inspection
    Q-->>E: Sign-off AWS D3.6M Class A
    E->>H: Depressurise and recover habitat
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define wet welding. State two advantages and two limitations of wet welding in ship repair.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Wet welding is an arc welding process performed with the electrode, arc and weld pool directly exposed to the surrounding water, using specially coated waterproof electrodes and without any physical barrier excluding the water.

**Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. Quick mobilisation — no habitat construction needed, ideal for emergency casualty response.
2. Low equipment and operational cost compared to dry/hyperbaric systems.
3. Diver can reach inaccessible locations easily.

**Limitations (1 mark — any two):**
1. Severe hydrogen-induced cracking due to rapid quenching by water (~25× faster cooling).
2. Poor weld quality with high HAZ hardness; only AWS D3.6M Class C attainable.
3. Limited to depths <100 m because arc becomes unstable at higher pressure.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**
*Distinguish between dry welding and hyperbaric welding. Why is helium often preferred over argon in deep hyperbaric welding?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Distinction (2 marks):**

| Feature | Dry Welding | Hyperbaric Welding |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| Pressure | Approximately atmospheric | Equal to ambient hydrostatic pressure |
| Depth | <30 m typically | 30–300 m |
| Quality | AWS Class B | AWS Class A |
| Shield Gas | Argon-based | Helium-rich mixtures |
| Cost | Medium | Very high |

**Helium preference (1 mark):** At high ambient pressure, the higher ionization potential and lower density of helium compared to argon produces a more stable, hotter arc with better penetration and reduced spatter, enabling consistent weld quality at depth.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **wet Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** process used in ship hull repair. Discuss the role of waterproof electrode coatings and the formation of the gaseous envelope around the arc.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wet SMAW repair is being carried out on a 14 mm AH36 hull plate at 25 m depth with the following parameters: V = 30 V, I = 200 A, travel speed = 120 mm/min. Calculate the (i) heat input and (ii) absolute hydrostatic pressure at the repair location. Comment on whether the parameters comply with AWS D3.6M Class C limits.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Process Explanation [7 marks]:**

1. **Definition and Setup [2 marks]:** Wet SMAW uses a stick electrode with a special cellulosic + rutile + mineral coating that is waterproof (insulated with a plastic or varnish layer). The diver manually strikes an arc on the hull plate under water without any chamber.

2. **Arc Envelope Formation [3 marks]:** When the arc strikes, the intense heat (~6000 °C) vaporises the surrounding water and decomposes the electrode coating, producing a bubble consisting of **H₂, CO, CO₂ and H₂O vapour**. This bubble, called the **gaseous envelope**, momentarily displaces water from the arc zone, allowing stable metal transfer across the arc.

3. **Weld Metal Solidification [2 marks]:** As the arc moves forward, the bubble collapses and the weld pool is quenched by the surrounding seawater. Cooling rates are extremely high, leading to **martensitic microstructures** in the HAZ, increased hardness and high susceptibility to **hydrogen-induced cracking**.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**(i) Heat Input [4 marks]:**

$$
H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{30 \times 200 \times 60}{120 \times 1000}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{360000}{120000} = 3.0 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

**(ii) Hydrostatic Pressure [2 marks]:**

$$
P_{hyd} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 25 = 251{,}381 \text{ Pa} \approx 2.51 \text{ bar}
$$

$$
P_{abs} = 1 + 2.51 = 3.51 \text{ bar}
$$

**Compliance Comment [1 mark]:** Heat input of **3.0 kJ/mm exceeds** the AWS D3.6M Class C limit (~2.5 kJ/mm for AH36). The welder should **reduce current or increase travel speed** to bring H below 2.5 kJ/mm, or switch to **dry welding** if quality-critical.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **dry welding, hyperbaric welding and cavity welding** with respect to (i) operating principle, (ii) typical depth range, (iii) AWS D3.6M weld class achievable and (iv) relative cost.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a schematic flowchart, describe the step-by-step procedure for **carrying out hyperbaric welding inside a pressurised habitat** at 75 m depth on a submarine pressure hull. Include safety, shielding gas and NDT considerations.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparative Table [7 marks — 1.75 each row]:**

| Parameter | Dry Welding | Hyperbaric Welding | Cavity Welding |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|------------------|
| (i) Operating Principle | Local open-bottom habitat displaces water; near-atmospheric welding inside | Fully sealed habitat pressurised to ambient hydrostatic pressure; He-O₂ atmosphere for divers | Epoxy/framed cavity pumped dry; temporary patch welding |
| (ii) Typical Depth | 0–30 m | 30–300 m | 0–10 m |
| (iii) AWS D3.6M Class | Class B | Class A (highest) | Class A (if executed well) |
| (iv) Relative Cost | Medium | Very High (₹ 5–10 Lakh/day on offshore) | Low |

**(b) Hyperbaric Welding Procedure [7 marks]:**

1. **Pre-Weld Prep [1.5 marks]:** Clean hull surface to SA 2.5; fit-up with pre-qualified WPS; place habitat and bolt/magnet-seal to hull; pressurise habitat to **P_abs = 1 + 7.5 = 8.5 bar** (75 m depth).
2. **Atmosphere Control [1.5 marks]:** Purge with **He-O₂ mix (typically 80/20)** for diver breathing; establish He-Ar shielding gas through GTAW nozzle.
3. **Welding [1.5 marks]:** Strike arc with **elevated open-circuit voltage** (~10 V above surface value); maintain interpass temperature ≤ 200 °C; track voltage/ amperage on digital recorder.
4. **NDT [1.5 marks]:** Perform **100% Visual + MPI/RT** of completed welds; qualifying criteria per **AWS D3.6M Class A**; sign-off by certified QC engineer.
5. **Depressurisation & Habitat Removal [1 mark]:** Slow controlled depressurisation (≤ 0.3 bar/min to prevent diver decompression sickness); remove habitat; final hull survey.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "dry welding" with "hyperbaric welding"** — they are NOT the same. Dry welding = local habitat at ~1 bar; Hyperbaric = habitat pressurised to depth pressure. (Lose 2 marks if confused.)
> 2. **Wrong unit of heat input** — write **kJ/mm**, not J/mm or kJ/m. Examiner deducts 0.5 mark for unit error.
> 3. **Skipping depth-to-pressure conversion factor** — use **1 bar per 10 m of seawater**, not 1 atm/10 m. Many students forget that seawater is denser than fresh water.
> 4. **Omitting AWS D3.6M class** — every underwater welding answer must cite the applicable weld class (A/B/C).
> 5. **Mixing up cavity welding with dry welding** — cavity welding uses epoxy-cement frame + water pumping; dry welding uses a metal habitat. Examiner explicitly tests this in Part B.
> 6. **No schematic in Part B** — KTU board deducts **2 marks** if no sketch/flowchart is provided for an explanatory question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Wet, Dry, Hyperbaric & Cavity Welding**

- ☐ Wet welding = water in contact with arc; cheapest; AWS Class C; max depth ~100 m.
- ☐ Dry welding = open habitat at ~1 bar; AWS Class B; max depth ~30 m.
- ☐ Hyperbaric welding = pressurised habitat = depth pressure; AWS Class A; depth 30–300 m.
- ☐ Cavity welding = epoxy-framed temporary dry zone; emergency patches; <10 m.
- ☐ Heat input formula: **H = (V × I × 60) / (S × 1000)** in **kJ/mm**.
- ☐ Hydrostatic rule: **1 bar per 10 m** of seawater; total = **1 + 0.1 h** bar.
- ☐ Seawater density **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³**; g = 9.81 m/s².
- ☐ Hydrogen solubility in weld metal is **~10× higher** underwater than in air — primary cause of cracking.
- ☐ Wet cooling rate is **~25× air** cooling rate → martensite → HAZ hardness >400 HV.
- ☐ Helium preferred over Argon in deep hyperbaric arc because of higher ionisation potential.
- ☐ **AWS D3.6M** is the governing standard; cite Class A/B/C in every answer.
- ☐ Always include a **schematic / flowchart** in Part B answers (KTU marking scheme rewards 2 marks).
- ☐ Cavity welding ≠ dry welding — distinguish clearly to avoid valuation deduction.
- ☐ Cooling time **t8/5** increases by ~50% when moving from wet to dry under identical heat input.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding Arrangements and Special Precautions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Welding Arrangements and Special Precautions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Welding Arrangements — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding Arrangements** in shipbuilding refer to the systematic planning of **welding positions, joint geometry, accessibility, sequence of weld deposition, fixture/clamping methods, and shop/yard layout** that enable high-integrity welds to be made safely and efficiently on large, fabricated steel structures under varying environmental and positional constraints.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, welding arrangements encompass three primary layers:

1. **Geometrical Arrangement** — Choice of welding position (1G, 2G, 3G, 4G, 1F–4F), joint type (butt, fillet, T, lap), edge preparation (V, U, J, X, K-groove), and groove angle.
2. **Operational Arrangement** — Weld sequence (backstep, block, cascade), pre-set (distortion counter-measure), clamping/strong-back fixtures, and accessibility openings.
3. **Environmental Arrangement** — Shelter/tent arrangement, preheating setup, interpass temperature control, and wind/water shielding for out-of-position and offshore welding.

### 1.2 Special Precautions — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Precautions** are the additional safety, metallurgical, and procedural safeguards imposed beyond standard welding practice, necessitated by the unique demands of shipbuilding: thick-plate welding (up to **100 mm**), high-strength steels (AH36, DH36, EH36), low-temperature service, hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC), lamellar tearing susceptibility, and hazardous onboard environments (confined spaces, elevated platforms, marine atmosphere).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

Think of welding arrangements like **conducting an orchestra**:
- The **musicians (welders)** cannot all play at once — they must be **sequenced (weld sequence)**.
- The **concert hall layout (shipyard block layout)** determines where each section plays (welding position).
- The **conductor's baton (WPS — Welding Procedure Specification)** tells each musician exactly what to do, in what key, and at what tempo.
- **Special precautions** are like the fire-extinguisher, ear-protection, and air-quality control — not part of the music itself, but without them the performance is unsafe or impossible.

Just as a poorly seated orchestra produces muddy sound, **poor welding arrangement produces residual stress, distortion, lamellar tearing, and ultimately — structural failure at sea**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key metrics governing welding arrangements in shipbuilding (per **AWS D1.1, IACS S21, ISO 3834, and IACS UR Z17/Z23**):
> - **Heat Input (E)** = **≤ 2.0 kJ/mm** for AH/DH/EH grade steels (typical upper limit)
> - **Interpass Temperature** = **≤ 200 °C** (commonly 150–180 °C for high-strength ship steels)
> - **Preheating Temperature** = **75–150 °C** depending on CEV (Carbon Equivalent Value)
> - **Hydrogen Control** = **≤ 5 ml/100 g** deposited metal (H4 / H5 grade consumables per AWS A5.1)
> - **Weld Position Efficiency Factor** = 0.6 (overhead) to 1.0 (flat/1G)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Welding Positions (ISO 6947 / ASME IX)

Shipbuilding uses five fundamental welding positions; each has a different gravity effect on the molten weld pool:

| Position | Code | Gravity Effect | Typical Shipbuilding Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flat | **1G / 1F** | Pool stays in place — easiest | Panel welding in workshop, sub-assemblies |
| Horizontal | **2G / 2F** | Pool tends to sag toward lower plate | Side-shell seams at quay, bulkhead landings |
| Vertical (upward) | **3G / 3F-up** | Pool held by surface tension + travel angle | Web-to-flange joints, sheer strakes |
| Vertical (downward) | **3G-down** | Gravity assists — fast but lower penetration | Temporary welds, thin-plate overhead-like |
| Overhead | **4G / 4F** | Pool falls — hardest, high spatter | Deck-to-side-shell, overhead in double-bottom |

> [!NOTE]
> In ship erection, **70–80%** of production welds are made in the **flat (1G/1F) and horizontal (2G/2F)** positions because panels are sub-assembled **upside-down (flip-up method)** before being erected into the block.

### 2.2 Weld Sequencing Strategies

The four classical sequences used to control **distortion and residual stress** in ship panel fabrication:

1. **Backstep Sequence** — Each successive bead is deposited *opposite* to the previous one, returning toward the starting end. *Effect*: Compresses previously deposited beads → reduces angular distortion.
2. **Block Sequence** — The joint is divided into **segments**; segments are welded in alternating fashion (1→3→5 then 2→4→6). *Effect*: Balances shrinkage, minimizes longitudinal bow.
3. **Cascade (Skip) Sequence** — Welds skip beads (every 2nd or 3rd bead). *Effect*: Allows heat to dissipate between passes → minimizes HAZ softening.
4. **Symmetric (Balanced) Sequence** — Welds deposited simultaneously on both sides of the neutral axis (used in **double-sided butt welds** in hull plating). *Effect*: Near-zero net distortion.

### 2.3 Distortion Control — Pre-Set and Strong-Backs

Two shipyard-standard mechanical arrangements:

- **Pre-set (Presetting)**: Plates are deliberately **bent inward** by an angle $\alpha$ *before* welding so that after welding-induced angular distortion $\beta$, the assembly becomes straight.
- **Strong-backs / Back-up Bars**: Temporary steel bars tack-welded across the joint to **increase section stiffness** and resist longitudinal bending during welding.

### 2.4 Special Precautions — Categorical Framework

| Category | Specific Precaution | Trigger / Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| **Metallurgical** | Preheating | CEV > 0.40 % or combined thickness > 50 mm |
| **Metallurgical** | Post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) | Thick hatch corners, engine-seat welding |
| **Hydrogen Control** | Use of low-hydrogen electrodes (basic / cellulosic with baking) | Tensile > 490 MPa steel or service temp < 0 °C |
| **Distortion** | Pre-set + symmetric sequence | Plate ≤ 12 mm and length > 2 m |
| **Lamellar Tearing** | Balanced weld leg, buttering, low-strength fillet first | T-joints with through-thickness strains |
| **Environmental** | Wind-shelter tent (wind speed > **2 m/s** for FCAW/GTAW) | Outdoor or quayside erection welds |
| **Environmental** | Drying oven for electrodes (350 °C / 1 hr minimum) | All low-hydrogen consumables |
