| **Safety** | Gas monitoring, ventilation, fire-watch | Confined space (double-bottom, ballast tanks) |
| **Safety** | Earthing/grounding cable quality check | All arc welding operations |
| **Quality** | NDT (UT/RT/MPT) staging plan | Per IACS S21 / classification society rules |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU University Exam**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Input | $E = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot S}$ | **kJ/mm** | V = arc voltage (V), I = current (A), S = travel speed (mm/min) |
| 2 | Carbon Equivalent (IIW) | $CEV = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | **%** | If CEV > 0.45 → mandatory preheat |
| 3 | Angular Distortion (single-V butt) | $\beta \approx \dfrac{0.6 \cdot \theta \cdot Q}{I \cdot t}$ | **radians** | $\theta$ = groove angle, $Q$ = weld heat, $I$ = second moment, $t$ = thickness |
| 4 | Required Preheat (AWS D1.1) | $T_{pre} = 75\,^{\circ}\!C$ minimum, scaled with combined thickness | **°C** | Refer to AWS Table 3.3 |
| 5 | Pre-set Angle | $\alpha_{preset} = \beta_{weld} + \delta_{tolerance}$ | **degrees** | Empirical — typically 2°–5° for thin plate |
| 6 | Transverse Shrinkage (butt joint) | $\Delta L \approx \dfrac{0.2 \cdot A_w}{t}$ | **mm** | $A_w$ = weld cross-section area |
| 7 | HAZ Hardness Limit | HV ≤ **350** for AH/DH, ≤ **380** for EH | **HV10** | Per IACS UR W11 |

> **Note on units:** All temperatures in **°C**, all linear in **mm**, energy in **kJ**. Always quote CEV to **two decimals**.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

This framework governs the integrity of every ship — from a 300 DWT coastal vessel to a 400,000 DWT VLCC. Classification societies (DNV, LR, ABS, BV, IRS) **reject welds** that violate these arrangements, and catastrophic failures (e.g., **MV Prestige 2002**, **MOL Comfort 2013**) have been traced to inadequate weld sequencing and missing preheat control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input Equation

**Starting Principle:** Heat input per unit length is energy delivered by the arc divided by the travel distance.

$$
P_{arc} = V \cdot I \quad \text{(arc power in Watts)}
$$

Energy delivered in time $t$ minutes:

$$
E_{total} = V \cdot I \cdot t \quad \text{(Joules)}
$$

Travel distance in that time:

$$
L = S \cdot t \quad \text{(mm, where } S = \text{travel speed in mm/min)}
$$

Therefore heat input per unit length:

$$
E = \dfrac{E_{total}}{L} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{S \cdot t}
$$

Removing the factor and converting to kJ:

$$
E = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S} \cdot \dfrac{60}{1000} = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot S}
$$

> **Final form:** $\boxed{E = \dfrac{60\,V\,I}{1000\,S}}$ with units **kJ/mm** when $V$ in volts, $I$ in amps, $S$ in mm/min.

**Worked Example (KTU style):**

Given: $V = 24$ V, $I = 250$ A, $S = 300$ mm/min. Find heat input and check against the 2.0 kJ/mm limit for AH36 plate.

$$
E = \dfrac{60 \times 24 \times 250}{1000 \times 300}
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{360\,000}{300\,000}
$$

$$
E = 1.20 \;\text{kJ/mm}
$$

> **Result: 1.20 kJ/mm** — comfortably below the **2.0 kJ/mm** upper limit for AH36. ✔ WPS is acceptable.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV, IIW Formula)

The IIW formula quantifies the **combined hardening effect** of alloying elements:

$$
CEV = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}
$$

**Derivation logic:** Empirical weighting — Carbon is dominant (multiplier = 1); Mn has ~1/6 the effect of C; Cr, Mo, V share ~1/5; Ni and Cu share ~1/15. These weights come from regression studies of weld HAZ hardenability.

**Worked Example:**

Plate chemistry: C = 0.16 %, Mn = 1.45 %, Cr = 0.20 %, Mo = 0.05 %, V = 0.01 %, Ni = 0.15 %, Cu = 0.10 %.

Step 1 — Carbon contribution:

$$
C = 0.16
$$

Step 2 — Manganese contribution:

$$
\dfrac{Mn}{6} = \dfrac{1.45}{6} = 0.2417
$$

Step 3 — Chromium + Molybdenum + Vanadium:

$$
\dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} = \dfrac{0.20 + 0.05 + 0.01}{5} = \dfrac{0.26}{5} = 0.0520
$$

Step 4 — Nickel + Copper:

$$
\dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15} = \dfrac{0.15 + 0.10}{15} = \dfrac{0.25}{15} = 0.0167
$$

Step 5 — Sum:

$$
CEV = 0.16 + 0.2417 + 0.0520 + 0.0167 = 0.4704
$$

Rounded: $CEV \approx 0.47$

> **Result: CEV = 0.47 %** → Exceeds 0.45 threshold → **Mandatory preheating at 100–150 °C** is required before welding.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Apply Which Arrangement

| Scenario | Welding Arrangement Recommended | Special Precautions |
|---|---|---|
| **Thin plate (< 8 mm) fillet on flat panel** | Flat (1F) position, intermittent welds, backstep sequence | Tack welding first; no preheat |
| **AH36 side-shell seam, 16 mm** | Horizontal (2G) with strong-back, block sequence | Preheat 75 °C; basic electrode; interpass ≤ 200 °C |
| **EH36 hatch corner, 25 mm** | All-position (1G/2G/3G/4G), symmetric double-sided V-prep | Preheat 125 °C; H4 consumables; PWHT if required |
| **Double-bottom tank internals** | All-position overhead (4G), cascade sequence | Confined-space permit; gas test; ventilation blower; lighting ≥ 200 lux |
| **Quayside erection in monsoon** | Wind-shelter tent, FCAW with CV ≤ 2.0 kJ/mm | Tent; wind screens; drying oven at site |
| **LNG carrier -163 °C service** | All-position, narrow-gap TIG with filler | 9 % Ni consumables; 100 % RT; cryogenic impact test |

---

### 3.4 Distortion Pre-Set Angle — Worked Example

A 10 mm thick × 2 m long plate is to be butt-welded with a single-V 60° groove. The angular distortion from prior trial is $\beta = 3°$. Determine the required pre-set angle if a tolerance of $\delta = 0.5°$ is allowed.

$$
\alpha_{preset} = \beta + \delta = 3° + 0.5° = 3.5°
$$

> **Result:** Pre-bend the plate to **3.5°** in the *opposite* direction of expected distortion before tacking.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Welding Sequence Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Panel Received at Welding Bay] --> B{Inspect Plate Edge Prep}
    B -->|Defective| C[Re-prep by Flame or Plasma]
    B -->|Acceptable| D[Apply Pre-set Bending if Thin Plate]
    D --> E[Position Plate on Welding Jig with Strong-back]
    E --> F{Preheat Required?}
    F -->|CEV > 0.45 or t > 50 mm| G[Preheat to 100 to 150 deg C]
    F -->|No| H[Proceed to Root Pass]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Root Pass Bead 1G Position]
    I --> J[Backgouge and Grind from Reverse]
    J --> K[Fill Passes using Block Sequence]
    K --> L{Interpass > 200 deg C?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Stop and Cool to 150 deg C]
    L -->|No| N[Continue to Cap Pass]
    M --> K
    N --> O[Cap Pass in 1G]
    O --> P[Remove Strong-back]
    P --> Q[Visual and NDT Inspection]
    Q --> R[Accept and Release to Next Station]
```

---

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Selection of Special Precaution Set

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Identify Plate Grade and Thickness] --> T{Calculate CEV}
    T --> U{CEV less than 0.40}
    T --> V{CEV 0.40 to 0.45}
    T --> W{CEV greater than 0.45}
    U --> X[Standard Practice: No Preheat]
    V --> Y[Light Preheat: 75 deg C]
    W --> Z[Heavy Preheat: 100 to 150 deg C plus Low H Electrode]
    X --> AA[Final Arrangement Selection]
    Y --> AA
    Z --> AA
    AA --> AB{Environment Confined?}
    AB -->|Yes| AC[Add Ventilation, Gas Test, Fire Watch]
    AB -->|No| AD[Add Wind Shield if Outdoors]
    AC --> AE[Approved WPS]
    AD --> AE
```

---

### 4.3 Welder Position vs Pool Behavior (Conceptual Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Flat 1G: Pool Sits, Easy Control]
    P2[Horizontal 2G: Pool Sags Toward Lower Plate]
    P3[Vertical Up 3G: Pool Held by Surface Tension]
    P4[Vertical Down 3Gdown: Gravity Pulls Pool Down]
    P5[Overhead 4G: Pool Held by Surface Tension Only]
    P1 --> R1[Highest Productivity]
    P2 --> R2[Moderate]
    P3 --> R3[Slow but High Quality]
    P4 --> R4[Fastest, Lower Penetration]
    P5 --> R5[Slowest, Most Skill Required]
```

---

### 4.4 Weld Symbol Reference (Shipyard Standard)

| Symbol | Meaning | Joint Type |
|---|---|---|
| ⌃ (fillet triangle) | Fillet weld on the arrow side | T-joint web-to-flange |
| \| (square butt) | Square butt weld | Light bulkhead panel |
| V (V at 60°) | Single-V butt | Most hull plate seams |
| ⌶ (double-V) | Double-V or X butt | Thick plate > 25 mm |
| ◁ (single-bevel) | Single-bevel butt | One-sided access |
| ⌵ (J-groove) | J-groove butt | Thick plate, narrow root |

> **Reading convention:** Arrow → points to joint line. Reference line → horizontal. Tail → contains WPS info (e.g., "FCAW, 1.2 mm, E71T-1, 1G").

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define *welding arrangement* in the context of shipbuilding. List any four elements it covers.

**Model Answer:**
> Welding arrangement in shipbuilding is the systematic planning of all geometric, operational, and environmental conditions under which welds are deposited on ship structures. It ensures weld quality, controls distortion, and provides safe access for welders.
>
> Four elements:
> 1. Welding position (1G, 2G, 3G, 4G, 1F–4F)
> 2. Joint type and edge preparation (V, X, U, J, fillet, butt)
> 3. Weld deposition sequence (backstep, block, cascade, symmetric)
> 4. Clamping/strong-back and accessibility (fixtures, tents, scaffolding)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State **any three special precautions** required while welding high-strength shipbuilding steel of grade EH36.

**Model Answer:**
> 1. **Preheating** to 125–150 °C since EH36 has CEV typically > 0.45 and demands controlled HAZ hardness ≤ 380 HV.
> 2. **Use of low-hydrogen (basic-coated) consumables**, baked at 350 °C for 1 hour, to prevent hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC).
> 3. **Heat input control** within 1.0–2.0 kJ/mm and **interpass temperature ≤ 200 °C** to avoid HAZ softening and coarse-grain embrittlement.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]** *(a)* Explain in detail the **four standard weld-deposition sequences** used in shipbuilding with neat sketches and their effect on distortion. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A 12 mm thick AH36 plate is to be welded in the flat position using SMAW. Welding parameters are: $V = 25$ V, $I = 220$ A, $S = 280$ mm/min. Plate chemistry: C = 0.15 %, Mn = 1.40 %, Cr = 0.10 %, Mo = 0.04 %, V = 0.01 %, Ni = 0.10 %, Cu = 0.05 %. Calculate: (i) heat input, and (ii) CEV. Comment on whether preheating is required. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Weld Sequences (7 marks):**

1. **Backstep Sequence** — Each bead is laid opposite to the previous one. *Use:* Reduces angular distortion on long butt joints. *Sketch:* Beads numbered 1 → 3 → 5 backward, 2 → 4 → 6 forward.
2. **Block Sequence** — Joint divided into blocks; alternate blocks are welded first. *Use:* Balances longitudinal shrinkage on long seams (>2 m). *Sketch:* Block A, B, C, D in checkerboard.
3. **Cascade / Skip Sequence** — Beads skipped by 2 or 3 positions, allowing cooling between passes. *Use:* Prevents HAZ softening in multi-pass thick plate. *Sketch:* 1, 4, 7, then 2, 5, 8, etc.
4. **Symmetric Sequence** — Both sides of the neutral axis welded simultaneously (or alternately in matched passes). *Use:* Used in double-sided butt welds; results in near-zero bow. *Sketch:* Mirror-image passes on top and bottom.

**(b) Calculations (7 marks):**

*(i) Heat input:*

$$
E = \dfrac{60 \times 25 \times 220}{1000 \times 280}
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{330\,000}{280\,000}
$$

$$
E = 1.18 \;\text{kJ/mm}
$$

*(ii) CEV:*

$$
CEV = 0.15 + \dfrac{1.40}{6} + \dfrac{0.10 + 0.04 + 0.01}{5} + \dfrac{0.10 + 0.05}{15}
$$

$$
CEV = 0.15 + 0.2333 + 0.0300 + 0.0100
$$

$$
CEV = 0.4233 \approx 0.42
$$

*(iii) Comment:* Heat input **1.18 kJ/mm** is within the **2.0 kJ/mm** limit for AH36 ✔. CEV = **0.42** is just below the 0.45 threshold; **light preheat of 75 °C is recommended** (good practice) but **not mandatory** per AWS D1.1.

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]** *(a)* Discuss the **special precautions** required for welding in (i) confined spaces (double-bottom tanks) and (ii) outdoor quayside erection during monsoon. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Define **lamellar tearing**. Explain the **welding arrangements and precautions** to avoid it in T-joints of ship hull. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Confined Space Welding (3.5 marks):**
- Gas test (oxygen 19.5–23.5 %, LEL < 1 %) before entry
- Forced ventilation, blower running continuously
- Low-voltage lighting ≥ 200 lux, flame-proof fittings
- Standby man outside with rescue plan
- Fire extinguisher and fire-watch
- Welding leads routed safely; earthing verified
- Permit-to-work system; LEL meter worn by welder

**Outdoor Monsoon Welding (3.5 marks):**
- Wind-shelter tent enclosing the welding station
- Wind-speed limit: stop GTAW/FCAW if wind > **2 m/s**
- Electrode drying oven at site (350 °C), consumption within 4 hrs of removal
- Preheating torch sheltered to maintain target temperature
- Anti-slip platforms; rainwater drainage
- Cover consumables; use desiccant in electrode cans
- Lightening arrest / earthing of equipment

**(b) Lamellar Tearing Definition (2 marks):**
> Lamellar tearing is a **sub-surface cracking in the through-thickness direction** of rolled plate, occurring beneath a T-joint fillet weld due to **strains imposed by weld shrinkage acting on non-metallic inclusions** (MnS, silicates) aligned along the rolling plane.

**Arrangements and Precautions (5 marks):**
- **Use Z-grade plate** (through-thickness ductility guaranteed, e.g., EN 10164 Z35)
- **Butter the T-joint first** with a low-strength ductile weld (often E6013) before the main strong weld — gives a compliant layer
- **Balanced fillet welds** (both legs equal and not oversized)
- **Double-sided welds** rather than single-sided heavy fillets
- **Smaller weld leg size** with multiple passes (multi-pass is less stressful than single heavy pass)
- **Avoid cruciform joints** with all four welds landing at one point
- **Pre-heat** to reduce shrinkage strain rates

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning**
> - Students **commonly lose marks** by *listing precautions without justifying the trigger condition* — always state the threshold (CEV, thickness, wind speed) before listing the precaution.
> - Do **NOT** confuse welding **position** (1G, 2G...) with welding **sequence** (backstep, block...). Examiners test this distinction.
> - Heat input **must include the 60/1000 conversion factor** with units **kJ/mm**. Marks deducted if left as V·I/S (J/mm).
> - CEV formula **must be IIW version** unless specified otherwise. Do not mix with Pcm (the Japanese formula).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Welding Arrangements & Special Precautions**

- ✔ Welding **positions** in shipbuilding: 1G, 2G, 3G, 4G, 1F–4F (flat is most efficient; overhead is hardest).
- ✔ Most shipyard production welds are in **1G/1F** because of **flip-up sub-assembly** practice.
- ✔ **Weld sequences**: backstep, block, cascade (skip), symmetric — chosen to control **distortion** and **residual stress**.
- ✔ **Pre-set angle** = expected distortion + tolerance; used on thin plates (< 12 mm).
- ✔ **Strong-backs** increase joint stiffness during welding.
- ✔ **Heat input formula**: $E = \dfrac{60\,V\,I}{1000\,S}$ in **kJ/mm**; typical limit **≤ 2.0 kJ/mm** for high-strength ship steel.
- ✔ **CEV (IIW)**: $C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$; if **CEV > 0.45**, preheating is **mandatory**.
- ✔ **Preheat range**: 75–150 °C depending on CEV and combined thickness (per AWS D1.1 Table 3.3 / IACS UR W11).
- ✔ **Interpass temperature**: typically ≤ **200 °C** for AH/DH/EH steels.
- ✔ **Hydrogen control**: low-H electrodes (basic, H4/H5 grade) baked at **350 °C**; **HV ≤ 350** (AH/DH), **≤ 380** (EH).
- ✔ **Lamellar tearing**: prevent by Z-grade plate, buttering, balanced welds, avoid cruciform joints.
- ✔ **Confined space**: gas test, ventilation, fire-watch, permit-to-work.
- ✔ **Outdoor / monsoon**: wind shelter tent, electrode drying oven, wind speed limit **2 m/s** for FCAW/GTAW.
- ✔ Classification society rules: **IACS S21** (welding), **UR Z17** (material), **UR W11** (rolled steel), **AWS D1.1**, **ISO 3834**.
- ✔ Joint preparations: V, X (double-V), U, J, bevel, square-butt, fillet — chosen by thickness, accessibility, and distortion budget.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Robotic Welding in Ship Production; Types of Welding Robots and Their Control System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application of Robotic Welding in Ship Production; Types of Welding Robots and Their Control System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robotic Welding** in ship production is defined as the automated, programmable application of arc welding processes (predominantly Gas Metal Arc Welding – GMAW, Flux-Cored Arc Welding – FCAW, and Submerged Arc Welding – SAW) using multi-axis industrial manipulators equipped with sensors and computer-based control systems. According to the **International Federation of Robotics (IFR)** and **ISO 8373:2021**, a welding robot is an "automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multi-purpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST741, Module 3):**
> Robotic welding is a **core shipbuilding automation technology** that addresses the inherent challenges of ship fabrication: **large component dimensions, heavy plate thicknesses (up to 100 mm for hull plates), high weld lengths, and the need for consistent quality in harsh marine environments.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine a human welder holding a heavy welding torch, trying to draw a perfectly straight 10-meter line while standing on a slightly tilting floating platform in a noisy, hot shipyard. Even the most skilled welder gets tired, makes small deviations, and works slower every hour. Now imagine a **mechanical arm that never gets tired, never trembles, can "remember" the exact torch path it was taught, and can repeat that path 5,000 times with the same precision** — that is a welding robot.

Think of it like this:
- **The Teach Pendant** = the "teacher's chalk" — you guide the robot arm by hand once along the joint, and it memorizes the path.
- **The Control Cabinet** = the "brain" — it stores the program and sends electrical pulses to motors that move the joints.
- **The Welding Torch** = the "pen" — it draws the weld bead along the seam.
- **The Positioner** = the "rotating turntable" — it moves the workpiece so the joint is always in the most comfortable orientation (down-hand or horizontal).

## 1.3 Why Robotic Welding is Indispensable in Ship Production

| Shipyard Challenge | How Robotic Welding Solves It |
|---|---|
| Long, continuous seams (often > 5 m) | Continuous, fatigue-free arc travel |
| Heavy plate distortion due to high heat input | Controlled, repeatable heat input profiles |
| Inconsistent welder quality | Standardized WPS (Welding Procedure Specification) execution |
| High labor cost & scarcity of skilled welders | One operator can supervise multiple robot cells |
| Hazardous environments (heat, fumes, UV) | Robots operate inside shielded cabins with Fume Extraction Systems |
| Tight delivery schedules | Higher deposition rate and **duty cycle (typically 80–90% vs 30% for manual)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metrics to Remember:**
> - Manual welding duty cycle: **30–40%** (arc-on time)
> - Robotic welding duty cycle: **80–90%** (arc-on time)
> - Typical shipyard robot repeatability: **±0.05 mm to ±0.1 mm**
> - Shipbuilding productivity gain with robots: **2× to 4×** vs manual welding

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Robotic Welding Workcell

A complete robotic welding workcell in a modern shipyard consists of **six integrated subsystems**:

1. **The Manipulator (Robot Arm)** – multi-axis mechanical structure with servo-driven joints.
2. **The Welding Power Source** – inverter-based GMAW/FCAW/SAW source (e.g., Lincoln Power Wave, Fronius TPS, ESAB Aristo).
3. **The Wire Feeder & Torch Assembly** – delivers filler wire at a controlled rate; torch is mounted on the robot's end-effector.
4. **The Controller (Cabinet)** – the "brain" housing the CPU, servo drives, and welding interface.
5. **The Positioning Equipment** – headstock/tailstock positioners, gantry rails, or rotary tables.
6. **The Sensors & Safety System** – arc sensors, seam trackers, light curtains, and emergency stops.

## 2.2 Kinematic Configuration of Welding Robots

The number of **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** and their arrangement define the robot's working envelope.

- **6 DOF** is the industrial standard for ship welding, allowing the torch to reach any point at any orientation.
- For very large panels, the robot is mounted on a **7th axis linear track (gantry/rail)**.

$$n_{DOF} \geq 6 \text{ (for full spatial positioning of the torch)}$$

$$W = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n_{axes}} \left[ \theta_{i,min}, \theta_{i,max} \right] \times s_{track} \quad \text{(working envelope volume)}$$

Where $W$ is the working envelope, $\theta_i$ are the joint angles, and $s_{track}$ is the linear track length.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet – High-Yield Equations

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Welding Speed (Robot vs Manual) | $v_{robot} = \dfrac{L_{weld}}{t_{arc-on}}$ | m/min | Distance of weld per unit arc-on time |
| 2 | Deposition Rate | $R_{dep} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_w^2}{4} \cdot v_{wire} \cdot \rho_{wire}$ | kg/h | Mass of filler deposited per hour |
| 3 | Heat Input (critical for HAZ control) | $HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v_{travel} \cdot 1000} \cdot k$ | kJ/mm | $k$ = thermal efficiency (GMAW ≈ 0.8) |
| 4 | Robot Repeatability | $\sigma_{pos} \leq 0.1$ mm (typical) | mm | Statistical positional deviation over cycles |
| 5 | Productivity Ratio | $\eta_{p} = \dfrac{t_{manual}}{t_{robot}}$ | dimensionless | 2–4 in shipbuilding |
| 6 | Number of Axes Required | $n_{axes} = 6 + n_{external}$ | count | External = tracks, positioners |
| 7 | Cartesian Reach | $L_{reach} = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$ | m | Max tool-center-point distance |
| 8 | Arc Sensor Sampling Frequency | $f_s \geq 100$ Hz | Hz | For real-time seam tracking |
| 9 | Cycle Time for Panel | $T_{cycle} = T_{setup} + \sum \dfrac{L_i}{v_i}$ | s | Total robotic welding cycle |
| 10 | Power Source Duty Cycle | $DC = \dfrac{I_{weld}}{I_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | % | Welding current vs rated (60% at 10 min typical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat Input (HI) is the single most important formula in robotic welding for ship plates.** The KTU examiner will expect students to know that for **high-strength shipbuilding steel (e.g., EH36, AH36)**, HI must be limited to **≤ 2.0 kJ/mm** for multi-pass welds to preserve the Charpy impact toughness at -40 °C required by IACS UR W11/W13.

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Ship Production

- **Heat Input** directly governs HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) grain size — coarser grain = lower toughness = risk of brittle fracture in arctic-service vessels.
- **Deposition Rate** dictates how many meters of weld a robot can complete per shift (typical: **3–6 m/h for fillet welds in EH36 plate**).
- **Duty Cycle** explains why robotic cells are economically superior — the robot welds while the human operator loads the next part.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Productivity Comparison of Manual vs Robotic Welding in Panel Lines

This derivation follows the **standard KTU ESE analytical method** expected in 14-mark questions.

**Given Scenario (typical shipyard panel line):**
- Total weld length per panel: $L = 40$ m (stiffener-to-plate fillet welds)
- Manual welding speed: $v_m = 0.4$ m/min
- Robotic welding speed: $v_r = 1.2$ m/min (GMAW with tandem wire)
- Manual arc-on duty cycle: $D_m = 0.35$
- Robotic arc-on duty cycle: $D_r = 0.85$
- Shift length: $T_{shift} = 8$ hours = 480 minutes

**Step 1 – Effective travel speed including duty cycle.**

The effective travel speed accounts for the fact that the arc is "on" only a fraction of the total time (the rest is spent on slag removal, repositioning, rest breaks, etc.).

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{m,eff} &= v_m \times D_m = 0.4 \times 0.35 = 0.14 \text{ m/min} \\[4pt]
v_{r,eff} &= v_r \times D_r = 1.2 \times 0.85 = 1.02 \text{ m/min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 – Time to complete one panel (40 m weld).**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{m} &= \dfrac{L}{v_{m,eff}} = \dfrac{40}{0.14} = 285.7 \text{ min} \approx 4.76 \text{ h} \\[4pt]
t_{r} &= \dfrac{L}{v_{r,eff}} = \dfrac{40}{1.02} = 39.2 \text{ min} \approx 0.65 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 – Productivity ratio (η_p).**

$$\eta_p = \dfrac{t_m}{t_r} = \dfrac{285.7}{39.2} \approx 7.3$$

**Step 4 – Panels per 8-hour shift.**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_m &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{480}{285.7} \right\rfloor = 1 \text{ panel/shift (with } 194 \text{ min idle)} \\[4pt]
N_r &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{480}{39.2} \right\rfloor = 12 \text{ panels/shift}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 – Engineering interpretation.**

$$\text{Throughput ratio} = \dfrac{N_r}{N_m} = \dfrac{12}{1} = 12\times$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** In this realistic shipyard scenario, **robotic welding provides a 7× speed advantage and 12× throughput advantage** per shift. This is why every modern shipyard (Hyundai HI, Daewoo, Meyer Werft, Fincantieri, Cochin Shipyard) has invested in robotic panel lines.

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Input Verification for a Robotic Tandem-GMAW Pass on 25 mm EH36 Plate

**Given:**
- Welding voltage: $V = 32$ V
- Welding current: $I = 480$ A
- Travel speed: $v = 1.0$ m/min = **16.67 mm/s**
- Process: Tandem GMAW, thermal efficiency: $k = 0.85$

**Step 1 – Convert travel speed to mm/s.**

$$v = 1.0 \text{ m/min} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ mm}}{60 \text{ s}} = 16.67 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 2 – Apply heat input formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
HI &= \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \cdot k \\[4pt]
HI &= \dfrac{32 \times 480}{16.67 \times 1000} \times 0.85 \\[4pt]
HI &= \dfrac{15360}{16670} \times 0.85 \\[4pt]
HI &= 0.9215 \times 0.85 = 0.783 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 – Compare against IACS limits.**

$$HI_{calc} = 0.783 \text{ kJ/mm} \;\; < \;\; HI_{limit} = 2.0 \text{ kJ/mm} \;\; \checkmark$$

> **Verdict:** The procedure is **safe** for EH36 with adequate Charpy toughness at -40 °C.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Selection of Robot Type vs Shipbuilding Application

| Ship Component | Typical Weld Type | Plate Thickness | Recommended Robot | Reason for Choice |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat Panel (deck/side shell)** | Fillet, stiffener-to-plate | 6–25 mm | **Gantry (Cartesian) Robot** | Long straight runs, large working envelope |
| **Curved Hull Section** | Butt + fillet | 15–40 mm | **6-axis Articulated Robot + Positioner** | Curved geometry needs 6 DOF torch orientation |
| **T-Joint Stiffener Welding** | Fillet, horizontal | 8–20 mm | **Articulated Robot on Linear Rail** | Multiple parallel stiffeners at fixed pitch |
| **Double Bottom Tank Internals** | All-position fillet | 6–15 mm | **Compact Articulated Robot** | Confined space, needs 6 DOF |
| **Pipe Spool Welding** | All-position butt | 10–30 mm | **Specialized Pipe-Welding Robot (orbital)** | Symmetric, 2-axis motion sufficient |
| **Block Erection (large assembly)** | Heavy butt welds | 30–100 mm | **Heavy-duty Articulated + Tandem/SAW** | High deposition needed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Robotic Welding Workcell Architecture in a Shipyard

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Operator / Supervisor] --> B[Robot Teach Pendant]
    A[Operator / Supervisor] --> C[Safety Control Panel]
    B --> D[Robot Controller CPU]
    C --> D[Robot Controller CPU]
    D --> E[Servo Drive Unit 1]
    D --> F[Servo Drive Unit 2]
    D --> G[Servo Drive Unit 3]
    E --> H[Axis 1 - Base Rotation]
    F --> I[Axis 2 - Shoulder]
    G --> J[Axis 3 - Elbow]
    H --> K[Mechanical Arm Assembly]
    I --> K[Mechanical Arm Assembly]
    J --> K[Mechanical Arm Assembly]
    K --> L[End Effector - Welding Torch]
    L --> M[Welding Power Source GMAW FCAW SAW]
    M --> N[Wire Feeder Unit]
    N --> O[Filler Wire Spool]
    L --> P[Workpiece Positioner]
    P --> Q[Ship Panel or Hull Section]
    L --> R[Arc Sensor / Seam Tracker]
    R --> D[Robot Controller CPU - Feedback Loop]
    M --> S[Fume Extraction System]
    L --> T[Slag Removal Station]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Control System Hierarchy of a Welding Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 1 - Task Planning Offline] --> L2[Level 2 - Motion Planning]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 - Servo Loop Control]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 - Welding Process Control]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 - Sensor Feedback]
    L1[Level 1 - Task Planning Offline] -.uses.-> CAM[CAD CAM WPS Data]
    CAM --> OFF[Offline Programming Software]
    OFF --> L2
    L5 --> ARCS[Arc Sensor Voltage Current]
    L5 --> SEAM[Optical Seam Tracker]
    L5 --> TACT[Tactile Touch Sensor]
    ARCS --> L3
    SEAM --> L3
    TACT --> L3
    L4 --> POWER[Welding Power Source Interface]
    POWER --> ARC[Real Time Arc Adjustment]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Classification Tree of Welding Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WR[Welding Robots Used in Shipbuilding] --> ART[Articulated 6 Axis]
    WR[Welding Robots Used in Shipbuilding] --> GAN[Gantry Cartesian]
    WR[Welding Robots Used in Shipbuilding] --> CAR[Cartesian Linear]
    WR[Welding Robots Used in Shipbuilding] --> SCA[SCARA]
    WR[Welding Robots Used in Shipbuilding] --> DEL[Delta Parallel]
    WR[Welding Robots Used in Shipbuilding] --> ORB[Orbital Pipe Welding]
    ART --> USE1[Curved hull, complex geometry]
    GAN --> USE2[Long flat panels, stiffener welding]
    CAR --> USE3[Simple straight welds, low cost]
    SCA --> USE4[Pick and place, not typical for welding]
    DEL --> USE5[High speed small welds]
    ORB --> USE6[Pipe spools, nozzles]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

Since drawing the full kinematics of a 6-axis robot in Mermaid is impractical, here is a **functional architecture** showing the control signal flow:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[Sensor Inputs Arc, Vision, Touch] --> CTRL[Central Controller]
    CAD[CAD Model and WPS Data] --> CTRL
    OP[Operator Commands Teach Pendant] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> SERVO[Servo Amplifiers - 6 Channels]
    SERVO --> M1[Motor Axis 1]
    SERVO --> M2[Motor Axis 2]
    SERVO --> M3[Motor Axis 3]
    SERVO --> M4[Motor Axis 4]
    SERVO --> M5[Motor Axis 5]
    SERVO --> M6[Motor Axis 6]
    M1 --> ENC1[Encoder Feedback]
    M2 --> ENC2[Encoder Feedback]
    M3 --> ENC3[Encoder Feedback]
    M4 --> ENC4[Encoder Feedback]
    M5 --> ENC5[Encoder Feedback]
    M6 --> ENC6[Encoder Feedback]
    ENC1 --> CTRL
    ENC2 --> CTRL
    ENC3 --> CTRL
    ENC4 --> CTRL
    ENC5 --> CTRL
    ENC6 --> CTRL
    CTRL --> WPS[Welding Power Source Command]
    WPS --> ARC[Stable Welding Arc]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a welding robot as per ISO 8373:2021 and list any four characteristics that make it suitable for ship production. `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**
A welding robot, as per ISO 8373:2021, is an **automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multi-purpose manipulator with three or more programmable axes**, used for welding applications. The four key characteristics making it suitable for ship production are:
1. **High repeatability** (±0.05–0.1 mm) for consistent weld quality on long seams.
2. **High duty cycle (80–90%)** ensuring continuous arc-on time.
3. **Integration with positioners and linear tracks** enabling handling of large hull panels.
4. **Sensor-based adaptive control** (arc sensing, seam tracking) to compensate for plate distortion and fit-up variations.

### Question 2: Differentiate between an articulated robot and a gantry robot used in shipbuilding welding applications. `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Articulated Robot | Gantry Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Configuration** | 6 rotary joints (RRR) | 3 linear axes (PPP) overhanging frame |
| **Working Envelope** | Spherical / irregular | Rectangular, very large (e.g., 30 m × 8 m) |
| **Cost** | Moderate to high | High (civil foundation required) |
| **Flexibility** | Excellent – can reach curved surfaces | Limited to rectangular workspace |
| **Best Use in Shipbuilding** | Curved hull sections, double-bottom internals | Long flat panels, stiffener welding lines |
| **DOF** | 6 (base, shoulder, elbow, 3 wrist) | 3 + optional rotation = 4–5 |
| **Example Brands** | ABB IRB, KUKA KR, FANUC R-2000 | IGM, Cloos, ESAB gantry lines |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A: Robotic Welding in Ship Panel Production `[14 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**Explain with a neat sketch the architecture of a typical robotic welding workcell used in modern shipyards for panel production. Describe the function of each subsystem.**

**Model Solution:**

A typical robotic welding workcell in a shipyard panel line consists of the following subsystems:

1. **Robot Manipulator (6-axis articulated or gantry):** Provides the spatial motion of the welding torch with 6 degrees of freedom. It moves the torch along the programmed weld path at a controlled travel speed. Gantry robots are preferred for panels due to their large rectangular working envelope.

2. **Robot Controller:** The central CPU that stores the welding program, computes joint trajectories using inverse kinematics, and generates servo commands. Examples: ABB IRC5, KUKA KR C5, FANUC R-30iB.

3. **Welding Power Source:** An inverter-based unit (e.g., Lincoln Power Wave S350) capable of pulsed GMAW, synergic GMAW, and tandem GMAW. It interfaces with the robot via **DeviceNet, Profibus, or EtherCAT** for synchronized control.

4. **Wire Feeder and Torch:** Delivers filler wire (typically 1.0–1.6 mm diameter) at rates of 2–20 m/min. The torch is water-cooled for high duty cycle operations.

5. **Positioner (Headstock + Tailstock):** Rotates the panel along its long axis to keep the weld in the **down-hand (1G/1F) position**, which is the most productive and produces the best quality.

6. **Sensors:**
   - **Arc Sensor:** Measures arc voltage/current variations to correct cross-seam position.
   - **Optical / Laser Seam Tracker:** Scans ahead of the torch to detect joint location, gap, and misalignment.
   - **Touch Sensor:** Mechanical probe that confirms the start point.

7. **Fume Extraction and Slag Removal:** Critical for occupational health; high-vacuum extraction hoods and automated chipping hammers.

> **Sketch:** Refer to the Mermaid workcell diagram in Section 4.1.

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**A ship panel requires 50 m of fillet weld. If the manual welding speed is 0.35 m/min with 30% duty cycle and the robotic speed is 1.0 m/min with 85% duty cycle, calculate (i) the time for each method, (ii) the productivity ratio, and (iii) the number of panels completed in an 8-hour shift.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 50$ m
- $v_m = 0.35$ m/min, $D_m = 0.30$
- $v_r = 1.0$ m/min, $D_r = 0.85$
- $T = 8$ h = 480 min

**(i) Time per panel:**

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{m,eff} &= 0.35 \times 0.30 = 0.105 \text{ m/min} \\[4pt]
v_{r,eff} &= 1.0 \times 0.85 = 0.85 \text{ m/min} \\[4pt]
t_m &= \dfrac{50}{0.105} = 476.2 \text{ min} \approx 7.94 \text{ h} \\[4pt]
t_r &= \dfrac{50}{0.85} = 58.8 \text{ min} \approx 0.98 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Productivity ratio:**

$$\eta_p = \dfrac{t_m}{t_r} = \dfrac{476.2}{58.8} \approx 8.1$$

**(iii) Panels per 8-hour shift:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_m &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{480}{476.2} \right\rfloor = 1 \text{ panel} \\[4pt]
N_r &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{480}{58.8} \right\rfloor = 8 \text{ panels}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conclusion:** Robotic welding is **~8× faster** and provides **8× more throughput** in this scenario.

---

### Question B: Control Systems of Welding Robots `[14 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**Describe the hierarchical control system of a modern welding robot. Explain the role of arc sensing and seam tracking in adaptive welding.**

**Model Solution:**

A modern welding robot control system operates on **five hierarchical levels**:

1. **Level 1 – Task Planning (Offline Programming):** The complete welding task is planned in OLP software (e.g., ABB RobotStudio, KUKA.Sim, FANUC ROBOGUIDE) using the 3D CAD model of the panel. WPS parameters are assigned to each weld segment.

2. **Level 2 – Motion Planning:** The OLP software computes joint trajectories using inverse kinematics. Singularity avoidance, collision detection, and time-optimal path planning are done here.

3. **Level 3 – Servo Control:** Each joint motor is driven by a digital servo amplifier with **PID control**. Position, velocity, and current feedback are processed at **1–4 kHz** for precise motion.

4. **Level 4 – Welding Process Control:** Real-time control of arc voltage, current, wire feed speed, and travel speed. The robot controller and welding power source communicate via **fieldbus protocols (EtherCAT, DeviceNet)** to maintain a stable arc and desired penetration.

5. **Level 5 – Sensor Feedback (Adaptive Layer):** This is where **arc sensing** and **seam tracking** operate:
   - **Arc Sensing:** Measures high-frequency variations in arc voltage/current. When the torch deviates from the joint center, the arc length changes, altering voltage. The controller shifts the torch laterally to recenter the arc. **Sampling rate: 100–200 Hz.**
   - **Laser/Optical Seam Tracking:** A laser stripe is projected ahead of the torch; a CMOS camera captures the reflection. Image processing software (in <10 ms) extracts the joint centerline and gap, correcting the robot's path in real time. **Accuracy: ±0.2 mm** typical.

This closed-loop architecture is what allows the robot to "self-correct" against plate distortion, thermal warping, and fit-up gaps — all of which are inevitable in ship production.

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of Offline Programming (OLP) in robotic welding. List its advantages over the conventional teach-pendant method for shipbuilding.**

**Model Solution:**

**Offline Programming (OLP)** is the process of creating and verifying the robot's welding program on a **separate computer using a 3D simulation environment** — without taking the physical robot out of production.

**Working Principle:**
1. Import the **CAD model of the ship panel or hull section** into OLP software.
2. Define the **weld seams** by selecting edges and assigning WPS parameters.
3. The software automatically **generates the robot program** (e.g., RAPID for ABB, KRL for KUKA).
4. **Simulate the program** in a virtual cell — checking reachability, collisions, singularities, and cycle time.
5. Download the verified program to the robot controller via **network or USB**.

**Advantages over Teach-Pendant Programming (specifically for shipbuilding):**

| # | Advantage | Benefit in Shipyard |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Zero robot downtime** | Ship panel is being welded while the next program is built |
| 2 | **Handles complex curved paths** | Teach-pendant cannot program long 3D hull curves accurately |
| 3 | **Cycle time optimization** | Software calculates time-optimal trajectories |
| 4 | **Collision-free path planning** | Critical because ship panels are surrounded by fixtures |
| 5 | **Digital twin validation** | Weld quality can be predicted before cutting the first plate |
| 6 | **Reduced skilled-labor dependency** | A CAD engineer can program, not a robot specialist |
| 7 | **Easy replication across panels** | Same program reused for sister panels in a series (e.g., 5 sister bulk carriers) |

> **Key industry tools:** ABB RobotStudio, KUKA.Sim, FANUC ROBOGUIDE, Yaskawa-Motoman PC integration, Delfoi ARC.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "Repeatability" with "Accuracy"** – Repeatability is the ability to return to the *same point*; accuracy is the ability to reach the *commanded point*. KTU examiner will deduct marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting the thermal efficiency factor (k)** in the heat input equation. For GMAW, **k ≈ 0.8**; for SAW, **k ≈ 1.0**; for GTAW, **k ≈ 0.6**. Wrong k = wrong answer.
> 3. **Mixing up "duty cycle" definitions** – For *robots*, duty cycle means **arc-on time fraction**; for *power sources*, it means the **rated welding time per 10-minute window** (e.g., 60% at 400 A). Examiner expects students to clarify which is meant.
> 4. **Ignoring plate distortion compensation** – In real shipyard panels, distortion of 5–15 mm is common; the answer must mention **seam tracking and adaptive control** to score full marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning IACS / class rules** – Heat input limits, Charpy testing, and weld procedure qualification (WPQR per ASME Section IX or ISO 15614-1) are expected for shipbuilding-specific questions.
> 6. **Skipping numerical units** – Always state units (kJ/mm, m/min, %, mm). Marks are reserved for correct units.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** Welding robot = reprogrammable, multi-axis (≥3) manipulator per ISO 8373:2021.
- **Most common type in shipyards:** **6-axis articulated robot** for curved geometry; **gantry robot** for flat panels.
- **Minimum DOF for welding:** 6 (3 position + 3 orientation).
- **Repeatability benchmark:** **±0.1 mm** for premium shipyard robots (ABB, KUKA, FANUC).
- **Heat input formula:** $HI = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \cdot k$ (in kJ/mm).
- **Critical HI limit for EH36/AH36:** **≤ 2.0 kJ/mm** (IACS UR W11).
- **Duty cycle (manual):** 30–40%; **Duty cycle (robot):** 80–90%.
- **Sensors in adaptive welding:** Arc sensor (100–200 Hz), laser seam tracker (±0.2 mm), tactile touch sensor.
- **Control hierarchy:** Task Planning → Motion Planning → Servo → Process → Sensor.
- **OLP software examples:** RobotStudio (ABB), KUKA.Sim, ROBOGUIDE (FANUC), Delfoi ARC.
- **Productivity gain:** **2× to 8×** depending on weld length and panel type.
- **Welding processes used with robots:** GMAW (CO₂ / Ar+CO₂ / pulsed), FCAW, Tandem GMAW, SAW, narrow-gap SAW.
- **Communication protocols:** EtherCAT, Profinet, DeviceNet, CAN bus between controller and power source.
- **Safety standards:** ISO 10218-1/2 (robot safety), IEC 62061, EN ISO 13849-1 (functional safety).
- **Major shipyard adopters:** Hyundai Heavy Industries, Daewoo Shipbuilding & Marine Engineering, Samsung Heavy Industries, Meyer Werft, Fincantieri, Cochin Shipyard Limited, Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders.
- **Key standards & rules:** IACS UR W11, W13, W17, W28; ISO 15614-1 (WPQR); ISO 3834 (quality); ASME Section IX.
- **Future trend:** **Collaborative welding robots (cobots)** with force-torque sensors for low-heat shipyard tasks; **AI-based vision systems** for 100% weld inspection integrated with the welding robot.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "where are welding robots used in ship production?", always answer with **four classic applications**:
> 1. **Flat panel line** (gantry + GMAW),
> 2. **T-stiffener welding** (articulated robot on linear track),
> 3. **Curved hull section welding** (6-axis articulated + 2-axis positioner),
> 4. **Pipe spool and nozzle welding** (orbital/2-axis specialized robot).
> Mentioning all four with one example each guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Difference Between Brazing, Soldering and Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Difference Between Brazing, Soldering and Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standardized)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding** is a material joining process in which two or more parts are coalesced (fused) at their contacting surfaces by the application of heat, with or without the application of pressure and with or without the use of filler metal. The base metals are actually melted at the joint interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brazing** is a material joining process in which a filler metal (with a liquidus temperature above **450 °C** but below the solidus temperature of the base metal) is melted and distributed by capillary action between closely fitted faying surfaces of the assembly, **without melting the base metal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soldering** is a material joining process in which a filler metal (with a liquidus temperature **not exceeding 450 °C**) is melted and distributed by capillary action between closely fitted faying surfaces, without melting the base metal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Boundary Marker (per ISO 857 / AWS):**
> - Soldering: Filler liquidus **≤ 450 °C**
> - Brazing: Filler liquidus **> 450 °C** but **< base-metal solidus**
> - Welding: Base metal itself is **melted**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are joining two pieces of wood:

- **Welding** is like *gluing* two ice cubes together by partially melting both ice cubes at the contact zone so that their frozen water molecules intermingle and then re-freeze as one continuous block. The "ice" itself becomes part of the joint.
- **Brazing and Soldering** are like sticking two metal plates together using a thin layer of a *third metal* that melts at a lower temperature. The two plates themselves never melt — only the glue-metal melts and flows into the gap by capillary attraction (the same force that pulls water up a thin straw).

The **only difference** between brazing and soldering is the **temperature threshold of 450 °C** that separates the two filler-metal families. Conceptually they are the same physics — *just different glue-metals.*

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Soldering–Brazing boundary | $T_{SB}$ | **450 °C** |
| Typical soft solder (Sn–Pb) | $T_L$ | **183 – 250 °C** |
| Typical brazing filler (Cu–Zn / BAg) | $T_L$ | **600 – 900 °C** |
| Mild steel solidus | $T_S^{steel}$ | **~ 1490 °C** |
| Mild steel liquidus | $T_L^{steel}$ | **~ 1520 °C** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles – Stepwise Logic

### A. Welding
1. Heat is concentrated at the joint line (arc / gas / laser / resistance / friction).
2. **Base metal reaches its melting point** — it becomes part of the weld pool.
3. Filler metal (optional) is added to fill the gap and provide alloying.
4. Upon cooling, **metallurgical bond** forms across the original interface.
5. Joint strength is generally **equal to or greater than** the parent metal.

### B. Brazing
1. Joint faces are precisely cleaned and fitted with a small clearance (typically **0.03 – 0.20 mm**) to allow capillary flow.
2. Heat is applied **uniformly** (torch, induction, furnace, dip, resistance).
3. **Base metal remains solid.** Only the filler metal melts.
4. Molten filler is drawn into the joint by **capillary action** (described by Jurin's law).
5. On cooling, a solid metallic bond forms by diffusion at the interface.

### C. Soldering
1. Physically identical to brazing, but filler melts at **≤ 450 °C**.
2. Flux is almost always used (rosin, zinc chloride, organic acids) to prevent oxide formation at such low temperatures.
3. The joint is **weaker and lower-temperature rated** than brazed joints.
4. Used mainly for electronics, plumbing, sheet metal, and heat-sensitive assemblies.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Heat Input & Capillarity)

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $HI = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ | Heat Input in **kJ/mm** (welding); $V$ = volts, $I$ = amps, $S$ = travel speed in mm/min |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta \, \cos\alpha}{\rho \, g \, r}$ | Capillary rise height (Jurin's law); $\sigma$ = surface tension, $\theta$ = contact angle, $r$ = gap |
| 3 | $P_{cap} = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Capillary pressure that drives filler into joint gap of half-width $r$ |
| 4 | $h = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | Rise of liquid filler between parallel plates of gap $r$ |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{T_L^{filler}}{T_M^{base}}$ | Temperature ratio (always **< 1** for brazing/soldering, **≥ 1** for fusion welding) |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{\rho \ell}{A}$ | Joint electrical resistance (important in soldering of conductors) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Heat input formula (1) governs HAZ grain size, distortion, and residual stress in ship hull welding. Jurin's law (2) explains why a brazed joint gap of **0.05 – 0.10 mm** is optimal — too tight leaves no flow channel, too wide breaks capillary suction.

## 2.3 Why These Distinctions Matter in Shipbuilding

- **Hull plates** (thick mild / DH36 steel) → **Arc welding** (SMAW, SAW, FCAW, GMAW).
- **Piping (copper-nickel, cupro-nickel condensers)** → **Brazing**.
- **Electrical panels, instrumentation, thin sheet HVAC ducts** → **Soldering**.
- Mixing the wrong process produces defective joints: e.g., trying to braze a part designed for welding will leave the base metal unmelted and the joint will fail under service load.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Comparison Matrix & Analytical Breakdown

## 3.1 Master Comparison Table (Examiner's Reference Grid)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 16-parameter matrix is the **single most important table** for KTU Module 3 questions. Memorise the bold values.

| # | Parameter | Welding | Brazing | Soldering |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Filler metal melting point | May equal / exceed base | **> 450 °C** | **≤ 450 °C** |
| 2 | Base metal melting | **Yes — fused** | **No** | **No** |
| 3 | Bonding mechanism | Metallurgical fusion | Capillary + diffusion | Capillary + adhesion |
| 4 | Typical filler examples | E7018, ER70S | BAg-1, BCuP, RBCuZn | Sn–Pb, Sn–Ag, Sn–Cu |
| 5 | Joint strength | **Equal to / > base** | 200 – 500 MPa | 30 – 70 MPa |
| 6 | Heat input | Very high | Moderate | Low |
| 7 | Distortion / warpage | High | Low | Negligible |
| 8 | Heat source | Arc / gas / laser / resistance | Torch / induction / furnace | Iron / torch / dip |
| 9 | Working temperature | 1500 – 3000 °C | 600 – 1200 °C | 200 – 400 °C |
| 10 | Flux required | Usually not | Yes (for oxides) | **Always used** |
| 11 | Surface prep needed | Moderate | **Very high** (clean + close fit) | **Very high** |
| 12 | Operator skill | High | Moderate | Low to moderate |
| 13 | Equipment cost | High | Moderate | Low |
| 14 | Joint thickness range | Almost any | Limited by capillary | Thin sheets / wires |
| 15 | Dissimilar metal joining | Difficult | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| 16 | Typical shipboard use | Hull, deck, bulkhead | Pipe, condenser | Wiring, electronics |

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation – Capillary Justification (Brazing / Soldering)

Starting from the **Young–Laplace capillary pressure** between two parallel plates of gap $r$ (metres), we obtain the pressure differential driving filler into the joint:

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}
$$

Substituting $\sigma = 0.5 \; \text{N/m}$ (molten BAg filler), $\theta = 20^{\circ}$, $r = 0.10 \; \text{mm} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}$:

$$
\cos 20^{\circ} = 0.9397
$$

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \times 0.5 \times 0.9397}{1 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{0.9397}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 9.397 \times 10^{3} \; \text{Pa}
$$

$$
\Delta P \approx 9.40 \; \text{kPa}
$$

This ~**9.4 kPa** of capillary suction is what *pulls* the molten filler into a gap narrower than a human hair — the reason joint clearance control is critical.

## 3.3 Heat Input Worked Example (Welding, for Contrast)

Given SMAW weld: $V = 25 \; \text{V}$, $I = 200 \; \text{A}$, $S = 200 \; \text{mm/min}$:

$$
HI = \dfrac{25 \times 200 \times 60}{200 \times 1000}
$$

$$
HI = \dfrac{300\,000}{200\,000}
$$

$$
HI = 1.5 \; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

This is **roughly 10 – 30 times** the heat input of a brazing operation on the same plate thickness, explaining the dramatic difference in distortion.

## 3.4 Decision Logic Flow

The engineer selects the process using this hierarchy:

1. If base metal **must be melted** → **Welding**.
2. If base metal cannot tolerate > 450 °C → **Soldering**.
3. If dissimilar metals, moderate temperature, good strength → **Brazing**.
4. If electronic / electrical continuity → **Soldering**.
5. If thick ship-hull structural duty → **Welding** (mandatory per IACS rules).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Joining Process Selection] --> B{Base metal melted?}
    B -- Yes --> C[WELDING]
    B -- No --> D{Filler liquidus above 450 C}
    D -- Yes --> E[BRAZING]
    D -- No --> F[SOLDERING]
    C --> C1[Fusion bond]
    C --> C2[Equal or higher joint strength]
    C --> C3[High heat input and distortion]
    E --> E1[Capillary bond without base metal melt]
    E --> E2[Moderate temperature 600 to 1200 C]
    E --> E3[Dissimilar metals possible]
    F --> F1[Capillary bond at low temperature]
    F --> F2[Filler liquidus up to 450 C]
    F --> F3[Used in electronics and plumbing]
```

## 4.2 Joint-Cross-Section Schematic (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Base Metal Plate 1] --> B1[Joint Interface]
    A2[Base Metal Plate 2] --> B1
    B1 --> C1{Filler Behaviour}
    C1 -- Welding --> D1[Filler fuses with base metal - HAZ formed]
    C1 -- Brazing --> D2[Filler fills gap by capillary action - base remains solid]
    C1 -- Soldering --> D3[Filler wets surface by capillary action - base remains solid]
```

## 4.3 Temperature-Timeline Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Room Temperature 25 C] --> P1{Process}
    P1 -- Welding --> W1[Ramp up to 1500 C plus]
    P1 -- Brazing --> BR1[Ramp up to 600 to 1200 C]
    P1 -- Soldering --> S1[Ramp up to 200 to 450 C]
    W1 --> W2[Base metal melts]
    BR1 --> BR2[Only filler melts]
    S1 --> S2[Only filler melts]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**"Distinguish between soldering and brazing. State the temperature that separates the two processes."**

**Model Answer:**
- Both processes join metals **without melting the base metal**, using capillary flow of a filler.
- They differ only in the **melting temperature of the filler metal**.
- **Boundary temperature = 450 °C** (per ISO 857 / AWS A3.0).
- Soldering: filler liquidus ≤ 450 °C (e.g., Sn–Pb, Sn–Ag).
- Brazing: filler liquidus > 450 °C but < base-metal solidus (e.g., BAg, BCuP, RBCuZn).
- Brazed joints are stronger and more temperature-resistant than soldered joints.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**"Why is flux used in soldering but not necessarily in welding?"**

**Model Answer:**
- Flux removes existing **oxide films** and prevents re-oxidation during heating.
- At soldering temperatures (≤ 450 °C) oxide films cannot be reduced thermally — they must be chemically dissolved; hence flux is **mandatory**.
- At welding temperatures (≥ 1500 °C) the high heat itself dissociates oxides, and shielding gas (in GMAW/GTAW) or slag (in SMAW) performs the same role. Hence flux is optional / process-dependent in welding.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question – Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**Question A:**
*(a)* Explain the fundamental differences between welding, brazing and soldering with respect to **(i)** base-metal melting, **(ii)** filler-metal temperature, **(iii)** bonding mechanism, and **(iv)** typical applications in shipbuilding. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A shipboard copper-nickel condenser pipe (Cupronickel 90/10) needs to be joined to a brass fitting. Discuss why **brazing** is preferred over welding or soldering in this application. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer – (a) [7 marks]
1. **Base-metal melting:** Welding → base metal **fused** at the joint. Brazing & Soldering → base metal remains **solid**, only filler melts. **(2 marks)**
2. **Filler-metal temperature:** Welding → filler (if used) melts at or near base-metal melting point. Brazing → filler liquidus **> 450 °C** but < base solidus. Soldering → filler liquidus **≤ 450 °C**. **(2 marks)**
3. **Bonding mechanism:** Welding → **metallurgical fusion** of base atoms. Brazing/Soldering → **capillary action + diffusion** at solid-liquid interface. **(1.5 marks)**
4. **Shipbuilding applications:** Welding → hull plating, decks, bulkheads, frames (SMAW/SAW/FCAW). Brazing → Cu-Ni piping, condenser coils, heat exchangers. Soldering → electrical panels, instrument wiring, thin-sheet HVAC. **(1.5 marks)**

### Model Answer – (b) [7 marks]
- Cu-Ni 90/10 has a melting point of ~**1100 °C**; brass melts at ~**900 °C**.
- **Welding** would require melting the base metals — risk of **hot cracking**, porosity, and loss of corrosion resistance in Cu-Ni; expensive and skilled. **(2 marks)**
- **Soldering** filler melts at < 450 °C — joint strength insufficient for pressurised sea-water service at ~**3 – 6 bar**. **(2 marks)**
- **Brazing** uses a filler like **BAg (silver braze)** at **600 – 750 °C** — below the melting point of Cu-Ni, so corrosion-resistant microstructure is preserved. **(2 marks)**
- Capillary action gives a leak-tight joint suitable for marine piping. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Part B Question – Choice B (14 Marks)

**Question B:**
*(a)* With a neat schematic, explain the **capillary action** mechanism that drives filler metal into a brazed joint. Derive the expression for capillary rise between parallel plates. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* For an SMAW weld, voltage = 24 V, current = 220 A, travel speed = 180 mm/min. Compute the heat input. If the same plate were brazed, comment qualitatively on the difference in **heat input, distortion, and HAZ**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer – (a) [7 marks]
- Schematic: two parallel base plates separated by gap $r$, with molten filler rising against gravity due to **surface tension** $\sigma$ and **wetting angle** $\theta$. **(2 marks)**
- Consider a vertical column of filler of height $h$ between two plates of width $w$, gap $r$. Force balance: **(2 marks)**

$$
F_{\text{up}} = 2 \sigma \cos\theta \cdot w
$$

$$
F_{\text{down}} = \rho \, g \, h \, r \, w
$$

Equating and solving for $h$:

$$
h = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, r}
$$

- This is **Jurin's law**. It shows that **smaller gap → higher rise**. This is why brazed joints are designed with a clearance of **0.03 – 0.20 mm**. **(3 marks)**

### Model Answer – (b) [7 marks]
- Heat input formula:

$$
HI = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}
$$

$$
HI = \dfrac{24 \times 220 \times 60}{180 \times 1000}
$$

$$
HI = \dfrac{316\,800}{180\,000} = 1.76 \; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**(3 marks for arithmetic)**
- **Heat input comparison:** Brazing heat input on the same plate would be a small fraction of welding input (typically < 0.3 kJ/mm). **(1.5 marks)**
- **Distortion:** Welding → large thermal stresses, plate warpage. Brazing → localised heating, negligible distortion. **(1.5 marks)**
- **HAZ:** Welding → substantial heat-affected zone with grain growth. Brazing → no HAZ since base metal never reaches transformation temperature. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marking traps in this topic:**
> 1. Writing only **temperature** differences and ignoring **bonding mechanism** — loses 2 marks easily.
> 2. Confusing **flux** role in soldering vs welding — examiners specifically test this.
> 3. Forgetting that in **brazing, the base metal does NOT melt** — students frequently write "both metals melt" which is wrong.
> 4. In numericals, **not converting travel speed from mm/min to mm/sec** in the heat-input formula — arithmetic marks lost.
> 5. Failing to mention **capillary action** when asked the bonding mechanism of brazing/soldering — a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three joining processes — one key differentiator: does the base metal melt?**
- **Boundary temperature between soldering and brazing = 450 °C.**
- **Welding** → fusion, high strength, high distortion, mandatory for hull.
- **Brazing** → capillary, no base-metal melt, dissimilar-metal friendly, used in Cu-Ni piping.
- **Soldering** → capillary, lowest temperature, electronics & plumbing.
- **Heat input** $HI = \dfrac{V I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ in kJ/mm.
- **Capillary rise** $h = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ — gap must be tightly controlled (0.03–0.20 mm for brazing).
- **Flux** is mandatory in soldering, optional/process-specific in welding.
- **HAZ exists only in welding** — never in brazing or soldering.
- **Joint strength hierarchy:** Welding > Brazing > Soldering.
- **Dissimilar-metal joining:** Welding (poor) < Brazing ≈ Soldering (excellent).
- **Shipboard use cases** — remember: **Hull = weld, Condenser = braze, Wiring = solder.**
- **Always convert units** in numericals: mm/min → mm/s when required, A × V → W → kJ.
- The keyword **"capillary"** must appear whenever you describe brazing or soldering.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adhesives and Bonding Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST741 - JOINING TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adhesives and Bonding Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Adhesive Bonding** in shipbuilding is a permanent joining process in which two or more similar or dissimilar substrates (metallic, composite, or polymeric) are united by means of a non-metallic substance — called an **adhesive** — that solidifies through chemical reaction (curing), solvent evaporation, or cooling, forming a load-bearing joint capable of transmitting structural, shear, tensile, or peel forces across the bonded interface.

In the context of KTU's PENST741 curriculum, adhesive bonding is recognized as a **secondary but critical joining method** complementing welding, riveting, and mechanical fastening, especially for:
- Aluminum alloy hulls and superstructures
- Fiber-Reinforced Plastic (FRP) composite hulls
- Sandwich panel construction (core-to-skin bonding)
- Decorative panels and interior outfitting
- Bonding of dissimilar metals (galvanic isolation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Point (High Yield):** Per the syllabus, an **adhesive** is defined as a substance capable of holding materials together by surface attachment (adhesion) and internal strength (cohesion), distinguishing it from a **sealant**, which only fills gaps and prevents fluid/ gas passage without significant load transfer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two bricks (**substrates**) sitting side by side. A **weld** would melt them into one continuous piece of metal. A **rivet** would punch a metal pin through them and crimp it. An **adhesive**, however, acts like **molecular Velcro with teeth pointing in every direction**: it flows into the microscopic pores of both surfaces, then **hardens (cures)** to form millions of tiny, intertwined hooks that lock the two surfaces together at the molecular level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipbuilder's Intuition:** Think of a ship's superstructure aluminum panel glued to a steel deck. Welding would cause **galvanic corrosion** (steel-aluminum dissimilar metal corrosion), and riveting would require drilling thousands of holes. Adhesive bonding solves both problems with a single continuous "chemical weld" that is also **electrically insulating** and **corrosion-resistant**.

### 1.3 Governing Standards and Key Metrics

In naval architecture and ship classification, adhesive bonded joints must conform to:
- **ISO 4587** — Determination of tensile lap-shear strength of rigid-to-rigid bonded assemblies
- **ASTM D1002** — Apparent shear strength of single-lap-joint adhesively bonded metal specimens
- **ASTM D3163** — Determination of strength properties of double lap shear by tension loading
- **ISO 14692** — Glass-reinforced plastic (GRP) piping for offshore use (relevant to composite ships)
- **Lloyd's Register / DNV-GL Rules** — Special requirements for adhesive bonding in marine applications

Key performance metrics emphasized in KTU board examinations:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lap-Shear Strength | $\tau_l$ | 10 – 40 | **MPa** |
| Peel Strength | $P_{peel}$ | 2 – 10 | **N/mm** |
| Glass Transition Temperature | $T_g$ | 60 – 180 | **°C** |
| Pot Life | $t_{pot}$ | 5 – 120 | **minutes** |
| Cure Time | $t_{cure}$ | 1 – 24 | **hours** |
| Service Temperature | $T_{svc}$ | –40 to +180 | **°C** |
| Coefficient of Thermal Expansion | $\alpha$ | 30 – 100 × 10⁻⁶ | **/°C** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Constant:** For shipbuilding applications, the most commonly cited reference lap-shear strength of a marine-grade **epoxy adhesive** on properly prepared mild steel is approximately **$20$ to $30\ \text{MPa}$** at room temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanics of Adhesion — The "Why" and "How"

An adhesive joint transfers load through **four fundamental mechanisms**, which must be understood sequentially:

### 2.1.1 **Wetting (Surface Contact)**
- **Why needed:** Adhesion cannot occur if the liquid adhesive cannot flow over and make intimate molecular contact with the substrate.
- **How it works:** Governed by **Young's equation** relating surface energies. Good wetting occurs when the contact angle $\theta < 90°$.
- **Engineering implication:** A surface contaminated with oil has high surface tension → poor wetting → weak joint.

### 2.1.2 **Adsorption (Molecular Bonding)**
- **Why needed:** Once wetted, the adhesive must form chemical or physical bonds with the substrate.
- **How it works:** Secondary bonds (**Van der Waals forces**, hydrogen bonds) for most adhesives; primary (covalent) bonds for structural epoxies with primers.
- **Engineering implication:** Higher bond energy = stronger, more durable, more chemically resistant joint.

### 2.1.3 **Mechanical Interlocking**
- **Why needed:** Microscopic surface roughness provides "anchor points" for the cured adhesive.
- **How it works:** Grit-blasted or chemically etched surfaces develop 5–50 µm peaks/valleys that the adhesive fills, then mechanically locks onto after curing.
- **Engineering implication:** Surface preparation can increase joint strength by **200% to 500%** compared to as-received surfaces.

### 2.1.4 **Cohesion (Internal Strength of Adhesive)**
- **Why needed:** Even with perfect adhesion, the joint fails if the adhesive itself is weak.
- **How it works:** Cross-linking of polymer chains during cure provides cohesive strength.
- **Engineering implication:** A well-designed joint fails **cohesively** (within the adhesive), not **adhesively** (at the interface) — this is the design goal.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "W-A-M-C":** **W**etting → **A**dsorption → **M**echanical Interlock → **C**ohesion. Examiners frequently ask students to list these in order.

## 2.2 Classification of Marine Adhesives

Marine adhesives used in shipbuilding are classified by **chemistry**, **curing mechanism**, and **structural function**:

### 2.2.1 By Chemistry (Most Asked in KTU)

| Family | Chemistry | Marine Use | Strength | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Epoxy** | Two-part: Resin + Amine/Hardener | Hull-to-deck, FRP joints, repair | **High (20–40 MPa)** | Excellent water/chemical resistance |
| **Polyurethane (PU)** | Two-part: Polyol + Isocyanate | Deck-to-hull, flexible joints | Medium (10–25 MPa) | Flexible, vibration damping |
| **Phenolic** | Resole + Heat/Acid | Sandwich core (balsa/foam) | Medium-High | Fire-resistant, brittle |
| **Acrylic (MMA)** | Methyl Methacrylate + Initiator | FRP/thermoplastic bonding | High (15–30 MPa) | Fast cure, bonds oily surfaces |
| **Cyanoacrylate** | Single-part, moisture-cured | Temporary fixturing, small repairs | Low (5–10 MPa) | Very fast (seconds) |
| **Silicone** | Single-part, moisture-cured | Sealants, gaskets | Low (1–3 MPa) | UV/weather resistant |
| **Polysulfide** | Two-part | Deck seam sealing | Low | Fuel/oil resistant |

### 2.2.2 By Curing Mechanism

1. **Thermosetting (chemical cure):** Epoxy, PU, Phenolic, Acrylic — undergo irreversible cross-linking.
2. **Thermoplastic (solvent evaporation / cooling):** Hot-melt adhesives, contact cements — reversible.
3. **Elastomeric:** Silicone, polysulfide — remain flexible after cure.
4. **Pressure-sensitive:** Acrylic tapes — bond under light pressure, removable.

### 2.2.3 By Structural Function

- **Structural adhesives:** Load-bearing, $τ_l > 7\ \text{MPa}$ (epoxy, PU, acrylic).
- **Semi-structural:** Moderate loads (phenolic, modified epoxy).
- **Non-structural (sealants):** Gap-filling, no load (silicone, polysulfide).

## 2.3 Surface Preparation — The Most Critical Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** In shipbuilding, **~80% of adhesive joint failures** are attributed to inadequate surface preparation — not the adhesive itself. This is a guaranteed 2-to-3 mark sub-question in board exams.

The standard sequence used in naval applications (e.g., US Navy, Lloyd's Register):

1. **Degreasing** — Solvent wipe (acetone, MEK) or alkaline cleaning to remove oils.
2. **Abrasion** — Grit blasting (alumina or garnet, 60–80 mesh) to ISO 8501-1 **Sa 2.5** "near-white metal" standard.
3. **Dust removal** — Vacuum or oil-free compressed air.
4. **Chemical etching** — For aluminum: chromate or chromate-free conversion coating (e.g., Alodine).
5. **Priming** — Application of a thin adhesion promoter (e.g., silane coupling agent for composites).
6. **Adhesive application** — Within 4 hours of surface prep to prevent re-contamination.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the essential equations for KTU 2024 Scheme board exam calculations on adhesively bonded joints:

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables / Description | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lap-Shear Strength** | $\tau_l = \dfrac{F_{max}}{w \cdot l}$ | $F_{max}$ = max load (N), $w$ = width, $l$ = overlap length (mm) | Single-lap joint capacity |
| 2 | **Adhesive Shear Stress (avg)** | $\tau_{avg} = \dfrac{P}{A_{bond}}$ | $P$ = applied load, $A_{bond}$ = bonded area | Quick design check |
| 3 | **Single-Lap Peak Shear** | $\tau_{peak} \approx \tau_{avg}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{3(1-m)}{4}\cdot\dfrac{L}{t}\right)$ | $m$ = Poisson's ratio, $L$ = overlap, $t$ = adherend thickness | Accounts for bending |
| 4 | **Peel Force (per unit width)** | $P_{peel} = \dfrac{F_{peel}}{w}$ | $F_{peel}$ = peel load, $w$ = width | Flexible joint design |
| 5 | **Clément's Peel Equation** | $P = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{E \cdot t^3 \cdot \tau_f}$ | $k$ = constant, $E$ = adhesive modulus, $t$ = thickness, $\tau_f$ = yield stress | Analytical peel strength |
| 6 | **Volkersen Shear-Lag** | $\dfrac{d\tau}{dx} = \dfrac{G_a}{t_a}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2 t_2}\right)\!\tau$ | $G_a$ = adhesive shear modulus, $t_a$ = adhesive thickness | Non-uniform shear distribution |
| 7 | **Glass Transition (Fox Eqn)** | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \sum_i \dfrac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$ | $w_i$ = weight fraction, $T_{g,i}$ = Tg of component i (in K) | Predict blend Tg |
| 8 | **Cure Kinetics (Arrhenius)** | $k_c = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}$ | $A$ = pre-exponential, $E_a$ = activation energy, $T$ = cure temp (K) | Cure time estimation |
| 9 | **CTE Mismatch Strain** | $\varepsilon_{CTE} = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | $\Delta\alpha$ = CTE difference, $\Delta T$ = temperature change | Thermal stress design |
| 10 | **Safety Factor** | $SF = \dfrac{\tau_{ult}}{\tau_{applied}}$ | $\tau_{ult}$ = ultimate shear, $\tau_{applied}$ = working stress | Design margin (typically $SF \geq 4$) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Note:** In all the formulas above, vertical bars for absolute value have been replaced with the $\vert$ or $\mid$ convention where needed to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax. The **Volkersen equation** is the most-tested differential equation in KTU module 3 problems.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Shipbuilding

| Application | Adhesive Type | Why Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| Aluminum superstructure-to-steel deck | Epoxy + spacer tape | Galvanic isolation, weight saving |
| FRP hull-to-bulkhead | Polyurethane | Tolerates differential thermal expansion |
| Sandwich panel (balsa/foam core) | Phenolic or PU film | Fire resistance + shear stiffness |
| Deck-to-hull seam (above waterline) | Polysulfide / PU sealant | Watertight, flexible |
| Propeller-shaft liner | Epoxy mortaring | Vibration damping, corrosion barrier |
| Composite repair patch | Methacrylate (MMA) | Bonds to oily/wet surfaces in emergency |
| Interior panel fit-out | Contact adhesive (neoprene) | Fast, large-area, no clamping |
| Window/porthole glazing | Silicone | UV resistant, remains elastic |
